unit.name: Case Packer
unit.cfj: Group and load cartoned units into shipping cases for tertiary packaging

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoned units from different products or lots being grouped for one shipping case
rows.plain: Avoid putting boxed units from different products or production groups into the same shipping case. This helps prevent products from being mixed up during delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Grouping different products/lots into one case is a core mixup error (relevance 9) with catastrophic recall/safety impact (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce commingling, but reliance on manual segregation leaves a persistent risk of mixed lots. Line clearance and segregation checks between products add setup time. Preventing mixed products depends on disciplined changeover procedures and operator vigilance. A mixed-product case is a serious, costly quality failure requiring investigation and potential recall.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case containing cartoned units from different products or lots
rows.plain: Avoid approving a shipping case that contains boxed units from different products or production groups. This helps ensure each case contains the correct items.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with mixed products/lots is a critical verification failure at the acceptance gate (relevance 9) with catastrophic recall/safety consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Procedural line clearance helps, but manual acceptance remains a recurring vulnerability to mixed contents. Content verification before acceptance takes additional time. Catching mixed lots at acceptance depends on operator judgment absent automated checks. Accepting a mixed-lot case is a severe quality and compliance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of products from different lots being packed into the same case
rows.plain: Make sure products from separate production groups are not packed together in one case. This helps keep products correctly identified and separated.
rows.imp_rat: Packing different lots into the same case strikes at the caser's core output integrity; severity is very high because lot mixing destroys traceability and triggers recalls in regulated products.
rows.sat_rat: The caser itself has little inherent lot-tracking, so separation depends on procedural discipline and flushing, leaving frequent residual risk of commingling. Preventing lot commingling relies on timed line clearing between lots, which forces deliberate pauses that slow the transition. Ensuring clean lot separation demands vigilant coordination between operators and traceability staff, making it attention-intensive and error-prone. A single mixed-lot case can trigger a broad recall or lot hold, so the financial exposure from failure is very high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator contact with moving caser components during alignment correction
rows.plain: Make sure the operator cannot touch moving parts while correcting the position of the case. This helps prevent hand injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact with moving caser components during alignment correction is central to safe casing operation, and the consequence is crushing or amputation injuries, making severity maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and procedures aim to prevent contact with moving parts, but frequent live corrections mean contact hazards are poorly controlled in practice. Alignment correction is often done quickly on the fly rather than after a full stop, prolonging exposure only briefly. Reaching into a running machine to correct alignment demands risky operator judgement that no tool reliably replaces. Proper stop-and-clear practice costs production time, incentivizing unsafe live adjustments that carry no equipment cost.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product residue, allergen, or hazardous substance remaining on machine surfaces, conveyors, and cavities before transfer
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover medicine, allergy-causing material, or dangerous substance as possible from the equipment before it is moved. This prevents those materials from being carried into another area or process.
rows.imp_rat: Product residue, allergen, or hazardous substance on surfaces before transfer is central to safe changeover, and severity is maximal given allergen cross-contact and toxic exposure risks.
rows.sat_rat: Manual cleaning leaves recurring residue in conveyors and cavities that is hard to verify as fully removed. Thorough surface, conveyor, and cavity cleaning is labor-intensive and slow. Removing allergens and hazardous residue to spec relies on disciplined manual cleaning technique. Cleaning agents, water, and validation swabs consume meaningful resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing the machine for return with unresolved pharmaceutical contamination
rows.plain: Make sure the machine is not returned for repair or service while medicine residue or other contamination is still present. Thorough cleaning protects people, equipment, and other work areas.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing machinery with unresolved pharmaceutical contamination is highly relevant to the packaging job's integrity in regulated production, and the consequences are catastrophic: cross-contamination of subsequent products, patient harm, and recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Without consistent verified cleaning validation, machines are sometimes released with unresolved contamination. Verifying contamination clearance before release adds swab-test and hold time. Judging whether pharmaceutical contamination is fully resolved needs QA/validation expertise not always applied at disposal. Swab testing, lab analysis, and rework of failed releases carry notable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an operator or maintenance technician being injured by unexpected cartoning machine movement during jam or fault clearing
rows.plain: Prevent the cartoning machine from moving unexpectedly while an operator or repair worker is clearing a jam or fault. This helps prevent serious injury.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from unexpected movement during jam clearing is a leading cartoning hazard, central to the job (relevance) and catastrophic in outcome including crushing or amputation (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where LOTO and guard interlocks are rigorously applied injuries are rare, but real-world shortcuts during jam clearing keep this a recurring residual hazard. Lockout/tagout procedures add deliberate time before intervention, which is the intended safety trade-off rather than a failing. Effective protection depends heavily on the technician correctly following LOTO discipline, which remains an error-prone human step. LOTO hardware is cheap, but production downtime during proper de-energization carries real cost that tempts shortcuts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual product or hazardous material remaining in the cartoning machine at transfer
rows.plain: Make sure no product or dangerous material is left inside the cartoning machine before it is handed over. This helps protect the next person who receives or uses the machine.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or hazardous material remaining at transfer is central to safe machine handover, and the consequences are catastrophic for cross-contamination, worker exposure, and regulatory violations.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning covers accessible surfaces but hidden cavities routinely retain residual product at transfer. Ensuring no residual product remains lengthens teardown with repeated inspection. Locating trapped product in cavities and dead legs requires machine-specific familiarity. Cleaning consumables and disposal of residue-laden waste add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons containing an incorrect or missing product unit or required insert
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes that have the wrong product, are missing a product, or lack a required leaflet or other insert. This helps ensure customers receive complete and correct packages.
rows.imp_rat: A carton with incorrect/missing product or insert is the direct failure of the core cartoning job (maximal relevance), and shipping such defects risks recalls, regulatory action, and consumer harm (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Presence-detection and reject systems catch most missing units and inserts, but thin or flexible inserts and sensor blind spots still allow some incomplete cartons through. Missing-unit and insert detection is inline and does not add cycle time when working. Presence sensors and insert detectors reduce reliance on operator vigilance for inserts. Reject of complete cartons and detection hardware add cost, though detection is cheaper than recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of grouped cartoned units being loaded into a shipping case assigned to another product or lot
rows.plain: Make sure grouped cartons are put into the shipping case marked for the correct product and batch. This prevents products from being sent with the wrong identification.
rows.imp_rat: Loading groups into a case assigned to another product/lot is a direct mixup/mislabel error (relevance 9) with severe recall and traceability consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Staging discipline and labels mitigate this, but manual matching leaves a recurring misassignment risk. Confirming case-to-product assignment adds verification time at changeovers. Correct assignment relies on operator attention to labeling and staging discipline. Loading into the wrong case triggers costly rework or product holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nonconforming cartons incorrectly classified as acceptable during discharge inspection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of faulty boxes that are mistakenly approved during the final check. This helps keep incorrect packages from being sent out.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying nonconforming cartons as acceptable is a false-accept escape at final inspection, central to guaranteeing output quality (high relevance) and directly releases defects to customers (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vision and checkweigh systems reliably catch gross defects, but false-negatives on marginal defects remain a persistent recurring gap. Discharge inspection runs at line speed so classification happens without slowing throughput. Calibrating inspection thresholds to avoid false-accepts demands quality-engineering expertise that is not always on hand. Escaped nonconforming cartons cause complaints and recalls, and current inspection reduces but does not eliminate this expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoned units from different products, strengths, or lots being grouped in one shipping case
rows.plain: Prevent cartons containing different products, product strengths, or production groups from being packed together in one shipping box. This helps ensure customers receive exactly what was intended.
rows.imp_rat: Grouping different products/strengths/lots into one case is a critical mixup at the grouping step (relevance 9) with catastrophic recall/safety consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Barcode verification and one-lot-per-line practices catch most, but manual grouping steps leave residual error. Grouping controls are performed inline without meaningful added delay. Correct grouping still leans on vigilant operators and clear setup instructions. Wrong grouping wastes materials and labor when detected downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a shipping case contains cartoned units from different products, strengths, or lots
rows.plain: Prevent a shipping box from containing cartons with different products, product strengths, or production groups. This helps avoid sending customers the wrong combination of products.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing products/strengths/lots in one case is a classic product-mixup, central to correct grouping (relevance 9); severity is catastrophic (10) with recall and patient-safety exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Physical segregation, line clearance and vision/scanning reduce but do not eliminate commingling risk. Line clearance and changeover checks take time but are embedded in normal cadence. Preventing mixed-product cases depends heavily on operator attention and segregation discipline. A mixed case triggers costly rework or rejection, motivating existing controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of residual pharmaceutical or biological contamination on or inside the warehouse caser at handoff
rows.plain: Leave as little medicine residue or biological contamination as possible on or inside the warehouse packing machine when it is handed back. This reduces the risk to the provider and the next user.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pharmaceutical or biological contamination at handoff is central for regulated casing operations and severity is very high because cross-contamination can compromise patient safety and trigger recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation was designed for production, not for equipment slated for disposal, so residual contamination at handoff remains a frequent unresolved surprise. Decontamination-then-verification cycles are slow, and repeated swabbing to prove residual contamination is gone adds days before handoff. Confirming absence of pharmaceutical or biological residue on a large caser demands trained QC/decon personnel and validated cleaning knowledge that few disposal crews possess. Solvents, PPE, and repeated analytical assays add real cost, and a failed release forces expensive re-cleaning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-run product or packaging materials remaining in the caser after changeover
rows.plain: Make sure no product or packaging from the previous run is left in the packing machine after switching to a different product. This prevents the old product from mixing with the new one.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-run materials remaining after changeover is a core changeover-quality error central to reliable casing; severity is high due to product mix-up, contamination, and lot-integrity failures.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies almost entirely on visual sweep checklists with no automated detection, so residual material slipping through is a recurring, poorly-mitigated failure. Manual clearing of residual product or packaging after changeover adds unpredictable minutes to line restart because operators must physically inspect the machine's internal magazines and hoppers. Catching every stray blank or product remnant depends heavily on operator diligence and familiarity with the caser's hidden pockets, so quality varies with who is on shift. Missed prior-run materials cause mixed-product cases that get rejected or recalled, wasting both the trapped material and the affected finished cases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel remain in the caser's safeguarded area when the cycle is initiated
rows.plain: Make sure no people are still inside the machine’s protected operating area when the cycle starts. This helps prevent people from being hurt by the moving equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel present in the safeguarded area at cycle start is a defining life-safety error central to caser operation and catastrophic when it occurs; both proxies are maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Perimeter guarding and presence detection catch most intrusions, but occlusion, muting misuse and gaps around infeed/outfeed leave recurring exposure. Area scans and light-curtain checks add little cycle time before initiation. Presence-sensing removes reliance on the operator visually confirming the zone is clear, but manual override still depends on judgement. Light curtains, area scanners and safety mats add appreciable cost and are not always fully deployed around large casers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a nonconforming protective response is recorded as passing against the approved safety specification
rows.plain: Do not record a safety response as successful unless it actually meets the approved safety requirements. This keeps test results accurate and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Recording a nonconforming protective response as passing against the approved safety spec is a data-integrity failure central to validation, and its consequences are catastrophic because it masks a real hazard and undermines compliance standing.
rows.sat_rat: Signed protocols reduce false passes, but ambiguous criteria and confirmation bias still let nonconforming responses be recorded as passing. Recording and reviewing test results adds little time to validation. Judging pass/fail against an approved safety spec requires disciplined reviewers who understand acceptance criteria. A false pass is cheap to record but potentially very costly downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of caser surfaces and product-contact components with contaminant residues above release limits
rows.plain: Remove enough leftover dirt, germs, and product from the machine and product-touching parts to meet the allowed limits. This helps ensure the machine is ready for safe production.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminant residues above release limits on product-contact surfaces are central to compliant casing and severity is very high since exceeding release limits directly breaches quality standards and risks recall.
rows.sat_rat: Concealed and hard-to-reach contact components frequently retain residues above limits, so results vary and re-cleaning cycles are common. Swabbing every product-contact surface and awaiting lab results is time-consuming and often bottlenecks the whole disposal timeline. Interpreting release limits and sampling representative surfaces requires validated cleaning expertise not typically held by teardown crews. Analytical testing per surface and rework of failed areas consumes budget that disposal projects rarely allocate generously.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packed lot enters shipment flow before final disposition approval
rows.plain: Do not let a packed batch be shipped before the final approval is given. This prevents unapproved product from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: A lot entering shipment before disposition approval bypasses the release gate (relevance 9) and can send unapproved product to market with catastrophic compliance consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine locations and ERP status locks make premature shipment-flow entry infrequent. Disposition hold delays shipment slightly but is standard workflow. Enforcement relies on QA and warehouse discipline more than specialized skill. Premature shipment can force costly retrieval, so systemic holds are maintained.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that emergency-stop activation fails to bring the caser to a safe state
rows.plain: Make sure pressing the emergency-stop button reliably stops the case-packing machine and leaves it in a safe condition. This protects people when something goes wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Emergency-stop failure to bring the caser to a safe state defeats the last-line safety function, making it highly central, and its failure can cause fatal injury, so severity is maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Rated safety relays and documented tests make e-stop failures uncommon, though wiring or configuration errors still occasionally defeat the safe-state. E-stop functional testing is a brief, scheduled commissioning task. Testing that an e-stop achieves a safe state requires controls technicians familiar with safety circuits. Testing is low-cost and prevents expensive incidents and downtime.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous motion continues when a caser guard is opened
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous machine movement stops when the case-packing machine's guard is opened. This protects anyone who opens the guard.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous motion continuing when a guard is opened defeats the primary interlock protection of the caser, making it highly central, and the consequence is severe entanglement injury.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked guards are a mature norm, though bypassed or miswired interlocks occasionally allow motion to continue when a guard opens. Guard-interlock verification is a quick, discrete check during setup. Confirming interlock-triggered stops requires controls competence that is widely available among commissioning staff. Interlock hardware and testing are inexpensive and standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packed lot failing release criteria is approved for release
rows.plain: Prevent a packed batch that does not meet the requirements for release from being approved for shipment or use.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a failing lot defeats the quality-gate purpose central to the packing job (relevance 9); severity is maximal (10) because non-conforming product reaches market, risking recall and patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Mature QMS release-gate controls and electronic status locks make approving a failing lot uncommon though not impossible. Disposition gating adds review time but is a routine step, not a chronic delay. Release decisions rely on trained QA judgement that most sites reliably staff. An erroneous release is costly through recall, but formal hold-and-release controls limit occurrence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while removing a rejected or jammed carton during an active production run
rows.plain: Protect the operator from injury while removing a damaged or stuck carton during production. This helps prevent contact with moving equipment or other hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury while removing jammed cartons during active runs is a frequent, central cartoning scenario (relevance) with severe consequences from moving mechanisms (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because reject/jam clearing often happens without a full stop, guarding cannot fully cover it and injuries from reaching into moving mechanisms remain a common residual risk. Removing a jam during an active run tempts operators to reach in quickly rather than stop the line, so time pressure directly undermines safety. Safe reject clearing during running production relies heavily on operator judgement and vigilance, which is inconsistent. Stopping the line to clear a single reject costs throughput, creating economic pressure to intervene while running.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual product or contaminants being released into the sterile fill-finish environment during caser decommissioning
rows.plain: Prevent leftover product or harmful dirt from entering the clean filling area while the carton-packing machine is taken out of service. This keeps the area and nearby product protected.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing residual product or contaminants into a sterile fill-finish environment during decommissioning is highly relevant given the regulated context, and severity is maximal because sterile-environment breaches can cause batch loss, patient risk, and regulatory action.
rows.sat_rat: Preventing contaminant release into an operational sterile fill-finish area during teardown is a poorly bounded problem with recurring deviations. Containment and cleaning before breaking into the sterile line extends the decommissioning schedule considerably. Maintaining sterile-area integrity during decommissioning demands cleanroom discipline that disposal crews rarely have. Containment tenting, gowning, and area re-qualification carry high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolation points that continue transmitting electrical power, air pressure, hydraulic pressure, or mechanical motion after isolation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of places where electricity, pressurized air, pressurized liquid, or moving force is still getting through after the energy is supposed to be shut off. This helps prevent unexpected movement, release, or injury.
rows.imp_rat: Isolation points still transmitting energy after isolation is a foundational safety error in casing maintenance, and residual live energy can cause fatal harm, giving maximal severity.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation points that leak power or pressure after nominal isolation still occur, especially with aging valves, so verification catches most but not all. Verifying each isolation point is truly dead adds inspection time to the sequence. Checking that valves, breakers, and blocks actually stopped transmission requires methodical verification skill. Verification tools are cheap and already part of the toolkit.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous areas that remain accessible after the caser guards are installed
rows.plain: Limit how many dangerous areas can still be reached after the guards are put on the case-packing machine. This helps keep people away from moving or otherwise dangerous parts.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous areas remaining accessible after guards are installed is a central guarding-verification error for casers, and the consequence is worker contact with moving parts, so severity is maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and gap gauges catch most exposures, but as-built deviations frequently leave a residual accessible hazard until a formal risk assessment. A guard-gap survey after installation is a short, bounded inspection. Identifying remaining reach-in and gap hazards requires knowledge of safety-distance standards that trained safety staff hold. Fixing accessible gaps late means rework of guarding, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of caser attachment points failing during lifting
rows.plain: Make sure the points holding the case-making machine do not break while it is being lifted. This helps prevent the machine from falling or being damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Caser attachment points failing during lifting is central to safe handling, and a dropped caser causes crushing fatalities and equipment loss, warranting maximal severity.
rows.sat_rat: Attachment-point failures are infrequent under rated practice but degraded or corroded points on end-of-life machines raise uncertainty. Inspecting and selecting attachment points adds a short pre-lift step. Judging whether original lifting eyes are still sound on retired equipment requires rigging expertise. Certified slings and shackles are inexpensive relative to the lift.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of materials from different batches or component lots being commingled at the case-packing station
rows.plain: Keep materials from different production batches or part groups from being mixed at the box-packing station. Mixing them can cause products to be packed with the wrong materials or records.
rows.imp_rat: Commingling different batches/lots at the station is a direct mixup at the source (relevance 8) with catastrophic recall/traceability consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce commingling, but station-level mixing remains a persistent manual-control risk. Segregating batches and lots adds line-clearance time. Preventing commingling depends on disciplined material handling and vigilance. Commingled lots create costly quality investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a lubricant or hydraulic-fluid release contaminating the sterile fill-finish environment during removal, holding, or transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that released oil or pressure fluid contaminates the clean production area while it is being removed, stored, or moved. This helps protect the product and the work area.
rows.imp_rat: A lubricant or hydraulic-fluid release into the sterile fill-finish environment during removal is highly relevant in the regulated context and carries catastrophic severity due to sterility breach, contamination, and potential batch destruction.
rows.sat_rat: Fluid leaks during removal and transfer through clean areas are a common, hard-to-fully-eliminate occurrence despite draining. Draining and capping fluid lines before movement adds handling time. Knowing where lubricants and hydraulic fluid can leak requires familiarity with the specific machine. Drip trays, absorbents, and disposal add modest cost.

rows.source_job: Position empty cases at loading station
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of positioning the wrong case format for the scheduled fill cycle
rows.plain: Put the correct size and type of empty case in place for the planned production run. This helps prevent the wrong packaging from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Positioning the wrong case format for the scheduled fill cycle directly defeats the casing job (high relevance) and results in misformed or unusable cases and stoppages when detected (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Case-magazine keying and format checks reduce errors, yet mismatches still slip through when schedule changes are communicated poorly. Verifying the correct case format for the scheduled cycle is a quick check but a wrong format forces a full stop and re-setup. Selecting the right case magazine/format depends on operator attention to the schedule, a routine but human-dependent judgement. Wrong-format cases are typically scrapped and the fill product may be lost, producing moderate waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the safety technician is exposed to hazardous motion or energy during validation
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that the safety technician is exposed to moving parts or stored power during testing. This helps prevent injury while checking that safety features work.
rows.imp_rat: Exposing the safety technician to hazardous motion during validation is central to safe validation of the caser, and the consequence is severe or fatal injury, giving maximal severity.
rows.sat_rat: Guarded test modes and procedures reduce exposure well, but validation inherently requires working near partially live equipment where residual risk persists. Protecting the technician during validation adds controlled, incremental time to the process. Safe validation of live protective functions leans heavily on scarce, experienced safety technicians exercising constant judgement near hazards. Proper guarding and procedures cost little versus injury exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a carton failing fill-accuracy, seal-integrity, or geometry checks continues downstream without rejection
rows.plain: Make sure a carton that fails checks for the amount inside, the seal, or its shape does not continue to the next step. This prevents defective cartons from being treated as acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Letting a carton that failed fill/seal/geometry checks continue downstream defeats the quality-assurance heart of the cartoning job (high relevance) and sends defective product to market with recall/safety consequences (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reject-confirmation feedback loops are mature and widely deployed, though gate jams or timing drift occasionally let a failing carton slip through. Inline reject mechanisms fire within the machine cycle so failing cartons are diverted with minimal delay. Setting reject-confirmation logic and gate timing requires trained technicians, but once configured it needs little ongoing judgement. Reject verification hardware adds cost but prevents costly downstream contamination of good product streams.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the caser being configured for the wrong SKU or case format during changeover
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the case-packing machine is set up for the wrong product or box type during a change. This helps ensure products are packed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Configuring the caser for the wrong SKU or case format during changeover is central to the casing job (relevance high) since every subsequent case will be mispacked, and the consequence is a run of bad cases, product damage, and downtime to correct (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-management systems on newer casers substantially reduce misconfiguration, but manual change-part machines still see recurring setup errors. Loading the correct SKU/case-format recipe is fast on recipe-driven casers but slow and error-prone on machines requiring manual mechanical adjustment. Correct configuration hinges on the operator selecting the right stored recipe and setting change parts properly, so it leans on trained personnel and remains the dominant vulnerability. A wrong configuration produces scrap cases and mis-packed product plus rework labor, a moderate but real material and time cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced into the sterile manufacturing environment during adjustment
rows.plain: Make it less likely that germs or other dirt enter the clean production area while the machine's moving parts are adjusted. This helps protect the product and the work area.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into a sterile environment during adjustment is relevant to maintaining aseptic packaging conditions, and severity is maximal because it can compromise entire sterile batches and patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and cleanroom practice limit contamination during adjustment, but any intrusion into an aseptic zone still carries meaningful, hard-to-fully-eliminate risk. Gowning, line clearance and re-cleaning after adjustment add substantial time in aseptic areas. Contamination-controlled maintenance in sterile zones requires specialized aseptic-behavior training that not all mechanics have. Cleanroom consumables, gowning and requalification make contamination control expensive per intervention.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual product or packaging material from the previous run remains in the caser at startup
rows.plain: Make sure leftover product or packing material from the previous run is not still inside the box-packing machine when it starts. This helps prevent mix-ups and unwanted material in the new run.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or packaging from a previous run at startup is directly central to correct casing output; severity is high because it causes mislabeled/mixed cases and quality holds in regulated environments.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanout procedures exist but are inconsistently executed between runs, so residual material remaining at startup is a fairly common cause of early faults. End-of-run and pre-start clearing routines add a short setup step when actually done. Detecting leftover product or packaging relies on operator visual inspection and thoroughness. Residual material left in the machine is minor waste with little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units incorrectly grouped or loaded after restart
rows.plain: Reduce the share of boxed units that are put into the wrong groups or loaded incorrectly after the line starts again. This helps prevent packaging mix-ups after a stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Units incorrectly grouped/loaded after restart is a core correctness error at a vulnerable transition (relevance 8) with serious escape potential (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Restart is a known weak point where mis-grouping spikes, and status-quo purge-and-check routines are inconsistently applied. Post-restart errors surface quickly during the first cases after resumption but only after some defective cases are produced. Clean restarts depend on operator discipline to purge and verify, an error-prone manual judgement. The first cases after restart are frequently scrapped, a small but repeated material and labor loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected or quarantined cases transferred into releasable inventory
rows.plain: Keep rejected or held cases from being moved into the stock that is approved for use or shipment. This helps prevent unsuitable product from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected/quarantined cases transferred into releasable inventory is a critical containment breach (relevance 8); defective product entering saleable stock is catastrophic (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Physical quarantine and ERP status locks reliably prevent most reject transfers into inventory. Segregation handling of rejects adds minor time. Keeping rejects out relies on procedural discipline more than skill. Reject-to-releasable transfer risks recall and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel being injured during the commissioning trial run
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that anyone is injured during the first trial of the newly installed equipment. The trial should show that the equipment works safely.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury during the commissioning trial run is central to safe bring-up of the caser, and the consequence is direct human harm, so severity is maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work, exclusion zones, and briefings work well, yet first-motion trials remain among the higher-residual-risk moments. Trial-run safety setup and briefings add planned time but do not dominate the schedule. A safe commissioning trial depends on experienced supervisors coordinating people and machine states under dynamic conditions. Preparation is cheap compared to injury and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case whose external identity does not match its contents
rows.plain: Make sure the outside label of a packed case matches what is actually inside. This helps prevent the wrong product from being shipped or used.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case whose external identity mismatches contents is a core acceptance error (relevance 8) leading to serious mislabeling and recall exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Barcode verification systems make this highly reliable where deployed, but manual label matching still allows recurring mismatches. Label-to-content checks add brief time at the closing point. Matching external identity to contents requires operator care unless scanned automatically. Identity mismatches cause costly downstream recalls and holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that emergency stops fail to halt the cartoning machinery from operator work positions
rows.plain: The emergency-stop controls should stop the packing machinery when pressed from any operator work position. This gives workers a quick way to prevent injury or further damage.
rows.imp_rat: E-stops failing to halt machinery from operator work positions defeats the last-resort safeguard where people actually stand, so it is highly relevant and catastrophic in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Hardwired, safety-rated e-stop systems are a mature, well-verified practice that reliably halts the machine from operator positions. E-stop testing from each work position is a fast, repeatable step in preparation. Pressing and confirming each e-stop halts motion needs little specialized skill beyond following the checklist. E-stop circuits are inexpensive and standard, adding negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons containing omitted or mispositioned products because of incoming product variation
rows.plain: Make sure every carton gets the right products in the right positions, even when incoming products differ in size or shape. This reduces missing or badly placed products.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted or mispositioned products from incoming variation directly defeats the cartoning job (high relevance), and missing-unit cartons reaching customers can trigger recalls and compliance issues (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vision and presence sensors handle typical variation well, yet edge-case incoming variation still yields intermittent omitted or mispositioned products. Product-presence checks and reject stations act in real time so bad cartons are caught within the cycle, but upstream product variation still passes through before detection. Skilled operators must tune infeed timing and product-orientation devices to handle variation, and this manual finesse remains a recurring dependency. Missed or mispositioned products drive rework and scrap, and current sensing catches most but not all before value is added.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case or product jams that interrupt the packing cycle
rows.plain: Prevent cases or products from getting stuck and stopping the packing process. Fewer jams keep packing moving smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Case or product jams that interrupt the packing cycle are the single most central error to the caser's core throughput job, though individual jams are usually recoverable, giving moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sensors and guides reduce jams but variable carton quality and product presentation make jam interruptions a chronic, frequent occurrence. Jams stop the packing cycle and consume operator time to locate and clear each occurrence. Preventing and clearing jams relies on operator experience reading the machine's behavior and material quirks. Jams waste some product and packaging and drive labor cost, though per-event loss is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that interlocks fail to stop hazardous motion when guards are opened
rows.plain: Make sure the safety controls stop dangerous movement when a protective cover or barrier is opened.
rows.imp_rat: Interlock failure to stop hazardous motion when guards open is central to safe cartoning operation (relevance) and can cause crushing or amputation injuries when it occurs (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature safety-rated interlock hardware and CE/ISO 13849 compliance practices make interlock-triggered stops highly dependable during preparation. Interlock function checks are quick to perform during preparation with standard test procedures. Verifying interlock stop function is routine for commissioning technicians using established checklists, requiring modest specialized skill. Interlock switches and verification add minor cost relative to the machine, well within standard budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoning machinery restarting unexpectedly during intervention
rows.plain: Prevent the packing machinery from starting again unexpectedly while someone is working on it. This helps protect the person making the intervention.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected restart during intervention is a classic fatal machinery hazard, central to safe servicing (relevance) and catastrophic when someone is inside the machine (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Guard-interlock and lockout practices make unexpected restart during intervention uncommon, but bypassed guards and incomplete LOTO still cause occasional restart events. Interlocked guards and LOTO prevent restart without adding operational delay once procedures are set. Guard interlocks reduce reliance on the technician remembering to prevent restart, though LOTO still requires disciplined application. Interlock switches and lockout devices are low-cost standard equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case with an incorrect carton quantity
rows.plain: Avoid accepting a packed case that contains the wrong number of cartons. This helps ensure customers receive the quantity they were supposed to get.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with incorrect carton quantity is a verification failure central to the job (relevance 9); it causes downstream count discrepancies and returns (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Count sensors and checkweighers catch most errors where installed, but manual acceptance decisions remain a recurring weak point. Verification checks add a small time cost at the acceptance point. Detecting an incorrect quantity before acceptance leans on operator diligence unless automated counting is installed. Accepting a wrong-count case propagates costly downstream corrections and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or mechanical energy sources left unidentified before caser disassembly
rows.plain: Before taking apart the case-packing machine, identify all sources of electricity, air pressure, fluid pressure, and stored mechanical force.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified energy sources before disassembly is a core lockout/tagout error for maintaining casers, and failing to identify them can cause fatal electrocution or crushing, so severity is catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Missed or undocumented energy sources are a classic cause of incidents, and status-quo drawings are often outdated for retired equipment. Surveying and mapping all energy sources adds upfront time before disassembly can begin. Correctly identifying every electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic and mechanical source depends on reading drawings and system knowledge that less-experienced crews lack. Energy-source identification is mostly labor cost with little material expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant contaminating sterile fill-finish materials or product-contact areas
rows.plain: Keep oil or grease away from sterile materials and areas that touch the product. This prevents the product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant contaminating sterile fill-finish materials is relevant to safe machine operation, and severity is catastrophic because chemical contamination of product-contact areas endangers patients and voids batches.
rows.sat_rat: Approved-lubricant programs and sealed lube points reduce contamination risk, yet over-greasing and migration into product-contact areas still occur. Using food/pharma-grade lubricants and containment adds little routine time. Selecting approved lubricants and avoiding migration to product-contact zones requires disciplined, trained practice. Registered H1/sterile-compatible lubricants and controlled dispensing cost more but are standard budgeted items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving a guard, interlock, safety sensor, or protective circuit bypassed after functional checks
rows.plain: Make sure every guard, safety lock, sensor, and protective system is turned back on after testing. This prevents the machine from being left in an unsafe state.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving a guard, interlock, or protective circuit bypassed after checks directly defeats installed safeguards (relevance) and creates severe injury exposure during subsequent operation (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Lock/tag and bypass-log practices exist but reliance on memory means forgotten bypasses remain a real recurring failure mode. A final walk-down to remove temporary bypasses adds a short defined step at the end of the job. Recognizing every jumper, forced input, or defeated interlock requires disciplined technician awareness that varies between individuals. Bypass-tracking tags and logs are inexpensive relative to the consequences of leaving one in place.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material or visible contamination inside a packed case remaining undetected
rows.plain: Make sure no foreign objects or visible dirt inside a packed case go unnoticed. This helps prevent contaminated or damaged products from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected foreign material/contamination inside a packed case is a critical contamination escape (relevance 7) with severe patient-safety/recall consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual inspection is the primary control and routinely misses contamination, leaving a persistent gap. Inspecting for contamination inside packed cases adds time before closure. Detecting foreign material or contamination reliably requires keen, sustained operator attention that is hard to maintain. Undetected contamination causes costly recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that caser motion continues after the operator initiates a stop
rows.plain: Lower the chance that the case-packing machine keeps moving after the operator presses stop. This helps prevent injury and damage.
rows.imp_rat: Motion continuing after a commanded stop is a direct failure of the primary safety control and catastrophic — personnel in the zone can be fatally injured. Both proxies are at the top.
rows.sat_rat: Monitored stop circuits with feedback detect welded contactors and prevent continued motion, making persistence of motion rare on maintained machines. Once a stop is commanded, motion ceases within the designed braking window without added delay. No operator skill is involved; the safety logic enforces the stop autonomously. Force-guided contactor feedback and safe-torque-off drives are standard and modestly priced in modern casers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of shipping cases released with unresolved grouping, loading, closure, or protection deviations
rows.plain: Do not release shipping boxes when there are still unresolved problems with how items are grouped, loaded, closed, or protected. This helps products arrive safely and in the expected condition.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases with unresolved grouping/loading/closure/protection deviations bypasses quality gates (relevance 8) and can send non-conforming shipments to market (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-closure gates exist but real gaps persist where minor deviations are informally cleared. Holding cases with open deviations delays release but is procedurally required. Judging whether a deviation is resolved requires quality expertise that can be inconsistently applied. Releasing with unresolved deviations risks costly downstream failure or recall.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required safety scenarios left untested during validation of the warehouse caser
rows.plain: Test as many of the warehouse machine’s possible safety situations as needed, so fewer important cases are missed. This helps show that the machine can protect people in different dangerous situations.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving safety scenarios untested during validation is highly central to a caser's job in a regulated environment, and the consequences are severe because an unvalidated protective function can lead to operator injury or regulatory non-compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols list scenarios, yet schedule pressure regularly leaves some required cases untested or deferred. Running every required safety scenario is time-consuming and often compressed under commissioning pressure. Knowing which scenarios must be tested demands safety engineers versed in the risk assessment and applicable standards. Skipped scenarios risk expensive post-startup incidents but testing itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical cartoning-machine alert goes unnoticed before affected cartons proceed downstream
rows.plain: Lower the chance that an important machine warning is missed before affected cartons move to the next stage. This helps staff deal with the problem before more cartons are affected.
rows.imp_rat: An unnoticed critical alert lets defective cartons proceed downstream, undermining the machine's job of producing correct, quality-controlled cartons (high relevance); the consequences—defective product reaching customers or recalls—are severe.
rows.sat_rat: HMI alarming and stack lights are standard, yet alarm flooding and unattended stations still let critical alerts go unnoticed. Alarms fire immediately, but operator response time depends on presence at the HMI. Interpreting which alerts are critical still requires operator experience to avoid overlooking key warnings. A missed alert lets defective cartons proceed, and alarm systems reduce but do not eliminate this loss.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to unexpected stored or restored energy during safety verification
rows.plain: Prevent the technician from being exposed to power that remains stored or suddenly returns while checking the machine’s safety systems. This helps protect the technician from unexpected movement or other harm.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to stored or restored energy during safety verification is a core hazard-control error, and the consequence is fatal or disabling injury, warranting maximal severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard energy-verification steps are effective but occasionally miss residual pneumatic or capacitive energy on unfamiliar caser models. Stored-energy dissipation and verification steps add modest, planned time. Recognizing pneumatic, spring, and capacitive stored energy demands trained technicians who follow energy-control procedures. Verification tooling is inexpensive relative to the harm it prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of carton groups containing more or fewer units than the specified case quantity
rows.plain: Have fewer cartons or groups with the wrong number of units in them. This helps ensure each carton contains the amount that was requested.
rows.imp_rat: Getting the specified case quantity right is the essence of grouping (relevance 9); wrong counts cause shipment errors and reconciliation failures (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Fixed pockets, indexing conveyors and count sensors make correct quantity fairly consistent, though manual grouping still produces occasional miscounts. Counting to the specified case quantity is quick when guides or count sensors are present but slows manual stations. Accurate counting relies on operator vigilance, which degrades over long shifts. Wrong-count cases waste materials and labor when reopened and corrected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying the wrong tooling or control parameters for the scheduled SKU
rows.plain: Prevent the wrong tools or machine settings from being used for the product scheduled next. This helps avoid incorrect production and possible damage or safety problems.
rows.imp_rat: Applying wrong tooling or control parameters for the scheduled SKU corrupts the entire run and is core to correct cartoning; severity is high because it produces mislabeled or defective product batches requiring rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Stored SKU recipes and parameter locking are mature, yet manual tooling swaps remain a residual source of mismatch error. Recipe-driven changeover loads SKU parameters quickly, minimizing setup delay. Verifying correct tooling for the SKU still leans on operator knowledge despite recipe systems. Wrong tooling or parameters cause scrapped cartons and downtime that stored recipes substantially reduce.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recording a passing result for a safety function that fails to respond correctly
rows.plain: Prevent someone from marking a safety feature as working when it does not respond correctly. This helps ensure that a real safety failure is not overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Recording a false pass for a failing safety function is central because it lets defective safeguards remain in service (relevance), and consequences are severe since latent failures cause injuries later (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual functional checks with paper sign-off catch most cases but human confirmation bias and ambiguous responses still let false passes recur, so consistency is only moderate. Documented functional test procedures with checklists take defined time but do not by themselves prevent a false pass from slipping through. Correctly interpreting a safety-function response still leans heavily on an experienced technician's judgement, so a less-skilled tech can wrongly record a pass. Standard test tooling and forms are cheap, but the cost of an undetected failed safety function is catastrophic and asymmetric.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protective-function requirements lacking unambiguous acceptance criteria in the approved safety specification
rows.plain: Make sure every safety function has clear rules for what counts as passing. This makes safety checks consistent and prevents uncertainty about whether protection works.
rows.imp_rat: Protective-function requirements lacking unambiguous acceptance criteria undermine the whole safety-validation job, and severity is high because ambiguity can let an unsafe machine pass or trigger disputes and compliance gaps.
rows.sat_rat: Approved safety specs frequently contain qualitative or open-ended criteria, so ambiguity in acceptance thresholds is a common, poorly-mitigated gap. Clarifying vague acceptance criteria late in the process creates review delays. Writing unambiguous, testable protective-function criteria demands functional-safety expertise that is genuinely scarce. Ambiguity forces rework of protocols and disputes, a recurring soft cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of loaded packaging component types that do not match the approved production batch
rows.plain: Make sure the packaging materials loaded into the machine match the approved production run. This prevents the wrong boxes or other packaging from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Loaded packaging components not matching the approved batch is central to correct cartoning output, and severity is high due to mixed-product errors, mislabeling, and batch rejection in regulated settings.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode-scan verification helps where deployed, but many lines still rely on manual matching, leaving frequent mismatch exposure. Component verification at setup adds a brief check before the run begins. Confirming that loaded components match the batch depends heavily on operator attention and manual cross-checking. Wrong packaging components trigger reruns and material scrap, only partly guarded by barcode checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of retained fluids or residue being released into the surrounding sterile facility during removal
rows.plain: Keep leftover liquid or material from escaping into the clean facility when it is removed. This helps protect the area from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Retained fluids or residue released into the surrounding sterile facility during removal is a contamination event with high relevance in regulated operations and severe consequences to facility sterility and compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Retained-fluid release into the surrounding sterile facility remains a recurring risk given incomplete draining of legacy equipment. Sealing and containing retained fluids before removal lengthens the operation. Anticipating trapped fluid pockets in a retired caser requires machine-specific knowledge. Containment materials add modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing hydraulic fluid, residue, or debris into the controlled manufacturing area during caser uninstallation
rows.plain: Avoid letting oily fluid, leftover material, or dirt spill into the clean manufacturing area while removing the case-packing machine. This helps keep the area clean and the products safe.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing hydraulic fluid, residue, or debris into the controlled manufacturing area during uninstallation is a serious contamination error, central to safe decommissioning and severe given the risk to the controlled environment and product.
rows.sat_rat: Residual hydraulic fluid and debris release during uninstall is a frequent contamination source that current controls only partially prevent. Pre-drain and spill-control setup adds time before uninstallation. Containing releases into a controlled manufacturing area needs both cleanroom and mechanical judgement. Absorbents, containment, and cleanup labor add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous cartoning machinery motion continues after correct emergency-stop actuation
rows.plain: Make sure the cartoning machine stops moving when the emergency-stop button is pressed correctly. This helps protect people from dangerous movement.
rows.imp_rat: Motion continuing after correct e-stop actuation defeats the primary emergency safeguard, so it is highly relevant to safe operation and catastrophic in severity, risking serious injury.
rows.sat_rat: Mature functional-safety standards (SIL/PL-rated dual-channel stop circuits, brakes, and energy dissipation) make continued hazardous motion after correct actuation genuinely rare on modern machines. E-stop circuits are designed to arrest motion within milliseconds to a defined stopping distance, so the time dimension is well handled by standard practice. Operators require no special skill to benefit since compliance to ISO 13850 and category-rated stop functions is engineered in, not operator-dependent. Safety-rated stop circuitry adds modest cost that is a routine part of any compliant cartoner build today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser fails to initiate stopping when the emergency stop is triggered
rows.plain: Make sure the box-packing machine starts stopping when the emergency stop is triggered. This helps limit dangerous movement after an emergency.
rows.imp_rat: An emergency stop that fails to initiate is central to safe caser operation and catastrophic when it occurs — personnel can be crushed or entangled with no fallback. Both proxies are near-maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant, self-monitoring safety relays and ISO 13849 compliance make failure-to-initiate extremely rare, though legacy machines and untested circuits leave residual risk. E-stop circuits initiate stopping in milliseconds, so time-to-response is essentially a non-issue under standard practice. Category 3/ -rated e-stop systems require no operator skill to function once engaged, only pressing the button. Safety-rated stop circuits and dual-channel monitoring are standard machinery-directive components already priced into modern casers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of released shipping cases with incorrect, unreadable, or mismatched identification
rows.plain: Prevent shipped cases from having wrong, unreadable, or mismatched labels or tracking information. Correct identification helps ensure the right products reach the right destination.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect/unreadable/mismatched case identification breaks traceability core to the job (relevance 8); mislabeled shipments carry severe recall/safety exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Print-and-apply with verification catches most defects, though print degradation and manual labels leave gaps. Identification verification is a quick inline scan. Correct, readable ID depends on setup and print-quality monitoring. Mismatched or unreadable ID causes rework and scanning failures downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a technician or nearby worker being struck or trapped by unexpected mechanism movement during jogging
rows.plain: Prevent a worker from being hit or caught by a machine part that moves unexpectedly during controlled movement. This protects the technician and anyone nearby.
rows.imp_rat: Being struck or trapped during jogging is a common intervention hazard, quite relevant to safe servicing (relevance) and severe given the trapping potential of carton mechanisms (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-to-run and enabling devices reduce risk, but a second worker near an unexpectedly moving mechanism during jogging remains a real and under-guarded exposure. Jogging is a deliberate slow operation, so time is not the primary concern for this hazard. Safe jogging depends on the technician's care, coordination with nearby workers, and correct use of hold-to-run controls, all skill-dependent. Enabling devices and hold-to-run controls add little cost and are common on modern machines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases whose identification does not match the enclosed product or lot
rows.plain: Make sure the shipping label matches the product and batch inside the package. This helps prevent the wrong product from being sent or used.
rows.imp_rat: Case identification mismatching contents is central to traceability of the packing job (relevance 8); mislabeled shipments create serious recall and safety exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Automated label print-and-apply with scan verification substantially reduces mismatches though manual overrides remain a gap. Label verification is a quick inline step. Correct case ID depends on operator setup and reconciliation attention. Mismatched identification causes rework and potential rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prepared shipping cases that do not match the specified product and carton grouping
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that contain the wrong product or the wrong arrangement of cartons. This helps ensure shipments are packed as ordered.
rows.imp_rat: Prepared cases not matching specified product/grouping is a mixup precursor central to correctness (relevance 8) with serious escape potential (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Staging and changeover checks reduce mismatches, but manual preparation leaves recurring errors. Reworking mismatched prepared cases interrupts flow. Preparing the right case for the right grouping relies on staging discipline and attention. Mismatched prepared cases waste materials and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cleaning debris or residues entering the controlled fill-finish environment
rows.plain: Prevent bits of cleaning material or leftover residue from getting into the controlled product-making area. This helps keep the area clean and the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning debris or residues entering the controlled fill-finish environment is relevant to aseptic integrity, and severity is very high because particulate ingress can compromise sterile products.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning SOPs and post-work inspection catch most residues, but debris from repairs entering the controlled environment remains a recurring deviation source. Post-maintenance cleaning and line clearance are time-intensive but scheduled. Thorough debris removal to cleanroom standards depends on careful, trained technique. Cleaning validation, wipes and solvents are recurring costs but well-established.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous stored energy remaining in the machine after isolation
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous power or force is still stored in the machine after its energy sources have been shut off and separated.
rows.imp_rat: Stored energy remaining after isolation defeats lockout/tagout for maintenance, making it central to safe intervention (relevance) and severe with crushing or release injuries (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard LOTO covers most stored energy, but hidden accumulators and capacitors are a recurring source of residual-energy surprises. Bleeding down stored energy adds discrete verification steps that lengthen the isolation sequence somewhat. Identifying every stored-energy reservoir (springs, capacitors, accumulators) demands machine-specific knowledge that isn't always at hand during disposal. Discharge tools are cheap but the labor to trace all stored-energy sources adds moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases packed outside the approved grouping and loading configuration after restart
rows.plain: After a restart, make sure shipping cases are packed and loaded in the approved way. This helps prevent incorrect packing or unstable loads.
rows.imp_rat: Packing outside approved configuration after restart is a core correctness error (relevance 8); restart states are a common escape point with serious consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: First-article checks after restart catch most nonconforming cases but drift and misjudgement still let some through. Confirming the grouping and loading configuration after restart takes a short inspection window. Judging conformance to the approved configuration depends on trained visual assessment. Cases packed out of configuration must be broken down, wasting labor and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator cannot determine whether all caser motion has ceased
rows.plain: Make sure the operator can clearly tell that all movement of the box-packing machine has stopped.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to confirm all caser motion has ceased is central to safe intervention, and the consequence of acting on a wrong assumption is severe entanglement or crush injury.
rows.sat_rat: Without safe standstill monitoring or clear indicators, operators often cannot be certain motion has stopped, a common ambiguity that drives premature entry. Operators must wait and watch to confirm all motion has ceased, adding uncertain seconds to any intervention. Judging complete standstill relies on operator observation of hidden coasting components rather than a definitive indicator. Zero-speed sensors and standstill indicators are inexpensive but seldom fitted on typical casers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product units damaged during carton erection, filling, or closing
rows.plain: Prevent product units from being damaged while boxes are formed, filled, or closed. This helps keep the finished products usable.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging units during erection, filling, or closing spans the whole cartoning cycle and defines success of the job, so relevance is very high; damaged output is waste and potential leaks, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature machine designs handle products gently when correctly set, but the multiple contact points across erection, filling and closing leave a persistent low damage rate. Handling damage across erect/fill/close stages does not chiefly cost time, though rework does when it occurs. Minimizing multi-stage handling damage requires broad setup expertise across several mechanisms. Each damaged unit is scrapped, and cumulative damage across stages adds meaningful material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product or packaging being contaminated during jam removal from the cartoning machine
rows.plain: Prevent the product or its packaging from becoming dirty or unsafe while a jam is being cleared. This protects the items being made.
rows.imp_rat: Preventing contamination during jam removal is central to cartoning in regulated/food-pharma contexts where the carton protects product integrity, giving solid relevance; contamination can spoil batches or trigger recalls, so severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Jam removal typically involves manual intervention near open product with no standard contamination barrier, so contamination during clearing remains a poorly controlled recurring risk. Manual jam clearing interrupts production and the cleanup steps add time, but time is not the core failure here. Preventing contamination during a manual reach-in depends on the operator's hygiene discipline and awareness, which is inconsistent. Contamination events cause scrap and potential recall costs, and thorough post-clearing sanitation adds cost.

rows.source_job: Disconnect the machine from facility utilities
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic energy remains in the cartoning machine after utility disconnection
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous stored power, air pressure, or liquid pressure is gone from the cartoning machine after it is disconnected. This helps prevent unexpected movement, release, or injury.
rows.imp_rat: Residual electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic energy after disconnection defeats safe isolation, highly relevant to protecting maintainers (relevance) and severe due to unexpected energy release (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established verification practice catches most cases, but multi-energy machines still leave occasional residual pressure or charge. Verifying zero-energy state across electrical, pneumatic, and hydraulic lines is a defined but multi-step task. Confirming full de-energization across three utility types requires cross-disciplinary competence that generalist disposal crews may lack. Test instruments and disconnection labor are modest, routine costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of products damaged during insertion into cartons
rows.plain: Keep products from being damaged while they are put into boxes, so more products arrive in usable condition.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging products during insertion into cartons strikes at the heart of the cartoning job, which is to package goods intact, so relevance is very high; each damaged unit is scrap and reputational risk though usually contained, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Well-set machines insert reliably, but variation in product presentation and worn tooling keep a steady low rate of insertion damage that setup alone cannot eliminate. Insertion speed is tuned during setup, and damage-related slowdowns are a tuning trade-off rather than a time failure. Achieving gentle insertion depends on skilled changeover setup of guides, timing, and force adjustment. Damaged product is scrapped, creating recurring material loss when insertion is mis-tuned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an incorrect carton blank format entering the erecting mechanism
rows.plain: Prevent a flat carton piece of the wrong size or shape from entering the box-forming machine. This helps avoid jams and incorrectly made boxes.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect carton blank entering the erecting mechanism directly undermines the core cartoning function, causing jams and malformed cartons; severity is significant through downtime and scrap but is usually recoverable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Magazine format sensors and job recipes catch many mismatches, but manual loading still allows the wrong blank to enter occasionally. Blank-format detection at the magazine flags mismatches quickly at run start. Correct blank loading relies on operator diligence and recognition of the right format, an attention-dependent step. A wrong blank format wastes material and run time, partly mitigated by format sensing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser retains hazardous product or cleaning residue at handoff for return
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous product or cleaning residue remains in the packing machine when it is handed back. This protects the people who receive, move, or repair it.
rows.imp_rat: Retaining hazardous product or cleaning residue at handoff is highly relevant when the caser handles pharma/chemical goods, and severity is very high because residue can cause downstream contamination, worker exposure, or regulatory violations.
rows.sat_rat: Handoff-cleanliness certification exists in principle but retained residue at handoff still recurs when cleaning validation is skipped for scrapped equipment. Final decontamination and a cleanliness sign-off add a defined step before handoff. Confirming absence of hazardous product or cleaning residue at handoff requires QA-level judgement. Verification swabbing and documentation add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that affected cartoned units become mixed with acceptable units during stoppage resolution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that damaged or questionable boxed items get mixed back in with acceptable ones when a production line is restarted. This helps prevent unsuitable products from being treated as good.
rows.imp_rat: Affected units mixing with acceptable ones during stoppage resolution is a mixup during recovery (relevance 7); allows defective product into good stock, high severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Segregation practice exists but under stoppage pressure suspect and good units do get commingled, so containment is only moderately reliable. Segregating affected units adds handling time during resolution. Proper quarantine relies on operators knowing which units are suspect, an attention-dependent judgement. Mixing forces broader rejection or investigation, escalating waste.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contaminants removed from the machine spread into adjacent sterile production areas
rows.plain: Keep dirt, germs, and other unwanted material removed from the machine from spreading to nearby areas used for germ-free production. This protects surrounding equipment and products.
rows.imp_rat: Spread of removed contaminants into adjacent sterile areas is moderately relevant to containment during cleaning, and severity is very high given facility-wide sterile compromise risk.
rows.sat_rat: Containment practices help but contaminant migration into adjacent sterile zones remains a recurring risk during disposal. Containing removed contaminants during teardown slows the process with staging and bagging steps. Preventing cross-contamination into adjacent sterile areas requires cleanroom containment discipline. Barriers, containment bags, and airlocks add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product containers crushed as carton flaps close
rows.plain: Fewer product containers should be crushed when the carton flaps are folded shut. This prevents damaged products and wasted containers.
rows.imp_rat: Product containers crushed as carton flaps close is a direct failure of the closing step central to cartoning, so relevance is very high; crushed containers become scrap and can leak, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard closing mechanisms are consistent once set, but dimensional variation and marginal fill heights still produce occasional crushing during flap closure. Flap-closing occurs at line speed and damage does not primarily manifest as lost time. Correct compression bar and flap-folder adjustment requires setup skill to avoid crushing tall or fragile containers. Crushed containers are scrapped product plus wasted carton, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking mixed packaging material identities or lots in the station supply
rows.plain: Make sure different packaging materials or batches are not mixed together at the work station. This helps prevent the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking mixed material identities/lots in station supply is a mixup-detection failure (relevance 7) with serious recall/safety severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Visual checks are the main control, leaving recurring risk that mixed identities go unnoticed. Checking supply for mixed identities adds brief inspection time. Spotting mixed material identities in the supply demands sustained operator attention and label literacy. Overlooked mixed materials cause costly rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of packaging materials added after verification bypassing confirmation against active batch requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that packaging materials added later skip the check against the requirements for the current batch. This helps prevent the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Materials added after verification bypassing confirmation is a mixup gate bypass (relevance 7); unverified materials risk serious mislabel/recall (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures exist but post-verification additions frequently bypass confirmation, a recurring control gap. Reverifying materials added after initial checks costs extra time. Remembering to confirm post-verification additions depends on operator discipline. Unverified additions risk costly wrong-material use.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of hazardous caser motion after the emergency stop is triggered
rows.plain: Make the carton-packing machine’s dangerous movement stop as quickly as possible after the emergency-stop button is pressed. This helps reduce the chance and severity of injury.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged hazardous motion after the e-stop directly undermines the safeguard's core purpose; even a short overrun can cause severe crush injury, making both relevance and severity very high.
rows.sat_rat: Stop-time is fairly consistent on well-maintained machines but degrades with brake wear and is seldom re-verified after commissioning, leaving real gaps. Servo-braked and pneumatically dumped drives arrest motion quickly, but heavy conveyors and inertial loads still coast measurably before halting. Stopping performance is designed into the drive/brake hardware and needs no operator finesse. Dynamic braking and dump valves add modest cost and are common on newer machines but often omitted on older casers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser cycle resumes before required safeguards are restored
