rows.plain: Do not restart the carton-packing machine until all required safety protections are back in place. This helps prevent the machine from moving while people may still be at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Cycle resuming before safeguards are restored is a core interlock failure central to safe operation and severe, exposing workers to moving machinery mid-intervention.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked guards reliably block cycle resumption until closed, though switch defeating and worn interlocks remain a persistent minority failure mode. Guard-closed interlocks confirm restoration essentially instantly, adding no delay. Interlock logic enforces safeguard restoration independent of operator memory or skill. Guard interlock switches are cheap and standard on compliant machinery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of discharged cartons released downstream before required inspection is completed
rows.plain: Keep cartons from moving to the next step until the required inspection is finished. This helps prevent unchecked or faulty cartons from being sent onward.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cartons downstream before required inspection completes bypasses the verification step of the job (high relevance) and allows unverified, potentially defective product to escape (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Machine interlocks that gate discharge on inspection completion are standard and dependable, with only rare bypass or sensor faults. Interlocks hold cartons until inspection completes, adding negligible delay to the flow. Configuring the release interlock requires setup knowledge but demands little operator vigilance afterward. Preventing premature release avoids downstream waste, and interlock hardware is a modest, well-justified cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sealing material entering the carton and contacting product containers
rows.plain: Keep pieces of sealing material from falling into the carton and touching the product containers. This helps protect the containers from foreign material.
rows.imp_rat: Sealing material shedding into the carton and contacting product containers directly undermines the packaging job's protective purpose, so relevance is high; contaminated product reaching consumers is a severe quality and compliance failure.
rows.sat_rat: Adhesive and film application is fairly well controlled but stray glue strings and trim debris entering cartons still occur intermittently without reliable automatic detection. Detecting stray sealing material rarely adds process time when controls work, but rework does when it fails. Preventing glue strings or film scraps entering cartons depends partly on setup skill and maintenance of applicators. Contaminated product is scrapped, so failures carry direct material cost.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual fluids escaping into the surrounding sterile fill-finish facility during drainage
rows.plain: Prevent leftover liquid from leaking into the surrounding clean facility while it is drained from the machine. This helps keep the area clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Residual fluids escaping into a sterile fill-finish facility during drainage is somewhat peripheral to cartoning mechanics (moderate relevance) but risks batch contamination and regulatory failure with severe consequences (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Containment methods exist but drainage in a sterile environment still risks recurring escape onto controlled floors. Contained drainage inside a sterile suite requires slow, careful staging to avoid escape. Preventing fluid escape into a sterile fill-finish room demands cleanroom discipline and drainage know-how. Containment trays, sterile-compatible absorbents, and cleanup add meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser restarts before the hazard is cleared
rows.plain: Make sure the case-packing machine stays stopped until the danger has been removed.
rows.imp_rat: An unexpected restart before the hazard is cleared strikes at the heart of safe intervention and can severely injure workers inside the machine; both relevance and severity are high.
rows.sat_rat: Anti-restart interlocks requiring a separate reset are widely deployed and effective, though defeated guards and bypassed resets remain a known field problem. Restart interlocks impose no meaningful delay once the hazard is genuinely cleared. Deliberate reset-then-start sequences reduce dependence on operator discipline but do not eliminate it. Manual-reset relays and guard interlocks are inexpensive and standard equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that installed guards leave hazardous areas of the cartoning machinery accessible
rows.plain: Make sure the protective covers do not leave dangerous moving parts of the box-packing machine within reach.
rows.imp_rat: Guards leaving hazardous areas accessible directly undermines physical safeguarding (relevance) and can result in contact injuries with high-speed carton mechanisms (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized guarding designs and risk assessments reliably enclose most hazards, though awkward access points occasionally slip through. Guard fit-up and gap checks during preparation are done efficiently against documented layouts. Assessing residual access requires trained safety personnel applying reach and gap standards, a manageable but real skill demand. Fixed and interlocked guarding is a standard included cost, though retrofits to close gaps can add expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury from unexpected machine movement or stored-energy release during fault repair
rows.plain: Prevent workers from being injured by sudden machine movement or the release of stored power while fixing a fault.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury from unexpected movement or stored-energy release during fault repair is central to safe maintenance (relevance) and severe with potential for serious harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature, regulated LOTO practice makes protection during fault repair highly consistent, with residual failures mostly from procedural shortcuts. LOTO adds procedural time before repair but this is universally built into maintenance planning. Lockout/tagout is standard trained practice that any qualified technician is expected to execute without deep expertise. Locks, tags and hasps are low-cost and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the case packer is configured for a format differing from the packaging order
rows.plain: The box-packing machine should be set up for the same package size and style listed in the order. A mismatch could lead to incorrect packing.
rows.imp_rat: A machine configured for the wrong format directly derails the packing job (relevance 8) and can produce entire runs of mispacked cases before detection (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-based changeover with order paperwork catches most mismatches but manual selection still lets occasional wrong-format setups through. Verifying the loaded format against the order takes only moments today via HMI recipe selection but is often skipped under time pressure. Correct format selection depends heavily on operator attention and familiarity with recipe naming conventions. A wrong format run wastes cases and cartons and forces rework, a cost operators actively try to avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staging an incorrect case, divider, or label variant for the active batch
rows.plain: Workers need to choose the correct box, divider, and label for the batch being prepared. Using the wrong items could cause the products to be packed or identified incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Staging an incorrect case/divider/label variant for the batch is a mixup/mislabel precursor (relevance 7) with serious recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Staging checklists mitigate errors, but manual staging still allows recurring wrong-variant events. Confirming the correct variant at staging adds setup time. Staging the right variant relies on operator interpretation of batch records. Wrong-variant staging wastes materials and forces rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of potentially affected cases omitted from containment
rows.plain: Make sure all products or cases that might be affected by a quality problem are contained. This prevents potentially affected items from being used or distributed.
rows.imp_rat: Cases omitted from containment defeats containment of suspect product (relevance 7); allows potentially defective product to escape, high severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Containment procedures exist but scoping completeness is inconsistent and a recurring escape source. Containment scoping can delay while affected units are identified. Defining containment scope demands experienced investigator judgement. Omitted cases risk expensive escapes and recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which demonstrated sustained throughput falls below the required throughput under intended production conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment can consistently produce close to the required amount under normal working conditions. This shows whether it is suitable for the intended job.
rows.imp_rat: Sustained throughput falling below required rate strikes at the very purpose of the cartoning machine, so relevance is high; severity is meaningful via lost output but is an economic rather than safety consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers quote nameplate speeds while sustained throughput under intended conditions varies, so shortfalls are a recurring and imperfectly guarded surprise. Throughput demonstration via FAT/SAT is a scheduled step but repeat trials when rates fall short add delay. Interpreting rated vs. sustained throughput under real product conditions requires experienced process engineers who understand OEE deductions. Underperformance discovered late forces costly rework or line rebalancing, and validation runs consume material and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of latent mechanical, electrical, or structural defects remaining undetected during the preacquisition inspection
rows.plain: Find hidden mechanical, electrical, or structural problems before buying the used case-packing machine, so it is safe and suitable to acquire.
rows.imp_rat: Latent defects undetected during preacquisition inspection are quite relevant to obtaining a working caser; severity is high because hidden mechanical/electrical faults cause failures, downtime, and safety hazards after commissioning.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection practice is mature but inherently limited in exposing hidden defects, so latent faults still slip through at a meaningful rate. Pre-acquisition inspections are scheduled events that add time but are a normal part of buying used or new equipment. Detecting latent mechanical, electrical, or structural defects depends on an experienced inspector's judgement, and quality varies with who shows up. A missed latent defect becomes an expensive post-purchase repair, while the inspection cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while extracting an unusable case from the warehouse caser
rows.plain: Prevent workers from being hurt while removing a case that cannot be used from the case-packing machine.
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury while extracting an unusable case is a recurring operational hazard fairly central to daily casing work and severe in bodily consequence, with pinch and laceration risk.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout/tagout procedures exist but are inconsistently followed for quick clears, so injury risk during extraction remains a recurring, poorly controlled hazard. Extracting a jammed or unusable case is a slow manual intervention that halts production for minutes. Clearing an unusable case relies heavily on the operator's technique and hazard awareness inside pinch- and edge-rich mechanisms. The intervention consumes labor time and occasional tooling but little material, so direct cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of products damaged during cartoning when incoming dimensions or weights vary
rows.plain: Reduce damage to products when the incoming items differ in size or weight. The packing machine should handle these normal differences without harming the products.
rows.imp_rat: Product damage when incoming dimensions or weights vary tests the machine's ability to do its core job under real-world variation, so relevance is high; damage is scrap but generally recoverable via adjustment, giving moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Most cartoners are set for nominal dimensions and lack adaptive handling, so variation in incoming product size or weight reliably produces elevated damage — a poorly served pain. Adapting to varying incoming dimensions often forces line slowdowns, but the core loss is defects not delay. Compensating for input variation demands considerable operator skill and constant adjustment, a heavy vigilance burden. Input variation drives elevated scrap and rework costs that are hard to contain.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of carton blanks carrying particulate or microbial contamination after magazine loading
rows.plain: Keep dirt and germs off the flat carton pieces as they are loaded into the machine. Clean carton pieces help protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Carton blanks carrying particulate or microbial contamination after loading is moderately central to sterile packaging, with high severity given microbial ingress into product-contact packaging.
rows.sat_rat: There is little inline particulate or microbial detection on blanks, so this need is largely unaddressed by standard cartoner practice. Contamination is rarely detected in real time, so any check occurs long after loading. Preventing contamination depends on handling discipline and hygiene practices rather than machine safeguards. Contaminated blanks risk product recalls, a high-cost exposure with minimal inline mitigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of caser power, utility, or control requirements not validated against available facility capacity and interfaces
rows.plain: Make sure the carton-packing machine's power, supplies, and controls match what the facility can provide. This prevents problems caused by missing capacity or incompatible connections.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to validate power, utility, or control requirements against facility capacity is relevant to a successful install, and severity is meaningful because mismatches can cause equipment failure, delays, or unsafe operation.
rows.sat_rat: Facility capacity data is often incomplete or out of date, so unvalidated requirements slip through fairly often despite some standard utility-checklist practice. Validating power, utility, and control needs against facility capacity involves back-and-forth with facilities staff that adds delay. This validation needs electrical and controls expertise to interpret both the caser spec and the facility's available capacity correctly. Undetected capacity shortfalls trigger expensive retrofits after purchase, while the check itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartons containing a product variant that does not match the intended carton
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that contain the wrong version of a product. This helps ensure each carton has the product it is supposed to contain.
rows.imp_rat: A carton containing the wrong product variant is a fundamental mispack that defeats the packaging job, so relevance is high; wrong-variant packs pose labeling, allergen, or dosing risks that can trigger recalls, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode and vision checks catch most variant mismatches, but changeover setup errors and unscanned SKUs leave a residual mismatch rate. Automated variant verification adds no meaningful cycle time when configured. Vision and code-reading systems reduce dependence on operators visually distinguishing similar variants. Vision/scanner systems carry moderate cost that is standard on mixed-variant lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the caser tipping during lifting or loading
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the carton-packing machine tips over while it is being lifted or loaded. This helps prevent injuries and equipment damage.
rows.imp_rat: A caser tipping during lifting or loading is central to safe installation, and the consequence is severe crushing injury or major equipment damage, warranting high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard rigging practice handles most lifts safely, but uncharacterized center of gravity on retired machines makes occasional tipping a real risk. Proper rigging and slow controlled lifts add time to the loading operation. Preventing tipping of a top-heavy caser depends on rigger experience in center-of-gravity assessment. Forklifts, slings, and spreader bars are standard, already-owned equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented case-packing or shipping-integrity defects are omitted from disposition assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that known packing or shipping defects are left out when deciding whether a production lot can be released. Every relevant defect should be considered before the decision is made.
rows.imp_rat: Documented defects omitted from disposition assessment is a review-completeness failure (relevance 7); overlooked defects can lead to release of non-conforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation logs feed disposition, but manual handoffs let some documented defects slip from assessment. Assembling defects for disposition adds review time. Ensuring all defects reach assessment relies on reviewer thoroughness. Omitted defects risk downstream failure and recall costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton seals reopening during downstream conveyance
rows.plain: Reduce the number of carton seals that come open while the cartons move through the rest of the process. This helps keep the products protected inside.
rows.imp_rat: Seals reopening during downstream conveyance defeats the durability of the cartoning outcome (high relevance) and exposes product to contamination, damage, or loss after the machine (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Adhesive and compression systems are established, but glue quality, temperature drift, and compression dwell variation still cause intermittent seal reopening. Seal reopening typically manifests downstream after the machine cycle, so detection is delayed beyond the cartoner itself. Achieving durable seals depends on operator skill in setting glue temperature, compression, and dwell. Reopened seals cause downstream jams and rejected product, and glue-system variability keeps this cost recurring.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual pharmaceutical material escaping during caser movement and loading
rows.plain: Keep leftover medicine from escaping while the carton-packing machine is moved and loaded. This helps protect workers and the surrounding area from residue.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pharmaceutical material escaping during caser movement and loading is moderately central to the handling task but severe because it risks contamination of surroundings and exposure to hazardous product.
rows.sat_rat: Residual product escaping during movement is common when cleaning was incomplete, so containment is a partial control. Wrapping or bagging the caser to contain residue during movement adds time. Judging where residual pharmaceutical material clings requires product knowledge. Containment wrap and bags are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected caser energization while the technician is making electrical connections
rows.plain: Prevent the equipment from unexpectedly receiving electricity while a technician is connecting it. This helps protect the technician from electric shock or other harm.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected energization while making electrical connections is a critical safety error during caser installation, and the consequence is electrocution, so severity is maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Mature, enforced lockout/tagout practice makes unexpected energization during connection rare where discipline holds. Lockout during connection adds minutes, a negligible schedule impact. LOTO is a trained, credentialed routine that connection technicians perform habitually. The procedure is essentially free beyond a lock and tag, with large avoided-injury value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of released shipping cases with an incorrect carton count or loading configuration
rows.plain: Prevent shipped cases from having the wrong number of cartons or the wrong arrangement inside. This reduces the chance that customers receive incomplete or incorrectly packed shipments.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases with incorrect carton count/loading is a core escape from the packing job (relevance 8) causing shipment discrepancies and returns (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Automated counting and check-weighing make incorrect counts fairly uncommon at release. Count verification is fast, often automated inline. Correct count relies on setup and sensor calibration more than skill. Wrong counts cause reconciliation waste and customer credits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons leaving the production run with incomplete closure
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes that leave the production run without being fully closed. This helps protect the products during handling and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons leaving with incomplete closure directly fails the seal/finish outcome of cartoning (high relevance) and can spoil, spill, or compromise product integrity downstream (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closure sensors and mechanical guides are mature, yet stock variation and tooling wear still produce occasional incomplete closures. Closure verification occurs inline so incomplete closures are flagged within the cycle. Closure tooling adjustment needs a trained setter, but routine operation is largely hands-off. Incompletely closed cartons cause rejects and rework, mitigated well by closure detection but not fully eliminated.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying an incorrect case quantity to the active product and case configuration
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of using the wrong number of items for the product and box arrangement being packed. Correct counts help ensure each box contains what it should.
rows.imp_rat: Applying an incorrect case quantity to the active configuration corrupts core grouping accuracy (relevance 8); causes systematic count errors across a run (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven machine settings make this consistent on automated lines, but manual configuration remains a recurring error source. Confirming the correct case quantity setting at changeover adds setup time. Applying the right quantity depends on correctly reading the batch record and configuring the station. An incorrect quantity applied across a run wastes materials and forces rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that potentially affected cases are released before deviation impact is established
rows.plain: Hold back any cases that might be affected until the impact of the problem is understood. This prevents possibly unsuitable cases from being released.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing potentially affected cases before deviation impact is established defeats containment (relevance 7); can lead to distribution of suspect product and recalls (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Containment practices exist but timely and complete impact assessment before release is inconsistently achieved. Containment decisions can delay release while impact is assessed. Establishing deviation impact demands investigator judgement that is a scarce resource. Releasing affected cases prematurely risks expensive recall exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartoned units linked to the wrong shipping-case parent across the packaging sequence
rows.plain: Keep the number of cartons assigned to the wrong shipping case as low as possible during packaging. Correct links make it possible to trace each carton to the case it was packed in.
rows.imp_rat: Units linked to the wrong shipping-case parent corrupts serialization/traceability (relevance 7); breaks track-and-trace with serious recall/regulatory impact (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Vision-based aggregation and serialization systems are mature and widely deployed, handling correct parenting in most runs, though sensor misreads and buffer mismatches still cause occasional wrong-parent linkages. Parent-child association is generated in real time during packing so latency is rarely the issue. Configuring aggregation logic and camera/scanner triggers correctly requires serialization specialists, and misconfiguration is a common source of wrong linkages. Mislinked units can scrap whole cases or trigger recalls, making the cost exposure of this error high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartons containing products in the wrong position or orientation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes in which products are placed incorrectly or facing the wrong way. This helps ensure products are packed properly and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong position/orientation of products in cartons is central to producing correct packs (high relevance), though consequences are typically rework or aesthetic/functional flaws rather than catastrophic harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Orienting feeders and guides work well for stable products, but position and orientation errors persist for irregular or unstable items where tooling cannot fully constrain placement. Orientation control is handled at line speed and is not primarily a time problem. Correct orientation depends on skilled setup of orienting tooling and feeders for each product. Mis-oriented product usually means rework or scrap rather than throughput loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of released shipping cases containing foreign material introduced during grouping or loading
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipped cases that contain unwanted material added while products are being grouped or loaded. This helps prevent contaminated or damaged shipments from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: Released cases with foreign material introduced during grouping/loading is a contamination escape (relevance 7) with high safety/recall severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: GMP housekeeping and inspection reduce but cannot fully guarantee absence of foreign material at case level. Foreign-material checks add minor inspection time. Detection relies on operator vigilance and housekeeping discipline. Foreign material causes rejection, complaints and possible recall costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton or container damage capable of compromising sterile-product integrity remains unassessed
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that damaged cartons or containers that could harm the sterility of the product are left without review. Finding and assessing this damage helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Unassessed damage that could compromise sterile integrity is peripheral to grouping mechanics (relevance 6) but a compromised sterile barrier is a critical patient-safety event (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-integrity damage checks are mature and heavily regulated, so this need is fairly well served though rare gaps remain. Assessing damage that could compromise sterile integrity is embedded in existing protocols without major time penalty. Judging which damage compromises sterile-product integrity requires specialized packaging and sterility knowledge. Missed damage assessment risks recalls whose cost dwarfs the assessment effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between operator recognition of an imminent hazard and emergency-stop actuation
rows.plain: Press the emergency stop as soon as an operator notices an immediate danger. Acting quickly can reduce the chance or severity of harm.
rows.imp_rat: Minimizing time from hazard recognition to e-stop actuation is important for effective intervention (relevance) and reduces injury severity, though physical safeguards remain the primary protection (severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no mature automatic hazard-detection layer in typical cartoners, so the human-in-the-loop delay makes response time inconsistent and unpredictable. The delay between hazard recognition and actuation is dominated by unaided human reaction time, which standard hardware placement improves only marginally. Fast recognition depends on trained attentiveness and situational awareness, which vary widely between operators. Improving response mainly involves training and ergonomic layout rather than significant expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine settings that do not match the approved batch setup when readiness is confirmed
rows.plain: When the machine is ready, make sure its settings match the approved setup for the batch. This helps ensure the cartons are made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Machine settings not matching approved batch setup at readiness confirmation is core to producing conforming cartons, with serious consequences of running an entire batch incorrectly before detection.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-recipe systems restore settings consistently, but manual mechanical adjustments still escape automatic verification at times. Automated readiness confirmation checks settings at run start without significant delay. Judging whether every setting matches the approved setup requires experienced setter oversight. Mismatched settings produce defective cartons and rework that recipe recall reduces meaningfully.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from the onset of a carton formation, filling, or sealing deviation to operator recognition
rows.plain: Notice as quickly as possible when cartons are being formed, filled, or sealed incorrectly. Early notice allows the operator to correct the problem sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Fast operator recognition of formation, filling, or sealing deviations is central to keeping the cartoning run producing good output (high relevance); delayed recognition means accumulating scrap and downtime, a serious but recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor triggers are consistent for hard faults, but slow-developing deviations are recognized inconsistently and often late. Inline sensing shortens deviation-to-recognition to seconds where instrumented, but manual monitoring still lags on subtle deviations. Early recognition of subtle formation or sealing drift still depends on an experienced operator's eye. Faster recognition limits the volume of defective cartons produced, containing scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of isolating a circuit or utility connection that does not supply the target caser
rows.plain: Make sure the correct power source or service connection is shut off before work begins. This prevents workers from shutting off the wrong equipment while the intended equipment remains energized.
rows.imp_rat: Isolating the wrong circuit or utility during lockout is moderately relevant to servicing tasks but severity is very high because it can leave the target energized and cause electrocution or crush injury.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO procedures and labeling reduce wrong-source isolation, but mislabeled or shared panels still produce occasional errors. Tracing and verifying the correct circuit or utility can delay isolation when as-built drawings are outdated. Correct lockout/tagout tracing depends on qualified electricians and facilities staff familiar with the specific installation. A mis-isolation can cause outages to adjacent operations with significant downstream cost.

rows.stk: Engineering, procurement, or operations staff responsible for equipment selection.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate casers incorrectly assessed for compatibility with upstream, downstream, facility, and control interfaces
rows.plain: Correctly judge whether each machine that packs cartons into larger cases will work with the connected machines, building services, and control systems. This helps avoid choosing equipment that cannot be installed or used properly.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly assessing caser compatibility with upstream, downstream, facility, and control interfaces is central to a successful installation decision, and the consequence is costly integration failure or a stranded capital asset.
rows.sat_rat: Interface assessments today rely on fragmented drawings and vendor claims, so incorrect compatibility conclusions recur and remain the core unmitigated risk here. Assessing every candidate caser against upstream, downstream, facility, and control interfaces is a slow manual cross-check that lengthens the acquisition timeline. Correct interface compatibility judgement demands scarce integration engineers who understand both the caser and the surrounding line, so errors cluster where that expertise is thin. A wrong compatibility call surfaces late and forces costly rework or rejected equipment, but the assessment itself consumes modest resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser cycle restarts before the operator confirms the interruption is cleared
rows.plain: Keep the packing machine from starting again until the operator confirms that the problem or interruption has been cleared.
rows.imp_rat: A caser cycle restarting before the operator confirms clearance is central to safe fault recovery, and premature restart can catch a worker in the machine, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Deliberate reset-and-confirm sequences prevent most premature restarts, but rushed acknowledgments during production pressure remain a real gap. Confirmation-before-restart steps add only brief delay to resumption. Required manual reset reduces reliance on operator memory, though acknowledgment can be done reflexively. Manual reset and start-hold logic are standard, low-cost control features.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material remaining inside a prepared shipping case
rows.plain: Make sure no dirt or other unwanted material is left inside a prepared shipping box. This helps keep what is shipped clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material remaining inside a prepared case is a contamination error prior to loading (relevance 7) with high safety/recall severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Case inversion/blow-off and inspection reduce residual foreign material, but detection still depends on imperfect manual checks. Inspecting prepared cases for foreign material adds minor time. Detecting foreign material inside cases requires operator inspection diligence. Foreign material events are costly quality failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing an incorrect carton count after changeover
rows.plain: Fewer shipping cases should contain the wrong number of boxed products after switching the line to a different product size or format. Accurate counts help ensure customers receive the expected quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton count after changeover is a core count-defect at a known escape point (relevance 8); causes shipment discrepancies (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where count sensors or checkweighers exist detection is strong, but many stations rely on manual sampling that misses intermittent count errors. Counting cartons per case is fast but continuous verification is not practical, so checks are periodic. Detecting a short or over count requires attentive counting that fatigues operators. Incorrect carton counts trigger reworked cases and potential customer chargebacks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material being introduced into shipping cases during grouping or loading
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from getting into shipping boxes while they are being grouped or loaded. This helps ensure the boxes and their contents stay clean and meet required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material introduced during grouping/loading is a contamination error affecting integrity (relevance 7) with serious safety/recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Area control and line clearance mitigate introduction, yet ambient and handling sources leave residual risk. Preventive housekeeping is time-bounded within normal operations. Prevention depends on disciplined line clearance and attention. Contamination introduces material and investigation waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units or sterile barriers damaged by case-loading and distribution forces
rows.plain: Fewer boxed products or protective sterile coverings should be damaged while cases are loaded or shipped. Preventing this damage helps products arrive usable and protected.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to units or sterile barriers from loading/distribution forces undermines product integrity (relevance 7); compromised sterile barriers carry high patient-safety severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-barrier validation and dye/burst testing are well established, but subtle case-loading damage can still evade routine checks. Verifying sterile-barrier integrity after loading forces added inspection and test time. Assessing damage to sterile barriers demands regulated device-packaging expertise and validated test methods. Damaged sterile barriers cause costly scrap and potential recalls that current controls only partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton jams that stop the scheduled production run
rows.plain: Reduce carton jams that interrupt the planned production run.
rows.imp_rat: Carton jams that stop the scheduled run directly defeat the machine's core job of continuous carton production (very high relevance); each stoppage causes lost throughput but is typically cleared and recovered (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern cartoners with servo control and gentle handling reduce jams substantially, but jams remain a routine run interruption. Jams stop the run and clearing them consumes noticeable downtime despite quick-access designs. Clearing and preventing recurring jams draws on operator troubleshooting skill. Jam-induced stoppages waste production time and sometimes product, a recurring cost only partly reduced.

rows.source_job: Unload completed cases from caser outfeed
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the material handler being injured while removing or transferring completed cases
rows.plain: Prevent the person handling the goods from being hurt while removing or moving finished boxes.
rows.imp_rat: Material handler injury while removing completed cases is a frequent operational hazard central to the caser's output flow and severe, with strain, pinch, and impact injuries during routine handling.
rows.sat_rat: Ergonomic aids and conveyor handoffs cut injury frequency, yet manual case transfer still produces regular strain and impact incidents. Removing or transferring completed cases is a routine, reasonably quick handling task. Safe handling depends on manual lifting technique and load awareness, with training that is uneven across shifts. Roller tables, pallet jacks and conveyors that reduce manual transfer are common but not universally provided.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected cartoned units remaining in the active tally after exclusion from packing
rows.plain: Fewer rejected boxed products should still be included in the current count after they have been removed from packing. This keeps the production total accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected units remaining in the active tally corrupts count accuracy (relevance 7); leads to incorrect case quantities and reconciliation errors (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Reject handling is largely manual and reject tally errors persist as a recurring reconciliation problem. Adjusting the tally for rejected units adds reconciliation time. Correctly excluding rejects from the tally requires operator diligence. Miscounted rejects distort reconciliation and yield.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of case packing proceeding under obsolete or unapproved instructions
rows.plain: Make sure case packing does not continue using old or unapproved instructions. This helps ensure products are packed according to current, authorized directions.
rows.imp_rat: Packing under obsolete/unapproved instructions is a control failure central to correct output (relevance 8); can systematically produce non-conforming cases (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems and issued batch records make running on obsolete instructions uncommon. Verifying current instructions is a quick pre-run check. Version control reduces reliance on operator judgement about currency. Obsolete instructions cause costly nonconforming output.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material entering shipping cases during loading
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from getting into shipping boxes while they are being loaded. This helps protect the products during shipping.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering cases during loading is a contamination error affecting product integrity (relevance 7) with high safety/recall severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Good housekeeping and covered stations control most foreign material, though open loading points leave residual risk. Foreign material checks add minor inspection time during loading. Preventing foreign material entry depends on housekeeping discipline and operator awareness. Foreign material incidents are costly quality events but relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case checks skipped or invalidly documented before release
rows.plain: Make sure every required check of the finished boxes is completed and recorded correctly before the products are released. Skipped or unreliable checks could allow faulty products to leave the facility.
rows.imp_rat: Skipped or invalidly documented case checks before release undermine the verification gate (relevance 7) with serious compliance/escape consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SOP-mandated checks and review catch many gaps, but skipped or backfilled entries persist under production pressure. Performing and documenting checks consumes measurable line time. Valid documentation depends on operator training and attentiveness. Skipped checks that surface later drive costly rework and review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering sterile product-contact areas while the machine is idle
rows.plain: Keep germs and other unwanted material from getting into the parts that touch the product while the machine is not running. This helps keep the product safe when production resumes.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering sterile product-contact areas while idle is somewhat peripheral to active cartoning but severity is very high because undetected idle-state ingress compromises the next sterile run.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring and protective covers keep idle contamination fairly well controlled, with breaches occurring mainly during transitions in and out of idle. Covering openings and maintaining idle-state controls is quick. Maintaining aseptic integrity while idle relies on routine but attentive practice. Covers, sterile barriers and environmental monitoring are modest ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator selects an emergency-stop control that does not arrest the hazardous motion
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone presses an emergency-stop control that does not stop the dangerous movement. This helps protect people when an emergency occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Operator selecting an e-stop that does not arrest motion is a serious human-factors gap that delays hazard control (relevance) and produces severe injury when the wrong control is used in an emergency (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Most well-engineered machines wire all local e-stops to a common stop group so any button works, but on multi-zone integrated lines mismatched coverage still occasionally leaves an operator pressing an ineffective control. Reaching a functional stop is instantaneous once the correct control is pressed, so time is not the limiting factor. Under stress an operator may misjudge which of several e-stops on a line covers the specific hazard zone, so some cognitive reliance remains despite grouping conventions. Providing multiple correctly-zoned e-stops adds wiring and device cost but is standard on well-designed lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting packaging materials with an incorrect identity, specification, or market variant
rows.plain: Make sure the packaging materials are the correct type, design, and version before using them. This prevents products from being packed with the wrong materials or labeling.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting packaging materials with incorrect identity/spec/variant is a material-mixup gate failure (relevance 7) with serious mislabel/recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Material issue controls and label checks make wrong-material acceptance fairly uncommon, though manual verification leaves residual risk. Verifying material identity at receipt adds modest time before use. Confirming correct specification and market variant requires attention to labels and specs. Wrong material accepted causes costly rework and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of released shipping cases lacking evidence of required closure, identification, or contents checks
rows.plain: Release fewer shipping boxes unless there is proof that they were properly closed, identified, and checked for the right contents. This helps ensure shipments are complete and correctly documented.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases lacking evidence of required checks is a documentation-integrity failure central to release (relevance 7); creates significant compliance/recall exposure (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record requirements drive evidence capture, but omissions in manual records remain a recurring gap. Recording checks adds documentation time to the packing flow. Evidence completeness relies on operator documentation discipline. Missing evidence forces costly investigation or re-inspection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units passing the grouping point without addition to the active case tally
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxed units that pass the grouping point without being added to the current case count. This keeps the recorded count equal to the actual number of units.
rows.imp_rat: Units passing the grouping point without being counted corrupts the case tally (relevance 7); miscounts cause shipment discrepancies and traceability gaps (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Automated counters help, but manual tally at the grouping point leaves recurring untallied units. Reconciling untallied units after the grouping point adds time. Ensuring every unit is tallied depends on operator or sensor discipline. Untallied units create reconciliation discrepancies and potential loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of contamination introduced into the sterile fill-finish area while pneumatic components are open
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of dirt or germs brought into the clean filling area while air-powered parts are open. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the sterile fill-finish area while pneumatic components are open is somewhat tangential to the mechanical cartoning job (moderate relevance) but carries catastrophic consequences for product sterility and patient safety (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Open pneumatic components expose internal surfaces and oil mist to the sterile area, and current containment practices only partly control this, leaving notable residual risk. Opening pneumatic components in an aseptic area forces added containment and re-sanitization time. Servicing open pneumatic parts without contaminating the sterile field requires above-average aseptic discipline. Portable containment and requalification after opening lines are costly per event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator cannot reach the emergency-stop control without entering the hazard zone
rows.plain: Make sure the worker can press the emergency-stop button without having to enter the dangerous area. This allows the machine to be stopped quickly during an emergency.
rows.imp_rat: Requiring entry into the hazard zone to reach the e-stop critically hampers timely response (relevance) and severely raises injury risk because the operator must approach the danger to stop it (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice mandates accessible e-stops at each workstation, but on large or retrofitted cartoners some hazard points still lack a control within reach without approaching the danger. Correctly placed controls let the operator actuate without an approach delay, supporting fast response. Proper placement removes any need for the operator to know a special reach path, requiring no skill. Adding pull-cords, extra buttons, and perimeter devices is inexpensive relative to the machine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting packaging materials whose approved release status cannot be confirmed
rows.plain: Avoid accepting packing materials when their approved status cannot be confirmed. This helps ensure that only authorized materials are used.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting materials whose release status is unconfirmed bypasses material control (relevance 7); use of unreleased materials carries serious compliance/recall risk (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Release-status labeling and staging controls make unconfirmed status uncommon, though gaps persist with manual checks. Checking release status adds brief verification time. Confirming release status depends on operator access to status systems or labels. Using unreleased material risks costly quarantine and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the emergency-stop control disengages before the hazard is cleared
rows.plain: Once the emergency stop is pressed, it should stay active until the danger has passed. This helps prevent the machine from starting again too soon.
rows.imp_rat: An e-stop that disengages before the hazard clears exposes personnel prematurely, making it very relevant to safe intervention and severe because injury can occur during re-energization.
rows.sat_rat: Positive-latching, deliberate-reset e-stop hardware per ISO 13850 makes premature disengagement before hazard clearance very uncommon in standard installations. Latching e-stop devices hold the stop condition indefinitely, so there is no time pressure on the operator to keep the hazard suppressed. The mechanical latch-and-twist-release design means no operator judgement is needed to keep the control engaged. Latching e-stop buttons are cheap, standardized components carrying negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting the caser to an electrical supply with incompatible voltage, phase configuration, or grounding
rows.plain: Connect the case-packing machine only to a power supply that has the right voltage, wiring arrangement, and safety connection. This helps prevent damage, malfunction, or electric shock.
rows.imp_rat: Connecting the caser to incompatible voltage, phase, or grounding is central to correct commissioning, and the consequence is fire, electrocution, or destroyed drives, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Nameplate checks, electrical drawings, and metering are established practice that catch nearly all incompatibilities before energization. Verifying supply voltage, phase, and grounding against the nameplate is a quick pre-connection check. Licensed electricians routinely perform this and the required competence is standard trade knowledge. Correct verification avoids costly damaged drives and is cheap to do upfront.

rows.stk: Engineering, procurement, or operations staff responsible for equipment selection.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that selection criteria omit critical sterile fill-finish or line-integration requirements
rows.plain: When comparing machines that pack items into cases, the selection should include all important needs for sterile production and connection to the rest of the line. This helps ensure the chosen machine will work safely and properly with the whole operation.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile fill-finish or line-integration requirements in selection criteria is a moderately central acquisition-stage error; severity is significant since a mismatched caser can fail compliance or require costly rework, though it is not an operational failure of casing itself.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria completeness depends heavily on individual reviewer expertise rather than robust standard templates, so critical omissions recur in regulated environments. Building selection criteria that capture sterile fill-finish and line-integration requirements is a front-loaded task that can delay specification. Correctly capturing sterile and integration requirements demands scarce validation and process specialists, and their absence is exactly how critical criteria get omitted. An omitted sterile requirement discovered later is extremely costly to remediate, though drafting the criteria is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material entering glue, tape, adhesive, or carton blank supplies during replenishment
rows.plain: Keep dirt and other unwanted material out of glue, tape, adhesive, and carton supplies while they are being added. Clean supplies help the packaging work properly and protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering glue, tape, or blank supplies during replenishment is moderately relevant to consumable integrity, with serious consequences of contaminated adhesives affecting seal safety and product.
rows.sat_rat: Covered hoppers and filtered adhesive systems help marginally, but replenishment remains an open, largely unguarded contamination pathway. Foreign-material intrusion during replenishment is typically noticed only after downstream problems arise. Avoiding contamination in supplies rests on operator care and housekeeping, with no automatic safeguard. Foreign material in glue or blanks can spoil output and trigger recalls, an expensive and poorly contained risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units with unreadable identity, lot, or serialization data after loading or distribution
rows.plain: Keep product labels and tracking information readable and correct after products are packed or shipped. This helps products remain identifiable throughout handling and delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identity/lot/serialization data after loading breaks traceability (relevance 7); loss of serialization has serious regulatory and recall consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Inline verification and label placement standards make unreadable data uncommon but not eliminated after distribution abrasion. Post-loading readability verification adds inspection time. Ensuring codes survive loading and distribution needs coding and vision expertise. Unreadable identity/serialization data causes rejects and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required safety functions left unverified after functional checks
rows.plain: Make sure every required safety feature is checked and confirmed to work. Any safety feature left unchecked could fail without being noticed.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving required safety functions unverified after checks means unconfirmed safeguards (relevance) and can allow undetected failures leading to injury (severity), though it is one step removed from the hazard itself.
rows.sat_rat: Structured checklists and CMMS task lists make full coverage the norm, though a function occasionally goes unverified when the list is outdated or a step is skipped. Running the full battery of required safety-function checks is time-consuming but scheduled into planned maintenance windows. Knowing the complete set of required safety functions demands training and up-to-date reference to the machine's safety validation, which competent techs generally have. Comprehensive checklists cost little to maintain and reduce expensive rework or incident exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues while removing components from the retired warehouse caser
rows.plain: Keep technicians from coming into contact with dangerous leftover material while they remove usable parts from the retired warehouse packing machine. This helps protect their health during the work.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during component removal is a decommissioning-phase concern, moderately central to the caser's ongoing packing job, but the severity is high because chemical exposure can cause irreversible harm to workers.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure during teardown is inconsistently controlled because residue locations in retired equipment are frequently undocumented. Component-by-component removal with containment slows the teardown. Knowing which components harbor hazardous residue requires product and process knowledge that disposal crews often lack. PPE and containment consumables add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing a carton quantity that differs from approved packing requirements
rows.plain: Make sure every shipping case contains the approved number of cartons. This prevents shipments from being packed incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Carton quantity differing from approved packing requirements is central to grouping correctness (relevance 8); creates count discrepancies downstream (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Count verification against master data is reliable but setup-selection errors leave a residual gap. Quantity verification is a rapid automated or manual step. Conformance to approved quantity relies on correct recipe/setup selection. Quantity deviations cause reconciliation loss and rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous retained materials during removal from the warehouse caser
rows.plain: Keep the technician from touching dangerous leftover material while removing it from the packing machine. This helps protect the technician from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous retained materials during removal is a decommissioning error with moderate centrality to the running job, but severity is high given the potential for toxic exposure and lasting harm to technicians.
rows.sat_rat: Retained materials are often only partially characterized at end of life, so exposure control is uneven. Careful staged removal to avoid disturbing retained materials adds time. Recognizing and safely extracting retained hazardous materials relies on specialized hazard awareness. Containment and disposal consumables add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing an incorrect carton count or arrangement
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that have the wrong number of cartons or have them arranged incorrectly. This helps ensure customers receive complete and properly packed shipments.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton count or arrangement in cases is a core packing defect (relevance 8) producing shipment errors and rework (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Validated pack patterns and check-weighing reliably catch most arrangement errors. Arrangement checks are performed quickly at case formation. Correct arrangement depends on pack-pattern setup and operator verification. Incorrect arrangement risks damage and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of operating modes and access points omitted from safety-system verification
rows.plain: Check every way the system can operate and every place people can enter or reach in when testing the safety system. This reduces the chance that an unsafe situation is missed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting operating modes or access points from verification is moderately relevant as a coverage gap and severe when an unverified mode conceals a dangerous fault, though administrative in nature.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage gaps are a recognized weak point because rarely-used modes and secondary access points are commonly omitted from verification. Exhaustively cycling every mode and access point during preparation is time-consuming and often abbreviated under schedule pressure. Knowing which modes and access points must be tested demands experienced commissioning judgement that varies by person. Thorough coverage adds labor hours but no major material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of flap-folding jams halting carton flow
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times folded carton flaps get stuck and stop the cartons from moving. This keeps cartons flowing smoothly for closing and sealing.
rows.imp_rat: Flap-folding jams halting carton flow strike at the core carton-forming function of the machine (high relevance); they halt production and generate scrap but are recoverable interruptions (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-engineered folding sections are reliable for consistent stock, but stiffness and crease variation still trigger periodic folding jams. Flap-folding jams halt flow and require operator intervention, adding downtime. Preventing folding jams needs skilled setup of plows, rails, and timing. Each halt wastes throughput and occasionally damages cartons, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues during caser decontamination
rows.plain: Keep the technician from touching harmful leftover substances while cleaning and disinfecting the packing machine. This helps protect the technician from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during decontamination is moderately relevant to caser servicing, and the consequence can be chemical burns or toxic exposure, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established decon SOPs from manufacturing exist but their consistent application during disposal is patchy, leaving real exposure gaps. Decontamination cycles and wipe-downs add meaningful time before handling. Choosing correct agents and PPE for pharmaceutical residues demands specific hazard knowledge many disposal crews lack. Cleaning agents, PPE, and waste handling add moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-batch materials remaining in the case-packing area after line clearance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that materials from an earlier batch are left in the case-packing area after it has been cleared. This helps prevent mix-ups between batches.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch materials remaining after line clearance is a classic mixup/contamination risk (relevance 7) with serious recall/safety severity (8).
rows.sat_rat: Documented dual-verified line clearance is mature practice that reliably removes prior-batch materials. Line clearance consumes defined changeover time. Effective clearance relies on disciplined checklist execution. Residual materials cause mix-up rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the required carton orientation and arrangement within each shipping case
rows.plain: Place the cartons as close as possible to the required direction and arrangement inside each shipping case. This helps keep the cases stable and correctly packed.
rows.imp_rat: Correct carton orientation/arrangement is core to properly loading cases (relevance 8); deviations cause fit/closure and protection issues but are usually caught and reworked (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Semi-automatic case packers and pattern guides handle most cases consistently, but manual stations still see recurring orientation deviations. Manual pattern arrangement adds handling time whenever cartons must be reoriented before loading. Correct orientation depends heavily on operator attention and familiarity with the case pattern, making it error-prone during fatigue or line speed increases. Misarranged cases can be reworked at little material cost but consume labor time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a packed case with an incomplete or unsecured closure
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of accepting a shipping case that is not fully closed or is not securely fastened. This helps keep the contents protected during handling and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with incomplete/unsecured closure is an acceptance-gate failure (relevance 7); leads to case opening and product loss in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Automatic tapers/gluers seal consistently, but incomplete closures still occur and slip past manual acceptance occasionally. Checking closure integrity adds a brief step at case discharge. Detecting incomplete closures relies on operator inspection unless sensors verify seals. Unsecured cases risk product loss and rejection downstream.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required caser safety checks that produce inconclusive results before commissioning
rows.plain: Reduce the number of safety checks on the packing machine that give unclear results before the machine is put into service. This helps workers know whether the safety systems are ready.
rows.imp_rat: Inconclusive safety checks before commissioning are relevant to getting the caser reliably into service, and severity is elevated because ambiguous safety results can delay commissioning or allow an unsafe machine to proceed.
rows.sat_rat: Well-defined test methods reduce ambiguity, but instrumentation limits and setup issues still produce inconclusive results before commissioning. Inconclusive checks force repeat testing that adds unplanned delay. Distinguishing a genuine result from an inconclusive one needs experienced technicians who recognize marginal outcomes. Retests and troubleshooting consume modest extra resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual product or hazardous material remaining on the caser at transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leftover product or harmful material remains on the packing machine when it is handed over. This helps make the equipment safer for its next owner or user.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or hazardous material remaining on the caser at transfer is moderately central to safe decommissioning handoff, with high severity due to contamination and exposure risk to downstream handlers and environments.
rows.sat_rat: Residue remaining at transfer is a documented gap because thorough end-of-life cleaning verification is inconsistently performed. A final cleaning and verification before transfer adds a bounded step. Verifying the caser is free of hazardous residue at transfer takes inspection judgement and possibly swab knowledge. Cleaning verification adds modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replenishments involving a consumable that does not match the active production batch
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers add a supply that is not meant for the production batch currently being made. This helps prevent mix-ups and protects the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Replenishing with a consumable not matching the active batch is relevant to run integrity, with moderately serious consequences of contaminated or mislabeled cartons requiring rework.
rows.sat_rat: Few lines have scan-verified replenishment, so wrong-consumable errors during refill remain a common, poorly guarded failure. Replenishment checks are quick but happen mid-run when attention is divided. Verifying a consumable matches the active batch relies almost entirely on operator vigilance during refills. A mismatched consumable can contaminate a whole run, a costly error only loosely controlled today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in determining whether observed safety-function responses meet approved acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make it easier to tell clearly whether the safety features responded in an acceptable way. This helps prevent uncertain or disputed safety checks.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in judging safety-function acceptance criteria is moderately central to reliable qualification, and its consequences are serious because an undetected safety failure can injure operators and invalidate validation.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are often qualitative or under-specified, so different technicians reach different pass/fail conclusions, making outcomes inconsistent. Deliberating over whether a borderline response passes can stall a maintenance window. Judging conformance against acceptance criteria demands experienced interpretation, and ambiguous criteria push heavy reliance on scarce senior judgement. Clearer written acceptance criteria are cheap to produce yet frequently absent or vague in practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packed shipping cases with closure pattern, position, or coverage outside specification
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping boxes whose closures are in the wrong pattern or position, or do not cover the required area. This helps keep packed cases secure and consistently closed.
rows.imp_rat: Closure pattern/position/coverage out of spec undermines case integrity core to the job (relevance 7); risks case opening in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sealers apply consistent closures when maintained, but drift produces recurring out-of-spec seals. Adjusting closure quality adds minor time at the sealer. Proper closure pattern depends on machine setup rather than operator skill. Out-of-spec closures waste sealing material and cause rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of planned case formats omitted from the caser carton dimension requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the box-packing machine's size requirements cover every planned box type, so no box format is left out.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting planned case formats from carton dimension requirements directly undermines the caser's job of handling intended formats, though the consequence is production rework and jams rather than injury.
rows.sat_rat: Format lists are usually reasonably complete but low-volume or future SKUs are frequently forgotten, leaving predictable gaps. Compiling carton dimensions is straightforward once formats are listed and rarely delays preparation. It mainly requires diligent enumeration of formats rather than deep expertise, though completeness depends on attentive review. An omitted format means a change order or manual handling, a moderate and recoverable cost.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from unexpected machine movement or stored-energy release during component extraction
rows.plain: Prevent the technician from being hurt by unexpected machine movement or force released from the machine while removing parts. The machine must be made safe before parts are taken out.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from unexpected movement or stored-energy release during component extraction is a maintenance hazard not central to cartoning throughput, so relevance is moderate; crush or impact injuries make severity very high.
rows.sat_rat: Blocking and de-energization procedures reduce incidents, but disassembly of unfamiliar machines still produces surprise movements. Careful component extraction with movement restraint slows the teardown noticeably. Anticipating stored-energy release and unexpected movement during extraction leans heavily on experienced technician judgement. Rigging, blocking, and restraint gear are moderate costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartons loaded with an incorrect product quantity
rows.plain: Put the correct number of products in every box, so customers receive the quantity they were promised.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons loaded with an incorrect product quantity is a core count-integrity failure of the cartoning job, so relevance is high; short or over counts trigger customer complaints, regulatory issues, and recalls, giving fairly high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inline counting and checkweighing catch most miscounts reliably, though small or nested items still occasionally evade detection. Count verification is automated inline and does not slow the run when functioning. Checkweighers and count sensors remove reliance on operator counting skill. Sensors and checkweighers are a standard, justified cost on quantity-critical lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a prepared shipping-case bottom closure failing under the specified carton load
rows.plain: Make sure the bottom flap of a prepared shipping box is unlikely to come open under the weight it must carry. This helps keep the shipment secure.
rows.imp_rat: A bottom closure failing under load causes catastrophic dropout during handling (relevance 7); product loss and damage make severity substantial (7).
rows.sat_rat: Bottom sealers and formed-case designs make bottom failure uncommon, though poorly sealed cases still fail occasionally. Verifying bottom closure integrity adds negligible time when forming cases. Proper bottom sealing depends on correct machine setup or operator forming technique. A failed bottom drops product, a costly loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator activates a non-stop control when attempting the emergency stop
rows.plain: Make sure the emergency-stop control is easy to identify and does not look or act like a control that keeps the equipment running. This helps the operator stop the equipment quickly in an emergency.
rows.imp_rat: An operator hitting a non-stop control in an emergency defeats the safeguard entirely; control ambiguity is moderately central to safe operation and the injury consequence is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Distinctive, palm-operated e-stops make wrong-control activation uncommon, though crowded HMIs and non-standard retrofits create residual confusion. A correctly hit e-stop stops instantly; the concern is choosing the right control, not speed. Standardized red-mushroom-on-yellow e-stop conventions reduce reliance on operator judgement, but panic-driven mis-selection among nearby controls still occurs. Compliant color-coding and physical differentiation of e-stop devices are cheap and near-universal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unintended electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic energy release during caser disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from unexpectedly releasing electricity, compressed air, or pressurized liquid while it is being disconnected and removed.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended energy release at disconnection is a maintenance/servicing hazard adjacent to the core casing job but severe in consequence — arc, pneumatic whip, or hydraulic injection injuries.
rows.sat_rat: Mature multi-energy LOTO practice reliably prevents most unintended releases, with residual failures tied to procedural lapses rather than missing methods. Lockout/tagout and bleed-down procedures add a predictable, short delay to disconnection. Standard LOTO training is widely held, so disconnection rarely depends on scarce specialists. LOTO hardware and verification labor are cheap relative to the risk they mitigate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a closed shipping case opening during downstream handling
rows.plain: Keep sealed shipping cases from opening while they are being moved or handled later. This protects the contents from damage or loss.
rows.imp_rat: A closed case opening during downstream handling defeats the protective job (relevance 7); leads to spillage and product damage (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Modern sealers give consistent closure, so cases opening in downstream handling is relatively uncommon. Ensuring secure closure adds little time at sealing. Reliable closure depends on machine tape/glue application rather than operator skill. Cases opening downstream cause product loss and handling costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton grouping count or orientation differs from the scheduled case configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the number of cartons and the way they face match the planned case arrangement. This helps the finished cases be packed correctly and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of grouping count/orientation from the scheduled configuration affects core correctness (relevance 8); typically produces fit/rework issues rather than escapes (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe presets plus first-case inspection catch many errors, but subtle orientation faults still slip past manual verification. Confirming grouping count and orientation against the case spec is a quick visual check but adds setup minutes when done rigorously. Reading the case configuration correctly requires trained interpretation of grouping patterns that less-experienced operators get wrong. Miscounted or misoriented groups generate scrapped cases and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product or packaging obstructing the caser's transport path at startup
rows.plain: Prevent products or packaging from blocking the machine's path when the case-packing machine starts.
rows.imp_rat: Product or packaging obstructing the transport path at startup is highly central to the caser doing its job, and severity is moderate since it typically causes jams and downtime rather than irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Photoeyes and startup interlocks catch many blockages, but residual debris on the transport path still causes startup jams with some regularity. A pre-start clearing check is quick when performed but is often skipped to save startup time. Recognizing path obstructions before startup depends on operator diligence rather than a sensing aid. Clearing obstructions costs negligible material and only brief labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product and case positions conflict with the caser's restart sequence
rows.plain: Make sure the products and boxes are in positions that match the machine's restart steps. This helps the packing machine restart without jams or mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Product/case positions conflicting with the restart sequence is fairly central to smooth restart; severity is moderate as it typically causes jams or misfeeds and restart failures rather than lasting harm.
rows.sat_rat: Modern casers have home-position sensors and restart routines, but conflicts still occur often enough after manual intervention that outcomes remain inconsistent. When product and case positions conflict with the restart sequence, operators lose time troubleshooting and manually re-indexing the machine before it will cycle cleanly. Correctly staging positions for restart requires an experienced technician who understands the caser's home/index logic, so it strongly depends on scarce know-how. A botched restart sequence typically damages a few cases or products and consumes maintenance labor rather than large material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product units or cartons trapped when the packing cycle stops
rows.plain: When the packing cycle stops, leave as few products and boxes stuck inside the machine as possible. This makes recovery easier and reduces waste or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Product units or cartons trapped when the cycle stops directly affects the packing job and causes rework, but the consequences are typically limited to salvageable material loss and downtime rather than catastrophic harm.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standard mechanism to protect in-process units at stop, so the number trapped varies widely with the failure and is poorly controlled. Recovering or discarding trapped units adds handling time during each stop. Deciding what trapped product is salvageable takes routine judgement but little specialized skill. Product and cartons trapped when the cycle stops are frequently damaged and scrapped, a recurring material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of packed cases failing shipping-integrity acceptance criteria under routine production conditions
