rows.plain: Reduce how often packed products fail checks that show whether they can stay safely sealed during shipping. This helps products arrive without damaged packaging.
rows.imp_rat: Packed cases failing shipping-integrity criteria under routine conditions undermines the protective job (relevance 7); leads to distribution damage (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Validated case designs perform consistently, but real production variability still yields periodic integrity failures. Integrity acceptance testing is periodic, not per-case delay. Interpreting acceptance criteria needs packaging engineering judgement. Failures under routine conditions drive costly rework and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases with closures that open or loosen during distribution
rows.plain: Reduce the share of shipping packages whose closures open or become loose while being transported. This helps keep the contents protected until they arrive.
rows.imp_rat: Closures that open during distribution undermine the case's protective job (relevance 7); leads to product loss and damage in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sealer qualification and closure checks limit failures, but tape/glue variation still yields distribution openings. Closure verification adds minimal time inline. Reliable closure depends on equipment setup and operator checks more than deep skill. Failed closures in distribution cause product loss and complaint costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of released shipping cases containing cartoned units damaged during grouping or loading
rows.plain: Reduce how often shipped packages contain products that were damaged while being grouped or loaded. This helps prevent damaged products from reaching customers.
rows.imp_rat: Released cases containing units damaged during grouping/loading is a product-integrity escape (relevance 7); damaged product reaches customers (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Machine guarding and gentle-handling setups reduce damage, though residual mechanical damage still occurs. Damage inspection adds modest handling time. Detecting handling damage relies on operator inspection attention. Damaged units cause rejection and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product backs up ahead of the caser when incoming flow exceeds its handling capacity
rows.plain: Prevent products from piling up before the carton-packing machine when products arrive faster than it can handle them. This keeps the line moving smoothly and avoids jams or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Product backing up when incoming flow exceeds caser capacity is highly relevant to the throughput core job, though the consequences are typically recoverable flow interruptions rather than severe or irreversible.
rows.sat_rat: Photoeye-triggered accumulation and upstream stops handle typical flow mismatches well, though overflow and downstream spillage still occur during sustained surges. Backup accumulation is usually caught before it causes long stoppages, but clearing surges still costs time. Managing upstream flow relies on operator monitoring and manual line balancing rather than automatic control. Accumulation conveyors and buffers absorb surges at moderate capital cost and are commonly installed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or mechanical energy remaining in caser components after isolation
rows.plain: Make sure no trapped electrical power, air pressure, liquid pressure, or stored mechanical force remains in the packing machine after it is shut off and separated from its power sources. This prevents unexpected movement or release of energy.
rows.imp_rat: Residual stored energy after isolation is a lockout/tagout hazard moderately central to safe servicing and severe when latent energy discharges unexpectedly onto a worker.
rows.sat_rat: Residual energy in springs, accumulators, and capacitors is a known blind spot that LOTO addresses well electrically but less consistently for stored mechanical/hydraulic energy. Verifying full de-energization (bleeding accumulators, discharging capacitors) adds time that crews sometimes shortcut. Confirming zero residual energy across electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic and mechanical stores requires judgement about where energy hides. Test instruments and bleed fittings are inexpensive and generally already on hand.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packer restart is authorized before quality and safety conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: A stopped packing machine should not be restarted until the required quality and safety checks are complete. This helps prevent unsafe or defective products from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing restart before quality/safety conditions are met risks unsafe or non-conforming operation (relevance 6); can cause injury or defect escapes (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Restart checklists and supervisor sign-off give reasonable assurance, though under throughput pressure conditions are sometimes waved through. Restart authorization gates add some delay while checks are confirmed. Judging whether quality and safety conditions are truly met requires experienced supervisory judgement. Premature restart risks scrap and incidents, but the check itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unsupported floor-loading condition remains undetected before purchase
rows.plain: Find out before buying whether the floor can safely support the equipment and its load. This prevents purchasing equipment that the facility cannot safely hold.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected unsupported floor-loading condition before purchase is a moderate acquisition-check error, but severity is high because floor failure under a heavy caser can cause structural collapse and injury.
rows.sat_rat: Structural floor-loading checks are a recognized practice with reasonable reliability when triggered, but they are sometimes overlooked for lighter-looking equipment. Floor-loading verification requires structural review that adds a discrete step to acquisition due diligence. Assessing floor capacity against caser point loads needs a structural engineer, a competence not always engaged early. An undetected floor-loading problem forces costly structural reinforcement after purchase, while the check is comparatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to unexpected machine motion during diagnostic checks
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from moving unexpectedly while the technician checks what is wrong. This reduces the risk of injury during troubleshooting.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to unexpected machine motion during diagnostics is a servicing-safety concern peripheral to producing cartons, so relevance is moderate; being caught by moving mechanisms causes serious injury, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Because faults are frequently diagnosed with power on, exposure to unexpected motion during diagnostics remains a persistent, only partly mitigated risk. Diagnostics often require the machine live, so protective setup and cautious jogging add time without full isolation. Safe live troubleshooting depends heavily on the technician's judgement about what may move, a scarce and error-prone skill. Reduced-speed/jog modes and light curtains exist but are underused, and adding safe-diagnostic modes carries real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with flaps remaining partially open after sealing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons whose flaps are still partly open after sealing. This helps keep the packages closed and protects what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Flaps remaining partially open after sealing is a direct closure-quality defect central to the job (high relevance), typically causing rework, rejects, or presentation issues (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Folding and sealing mechanisms handle standard stock well, though flap spring-back and crease variation cause intermittent partial openings. Flap-position sensing detects partial openings in real time at the sealing station. Fine-tuning folding rails and plows for reliable flap closure remains a skill-dependent setup task. Partially open flaps generate scrap and rework that inline detection reduces but does not remove.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the caser being re-energized while disassembly or removal is underway
rows.plain: Prevent the box-packing machine from being turned back on while it is being taken apart or removed. This helps prevent unexpected movement and injury.
rows.imp_rat: Re-energizing during disassembly is a severe lockout failure that can crush or electrocute maintenance staff; moderately central to safe lifecycle handling but very severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Physical lockout with personal locks is a mature, highly effective barrier against re-energization during disassembly. Applying locks is quick and rarely delays the work. Lock-and-tag discipline is basic training that most technicians already possess. Padlocks and tags are trivially cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous energy remaining in the cartoning machine during decommissioning
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous electrical, mechanical, or stored pressure energy remains in the box-packing machine when it is taken out of service. This helps protect people working on or near it.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous energy remaining during decommissioning is less central to routine cartoning operation (relevance) but severe because uncontrolled stored energy can cause serious injury to dismantling workers (severity).
rows.sat_rat: OSHA/regulatory LOTO practice is mature and widely deployed, so hazardous energy rarely remains where the procedure is followed. Lockout/tagout de-energization procedures are routine and add only modest time to the decommissioning schedule. Trained maintenance staff and qualified electricians routinely perform energy isolation under established LOTO programs. Isolation hardware and procedures are inexpensive and already budgeted into standard decommissioning work.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of loaded cases failing closure, stacking, or distribution-handling requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the share of filled packages that fail requirements for closing, stacking, or being handled during delivery. This helps packages stay secure from the workplace to their destination.
rows.imp_rat: Cases failing closure/stacking/handling requirements affect the job's protective purpose (relevance 7); consequences are distribution damage and returns (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized transit-test protocols are mature and widely used, giving good but not complete assurance against handling failures. Distribution and stacking testing (ISTA/ASTM) adds weeks to configuration approval. Designing and interpreting transport-simulation tests requires specialized packaging-lab competence. Failed distribution testing means redesign and repeat testing, and field failures cost far more.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a battery short circuit or thermal event during removal, holding, or transfer
rows.plain: Avoid a battery short circuit or dangerous heating while batteries are being removed, stored, or moved. This helps prevent fires, explosions, and injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Battery thermal events during handling are peripheral to the everyday casing job but catastrophic when they happen, with fire and burn potential — high severity, moderate relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Established lithium-battery handling protocols and PPE make thermal events rare, though older or damaged packs still surprise crews occasionally. Battery removal adds handling steps but is a bounded task within a scheduled decommissioning window. Safe battery handling demands trained technicians who recognize damage, swelling, and thermal-runaway precursors, a skill that is not universal in general warehouse crews. Fire-rated containers, insulated tools, and disposal fees add cost but are modest against total decommissioning budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of uncontrolled caser movement after its floor restraints are removed
rows.plain: Keep the box-packing machine from moving unexpectedly after its anchors are removed. This helps prevent damage and injuries during removal.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled movement after floor restraints are removed is a specific relocation-phase error; severity is high because a shifting heavy caser can crush personnel or damage facilities.
rows.sat_rat: Rigging practice and temporary bracing usually contain movement, but unexpected shifts on unbalanced casers still occur. Assessing and controlling residual movement before releasing restraints adds a brief but necessary pause to the removal. Riggers judge center of gravity and residual energy largely by experience, so outcomes depend on crew competence. Uncontrolled movement risks damage and injury with high consequential cost, though preventive bracing is inexpensive.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel are exposed to hazardous substances while decontaminating the machine
rows.plain: Prevent workers from being exposed to harmful substances while cleaning the machine before it is moved. This helps make the transfer safer.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous substances during decontamination is a maintenance-safety concern outside the running job (low relevance), but chemical/biological exposure is a severe, potentially irreversible health consequence (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination SOPs and PPE reduce exposure well, but manual work still leaves recurring exposure risk. Decontamination is a lengthy manual scrubbing and wipe-down process before release. Safe handling of hazardous cleaning agents and pharma residues requires trained, attentive personnel in proper PPE. PPE, cleaning agents, and validated procedures add substantial cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required for the operator to reach and trigger the emergency stop after detecting a hazard
rows.plain: After noticing danger, the operator should be able to reach and press the emergency stop quickly. This helps stop the danger before someone is hurt or equipment is damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Operator reach time to the e-stop matters for hazard response but is partly a layout/design concern rather than the caser's core casing job; when the delay occurs, however, the injury consequences are severe.
rows.sat_rat: Placement is standardized by risk assessment, but blind spots and cluttered access mean the operator sometimes cannot reach a device in time. Multiple e-stop buttons and pull-cords along the machine shorten reach time, but operators still lose seconds identifying and moving to the nearest device. Locating the button demands trained situational awareness under stress, which varies widely between operators. Adding more e-stop stations is inexpensive and routinely done, so cost rarely limits coverage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous machinery motion exposing the technician during functional checks
rows.plain: The machine should not move dangerously and put the technician at risk during checks. This helps the technician test the safety features without being hurt.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous motion exposing the technician during functional checks is a servicing-safety error tangential to core carton production, so relevance is moderate; the resulting strike or entrapment injuries make severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Running checks in reduced-speed or guarded modes helps, but functional testing inherently exposes techs to motion, leaving recurring residual risk. Functional checks require running the machine, so safe-operating setup adds modest time. Positioning oneself clear of hazardous motion while observing function relies on trained situational awareness that varies. Guarding and hold-to-run controls are commonly fitted at low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases containing a carton quantity outside the specified grouping
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that contain the wrong number of cartons. Correct groupings help ensure orders are complete and shipments are accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Cases containing carton quantity outside spec is a core count-defect (relevance 8); wrong quantities cause shipment and inventory errors (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Count sensors, checkweighing, and vision are mature and make quantity errors rare in the specified grouping. Count verification is fast with sensors or checkweighers and adds little time. Specifying and monitoring carton quantity is routine and needs modest skill. Wrong quantities cause reship and rework costs that count-control largely prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected machine movement exposing the technician to injury during calibration
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from moving unexpectedly while the technician is checking and adjusting it. This helps protect the technician from injury.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected machine movement during calibration touches the setup work needed to run the job (moderate relevance), and a technician injury is a severe, life-altering consequence when it occurs (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reduced-speed and hold-to-run practices reduce risk, yet calibration often needs motion present so unexpected movement remains a real hazard. Calibration in a controlled or jog mode adds setup time but fits within planned tasks. Safe calibration near live mechanisms depends on the technician's care and familiarity with the axis being tuned. Calibration fixtures and safe-mode controls are moderate-cost and generally available.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous fluids or adhesives being released during draining, staging, or transfer from the cartoning machine
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous liquids or glue from being released while they are drained, moved, or prepared for disposal. This helps protect workers and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Release of hazardous fluids or adhesives during draining/transfer is a servicing hazard, not central to running the carton line, so relevance is moderate; chemical exposure or spills cause serious harm and cleanup, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual draining with containment works but drips, cured adhesive, and awkward drain points cause frequent minor releases. Draining and transferring fluids and adhesives is a slow, careful manual operation during disposal. Clean draining of viscous adhesives and process fluids relies on operator technique and knowledge of drain points. Spill containment, absorbents, and proper disposal containers add real material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical product-protection, case-integrity, and handling requirements omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Include all important requirements for protecting the product, keeping the package intact, and handling it safely. This helps prevent damage or loss of protection.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical protection/integrity/handling requirements from evaluation criteria is a validation-scope gap (relevance 5); an incomplete criteria set lets serious failures escape (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and standards exist, but coverage depends on the specifier and important criteria are still routinely omitted. Building complete evaluation criteria upfront takes time but prevents later loops. Knowing every product-protection, integrity, and handling requirement to include demands broad cross-functional expertise. Omitted criteria cause missed defects that cost far more downstream than thorough criteria-setting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected machine motion after the operator initiates shutdown
rows.plain: Make sure the machine stops moving as expected after the operator presses the stop control. This helps prevent people or objects from being caught by continuing motion.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected machine motion after shutdown is a safety-adjacent control issue rather than the core cartoning throughput job, so relevance is moderate; however, when it occurs an operator can be struck by moving parts, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Stored energy in springs, gravity, or pneumatics can still cause residual motion after shutdown, though servo-braked modern machines handle this well in most cases. Controlled stop functions bring motion to rest promptly after shutdown initiation without meaningful delay. Stored-energy release is largely engineered in, requiring little operator awareness of residual motion. Brakes, servo controls, and energy-dissipation features are standard cost items on current machines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unintended release of stored energy during caser shutdown
rows.plain: Prevent stored power from being released unexpectedly when the packing machine is shut down and taken out of service. This helps protect people from sudden movement or other energy-related harm.
rows.imp_rat: Stored-energy release during shutdown is a servicing hazard moderately relevant to safe caser lifecycle and severe when it occurs, causing sudden mechanical or fluid injury.
rows.sat_rat: Standard shutdown checklists make unintended stored-energy release during shutdown uncommon when the sequence is followed. Controlled shutdown sequences take a defined amount of time and are seldom the schedule bottleneck. Following a documented shutdown sequence is routine for trained maintenance staff. Shutdown procedures cost little beyond the technician's time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of departures from validated instructions during case packing
rows.plain: Make sure workers follow the approved instructions when packing cases, with fewer departures from those instructions. This helps keep the packing process consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Departures from validated instructions during packing undermine process control (relevance 6); unvalidated operation risks compliance and defect escape (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: SOP discipline and supervision limit departures, but production pressure produces recurring deviations. Following validated instructions does not add time when adhered to. Adherence depends on training and supervision to prevent shortcuts. Departures cause deviations and rework costs.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of judging machinery as compatible when its carton-format, throughput, or line-interface requirements do not match the sterile fill-finish operation
rows.plain: Avoid deciding that a used or repaired machine will work when its package sizes, production speed, or connections do not fit the sterile product-filling operation. This helps prevent buying equipment that cannot work safely or effectively with the existing process.
rows.imp_rat: Misjudging machinery compatibility with carton-format/throughput/line-interface needs is a selection-phase error affecting whether the job can be done at all, so relevance is meaningful; a mismatch to a sterile fill-finish line causes costly rework and downtime, giving fairly high severity.
rows.sat_rat: URS-driven checklists and FAT protocols catch many mismatches, yet subtle format or interface incompatibilities still slip through into commissioning fairly often. Compatibility checks against carton-format and line-interface specs are routine during vendor selection but iterating on incomplete data can stretch the acquisition timeline. Correctly judging format, throughput, and interface fit for sterile fill-finish demands seasoned packaging engineers who are not always available on the buying team. The cost of a wrong compatibility call is enormous, but pre-purchase due-diligence effort itself is modest relative to machine price.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection activity introduces contamination into the sterile fill-finish area
rows.plain: Prevent checking the equipment for wear from bringing dirt or germs into the sterile filling area. This helps keep the products free of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection-introduced contamination into the sterile area is a specialized side activity relative to core cartoning throughput (lower relevance), yet compromised sterility is catastrophic for compliance and patient safety (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because inspection is minimally invasive, established gowning and behavior SOPs keep contamination fairly well controlled, with lapses from touch and particle shedding. Gowning and controlled entry for inspection add time but are routine. Inspecting without contaminating requires standard aseptic-behavior training that most sterile-area staff hold. Inspection is low-material-cost, mainly gowning consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected actuator movement caused by residual pneumatic pressure during service
rows.plain: Prevent machine parts from moving unexpectedly because pressurized air remains in the system during servicing. This helps protect the person doing the work.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pneumatic pressure causing actuator movement during service is a maintenance-phase hazard (moderate relevance), but the resulting crush/strike injury is severe (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where dump valves and bleed procedures exist residual-pressure movement is controlled, but trapped-air release remains a frequently overlooked failure mode. Bleeding pneumatic pressure adds a quick venting step before service. Knowing that accumulators and trapped air can drive actuators after isolation requires specific pneumatic awareness some techs lack. Exhaust/dump valves are cheap and often built into the machine's isolation block.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required format component is absent or incorrectly seated when readiness is declared
rows.plain: Before declaring the setup ready, make sure every required part is present and properly fitted. This prevents missing or loose parts from causing problems later.
rows.imp_rat: A missing/misseated format component when readiness is declared risks faulty startup (relevance 6); consequences are jams or defective initial output, usually caught (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on manual visual confirmation without sensors means absent or partially seated parts are a recurring, under-detected failure. Checking that every format component is seated adds inspection time before readiness is declared. Knowing which components must be present and how they seat correctly demands experienced judgement rather than a simple checklist. A missing or misseated part discovered mid-run causes jams and wasted material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of shipping-case material losing protective strength during distribution
rows.plain: The material used for shipping boxes should be less likely to lose its protective strength while products are being transported. This helps the boxes continue protecting what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Case material losing protective strength during distribution defeats the protective function (relevance 6); leads to collapse and product damage in transit (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Incoming material specs and supplier controls help, but humidity, lot variation and handling degrade protection unpredictably. Material qualification is upfront, not a per-run time cost. Assessing protective strength requires packaging specialist expertise. Weak material causes distribution damage and product loss costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged by compression or contact within the specified case arrangement
rows.plain: Prevent boxes of products from being crushed or damaged by squeezing or rubbing against other boxes in the planned case arrangement. This keeps the products in saleable condition.
rows.imp_rat: Compression/contact damage within the case arrangement affects product protection (relevance 6); damaged units mean waste and possible sterile compromise (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Compression testing and dividers reduce contact damage, but marginal arrangements still yield occasional crushed units. Detecting compression or contact damage requires added conditioning and inspection time. Judging compression tolerance within an arrangement needs knowledge of stacking strength and product fragility. Compression damage produces scrap and returns that erode margins under today's practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases that cannot be closed correctly with the specified carton arrangement
rows.plain: Reduce the share of shipping boxes that cannot be closed properly with the required arrangement of cartons. This helps packages fit securely and ship as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Cases that cannot close correctly halt the packing job (relevance 7); leads to rework and protection risk if forced (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Sealer settings and pattern validation resolve most closure issues, yet edge cases still slip through into runs. Closure problems typically appear during trials and force time-consuming redesign loops. Predicting whether a case will close correctly with a given arrangement takes packaging expertise and hands-on validation. Cases that won't close correctly are scrapped or reworked, wasting product and materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury while disconnecting, moving, or loading the caser for return
rows.plain: Prevent people from being injured while disconnecting, moving, or loading the packing machine for return.
rows.imp_rat: Injury while disconnecting/moving the caser for return is a lifecycle handling hazard less central to routine operation but severe — heavy-equipment crush and lifting injuries.
rows.sat_rat: Injuries during heavy-equipment removal remain a recurring risk despite manual-handling procedures, so outcomes are only moderately consistent. Safe rigging, team lifts, and manual handling steps slow the removal noticeably. Avoiding injury during disconnection and loading relies heavily on rigging judgement and constant attention that varies by crew. PPE, lifting aids, and extra personnel add cost but are standard budgeted items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required caser and facility compatibility inputs that are missing, outdated, or conflicting
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of required information about the case-packing machine and building that is missing, out of date, or contradictory. This helps confirm that the equipment will work properly in the intended location.
rows.imp_rat: Missing, outdated, or conflicting compatibility inputs affect the install/commissioning workflow moderately, and the consequences are correctable rework and delay rather than irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Facility and caser data typically live in scattered, uncontrolled documents with no single validated source, so missing, stale, and conflicting inputs are a chronic, poorly-served pain. Chasing missing or outdated compatibility inputs across vendors and facility records repeatedly stalls the acquisition workflow. Knowing which inputs matter and spotting stale or conflicting data requires experienced reviewers who are not always available. Rework driven by bad input data is expensive, though gathering the data itself is mostly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous mechanical, pneumatic, or electrical energy during inspection
rows.plain: The technician should avoid contact with dangerous moving parts, pressurized air, or electricity while checking the equipment for wear. This helps prevent serious injury.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous mechanical, pneumatic, or electrical energy during inspection is a maintenance-safety error tangential to core cartoning output, so relevance is moderate; the potential for electrocution or crushing makes severity very high.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-source energy isolation is well-drilled, so exposure during inspection is consistently controlled aside from missed secondary energy sources. Energy isolation before inspection is a brief, standardized step. Recognizing pneumatic, electrical and mechanical energy sources is core trained competence for maintenance staff. Isolation hardware and verification meters are inexpensive and already on hand.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from stored energy, sharp edges, or unexpected assembly movement during wear-part replacement
rows.plain: The technician should be protected from trapped pressure or movement, sharp edges, and parts shifting unexpectedly while replacing worn parts. This reduces the chance of cuts, crushing, or other injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Technician injury from stored energy or sharp edges during wear-part replacement is a maintenance-safety concern peripheral to producing cartons, so relevance is moderate; the consequence is direct human injury, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO plus PPE practice reliably controls stored energy and sharp-edge hazards during part replacement, with occasional lapses on residual spring or gravity energy. De-energizing and blocking assemblies before wear-part swaps is a routine pre-task step with predictable duration. Standard PPE and LOTO training cover this without requiring specialist skill. Guards, gloves and blocking devices are cheap and standard-issue.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of registration deviation from the target under intended production conditions
rows.plain: Keep the equipment’s alignment as close as possible to the intended position during normal production. This helps products and parts line up correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Registration deviation under normal production conditions degrades placement/print accuracy central to correct cartons (high relevance), but usually yields fixable quality drift rather than severe harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Registration control systems help, yet deviation under real speed and material variation remains a persistent tuning challenge. Dialing in registration under production conditions takes repeated trial runs and adjustment. Minimizing registration deviation depends on experienced technicians reading marks and tuning servos. Adjustment consumes some scrap material and technician hours but no major spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units damaged during grouping or loading into shipping cases during the shift
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxed products that are damaged while being grouped together or loaded into larger shipping boxes during the work shift. This helps keep products usable and avoids slowing down work.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during grouping/loading in the shift affects product integrity (relevance 6); results in waste and potential escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical handling is reasonably repeatable but jams and mis-timed loading still cause intermittent damage. Damage is typically found at inspection, adding rework time after the fact. Avoiding crush and jam damage depends on correct machine adjustment and attentive operation. Damaged cartoned units are scrapped, directly wasting finished product value.

rows.source_job: Position empty cases at loading station
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of operator strain or pinch injuries while positioning empty cases
rows.plain: Reduce how often workers strain themselves or get their fingers caught while placing empty boxes. This helps make loading safer and easier.
rows.imp_rat: Operator strain or pinch injuries while positioning empty cases are directly tied to routine casing operations, making this fairly relevant, though the severity of typical strain/pinch injuries is moderate and usually recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Where automatic case feed exists strain is rare, but manual case positioning still produces recurrent pinch and repetitive-strain injuries. Positioning empty cases is a fast, repetitive motion that does not itself add cycle delay. Injury avoidance depends on repetitive manual technique with little tooling assistance, tiring operators over a shift. Automated case erectors and magazines reduce manual handling but represent added capital not always present.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging embedded floor utilities during anchor installation
rows.plain: The person should avoid hitting or breaking pipes, wires, or other services hidden in the floor while securing the casing. This helps prevent damage, repairs, and possible injury.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging embedded floor utilities during anchor installation is a one-time install error of modest centrality to the caser's running job, but severity is elevated because it can damage facility infrastructure and cause costly repairs.
rows.sat_rat: Where as-built drawings and scanning are used it works well, but incomplete records mean embedded utilities are still struck with some regularity. Utility location before anchoring can add time if scans and drawings must be obtained. Interpreting floor-utility drawings and scan results requires specialized knowledge not always on the install crew. Striking embedded conduit or lines is expensive to repair and disruptive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of replacement-component misalignment after installation on the warehouse caser
rows.plain: Make sure replacement parts are lined up as accurately as possible after being installed on the machine that packs products into cases. Correct alignment helps the machine work reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement-component misalignment after installation moderately affects casing reliability; severity is medium-high because misalignment causes jams, accelerated wear, and rework but is usually correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment jigs and reference marks help, but final quality still varies technician-to-technician with recurring residual misalignment. Aligning replacement components during installation adds meaningful bench time when repeated adjustments are needed. Achieving correct alignment leans heavily on the technician's feel and experience, making outcomes dependent on scarce expertise. Misalignment wastes parts and consumes labor hours in rework but rarely destroys costly components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of understatement in the required caser cases-per-minute capacity relative to peak sterile fill-finish output
rows.plain: Make sure the required box-packing speed is not estimated too low compared with the highest filling speed. This helps the packing equipment keep up with production.
rows.imp_rat: Understating cases-per-minute capacity is a specification/sizing defect relevant to the caser meeting throughput demand; the consequence is a bottleneck against peak output, moderately impactful but not harmful.
rows.sat_rat: Established practice applies peak-demand and efficiency factors, yet optimistic uptime assumptions still occasionally leave the caser undersized against true peaks. Capacity sizing is done once during specification and rarely revisited, so it adds little to preparation time. Correctly translating peak sterile fill-finish output into required cases-per-minute requires process engineers who understand line surges and buffering. Understated capacity forces costly retrofits or a second machine, but standard derating margins usually catch gross errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incomplete or conflicting carton data are used to define shipping-case requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that missing or conflicting carton information is used when deciding how cases for shipping should be made. Clear information helps ensure the cases are designed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete/conflicting carton data used to define case requirements is a specification-input error (relevance 5); bad requirements can cause systematic misfit/defects (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Data reviews exist but siloed, out-of-date carton data still feeds case requirements, so errors persist. Reconciling conflicting carton data delays finalizing case requirements. Detecting incomplete or conflicting source data requires data-management diligence and domain knowledge. Decisions on bad data cause expensive rework and requalification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual drive tension deviation from the caser's specification after adjustment
rows.plain: After adjustment, make sure the machine's belt or material-pulling force is as close as possible to the required setting. This helps the machine run smoothly and keep parts properly lined up.
rows.imp_rat: Residual drive tension deviation after adjustment affects mechanical reliability of the caser's motion (moderate relevance), and if out of spec it can cause jams, slippage, or premature wear that halts casing (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: With tension gauges the outcome is fairly repeatable, but reliance on manual adjustment leaves recurring deviation from spec. Tensioning and re-checking drives is an iterative task that stretches out when specs are tight. Hitting the specified drive tension depends on the technician's calibrated feel or gauge use, a skill-heavy step. Tension gauges are inexpensive and off-spec tension mainly costs downstream wear rather than immediate outlay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the caser status information available to the operator at shift start
rows.plain: Make the machine's condition clear to the operator at the start of a shift. This helps the operator know whether it is ready to use and whether anything needs attention.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous caser status at shift start moderately affects safe, correct startup; severity is medium because unclear status usually leads to delays or avoidable errors rather than catastrophe.
rows.sat_rat: Handoff information is often incomplete, contradictory or lost between shifts, leaving genuine uncertainty about caser status that regularly causes wrong actions. Chasing down the true state of a caser at shift start burns operator time flipping through logs, tags and verbal handoffs. Interpreting fragmented or ambiguous status notes takes experienced judgement that newer operators lack. Ambiguity itself costs little directly but drives wasted rework when the wrong assumption is acted on.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified carton count and orientation do not fit within shipping-case tolerances
rows.plain: Make sure the planned number and arrangement of cartons fit properly inside the shipping case. This helps prevent damage or packing problems during shipment.
rows.imp_rat: Count/orientation not fitting case tolerances stalls loading (relevance 7); consequences are jams and rework rather than escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Established pack-pattern software and prototype trials catch most misfits, but tolerance stack-ups still occasionally surface in production. Fit checks are done during design and trial runs, but iterating on carton count and orientation still consumes days of layout and test cycles. Determining fit within case tolerances relies on experienced packaging engineers using CAD or pallet-optimization tools plus manual judgement. Getting fit wrong drives rework, scrapped cases, and re-qualification costs that the status quo only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the warehouse caser or heavy components shifting or tipping during unpacking
rows.plain: Keep the packing machine and heavy parts from moving or tipping while they are being unpacked. This helps prevent injuries and damage.
rows.imp_rat: Shifting or tipping of heavy components during unpacking is a one-time handling error, but severity is high due to serious crush-injury risk to personnel and potential equipment destruction.
rows.sat_rat: Rigging plans, lifting points, and cribbing are standard practice that keep tipping incidents uncommon though not eliminated. Rigging and unpacking planning adds modest time to the receiving stage. Safe unpacking of heavy, top-heavy components relies on trained riggers who understand load stability. Proper rigging gear is inexpensive versus a dropped or damaged caser.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton groups whose arrangement differs from the specified case pattern
rows.plain: Keep the groups of boxes arranged in the required pattern. This helps the boxes fit, move, and be handled as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Arrangement differing from the specified pattern is relevant to correct loading (relevance 7); consequences are fit and closure rework, not typically escape (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Pattern forming plates and guides give consistent results on automated packers, but manual stations still show recurring arrangement variance. Correcting pattern deviations interrupts the loading rhythm and adds time. Matching the specified pattern demands operator recognition and care, especially with complex layouts. Pattern rework mostly costs labor rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packing qualification activities compromise applicable sterile-area contamination controls
rows.plain: Make sure testing the case-packing process does not weaken controls that keep the sterile work area free from contamination. This protects the cleanliness of the area during testing.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification activities compromising sterile-area controls is peripheral to routine packing (relevance 5) but contamination of sterile areas is a severe safety event (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-area contamination controls are a mature, tightly enforced discipline, making this need well served. Contamination-control precautions during qualification are proceduralized and rarely delay the work. Executing qualification without breaching sterile-area controls requires disciplined trained personnel. A contamination breach is extremely costly, so investment here is prioritized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of floor anchors loosening during high-speed cartoning operation
rows.plain: Keep the bolts holding the machine to the floor from coming loose during fast box-packing work. This keeps the machine stable and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Floor anchors loosening during high-speed operation affects sustained running of the machine (moderate relevance), and can cause misalignment, damage, or a safety incident if unchecked (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Engineered anchoring per vibration specs is well understood, though high-speed dynamic loads occasionally loosen improperly installed anchors. Proper anchor installation and torque checks add modest time during setup. Correct anchor selection and torqueing follows established methods within reach of trained millwrights. Chemical or mechanical anchors are inexpensive relative to consequences of loosening.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cartoning machine misalignment after floor anchoring
rows.plain: Keep the packing machine properly lined up after it is fastened to the floor. This helps it operate correctly and reduces wear or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Machine misalignment after anchoring undermines reliable, accurate cartoning across the run (fairly high relevance), leading to persistent defects and downtime rather than catastrophic harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard leveling and laser-alignment practice reliably delivers acceptable alignment, though anchoring can still induce small residual misalignment. Leveling and alignment after anchoring is an iterative step that consumes noticeable setup time. Achieving alignment relies on skilled use of levels and lasers by experienced fitters. Shims and precision instruments are low cost within the overall install.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the machine being re-energized by another party before removal
rows.plain: Prevent someone else from turning the machine back on before it has been removed or made safe. This protects the people working on it.
rows.imp_rat: Re-energization by another party before lockout removal is a specific lockout/tagout gap not central to the cartoning function, keeping relevance modest; when it happens a worker can be killed or maimed, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Group-lockout and tagging conventions reliably prevent re-energization, though communication lapses across shifts occasionally allow it. Applying tags and locks is quick and does not materially delay the removal. Lockout discipline is basic trained practice requiring little specialized skill. Locks and tags are trivially cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packing conditions drift beyond validated limits during production
rows.plain: Keep the conditions for packing cases within the tested acceptable range during production. This helps keep the process working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Conditions drifting beyond validated limits during production risks out-of-control operation (relevance 6); can produce undetected defects (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Some parameters are monitored, but many case-packing conditions lack continuous alarms so drift can go unnoticed. Continuous monitoring is passive and adds no delay. Recognizing drift requires attentive interpretation of process signals. Undetected drift causes batch-scale waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping-case configurations lacking substantiated protection against applicable distribution hazards
rows.plain: Make sure every shipping package design has reliable evidence that it can protect the products from the problems they may face during delivery. This helps prevent damage in transit.
rows.imp_rat: Configurations lacking substantiated distribution-hazard protection is a validation-evidence gap (relevance 5); unsubstantiated protection can let field failures occur (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: ISTA-type qualification covers major formats but configuration changes often outpace substantiation. Distribution-hazard substantiation is upfront validation, not per-run. It requires specialized transport-simulation and packaging expertise. Unsubstantiated protection risks distribution loss costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the specified caser weight range and planned sterile-product packouts
rows.plain: Make sure the packing machine's allowed weight range matches the planned packages of germ-free products. This helps the machine handle the packages correctly.
rows.imp_rat: A weight-range mismatch to sterile packouts is a spec defect relevant to the caser correctly handling intended products; consequences are dropped or damaged cases and rework, moderate on both.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor weight specs and known product masses usually align well, though edge-case heavy packouts occasionally exceed assumed ranges. Weight-range specification is a discrete spec-time task that seldom drives schedule. Matching caser weight envelope to sterile packouts needs packaging engineers familiar with filled-case masses, a moderately specialized input. A mismatch means jams or a wrong-rated machine, but the cost is bounded when catalog ranges comfortably bracket typical packouts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartoned units being trapped or damaged during shipping-case closure
rows.plain: Prevent products from getting caught or damaged when packed cases are closed for shipping. This helps ensure the products arrive in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Units trapped/damaged during closure affects the loading step (relevance 6); results in damaged product and rework, generally detectable (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Case sealers with guide rails largely prevent trapping, though manual closure still causes occasional carton damage. Clearing trapped cartons during closure adds unplanned time. Avoiding trapping flaps requires operator care when guiding cartons into the case. Trapped or damaged cartons waste product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the original stoppage recurs during the first resumed cycle
rows.plain: Avoid having the problem that stopped the process happen again when the first cycle starts back up. This helps the operation resume safely and successfully.
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of the original stoppage on the first resumed cycle is relevant to reliable caser operation, but the consequence is primarily lost throughput and repeated intervention rather than harm.
rows.sat_rat: Without diagnostics identifying why the stop occurred, operators frequently restart into the same fault, making first-cycle recurrence a common frustration. A recurring stoppage on the first resumed cycle wastes time re-clearing and re-starting repeatedly. Avoiding recurrence depends on the operator correctly diagnosing root cause, which is often guesswork under pressure. Repeat jams waste minimal material but consume significant labor and downtime.

rows.stk: Engineering, procurement, or operations staff responsible for equipment selection.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate casers with missing or conflicting throughput, case-size, configuration, or commercial data
rows.plain: Reduce the number of possible case-packing machines with missing or conflicting information about speed, package sizes, setup, or cost. This makes it easier to compare and choose the right machine.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting throughput/case-size/commercial data on candidate casers matters at the selection stage; severity is medium because bad data leads to poor purchase decisions but is correctable before commitment.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor-supplied data is inconsistent and often incomplete, so conflicting or missing candidate data is a persistent problem only partly mitigated by RFQ templates. Reconciling throughput, case-size, configuration, and commercial data across candidates is a slow document-comparison effort. Interpreting and normalizing conflicting vendor data sets requires experienced procurement and process engineers. Buying on wrong data leads to expensive mismatches, though the comparison work itself is mainly labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the trial materials differ from the materials and formats intended for full production
rows.plain: Make the materials and packaging used in the test run as similar as possible to those planned for regular production. This matters because the trial will give a more reliable picture of how full production will work.
rows.imp_rat: Trial materials differing from production materials is relevant to validation accuracy for the caser, but consequences are mostly invalid trial results and later rework rather than acute harm.
rows.sat_rat: Trials commonly proceed with substitute cartons or non-final formats, so results poorly predict full-production behavior. Sourcing true production-representative materials for trials often lags, delaying meaningful validation. Judging whether trial stock adequately represents production materials takes experienced eyes that many teams lack. Using surrogate or off-spec trial materials wastes trial runs and risks buying equipment tuned to the wrong media.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged during grouping or case loading
rows.plain: Prevent boxed products from being damaged while they are grouped together or loaded into shipping cases. This helps them arrive in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during grouping/case loading affects integrity of the loaded product (relevance 6); results in waste and possible escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Qualified equipment and adjustments keep damage low but variability persists across formats. Preventive setup adjustments are within normal changeover time. Minimizing damage depends on equipment tuning skill and monitoring. Damage during loading wastes finished goods.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units loaded in orientations susceptible to distribution damage
rows.plain: Load boxed products in positions that protect them from damage during shipping. This helps keep them from being crushed, shifted, or otherwise harmed in transit.
rows.imp_rat: Units loaded in damage-susceptible orientations affects distribution survival (relevance 6); results in field damage, moderate consequence (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Fixtured loading and vision guidance control orientation well, though subtle susceptibilities can be missed at design time. Confirming orientation across a pattern adds modest inspection time. Identifying damage-susceptible orientations needs packaging and product-fragility knowledge. Wrong orientations drive damage costs that orientation-controlled loading partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases exceeding allowable mass limits when loaded as specified
rows.plain: Keep shipping cases within the allowed weight when filled as planned. This helps make them safe to move and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cases exceeding allowable mass when loaded as specified affects handling/compliance (relevance 6); creates injury and carrier-rejection risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Inline checkweighing is a mature, ubiquitous control that makes mass-limit breaches rare. Mass verification is near-instant with inline checkweighing. Ergonomic and regulatory mass limits are documented and easy to apply. Overweight cases risk carrier penalties and rejections that current weighing reliably avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of loaded shipping cases exceeding applicable mass or dimensional constraints
rows.plain: Keep loaded shipping cases within the allowed weight and size limits. This helps make them safe and acceptable to handle and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cases exceeding mass/dimensional constraints affect handling and compliance (relevance 6); consequences are handling injury risk and carrier rejection (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Checkweighers and dimensioners reliably flag out-of-spec cases, so violations are uncommon. Mass and dimension checks are quick against defined constraints. Applying documented mass/dimensional limits requires little specialized skill. Exceeding constraints triggers carrier surcharges and rework that checkweighing prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test-batch cartons formed with dimensional or structural defects
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons in the test batch that are misshapen or not strong enough. Properly formed cartons help protect the product and move through the line smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Test-batch cartons with dimensional or structural defects is relevant to validating forming quality, but severity is moderate since defects are detected during the test batch before production release.
rows.sat_rat: Forming sections are well engineered so most test cartons form correctly, with occasional dimensional defects during initial tuning. Forming trial cartons and inspecting dimensions adds bounded setup time. Correct forming setup relies on technician adjustment of forming tooling and knowledge of blank behavior. Rejected malformed cartons are a small material cost during preparation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test-batch cartons sealed incompletely or out of alignment
rows.plain: Reduce the number of test cartons that are not fully sealed or whose seals are out of place. Proper sealing helps keep the product inside and protected.
rows.imp_rat: Test-batch cartons sealed incompletely or misaligned is relevant to seal quality validation, but severity is moderate as defects are caught in the test batch rather than shipped.
rows.sat_rat: Standard test-batch sealing checks catch most defects, but incomplete or misaligned seals still appear during initial setup. Running and inspecting test-batch seals adds setup time proportional to the number of trial cartons. Achieving complete, aligned seals depends on technician adjustment skill with glue or heat and compression. Scrapped test cartons and adhesive are minor consumable costs.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of verification cartons whose closures remain outside specification after closing-mechanism adjustment
rows.plain: Make sure the cartons used for checking have closures that meet the required standard after the closing parts are adjusted. This shows that the machine is closing cartons properly.
rows.imp_rat: Verification carton closures out of specification after adjustment is relevant to closure quality, but severity is moderate since these are caught during verification before full production.
rows.sat_rat: Established closing-adjustment routines usually bring closures into spec, though out-of-spec verification cartons still occur before settling in. Adjusting closing mechanisms and re-checking closures involves iterative trial runs. Correct closure setup requires a technician's feel for the closing mechanism and carton stock. Verification cartons and glue consumed during adjustment are modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of approved caser shutdown steps omitted or performed out of sequence
rows.plain: Make sure workers do not skip or do the required shutdown steps for the case-packing machine in the wrong order. This helps prevent problems while taking the machine out of service.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting or misordering approved shutdown steps is moderately central to safe operation; severity is medium-high because improper shutdown can damage equipment or create residual hazards, though often caught.
rows.sat_rat: Documented shutdown SOPs and sign-offs make correct sequencing the norm, with occasional omissions on non-standard or aging casers. Following an approved shutdown checklist is routine and adds only modest, predictable time. Trained operators and maintenance staff generally know the shutdown sequence, though tribal knowledge fills gaps in written procedures. Skipping or reordering steps risks equipment or energy release damage that is costly, but the procedure itself is cheap to run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from unexpected movement of cartoning machinery during lubrication
rows.plain: Prevent the packaging machine from moving unexpectedly while a technician applies lubricant. This helps protect the technician from being caught or struck.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from unexpected movement during lubrication concerns maintenance rather than the production job itself (low relevance), but the bodily-harm outcome is severe when it happens (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because lubrication is typically done de-energized, unexpected-movement injury is fairly well controlled, with lapses only when techs grease running equipment for convenience. Lubrication is usually a shut-down task, so isolating first costs little extra time. Basic lockout before greasing points is a low-skill, well-understood routine. Isolation for lube tasks requires no special expenditure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected movement of drive components during adjustment
rows.plain: Prevent drive parts from moving unexpectedly while they are being adjusted. This helps protect the worker's hands and body from injury.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected drive-component movement during adjustment is a setup/maintenance hazard rather than core throughput (moderate relevance), with a serious injury potential when it occurs (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard isolation and mechanical restraint control most drive movement during adjustment, though back-driving and stored torque still cause occasional surprises. Locking or blocking drive components before adjustment is a short added step. Preventing drift of servo or spring-loaded drives during adjustment needs mechanical understanding that competent techs possess. Mechanical blocks and brake engagement cost little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of personnel injuries during disconnection, handling, loading, or unloading of the cartoning machine
rows.plain: Prevent people from being injured while disconnecting, moving, loading, or unloading the cartoning machine. This protects workers while the machine is being given away.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injuries during disconnection/handling/loading are logistics-adjacent to the core cartoning function (low relevance), yet any such injury is a severe, irreversible consequence (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented handling procedures keep injury counts low, though the multi-step cycle leaves recurring pinch and strain incidents. Safe handling across disconnect-through-unload adds planned time but no chronic delay. Established safe-handling training covers the full disconnect-to-unload cycle competently. PPE, rigging, and crew costs are routine and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury during disconnection, handling, or loading of the machine for return
rows.plain: Prevent people from being hurt while disconnecting, moving, or loading the machine for return. This helps ensure the machine can be shipped back safely.
rows.imp_rat: Injury during disconnection/handling for return is peripheral to the CFJ of cartoning products (relevance low, a return event is rare and outside normal production), but a personnel injury carries severe, potentially irreversible human-harm consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Mature material-handling practice makes handling injuries uncommon where lift plans are followed. Rigging and loading for return follow planned lift sequences that take predictable time. Safe heavy-machine handling depends on trained riggers and lift planners who are generally available. Cranes, forklifts, and rigging crews are significant but standard budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the risk assessment underrepresents worst-case carton, shipping-case, product, and environmental conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the risk review fully considers the most difficult expected conditions for cartons, shipping containers, products, and the surrounding environment. Otherwise, the process may seem safer on paper than it really is.
rows.imp_rat: Risk assessment underrepresenting worst-case conditions is a validation-adequacy gap (relevance 4); an underscoped assessment can allow field failures (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Risk-assessment practice exists but worst-case coverage is frequently incomplete, leaving latent failure modes. Building worst-case condition sets into risk assessments adds upfront planning time but is not the core loss here. Identifying true worst-case carton, case, product, and environmental combinations relies heavily on expert judgement. Underrepresented worst cases surface as costly field failures rather than upfront spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton misorientation disrupting group formation and case loading
rows.plain: Keep cartons from turning the wrong way often enough to interrupt sorting them into groups and loading them into shipping cases. This helps the packing line keep moving smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Carton misorientation disrupting group formation impedes throughput of the job (relevance 6) but produces stoppages and jams that are recoverable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Orientation guides help, but carton misorientation disrupting formation remains a common recurring nuisance. Misoriented cartons cause stoppages that consume time to clear and realign. Recognizing and correcting misorientation before it disrupts loading requires attentive operators. Disruptions cost throughput and occasionally damaged cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified carton arrangement fails to fit because of carton or case dimensional variation
rows.plain: Make sure the planned carton arrangement will still fit even when cartons or cases vary slightly in size. This helps prevent packing problems.
rows.imp_rat: Dimensional variation preventing fit disrupts loading (relevance 6); it mainly causes jams and slowdowns that are recoverable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Tolerance analysis and incoming-material specs help, but real-world corrugate and carton swelling still produces intermittent fit failures. Accounting for dimensional variation requires additional measurement and sampling that lengthens qualification. Interpreting carton and case dimensional variation demands statistical and materials knowledge that not every specifier has. Unaddressed variation causes jams and rejected cases, adding material waste that current practice under-controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in specified case dimensions, tolerances, carton orientation, and acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make the requirements for case size, allowed size differences, carton direction, and passing or failing clear and specific. This helps suppliers and workers know exactly what the packaging must be like.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in specified dimensions/tolerances/criteria is a definitional issue that indirectly affects packing correctness (relevance 5); unclear criteria can propagate defects (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Spec templates help, but ambiguous or under-defined case criteria remain a common source of downstream error. Clarifying ambiguous dimensions and acceptance criteria consumes back-and-forth time. Writing unambiguous tolerances and acceptance criteria requires disciplined specification skill. Ambiguity causes misbuilt configurations and rework whose cost outweighs careful specification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packed lots presented for disposition with unresolved case-packing deviations
rows.plain: Reduce the number of packed groups of products sent for a release decision while packing problems are still unresolved. This helps prevent questionable product from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Lots presented for disposition with unresolved deviations is a workflow/quality-hygiene concern (relevance 5); unresolved deviations risk erroneous release decisions (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Review gates catch many, but lots still reach disposition with open deviations under schedule pressure. Resolving deviations before disposition extends lot review time. Judging resolution completeness requires quality expertise. Unresolved deviations at disposition drive hold costs and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units damaged during group formation
rows.plain: Avoid damaging boxes while grouping them together. This keeps the products protected and ready to ship.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during group formation affects product integrity at an early step (relevance 6); typically results in waste, detectable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Collation systems and manual care keep damage during formation relatively low but not eliminated. Careful group formation adds marginal handling time. Avoiding damage during grouping depends on operator handling technique. Damaged units during grouping are scrapped, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons crushed, scuffed, torn, or opened during case loading
rows.plain: Prevent boxes from being crushed, marked, torn, or opened while they are loaded into larger cases. This keeps the products’ packaging in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons crushed/scuffed/torn during loading affects presentation and integrity (relevance 6); mostly cosmetic/waste consequences (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Guides and controlled machinery limit damage, but manual loading still crushes or scuffs cartons at a recurring rate. Handling to avoid carton damage slows manual loading slightly. Gentle handling to prevent crushing and scuffing depends on operator technique. Damaged cartons are discarded, wasting finished product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel are injured while accessing or removing the source of a case-packer disruption
rows.plain: Protect people from injury when they reach the source of a case-packing problem or remove it. This allows the problem to be cleared safely and quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury while clearing a disruption is peripheral to the packing job's output (relevance 3) but bodily harm to a worker is a severe consequence (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Machine guarding, interlocks and LOTO are mature, widely enforced practices that make injury during clearing uncommon. Lockout and safe-access procedures add time to interventions but that is accepted. Safe access depends on trained adherence to LOTO and guarding rules. Injuries are costly but the preventive measures themselves are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury during hands-on cartoning-machine training
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a worker will be hurt while learning to use the machine that puts products into boxes. Safe training helps operators learn the machine without unnecessary risk.
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury during hands-on training is a peripheral onboarding-safety issue rather than a core cartoning function, so relevance is modest; a training injury still means real harm and liability, keeping severity fairly high.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout practices and supervision reduce incidents, but hands-on exposure to live mechanisms keeps residual injury risk present. Safe hands-on training takes longer than simply running the machine, and time pressure erodes precautions. Injury avoidance depends heavily on the trainer's vigilance and the trainee's inexperience, a skill-dependent and error-prone situation. Dedicated supervised training time has a labor cost but requires little extra equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that insufficient dimensional clearance, load limits, or access obstructions prevent caser movement from the receiving point to the staging area
rows.plain: Make sure the case-packing machine can move from the receiving area to the staging area without hitting anything and can support its weight safely. This prevents delays, damage, and unsafe movement.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient clearance or access obstructions preventing caser movement to staging is relevant to logistics and installation, but consequences are primarily delay and rescheduling rather than lasting harm.
rows.sat_rat: Clearance and load-limit checks are established rigging practice, so failures are uncommon when the survey is actually performed, though ad hoc skips still cause occasional surprises. Route and clearance surveys from receiving to staging are a defined pre-delivery step that adds limited time when done properly. Basic dimensional and load-path checks are within the competence of most rigging and facilities crews, reducing reliance on rare experts. A blocked delivery path is costly to remedy on arrival, but standard measurement practice keeps prevention cheap.

rows.source_job: Remove machine anchors and foundation fixtures
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from sudden fastener release or fragmented material during cartoning-machine fixture removal
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a technician will be hurt when a fastener suddenly comes loose or broken pieces fly during fixture removal. Careful removal helps protect the technician from unexpected impacts.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from sudden fastener release or fragmented material during fixture removal is a narrow maintenance hazard largely separate from cartoning output, so relevance is modest; flying debris can cause eye or impact injury, giving fairly high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Correct tooling and PPE prevent most injuries, but seized or spring-loaded fasteners still occasionally fragment or fly. Fixture removal is done deliberately, adding modest time to protect against fastener release. Knowing which fasteners hold tension and how to release them safely is experience-dependent finesse. PPE and proper tools are low-cost and generally available.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential stacking, vibration, shock, and environmental stresses omitted from the load-integrity evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the load-safety review includes all important stresses from stacking, shaking, impacts, and surrounding conditions that could damage the load.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stresses from load-integrity evaluation is a validation-design gap peripheral to routine packing (relevance 4); an inadequate evaluation can let distribution failures escape (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Standard test batteries cover common stresses, but scope decisions are inconsistent and consequential stresses do get omitted. Adding stress scenarios to the evaluation lengthens the test program. Knowing which stacking, vibration, shock, and environmental stresses to include requires distribution-engineering expertise. Omitted stresses surface as field failures whose cost dwarfs the testing that was skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of scheduled running period with case-packing output below target
rows.plain: Keep the case-packing line producing close to its expected amount for most of the time it is scheduled to run.
rows.imp_rat: Output below target is a throughput/efficiency concern (relevance 5) with cost consequences but no direct quality escape (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Case-packing rarely holds target across an entire shift because it accumulates the effects of every downstream fault, starvation and changeover, making sustained on-target running the exception. Throughput dips are tracked on shift boards and OEE reports but detecting a sustained below-target trend still lags real time by minutes to a full shift. Holding output at target depends heavily on an experienced overseer reading the line and reacting, so consistency varies with who is running the shift. Lost output is visible in production reports but the cost of chronic under-target running is often absorbed rather than actively recovered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units damaged during Case Packer blockage removal
rows.plain: Reduce damage to packaged units while workers remove a blockage from the machine that packs them into shipping cases. This helps prevent waste and rejected product.
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during blockage removal affects product integrity during recovery (relevance 5); results in waste, generally detectable (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Damage during blockage removal recurs whenever clearance is rushed, giving inconsistent outcomes. Careful blockage removal takes longer than rushing it. Avoiding damage during blockage clearance requires operator technique. Units damaged during clearance are wasted product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared shipping cases that are malformed or not square when presented for loading
rows.plain: Make sure each shipping case is properly shaped and square before loading. This helps the cases fit, stack, and travel safely.
rows.imp_rat: Malformed/non-square cases presented for loading impede correct loading (relevance 6); typically cause jams/rework (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Case erectors produce mostly square cases, but malformed cases still appear at a recurring rate from worn blanks or setup drift. Reforming malformed cases interrupts loading and adds time. Recognizing and rejecting non-square cases relies on operator judgment. Malformed cases waste blanks and cause jams.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared shipping cases incorrectly oriented or positioned for loading
rows.plain: Make sure each prepared shipping case is facing the right way and placed correctly before it is loaded. This helps loading go smoothly and prevents handling problems.
rows.imp_rat: Cases incorrectly oriented/positioned for loading disrupts the loading step (relevance 6); mainly causes jams/rework (severity 4).
