rows.sat_rat: Template-based URS practice covers common requirements well, but omissions of less-obvious sterile requirements still recur. Building a complete compliance-laden specification takes considerable up-front time before quoting. Knowing the full set of pharmaceutical and sterile fill-finish requirements to specify depends heavily on scarce regulatory expertise. Omissions surface as change orders and requalification costs later, though good specs contain this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between specified throughput and expected production demand, product mix, and line constraints
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between specified throughput and actual demand/product mix affects capacity planning, but it is a specification-fit issue rather than a direct cartoning-execution error, keeping both proxies moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Demand forecasts are inherently uncertain and product mix shifts, so misalignment between spec and real demand is common. Aligning specified throughput to demand and product mix requires forecasting cycles that can lengthen planning. Translating production demand and product-mix scenarios into a throughput spec demands experienced planners and line engineers. Over- or under-sizing throughput wastes capital or capacity, a significant but recognized cost trade-off.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case-packing stoppages caused by packaging material depletion before batch completion
rows.imp_rat: Stoppages from material depletion before batch completion is a throughput/supply issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes delay (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Replenishment routines reduce depletion stoppages, but they still recur when monitoring lapses. Material depletion mid-batch stops packing and costs replenishment time. Avoiding depletion requires operator monitoring of consumables. Stoppages waste throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between incoming carton flow and achievable case-packing pace
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between incoming flow and achievable pace is a throughput-balancing issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); mainly efficiency loss (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Buffering and line control help, but pace mismatches remain a recurring source of interruption. Mismatched infeed and packing pace causes backups or starvation that waste time. Balancing pace requires operator adjustment and line coordination. Pace mismatch reduces throughput efficiency.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of shipping cases being unavailable when completed carton groups are ready for loading
rows.imp_rat: Cases unavailable when groups are ready is a supply-timing/throughput issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes idle time (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Case magazines and buffers usually keep cases available, so shortages at the loading moment are relatively infrequent. Case unavailability stalls loading until cases are supplied, costing time. Keeping cases available requires operator anticipation of supply needs. Stalls waste labor and throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional station adjustments required before uninterrupted case packing begins
rows.imp_rat: Additional station adjustments before uninterrupted packing is a setup-efficiency issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes minor delay (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Repeated tweaking reflects an unstable setup process rather than a confident one. Extra adjustment iterations before smooth running directly extend changeover duration. The number of adjustments needed depends on how expertly the initial setup was done. Each additional adjustment consumes product and material during trial runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the case-packer disruption source is misidentified before a response is authorized
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the disruption source before response is a troubleshooting/diagnostic issue peripheral to packing correctness (relevance 3); causes rework and possibly unsafe action (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Without good fault data operators often misattribute the source, so correct first-time diagnosis is inconsistent. Misdiagnosis extends response time as crews chase the wrong cause. Correct fault identification is squarely a diagnostic-expertise problem and is the main dependence on scarce troubleshooting skill. Wrong-cause responses waste labor and parts on ineffective fixes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoned-unit starvation at the case packer during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned-unit starvation is a throughput-flow issue peripheral to correct packing (relevance 4); causes idle time, minor consequences (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation conveyors and infeed buffers dampen many short interruptions, so full starvation stoppages are moderated though not eliminated. Upstream starvation is usually visible via accumulation sensors but buffering and operator response only partially shorten the idle windows. Anticipating starvation before it stops the packer takes practiced line-balancing judgement that not all operators possess. Starvation idles staffed equipment, wasting labor and capacity with little status-quo mechanism to recover it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of shipping-case supply interruptions during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Shipping-case supply interruptions are a throughput/logistics issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); cause idle time (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Magazine capacity and low-level alarms make blank supply fairly predictable, so interruptions are usually short and avoidable. Case-blank supply interruptions cause immediate stops, and replenishment time depends on operator restocking cadence. Restocking blanks is a low-skill task but anticipating runout takes routine attention. Interruptions idle the line with modest direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of blocked completed-case discharge during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Blocked completed-case discharge is a throughput/flow issue peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); causes delay, easily recovered (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Outfeed sensors and case-conveyor accumulation catch most blockages reliably, but downstream palletizer stalls still cause recurring backups. Blocked discharge is quickly signalled by a full outfeed but clearing downstream congestion still idles the packer for the duration. Resolving discharge blockage is largely manual clearing requiring little specialized skill. The blockage wastes staffed capacity while it persists, an absorbed throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for case-packing output to return to target after planned changeovers
rows.imp_rat: Time to return to target after changeover is a throughput-recovery metric peripheral to correctness (relevance 4); efficiency-only consequence (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Changeover procedures give a fairly repeatable ramp, but first-off adjustments still cause variable settling times. Return-to-target after changeover is a well-known ramp period that current SMED-style routines shorten but do not eliminate. Fast, stable restart after changeover leans on experienced setters, so ramp time varies with crew skill. Extended ramps waste capacity and generate startup scrap, a recognized recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of case-packing holds awaiting quality or documentation disposition during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Holds awaiting quality/documentation disposition are a delay/administrative concern (relevance 4); consequence is schedule impact, product contained (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Hold resolution depends on human availability and paperwork, making the duration erratic and often longer than needed. Quality/documentation holds can stall cases for extended periods while dispositions are chased manually. Resolving holds requires quality-authority judgement that is not always immediately on hand. Held cases tie up WIP and staff time awaiting sign-off.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unexplained variation in grouping, loading, damage, and rejection results across equivalent qualification runs
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained variation across qualification runs is a validation-analysis concern peripheral to routine packing (relevance 3); indirect consequences (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Statistical acceptance and replicate runs help, but residual unexplained variation remains a recurring headache in case-packing qualification. Investigating unexplained run-to-run variation lengthens qualification timelines with repeated reruns. Diagnosing subtle sources of variation across equivalent runs requires seasoned validation expertise that is often in short supply. Unexplained variation drives extra runs and material consumption that inflate qualification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of acceptable cartons rejected during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting acceptable cartons is a yield/waste inefficiency (moderate relevance), but the consequence is modest material loss rather than serious harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-tuned inspection systems keep false-reject rates low, but drift and conservative settings still produce avoidable rejects. False rejects don't stop the line but do reduce effective yield over time. Tuning reject thresholds to avoid over-rejection requires setup expertise. Every acceptable carton wrongly rejected is direct scrap and lost product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which each backup differs from the current approved control state
rows.imp_rat: Backups diverging from the current approved control state undermine restore fidelity, but this is a governance/records concern that only manifests during recovery, giving it low relevance to day-to-day cartoning and moderate, containable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are typically taken on irregular schedules, so they routinely lag the current approved control state. Reconciling a backup against the live control state is slow when done manually. Judging whether a backup matches the current approved state requires configuration-management discipline rarely present. Storage of frequent backups is cheap, so cost is not the limiting factor here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician calibrates against conflicting or obsolete target tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Calibrating against conflicting or obsolete tolerances is a documentation/procedural error peripheral to core cartoning function (relevance) with moderate downstream miscalibration risk (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled documents and revision management help, but obsolete specs, local copies, and version drift still let technicians occasionally calibrate to wrong targets. Confirming the current tolerance spec adds only brief lookup time. Selecting the correct target tolerance is a document-control matter more than a technical-skill one. Errors here waste rework but the control itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an interrupted update leaving the warehouse caser controls inoperable
rows.imp_rat: An interrupted update leaving controls inoperable would halt the caser (higher severity), but it is a rare maintenance-event error somewhat peripheral to routine packing; the batch cap holds importance at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Backup-and-rollback practices reduce risk, but interrupted or power-loss update events still occasionally leave controls inoperable. Update procedures include verification steps that add controlled time to the maintenance window. Following controlled update procedures requires moderate technician competence but is increasingly guided. A bricked controller can mean expensive downtime and vendor recovery, a real cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to uncontrolled mechanical or electrical energy during repair
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled mechanical/electrical energy during repair is a serious safety exposure (severity), but repair is a peripheral service activity relative to the core function (relevance). Kept within the 3.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Mature energy-control procedures make uncontrolled-energy exposure during repair uncommon where applied consistently. Full isolation of stored energy before repair adds verification steps but is routine. LOTO with stored-energy dissipation is standard training, reducing reliance on individual expertise. Isolation controls are inexpensive versus injury and equipment-damage costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish environment during repair
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of a sterile fill-finish environment during repair carries serious consequences (higher severity), but casing sits at the packaging end so relevance to the caser's core job is limited; ranking holds importance at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic repair protocols and monitoring keep contamination rare, but interventions in the sterile environment remain an inherent residual risk. Repairs in sterile areas add substantial gowning and line-clearance time by design. Preventing contamination during repair demands high aseptic skill from qualified personnel. A contamination event during repair risks costly batch loss and requalification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous motion while actuating the warehouse caser's safety controls
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous motion while actuating safety controls could injure a technician (severity), but this is a narrow maintenance-actuation scenario tangential to the core casing job (relevance). Bounded by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard safe-test practice covers most cases, but live actuation inherently retains residual motion-exposure risk. Testing safety controls under controlled conditions adds staged steps to the task. Safe actuation still depends on trained technicians understanding the machine's motion states. Procedural safeguards cost little relative to injury exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a defective warehouse caser safety control being recorded as passing during functional testing
rows.imp_rat: A defective safety control recorded as passing has serious safety severity, but it is a testing/documentation error only indirectly tied to the caser's packing job, so relevance is modest and importance stays under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Mandated safety-test protocols and checklists catch most defects, but false-pass escapes still occur when steps are rushed or misinterpreted. Functional safety testing follows defined procedures that take predictable time to run. Structured test protocols reduce reliance on individual skill, though correct execution still matters. Test equipment and documented procedures are established costs already budgeted in regulated environments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of debris or wash residue entering exposed sterile-product pathways during cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Debris or wash residue entering sterile pathways during cleaning is severe for product safety, though cleaning is an ancillary activity with modest relevance to casing operation; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Masking and controlled cleaning reduce ingress, but debris or wash residue still enters exposed pathways more often than desired. Protecting pathways before wash adds setup time to the cleaning routine. Knowing which pathways to isolate and how requires trained cleaning discipline. Ingress of debris can foul product downstream at significant cost, and protection consumes disposables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected warehouse caser movement exposing the technician to injury during component replacement
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected movement injuring a technician during component replacement is a real hazard (severity), yet component replacement is a peripheral maintenance task (relevance). Constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO and blocking practices largely control movement during part changes, with residual risk from stored energy or skipped steps. Isolation before component replacement adds standard setup time. Safe replacement depends on trained LOTO application, a routine competency. Safeguards are inexpensive against injury and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing foreign material into the product-packaging area during component replacement
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material in the packaging area during component replacement risks product integrity (elevated severity), but it is a maintenance-time event marginal to the core casing function (moderate relevance), held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Tool accountability and FOD practices are mature in pharma packaging areas, yet loose fasteners or debris during swaps still occasionally slip through. Component-change procedures include verification and cleaning steps that lengthen the task but are routinely scheduled. Preventing foreign material relies on technician diligence with tool control and part staging rather than a foolproof system. Foreign-material events trigger costly investigations and possible product rejection, though preventive controls are relatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a replacement component incompatible with warehouse caser requirements
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incompatible replacement component is a repair-support error rather than a central operational one (low relevance), but it can produce recurring faults and rework (moderate severity). Kept at/below the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented spare parts lists and stores control catch most mismatches, but substitutions and superseded parts still cause occasional incompatibilities. Verifying part compatibility against BOMs and manuals adds lookup time before ordering or fitting. Correct selection depends on knowledge of the exact machine configuration, which varies by technician. An incompatible part means wasted parts, expedited reorders and repeated downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous energy while inspecting the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous energy during inspection has meaningful harm potential (severity), yet inspection is an infrequent support task not central to the caser's core throughput function (relevance). Held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Widely deployed hazardous-energy control makes energized-inspection exposure rare where LOTO is enforced. Energy isolation before inspection is a quick, well-drilled step in most sites. Standard LOTO training makes de-energization a routine competency rather than expert-dependent. Isolation hardware is low-cost against the electrical injury it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish environment while inspecting the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during inspection has meaningful sterile-safety severity, yet inspection contamination is peripheral to the caser's throughput job (moderate relevance); capped by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: GMP cleanroom protocols and environmental monitoring catch most excursions, but human-borne contamination during unplanned maintenance remains a recurring residual risk. Gowning, line clearance and pre-entry protocols add substantial time before inspection can begin, and that overhead is well-established. Aseptic-area entry demands trained personnel and constant discipline, so contamination risk rises whenever less-experienced maintenance staff enter the sterile zone. Consumable gowning, environmental monitoring and potential batch loss on a breach make each maintenance intervention expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual guide misalignment after adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Residual guide misalignment after adjustment can cause packing errors, so it touches the CFJ, but its consequences are usually correctable jams or minor defects rather than catastrophic loss, keeping it within the low-importance batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Even with dial indicators, residual guide misalignment remains a common repeat issue that surfaces during commissioning runs. Iterative guide alignment adds bench time as the technician re-measures and re-shims. Correct guide alignment is a finesse task heavily dependent on technician experience and patience. Misalignment mostly costs rework labor and minor scrap rather than significant materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected caser movement during the pre-shift functional check
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected movement during the pre-shift functional check poses a safety risk (severity) but occurs during a preparatory check rather than core operation (relevance). Held to the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Defined pre-shift procedures with guarding make unexpected movement during checks uncommon but not fully eliminated. Pre-shift checks follow a defined routine that adds only minor time. Standardized checklists reduce dependence on individual judgement during the functional check. The checks are low-cost and prevent costly incidents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unsafe or unserviceable caser condition remaining undetected during the pre-shift check
rows.imp_rat: An unsafe or unserviceable condition undetected at the pre-shift check has safety severity, but pre-shift checking is a supporting activity rather than the packing job itself; batch ranking constrains importance to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized pre-shift checklists catch obvious issues reliably, but vigilance-dependent checks still let subtle unserviceable conditions slip through. Pre-shift checks are time-boxed routines that fit within startup schedules. Checklists lower the skill needed, but spotting subtle unsafe conditions still relies on operator attentiveness. Missed conditions can cause costly failures downstream, though the check itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the sterile fill-finish area during warehouse caser consumable replacement
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the sterile fill-finish area during consumable replacement carries real severity for product safety, but this is an occasional service activity with limited relevance to routine casing throughput; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated aseptic procedures and environmental monitoring hold most of the time, but contamination during interventions remains a known residual risk. Aseptic consumable replacement procedures deliberately add gowning and setup time. Maintaining sterility during replacement demands trained aseptic technique from qualified personnel. Contamination events trigger costly batch loss and reprocessing, and prevention consumes disposables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a consumable that does not match the warehouse caser service point
rows.imp_rat: Installing a consumable that mismatches the service point has moderate relevance to correct operation but generally low severity since it is caught quickly; kept within the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled kits and part-number verification make mismatches fairly uncommon, though lookalike parts still get installed wrong occasionally. Verifying a consumable matches its service point adds a brief check. Part numbers and kits reduce the skill needed to match consumables correctly. A mismatched consumable wastes the part and any downstream rework, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a backup associated with the wrong caser controller during restoration
rows.imp_rat: Selecting the wrong caser controller backup during restoration is a peripheral configuration-management error (low relevance to the core casing job) though it can cause misconfiguration and downtime (moderate severity). Capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backup management is often ad hoc with inconsistent labeling, so selecting a mismatched backup is a real recurring risk. Locating and confirming the correct controller backup can be slow when files are poorly labeled. Selecting the right backup relies on the technician's care with naming and version tracking rather than enforced matching. A wrong restore can cost extended downtime and re-commissioning effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the caser's product-contact areas during storage
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering product-contact areas during storage is a serious safety concern for severity, yet it is a pre-operation storage state with limited relevance to active casing; kept at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Bagging and sealed storage limit contamination, yet product-contact surfaces still pick up contamination during extended or improper storage. Protective covering and controlled storage add modest handling time. Proper product-contact protection during storage requires disciplined but teachable procedure. Covers, bagging and cleanroom storage space add cost that is often trimmed.

rows.source_job: Protect the caser from environmental damage
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of condensation forming inside caser enclosures during operation or storage
rows.imp_rat: Condensation inside enclosures is a peripheral maintenance/storage concern with modest relevance to the active casing job, and severity is moderate due to contamination and electronics risk; kept within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Condensation inside enclosures during temperature swings remains a recurring, poorly controlled problem in many facilities. Condensation prevention measures like purging or heating add little to routine time. Understanding dew-point and enclosure ventilation requires some specialized knowledge often lacking on floor teams. Enclosure heaters, breathers and climate control cost money and are inconsistently deployed.

rows.source_job: Protect the caser from environmental damage
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cleaning or process chemicals contacting incompatible caser materials
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible chemicals contacting caser materials can degrade components and threaten sterility, giving moderate severity, but relevance to the core casing job is secondary; held at or below the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning agents and documented procedures make incompatible contact fairly rare, though off-list chemical use still happens under time pressure. Confirming chemical compatibility adds a short reference check before cleaning. Material-compatibility charts reduce needed expertise but still require the technician to consult them. Approved cleaning chemicals cost more and incompatible-chemical damage can be expensive to repair.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a malfunctioning safety device is recorded as functional
rows.imp_rat: Recording a malfunctioning safety device as functional has notable safety implications (severity) but is a documentation/verification error rather than a core casing function (relevance). Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Functional test protocols exist, but without independent verification a malfunctioning device can still be recorded as good. Documenting safety-device test results is quick within the check routine. Correctly judging and recording device status depends on technician diligence and understanding of pass criteria. A false 'functional' record costs nothing until an incident, then costs heavily.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous caser motion during safety-device actuation
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous caser motion during safety-device actuation carries real injury consequences (elevated severity), but it is a maintenance-safety edge case peripheral to the routine casing job (lower relevance). Constrained by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guarded test modes and two-person protocols make hazardous exposure during safety actuation uncommon but not eliminated. Safe actuation of safety devices requires controlled test sequences that add time. Reduced-energy testing and safe-position procedures lessen but do not remove the need for skilled setup. Protective procedures are cheap compared with an injury or machine damage.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory, safety, or standards requirements omitted from the pre-acquisition assessment of cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulatory/safety requirements from pre-acquisition assessment is an administrative, one-time upstream task (low relevance to running the job); consequences can be compliance gaps but are addressable later (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established pre-acquisition assessment templates cover the common requirements well, but niche or updated standards are sometimes missed. Compiling all applicable regulatory, safety and standards requirements pre-acquisition is a lengthy documentation effort. Knowing the complete applicable requirement set relies on specialized regulatory and safety expertise. Omissions caught after purchase cause requalification and modification costs, though structured assessment limits them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the test-batch setup and the intended production configuration
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between test-batch setup and production configuration is a changeover verification concern, peripheral to steady-state cartoning (relevance), with moderate consequences of a bad first run (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Test setups often differ subtly from production conditions, so undetected mismatch is a recurring source of later surprises. Matching the test-batch setup to intended production takes effort to replicate materials, speeds, and formats. Recognizing every relevant configuration parameter to match demands experienced process judgement. Using production-representative material for tests adds some consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of final configuration changes absent from the handover baseline
rows.imp_rat: Final configuration changes missing from the handover baseline create documentation gaps rather than immediate operational failure, so relevance to producing cartons is low and severity is limited to later reconciliation and audit effort.
rows.sat_rat: Final changes are among the most likely to escape the baseline, making handover records a chronic weak spot. Capturing last-minute configuration changes into the handover baseline is a step easily lost in the rush to finish. It relies on personnel remembering to log final changes, a discipline-dependent and commonly neglected task. Updating the baseline costs only documentation time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of line stoppages while the operator identifies and responds to out-of-range inputs
rows.imp_rat: Line stoppages while the operator responds to out-of-range inputs cause productivity loss (moderate relevance), but the effect is short-term downtime with low lasting harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems flag out-of-range inputs reliably, yet resolution speed varies widely by operator and situation. When an out-of-range input triggers, the operator must diagnose and respond, and this troubleshooting extends line stoppage. Rapid correct response to an out-of-range alarm depends on operator diagnostic skill. Stoppage time has a throughput cost but little direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeated parameter adjustments caused by overcorrecting the initial deviation
rows.imp_rat: Repeated adjustments from overcorrecting deviations moderately affect setup efficiency (relevance) but mainly cost time and material, a modest consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual open-loop tuning offers no damping, so overcorrection and hunting recur frequently. Overcorrection triggers repeated adjustment cycles that extend the time to stabilize. Dialing in the right correction magnitude without overshooting is a finesse skill that varies greatly between operators. Each extra adjustment cycle wastes more cartons during the hunt.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tooling shifting unexpectedly while the technician repositions it
rows.imp_rat: Tooling shifting while being repositioned is a handling error during setup, peripheral to production job (relevance), with moderate misalignment consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Clamping and locating features help but tooling can still shift before it is locked, giving inconsistent results. Re-securing shifted tooling adds rework time to setup. Holding tooling steady while repositioning leans on operator dexterity and careful sequencing. Unexpected tooling shift can damage parts and waste setup effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a replenished consumable is loaded incorrectly and disrupts case forming or sealing
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly loaded replenished consumables disrupt case forming or sealing, which is fairly central to the caser's core job, but the consequence is typically a recoverable jam or stoppage rather than lasting harm. Capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Loading guides, keyed reels and pre-run checks catch most errors, but misloads still occasionally disrupt case forming or sealing. Correct consumable loading follows a set procedure adding little time. Proper loading depends on operator familiarity with orientation and threading, an error-prone manual step. Incorrect loading wastes consumables and causes downtime from jams or bad seals.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unknown contamination or residual-energy hazards block hands-on inspection of the caser
rows.imp_rat: Unknown contamination or residual-energy hazards blocking hands-on inspection has notable safety severity, but it is a maintenance-access issue peripheral to the packing job; the batch cap limits importance.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or incomplete decon/energy records mean crews frequently face unresolved hazard uncertainty that blocks safe hands-on inspection. Clearing unknown contamination or stored-energy status before hands-on work often stalls inspection while investigations run. Determining whether hidden hazards exist requires specialized decon and energy-isolation expertise that is scarce at disposal time. Precautionary full PPE and third-party surveys add cost when hazard status is uncertain.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damage to the warehouse caser, facility, or surrounding assets during removal, loading, and return transit
rows.imp_rat: Damage during removal, loading, and return transit has notable severity for asset value but is peripheral to the caser's operational job of forming and filling cases; ranking keeps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Mature rigging and transport practices keep damage uncommon, but tight facility egress and long transit still cause occasional incidents. Careful rigging and load planning add time but are standard and predictable. Experienced riggers and transport crews handle removal and loading routinely, though large casers test their judgement. Damage to the caser, facility, or assets in transit carries high cost, motivating protective measures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case jams after production resumes following changeover
rows.imp_rat: Case jams after changeover directly disrupt casing throughput giving decent relevance, and severity is moderate as jams are usually clearable, but the ranking caps this below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Jam-detection sensors help, but case-quality variation and post-changeover misalignment keep jam frequency stubbornly present. Case jams after changeover restart are a frequent cause of stop-clear-restart cycles that eat into ramp-up time. Clearing and preventing recurring jams depends on operator adjustment of case-erecting and guiding elements, a moderately skilled task. Jams mainly cost lost production time and occasionally a crushed case rather than large material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of items or packages damaged while their position is corrected
rows.imp_rat: Damaging items or packages during position correction touches product integrity so it has moderate relevance, but such damage is usually isolated and absorbable rather than catastrophic. Constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guide-based correction damages product only occasionally, but pinch points and hard stops still crush items at higher speeds. Damage during repositioning doesn't itself add time unless it stops the line. Gentle correction depends on properly tuned guide pressures and speeds set by experienced technicians. Each damaged package is wasted product plus disposal, a direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating an item while correcting its position at the warehouse caser infeed
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating an item while correcting its infeed position is a relatively rare edge event with limited relevance to the core casing job, though contamination could spoil product giving it slightly higher severity. Held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical guides handle most repositioning cleanly, but manual touch-ups during jams introduce inconsistent contamination risk. Position correction at infeed is usually automatic and adds little time when guides do the work. Contamination-free correction depends on well-set guides more than operator finesse, though manual intervention when jams occur raises risk. An item contaminated during correction is scrapped, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unusable case continuing through the casing process after attempted removal
rows.imp_rat: An unusable case continuing through the process defeats the removal step and propagates a defect downstream (relevance moderate), but the escaped case is typically catchable later (severity moderate), held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is often no automated confirmation that a removal actually cleared the fault, so escapes recur and outcomes are inconsistent. If a supposedly-removed bad case continues on, downstream detection and re-stopping wastes additional time. Confirming full removal relies on operator verification, which is easy to skip under production pressure. An unusable case escaping downstream can cause secondary jams or defective shipments, a compounding cost.

rows.source_job: Position empty cases at loading station
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminated empty cases entering the caser loading station
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated empty cases entering the loading station could compromise product (moderate severity) but is a peripheral input-quality concern for the casing function itself (relevance). Held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Empty-case cleanliness is only partially inspected in typical warehouse casing, so contaminated empties do slip through with recurring gaps. Inspecting incoming empties adds negligible time when handled by inline detection rather than manual pauses. Spotting a contaminated empty case relies on operator vigilance or basic sensors rather than specialized expertise. A contaminated case reaching product forces scrapping and cleanup, a real but infrequent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a case with an incorrect product quantity before discharge
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a case with incorrect quantity before discharge is a quality-verification lapse relevant to the packing job, but consequences are contained rework rather than irreversible harm, keeping importance at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Vision and sensor-based count checks with automatic rejection are standard and reliably intercept most bad cases before discharge. Reject/accept gating happens automatically at speed, so quantity checks before discharge do not slow throughput. Automated verification means operators rarely need to manually confirm counts, lowering the skill burden. Catching short/over cases before discharge avoids costly downstream rework and customer chargebacks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected run configuration does not match the scheduled product
rows.imp_rat: A run configuration mismatched to the scheduled product can produce wrong casing, moderately central to the job, but it is typically caught and reworked, so severity stays modest and importance is held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES/scheduling integration and recipe recall on many lines make this fairly consistent, though manual scheduling gaps still cause occasional mismatches. Confirming run configuration matches scheduled product adds a short verification step that rarely bottlenecks the line. Matching config to schedule is largely a procedural cross-check that most operators can perform with standard training. A mismatch wastes a production run's worth of mispacked cases, a meaningful but usually caught cost.

rows.source_job: Unload completed cases from caser outfeed
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of completed cases contaminated during unloading or transfer to the downstream station
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during unloading/transfer touches product integrity (severity) but is a downstream-handling issue somewhat tangential to the core casing action (relevance). Capped by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Enclosed transfer conveyors and guarding make transfer contamination relatively uncommon in standard installations. Contamination during transfer rarely adds time unless it triggers a cleanup stop. Clean transfer is mostly a matter of guarded conveyor design rather than operator skill. Contaminated completed cases mean lost finished product, which is expensive per unit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant reaching exposed sterile materials or controlled surfaces during caser maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant reaching sterile materials during maintenance poses a contamination hazard (elevated severity), but it is a maintenance edge case peripheral to routine casing (moderate relevance); the ranking caps importance.
rows.sat_rat: Use of controlled lubricants, drip guards and post-lube inspection covers most cases, but stray contamination still happens on tight geometries. Careful lubrication with barriers and wipe-down extends the maintenance window modestly. Avoiding lubricant migration onto controlled surfaces relies on technician care and proper application, an error-prone manual task. Food/pharma-grade lubricants and cleanup supplies add cost, and contamination can scrap exposed materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from unexpected caser movement during lubrication
rows.imp_rat: Injury from unexpected movement during lubrication is a genuine hazard (severity), yet lubrication is a routine maintenance sub-task not central to producing cased units (relevance). Capped by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO practice largely prevents unexpected movement during lubrication, with residual risk only where technicians skip isolation for quick jobs. Locking out before lubrication adds a brief isolation step to routine greasing. The isolation step is a basic trained competency, though lubing near moving parts still needs care. Safety measures are cheap relative to injury costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying a lubricant incompatible with caser specifications or sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.imp_rat: Applying an incompatible lubricant is somewhat relevant to casing operation since it can foul mechanisms, and severity is moderate given sterile fill-finish contamination potential, but ranking caps it below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled lubricant registers and dedicated storage make wrong-lube events uncommon, though mislabeling or substitution still occasionally slips. Verifying lubricant spec adds only a brief lookup step to the task. Approved-lubricant lists and labeled dispensers reduce the skill needed to choose correctly. Food/pharma-grade lubricants and controlled stocking cost more but are standard in regulated fill-finish.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous machine areas left accessible by inadequate guarding on the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous machine areas left accessible by inadequate guarding has high injury severity, but as a design/guarding defect it is somewhat peripheral to the core packing CFJ; the batch cap constrains importance to 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding assessment is a mature checklist-driven practice, so accessible hazardous areas are usually caught, with residual gaps on nonstandard configurations. Guarding checks are a routine part of a safety inspection and add minimal time. Identifying inadequate guarding against machine-safety standards is a well-defined competence widely held by EHS staff. Retrofitting guarding after purchase is costly, but the inspection to catch it is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that claimed safety certifications are invalid for the supplied caser configuration
rows.imp_rat: Invalid claimed safety certifications carry serious compliance consequences (severity), but this is a procurement/documentation error peripheral to the caser's operational job of packing cases, so relevance is limited and importance stays capped.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate review is standard practice, yet mapping certificates to the exact supplied configuration is often superficial, so mismatches still occur. Checking certification validity against the supplied configuration is a quick documentation review that adds little time. Confirming that a certificate actually covers the as-built configuration needs a compliance specialist who understands the standards. An invalid certification discovered late can halt a project, but verifying it costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of identified safety nonconformities unresolved at the warehouse caser acceptance review
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved safety nonconformities at acceptance review carry meaningful safety severity, but resolving review items is an administrative gate rather than the operational packing job, so relevance is limited under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance-review punch-list practice is established, so most nonconformities are resolved, but pressure to accept sometimes leaves items open. Tracking nonconformities to closure before acceptance adds review cycles that can extend the timeline. Judging whether a nonconformity is truly resolved requires competent safety reviewers, generally available in regulated firms. Accepting with open safety items risks costly downstream fixes, though tracking them is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-product residues or contamination hazards remaining unidentified during inspection of the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Prior-product residues remaining unidentified during inspection has real contamination severity, but inspection is a peripheral verification step relative to the core casing job; held to the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination inspection is inconsistent and history is often undocumented for used casers, so residual hazards frequently go unidentified. Inspecting for prior-product residues and contamination adds a discrete inspection task, especially for used equipment. Identifying contamination hazards from prior product requires specialized knowledge of the equipment's history and materials. Undetected contamination forces costly cleaning or rejection later, while the visual/swab check is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the warehouse caser's operating condition cannot be observed under representative load during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Not observing operating condition under representative load during inspection is moderately relevant to verifying caser function, but per the batch ceiling it ranks below prior items; consequences are a missed defect during commissioning rather than immediate operational failure.
rows.sat_rat: Representative-load conditions are frequently unavailable at the seller's site, so operating condition often cannot be observed as it will run, leaving a real blind spot. Arranging a representative-load run-off during inspection often requires special scheduling and setup that extends the process. Interpreting caser behavior under representative load requires an experienced operations eye to judge acceptability. Skipping a load test is cheap now but risks expensive acceptance of an underperforming caser later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented facility conditions differ from actual conditions at the intended caser location
rows.imp_rat: Documented facility conditions differing from actual conditions at the caser location is a peripheral, planning/setup error relative to the act of casing (low relevance), and its consequence is usually minor installation rework rather than direct casing failure (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation commonly diverges from actual site conditions, and walkdowns are not always thorough, so mismatches remain a frequent surprise. Verifying documented versus actual site conditions requires a physical site visit that can be scheduled but adds lead time. Spotting discrepancies between drawings and reality takes an experienced facilities engineer familiar with as-built drift. Acting on stale documentation causes costly install surprises, whereas a walkdown is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that changes to adjacent equipment invalidate the verified conveyor interface fit before installation
rows.imp_rat: A conveyor interface fit invalidated by adjacent equipment changes is a pre-installation edge case with low relevance to ongoing casing, and moderate severity since it delays installation; held below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Change control across adjacent equipment during acquisition is typically weak, so previously verified interface fits are silently invalidated fairly often. Re-verifying conveyor interface fit after adjacent-equipment changes requires re-checks that add time whenever changes occur. Recognizing that an adjacent change has invalidated a verified fit demands an integration engineer tracking configuration. An invalidated interface discovered at install is expensive to correct, though re-verification is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among production forecasts, packaging specifications, and warehouse flow assumptions
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts among forecasts and flow assumptions are a planning-data ambiguity peripheral to the caser's physical job; consequences are rework and misalignment rather than harm — low on both.
rows.sat_rat: There is no single source of truth reconciling these inputs, so contradictory assumptions routinely slip through undetected into procurement. Reconciling conflicting forecasts, packaging specs, and flow assumptions happens late and iteratively, adding delay each time a clash surfaces. Detecting silent conflicts across siloed documents relies heavily on a few veterans who hold the full picture in their heads. Unresolved conflicts propagate into wrong equipment sizing and material orders that are expensive to unwind later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time waiting for approved production forecasts, case specifications, and packout data before supplier engagement
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for forecasts and packout data is an upstream procurement/planning error largely peripheral to the caser's operational job; its consequence is schedule slippage, not physical harm — low on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Data that does arrive is often provisional or later revised, so the delay is compounded by uncertainty about whether the numbers will hold. Suppliers routinely wait weeks for approved forecasts, case specs, and packout data because upstream sign-offs stall in cross-functional review with no enforced deadline. Chasing and interpreting incomplete data demands experienced planners who know whom to escalate to, a scarce judgement not codified in any standard tool. Idle engineering time and rushed later rework inflate cost, though the direct spend during waiting is modest compared to the schedule hit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of configured product-handling values outside approved limits
rows.imp_rat: Configured product-handling values outside approved limits risk product damage with moderate relevance and severity, yet the ordering places it below the previous batch, capping importance at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Parameter limits are often built into recipes or PLC bounds, so out-of-range values are moderately well contained. Setting product-handling parameters within limits is a normal, quick configuration step. Knowing approved handling limits requires trained setup staff referencing the correct spec. Out-of-limit settings can damage product, incurring waste until adjusted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of configured case-dimension values that differ from the approved packaging specification
rows.imp_rat: Configured case-dimension values differing from spec cause fit and quality issues with moderate relevance and severity, but the ranking constrains importance to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Stored recipes and machine libraries help, but manual value entry still permits typos that deviate from approved specs. Entering case-dimension values is a brief configuration task during setup. Data entry against the packaging spec is straightforward but demands careful attention to avoid transcription slips. Wrong dimensions cause jams and rejected cases, wasting material until corrected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the configured product count or pack pattern differs from the approved packaging specification
rows.imp_rat: A configured product count or pack pattern differing from spec affects output correctness with moderate relevance and severity from non-conforming cases, but is held below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven controls reduce errors, but manual pattern entry against paper specs still admits recurring mismatches. Confirming count and pack pattern against spec is a quick setup check when documentation is available. Interpreting packaging specs into machine settings needs a knowledgeable setup technician. A wrong pattern discovered in production wastes cases and product until caught.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading case materials that do not match the approved specification or lot for the scheduled batch
rows.imp_rat: Loading off-spec case materials undermines correct casing output, so relevance is moderate, and a wrong-lot mismatch can trigger rework or traceability concerns (severity moderate); but the ranking caps this below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Lot labels and material staging procedures catch most mismatches, but manual verification leaves recurring gaps. Verifying incoming case material against the batch spec adds a short receiving/staging check. Matching lot and specification relies on operator diligence rather than deep expertise. Wrong material loaded means scrapped cases and a run stoppage, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of installed anchor points that fail to meet specified holding requirements
rows.imp_rat: Anchor points failing holding requirements carry high severity for stability and safety but low relevance to the day-to-day casing function; the ranking limits importance to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Torque specs and proof-load testing are mature practice, so anchor holding failures are uncommon where procedures are followed. Anchor installation and pull-testing follows established procedures that add modest predictable time. Correct anchor setting requires trained riggers, but the techniques are well-codified and widely practiced. Failed anchors mean re-drilling and wasted hardware, a moderate occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of the anchored caser from the specified level tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Level tolerance deviation of the anchored caser affects setup quality with modest relevance and moderate severity for long-term reliability, but it sits below the top batch and is held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Precision levels and documented leveling procedures make holding level tolerance a well-served, repeatable outcome. Leveling with shims and precision levels is a routine step that rarely dominates the schedule. Millwrights are well-trained in leveling machinery to tolerance using standard instruments. Shim material and time are minor costs in the overall installation.

rows.source_job: Connect caser to plant control system
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering incompatible communication requirements between the caser's control interface and the facility control architecture during integration
rows.imp_rat: Discovering incompatible communication requirements during integration is a one-time integration risk with limited relevance to steady-state casing and moderate severity from rework delays; capped by batch order.
rows.sat_rat: Interface specs are often incomplete or assumed compatible, so incompatibilities are frequently found only during integration rather than preparation. Communication mismatches surface late and cause substantial integration delays when they do appear. Detecting protocol incompatibilities early requires scarce controls-integration expertise spanning both caser and facility systems. Late-discovered incompatibilities require field rework, adapters or gateways that inflate project cost.

rows.source_job: Connect caser to plant control system
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that network configuration conflicts block communication between the caser and the facility control system
rows.imp_rat: Network configuration conflicts blocking caser communication is an integration/commissioning issue with modest relevance to core casing and moderate severity, since it stalls the line; held within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard IP address plans and managed switches catch many conflicts, though undocumented devices still cause recurring surprises. Network conflicts, when they occur, stall commissioning until IT resolves addressing, adding hours to days. Resolving IP/VLAN conflicts needs networking knowledge that machine-installation crews typically lack. Conflicts rarely consume material but can trigger IT callout and schedule costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing format components for the wrong case size or pack pattern
rows.imp_rat: Installing format components for the wrong case size disrupts casing quality with reasonable relevance and moderate severity from scrap or rework, but ranking limits it to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-part kits and labeling help, but mislabeled or similar-looking components still lead to occasional wrong-size installs. Verifying format components against the correct case size adds inspection time before the run. Selecting the right change parts relies on operator familiarity and correct part labeling, which varies by site. Wrong-format installation wastes setup labor and can scrap product before detection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between installed component positions and the required case geometry
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between component positions and required case geometry affects case quality (moderate relevance), but is a setup deviation caught in verification (moderate severity), held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Indexed mounts and setup fixtures make geometry fairly repeatable, though fine adjustment still varies by technician. Positioning components to case geometry follows setup guides that add moderate, predictable time. Achieving required geometry depends on skilled setup technicians using gauges and reference marks. Poor geometry causes malformed cases and material waste until dialed in.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product containers damaged while being loaded into cases
rows.imp_rat: Product containers damaged during loading into cases directly reduces the quality output of the caser's job, but the damage is usually contained scrap; batch ranking holds importance at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-controlled and cushioned loading reduces damage, but fragile containers still get dinged when settings drift, making it moderately consistent. Gentle loading motions can slow the pack cycle slightly, but this is a tuned tradeoff not a routine delay. Setting drop heights and grip tuning to protect containers requires some setup expertise but is not highly specialized. Damaged containers are direct product and packaging loss, and this waste recurs whenever loading forces are misadjusted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cases containing an incorrect product quantity after the packing cycle
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect product quantity in cases after the packing cycle directly reflects the caser's job, but the batch ranking places it below prior items; consequences are quality escapes that are usually detectable, so it sits at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Product-present sensors and pattern verification are mature and widely deployed, making incorrect case counts uncommon in practice. Count verification is inline and automatic on most casers, so it adds no meaningful time to the packing cycle. Automated count sensors and pattern-forming logic remove most reliance on operator counting skill. Incorrect-quantity cases that escape mean giveaway or shortfill claims, but inline detection keeps waste modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the downstream line is starved when caser output falls short of throughput demand
rows.imp_rat: Starving the downstream line when caser output lags directly affects line flow (moderate relevance), but starvation is recoverable and non-destructive (moderate severity), held within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation buffers and rate monitoring smooth most shortfalls, but sustained caser slowdowns still starve downstream at times. Downstream starvation from caser output shortfall is a direct throughput/timing loss to the line. Balancing caser output requires operators to monitor rates, a routine supervisory task not deeply specialized. Starving downstream stations wastes their available capacity and labor rather than consuming material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staging unreleased or expired packaging materials at the case packer
rows.imp_rat: Staging unreleased/expired materials risks non-conforming packaging; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Status labeling and quarantine controls make expired-material staging fairly uncommon. Checking release/expiry status adds brief staging time. Verifying status requires operator access to release information. Using unreleased or expired material triggers costly holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-lot material remaining at the station when the next lot begins
rows.imp_rat: Prior-lot material remaining at changeover risks mix-up; moderately relevant to loading job and serious, but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce carryover, but residual prior-lot material remains a recurring risk. Clearing prior-lot material adds changeover time. Thorough line clearance depends on operator diligence. Leftover prior-lot material causes costly mix-ups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of damaged or visibly contaminated packaging materials that pass verification
rows.imp_rat: Damaged/contaminated materials passing verification undermines packaging quality; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual inspection catches most gross damage but subtle defects recurrently slip through. Inspecting materials for damage adds brief verification time. Detecting damaged or contaminated materials relies on operator visual inspection. Passing damaged materials wastes downstream materials and effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a product or label mix-up when case packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Product/label mix-up on resume is a serious identity error relevant to grouping; capped at 3.0 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and label checks reduce mix-ups but manual verification still leaves residual risk. Verifying product and label identity at resume adds a short confirmation step. Correct identity confirmation depends on operator diligence in reading labels and codes. A mix-up causes costly recalls and rework.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a weak or incomplete case closure passing pre-release verification
rows.imp_rat: Weak/incomplete closure passing verification risks case failure in transit; relevant to protection, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual seal checks miss weak-but-intact closures, so incomplete closures still pass verification. Checking closure quality at pre-release is quick per sample. Assessing seal strength by eye demands experience. Weak closures cause case failure and product loss downstream.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an incorrect carton quantity or arrangement passing pre-release verification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton quantity/arrangement passing pre-release verification is a core grouping defect; serious but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling-based pre-release checks catch gross errors but intermittent count/arrangement faults still pass. Pre-release verification of count and arrangement adds a defined check step. Reliable verification depends on the checker's attentiveness and standard knowledge. Missed errors at release lead to downstream rework and chargebacks.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaged cartoned units remaining concealed in accepted shipping cases
rows.imp_rat: Damaged units concealed in accepted cases is a hidden quality defect relevant to loading; serious but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Once cases are sealed there is little means to detect internal damage, making this poorly controlled. Concealed damage isn't caught in normal timing since cases are already closed. Detecting hidden damage is very hard even for experienced operators. Concealed damaged units reach customers, causing complaints and returns.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a deformed shipping case being accepted as meeting integrity requirements
rows.imp_rat: Deformed case accepted as meeting integrity undermines protection function; relevant and consequential but held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual integrity inspection is subjective, so deformed cases occasionally pass acceptance. Judging case integrity is a fast visual check per sample. Recognizing a deformed-but-passing case requires trained judgement. Accepting deformed cases risks downstream collapse and product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a safeguard remains bypassed when case packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Safeguard remaining bypassed at resume is a safety/quality risk relevant to controlled operation; serious but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock status is often visible on the HMI, yet temporary bypasses left in place remain a known safety gap. Confirming no safeguard is bypassed is a fast check but sometimes overlooked at resume. Knowing which safeguards may have been bypassed during setup requires trained awareness. A bypassed safeguard rarely costs material but carries severe safety liability.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartons or cases from a prior batch remain when readiness is declared
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch cartons remaining at readiness is a mix-up risk relevant to grouping; serious but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance checklists are standard and reasonably effective, though hidden residual units still occasionally remain. Clearing prior-batch cartons and cases is a quick sweep but easy to rush. Line clearance is procedural and needs little specialized skill, mostly diligence. Residual material causes mix-ups and rework rather than large direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases whose recorded batch differs from the batch of their cartoned units
rows.imp_rat: Batch-mismatch in records is a traceability defect with recall implications; kept at ceiling per batch-2 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-code printing and scan checks catch most errors, but changeover transitions remain a recurring vulnerability. Batch mismatches surface at reconciliation rather than in real time. Correct batch tagging relies on operator changeover discipline. A wrong batch label can force rejection of good cases, a real waste and compliance risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases containing cartoned units from different batches
rows.imp_rat: Mixed-batch cases pose contamination/recall risk, relevant to correct grouping; consequences serious but capped at 3.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures at batch changes are standard but transition periods still allow occasional mixing. Mixed-batch cases are typically detected only during batch reconciliation. Preventing mixing depends on disciplined line clearance between batches. A mixed-batch case can invalidate traceability and force costly segregation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases whose recorded composition differs from the cartoned units loaded
rows.imp_rat: Recorded-composition mismatch affects traceability integrity of loaded cases; relevant and consequential but held at 3.0 ceiling per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Where barcode/scan verification is deployed record-to-content accuracy is fairly high, though manual steps still introduce occasional mismatches. Composition mismatches are usually found only during later reconciliation, well after packing. Accurate recording depends on correct manual entry or scanning discipline. Mismatches trigger investigation and potential recall exposure, a significant latent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases with no batch-record linkage
rows.imp_rat: Cases with no batch-record linkage breaks traceability; relevant to compliance of the job, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: MES/batch-record systems auto-link most cases where deployed, so orphaned cases are relatively rare. Missing linkage is discovered during audits long after packing. Ensuring linkage depends on consistent record-keeping discipline. An unlinked case may be unsaleable, a direct loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of shipping cases released before traceability verification is complete
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cases before traceability verification risks uncontrolled shipment; relevant and consequential, held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Release-gate controls are common but under pressure cases are sometimes released ahead of full verification. Traceability verification before release can add hold time when done properly. Judging verification completeness requires QA oversight. Early release risks recall and rework costs far exceeding the verification effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cartoned-unit movement or compression within shipping cases during distribution
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned-unit movement/compression risks distribution damage, central to protection; consequences moderate-high but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated fit and dunnage reduce movement, though loose patterns and variation still allow shifting. Void-fill and fit setup is part of normal changeover. Controlling movement depends on pack-design and setup judgement. Excess movement causes damage and product loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shipping cases with unresolved carton-to-case or batch traceability gaps
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved carton-to-case/batch traceability gaps is a compliance defect relevant to the job; capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization and batch records support traceability, but carton-to-case linkage gaps still surface. Traceability reconciliation adds record-review time. Resolving traceability gaps relies on record-keeping rigor. Gaps trigger costly investigations and hold time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case packing resumes before authorized restart conditions are verified
rows.imp_rat: Resuming before authorized restart conditions verified risks uncontrolled output; relevant and consequential, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Restart-authorization steps exist but are inconsistently enforced after brief stoppages. Restart verification adds brief hold time before resuming. Verifying restart conditions relies on supervisor judgement. Improper restart causes nonconforming output waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved doubt that sealed cases will protect cartoned units through handling and distribution
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved doubt over case protection touches core protection function; serious but capped at 3.0 per ranking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of shipping cases containing mixed product identities, lots, or disposition statuses
rows.imp_rat: Mixed product identities/lots/disposition in a case is a serious mix-up error; relevant to grouping, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and single-lot rules are standard and make mixed-identity cases rare when followed. Preventing mixed identities relies on line clearance that takes setup time. Enforcing segregation requires disciplined procedural adherence more than deep skill. Mixed lots cause expensive segregation, holds, or scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the specified carton grouping and approved packaging specifications
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from approved packaging specs is moderately relevant to correct grouping, and consequences are real but capped by ranking; scored at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and spec sign-off constrain deviation well in regulated settings, keeping mismatches fairly rare. Comparing grouping against approved specs adds review time. Detecting deviation from approved packaging specs requires document-control familiarity. Deviations cause nonconformances and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases whose traceability records do not match the physically loaded cartons
rows.imp_rat: Traceability records not matching physical cartons is a compliance defect; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated aggregation strongly reduces mismatches, though manual interventions still create occasional discrepancies. Reconciling traceability records with physical loads adds verification time. Managing aggregation integrity needs specialized track-and-trace skill. Record-to-physical mismatches trigger costly investigations and holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual cartons or prior-batch materials cause a product mix-up during a configuration switch
rows.imp_rat: Residual cartons causing product mix-up during switch is a contamination/mix-up risk; serious but held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance procedures control this well, though human lapses during switchovers still occur. Clearance during configuration switches adds changeover time. Thorough clearance depends on operator diligence rather than advanced skill. A residual-carton mix-up can cause recalls that dwarf clearance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect grouping or loading configuration is applied after a format switch
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect grouping/loading config applied after switch is a core defect; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and setup checks reduce misapplication, but changeover errors still occur without full automation. Verification after format switch adds restart time. Applying the correct configuration relies on operator/setup competence and recipe systems. A wrong configuration causes scrap and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of case volume left unused by the specified carton arrangement
rows.imp_rat: Unused case volume is an efficiency/cost-optimization concern peripheral to correct packing (relevance 3); trivial consequence (severity 3).
rows.sat_rat: Cube-optimization software gives consistent patterns, but practical constraints leave predictable void. Optimizing fill efficiency adds design iteration time. Maximizing cube utilization requires pattern-optimization expertise and tools. Unused case volume wastes corrugate and freight, a direct and recurring cost the status quo only partly minimizes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical grouping and case-loading failure modes remain unidentified under intended production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified failure modes under production conditions threaten reliable loading; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: FMEA practice is common but critical failure modes still slip through under real production conditions. Thorough failure-mode identification extends planning time but that is not the primary loss. Surfacing all critical grouping and loading failure modes depends on deep expert insight into the equipment. Unidentified failure modes translate into downstream defect costs rather than upfront spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoned units lacking a shipping-case parent after case completion
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned units lacking a case parent is a traceability aggregation defect; relevant and consequential but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Modern aggregation systems enforce commissioning checks that reliably flag units without a case parent, so this is comparatively well handled though partial-case restarts still create occasional orphans. Orphan detection usually runs at case close so timing is not the primary concern. Standard aggregation completeness checks are largely automated, needing limited operator intervention to catch orphans. An orphaned unit can hold or scrap a case, a modest but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unit identifiers associated with multiple shipping-case parents during packaging
rows.imp_rat: Unit identifiers with multiple case parents corrupts aggregation traceability; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Database uniqueness constraints and aggregation logic reliably prevent a unit from being tied to two parents in most deployments, making this fairly well served with only rare edge failures. Duplicate-parent conflicts are detected at commit time so latency plays no major role. Uniqueness enforcement is largely built into serialization databases, requiring little specialist attention once configured. A duplicated-parent identifier can invalidate aggregation and force rework, a bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of unit or shipping-case identifiers unreadable during grouping and loading
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identifiers during loading breaks traceability of the job; relevant and consequential but held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Industrial vision and code-grading systems are mature and achieve high read rates, so unreadable identifiers are a managed and comparatively well-served issue with occasional print-quality lapses. Unreadable codes trigger immediate reject/rescan cycles that slow the line, but throughput is a secondary concern. Camera and scanner setup for reliable reads requires vision-system specialists, though tuning is a well-known discipline. Unreadable identifiers force rejects and rework, causing real material and time waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing incompatible control software and firmware versions on the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Installing incompatible control software/firmware can disrupt caser operation (moderate relevance), but it is a maintenance-setup error usually caught before production, so severity is moderate and importance capped.
rows.sat_rat: Version registers and vendor guidance catch most mismatches, but incompatible firmware/software combinations still slip through in the field.
