Checking software and firmware compatibility adds a short verification step. Version compatibility matrices reduce reliance on expert knowledge but must be consulted correctly. An incompatible install can force costly recovery and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misdiagnosing the mechanical or electrical fault on the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Misdiagnosing a caser fault prolongs repair (moderate relevance) and can waste effort, but the consequence is delay rather than harm (moderate severity), capped by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic fault codes and manuals help, but ambiguous symptoms mean misdiagnosis remains a frequent, recurring problem. Diagnosis can consume significant downtime when the fault is intermittent or poorly documented. Correct fault diagnosis depends squarely on scarce experienced technicians and their tacit knowledge of the specific caser. Misdiagnosis wastes replacement parts and labor chasing the wrong root cause.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the warehouse caser fault recurring shortly after the repair
rows.imp_rat: A fault recurring shortly after repair reflects repair effectiveness (moderate relevance) and causes repeat downtime (severity), but it is a maintenance-quality concern peripheral to the core job. Bounded by the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong root-cause discipline, symptom-only fixes mean fault recurrence remains a common and frustrating outcome. Recurring faults force repeat interventions that consume additional downtime. Preventing recurrence hinges on getting root cause right, which depends on scarce diagnostic expertise. Repeat repairs multiply parts and labor costs and lost throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury from unexpected caser movement during cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury from unexpected movement during cleaning is severe when it occurs (higher severity), but it is a maintenance-safety event only tangential to the caser's functional job (low relevance); the ceiling still applies.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and guarding practice makes unexpected movement during cleaning rare where procedures are followed. LOTO before cleaning adds setup time but is a standard, accepted step. Established lockout procedures reduce reliance on individual judgement, though correct application still needs trained staff. The controls are inexpensive relative to the injury and downtime costs they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty when the technician classifies observed wear or damage against maintenance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty when a technician classifies wear or damage against criteria is an ambiguity affecting maintenance judgment; it is peripheral to the packing job and its consequences are usually minor misjudgments, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Wear-limit charts and photos help, but interpretation still varies widely between technicians, producing inconsistent decisions. Classifying wear against criteria is quick once judgement is made but slows when the technician second-guesses. This is fundamentally an expert-judgement task, and consistent classification demands scarce experienced eyes. Misclassification drives either premature replacement waste or costly late failures, both financially painful.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using conflicting specifications to set the caser's tension or alignment
rows.imp_rat: Using conflicting specs to set tension or alignment is a setup-documentation error of moderate relevance that can cause misadjustment and rework (severity). Kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document control is often imperfect with outdated drawings in circulation, so conflicting specs frequently misguide settings. Reconciling conflicting spec sources slows setup while technicians hunt the correct value. Resolving which specification is authoritative relies on experienced judgement about revisions. Wrong tension or alignment causes wear, rework and scrapped cases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving a detection sensor at an incorrect triggering position after calibration
rows.imp_rat: A mis-positioned detection sensor after calibration can cause detection errors (moderate relevance), but it is typically caught in verification and recoverable (moderate severity), keeping importance low.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated position feedback, an incorrectly left sensor position is only caught at functional test, so misfires recur intermittently. Verifying sensor trigger position after calibration adds test cycles but is a bounded task. Correct sensor positioning depends heavily on technician judgement and feel, and errors are common when settings are set by eye rather than fixtured. A mis-set sensor mostly costs rework and nuisance stops rather than large material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging a sensor or its mounting during adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Damaging a sensor during adjustment is a localized maintenance mishap with low relevance to casing output and modest severity since sensors are replaceable; scored well within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: With proper tools most adjustments are damage-free, but overtightening or slips still occasionally break sensors or mounts. Careful sensor adjustment adds only minor time when done deliberately. Avoiding sensor or mount damage during adjustment relies on the technician's care and dexterity. A damaged sensor is a moderate part cost plus downtime, an avoidable but recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unresolved contamination or product-residue status blocks release of the caser from the sterile fill-finish facility
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contamination status blocking release from a sterile facility carries compliance severity, but it is a release-gate documentation error peripheral to the packing CFJ, keeping relevance low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: In sterile fill-finish environments, ambiguous residue status routinely blocks release until laborious documentation is assembled. Awaiting QA disposition on contamination and product-residue status is a notorious source of release delay. Release decisions hinge on quality-assurance judgement and regulatory interpretation that few disposal crews can provide. Prolonged holds tie up floor space and re-cleaning budgets while release is pending.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants spreading from the caser to surrounding facility areas during decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants spreading during decontamination has real facility-contamination severity, but it is a cleaning-process error largely outside the caser's core packing job, so relevance is low and importance capped.
rows.sat_rat: Containment practice exists but spread to adjacent areas still happens during large-equipment decon in shared facilities. Setting up containment and controlled decon zones adds meaningful setup time before work can begin. Effective containment of aerosolized or transferable contaminants requires trained industrial-hygiene and decon staff. Containment enclosures, HEPA units, and controlled waste handling are significant recurring cost drivers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of caser movement after it has been secured
rows.imp_rat: Caser movement after it has been secured is an installation-stability error of moderate relevance; consequences are typically re-securing or minor misalignment, so both proxies remain low within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Proven restraint methods hold equipment reliably once secured, with rare slippage on poorly matched anchor points. Securing and verifying the caser is stationary is a quick standard rigging step. Standard tie-down and chocking techniques are within normal rigger skill sets. Straps, chocks, and blocking are cheap relative to the damage prevented.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required carton-formation, filling, and sealing observations absent from the test-batch record
rows.imp_rat: Missing carton-formation, filling, and sealing observations in the test-batch record is a documentation-completeness ambiguity, peripheral to machine operation (low relevance) with correctable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Test-batch records are commonly incomplete because manual capture of formation, filling, and sealing observations is inconsistently enforced. Recording every required observation during test batches is tedious and often shortcut under time pressure. Knowing which observations are required and capturing them consistently depends on disciplined, experienced staff. Documentation labor is the main cost, which is minor but frequently under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of major installed components absent from baseline records
rows.imp_rat: Major installed components absent from baseline records is a primarily administrative/inventory error that does not stop the cartoner from running, so both relevance to the CFJ and severity of consequences are low.
rows.sat_rat: Major components are usually captured in as-built lists, though occasional omissions of substituted or field-added parts persist. Listing major installed components in baseline records is a bounded documentation task at commissioning. Recording major components is straightforward and needs limited specialized skill. The documentation effort is low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that throughput figures are compared under non-equivalent carton, product, and sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Comparing throughput under non-equivalent conditions is a benchmarking/assessment error (low relevance to operating the machine) whose consequence is a misleading spec rather than direct harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers quote under varied favorable conditions and there is no enforced normalization, so non-equivalent comparisons are a persistent hazard. Insisting on equivalent test conditions for throughput comparison slows the evaluation cadence. Detecting non-equivalent carton, product and sterile conditions behind quoted figures requires deep experience buyers often lack. Apples-to-oranges throughput comparisons lead to costly mis-selection that only surfaces later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that fit is concluded from incomplete or nonrepresentative product-and-carton evidence
rows.imp_rat: Concluding fit from incomplete or nonrepresentative evidence is an evaluation-process ambiguity, peripheral to running the cartoning job (low relevance) with modest, correctable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no strong standard forcing representative evidence, so fit is frequently concluded from limited or idealized samples. Gathering representative product-and-carton evidence takes time that acquisition schedules often compress. Recognizing when evidence is nonrepresentative and insisting on proper trials is a judgement-heavy task requiring experienced evaluators. Concluding fit from thin evidence is cheap up front but expensive when it proves wrong post-purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of case contents being damaged while the displaced case is repositioned
rows.imp_rat: Damaging case contents when a displaced case is repositioned causes localized product loss (moderate relevance and severity), an absorbable defect kept under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is little machine assistance for safe repositioning, so damage occurs sporadically depending on handling. Repositioning a displaced case carefully takes extra handling time during recovery. Avoiding content damage while repositioning relies on operator care and technique. Damaged contents during repositioning are recoverable-material losses that recur but are usually limited in scope.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that units held in the caser are assigned an incorrect batch or case-count status after the interruption
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect batch or case-count status after an interruption is a data-integrity error moderately relevant to traceability, with modest downstream consequences (severity) that are usually reconcilable. Held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Held-unit status after faults is often tracked manually with weak machine memory, so incorrect status assignment recurs and is poorly guarded against. Reconciling batch or case-count status after an interruption forces manual review that delays restart. Correctly reassigning status to held units demands careful judgement about where the run was interrupted, a knowledge-heavy task. Mislabeled batch or count status can spoil traceability and force holds or rework, a significant cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptable cases or packaged units damaged while extracting an unusable case
rows.imp_rat: Damaging acceptable cases while extracting a bad one creates waste (moderate relevance) and the loss is localized and absorbable (low-moderate severity), so importance stays within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: With mostly manual extraction and cramped access, collateral damage happens unpredictably, giving inconsistent results. Careful extraction to avoid collateral damage slows the removal, adding to stoppage duration. Extracting a bad case without harming neighbors demands manual dexterity and judgement, heavily operator-dependent. Collateral damage to good cases and units during extraction is direct waste and the primary loss this need targets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of packaging-line stoppage caused by removing an unusable case
rows.imp_rat: Line stoppage duration from removing an unusable case affects throughput (moderate relevance) and delays are recoverable rather than catastrophic (low-moderate severity), keeping importance capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Fault-detection and access design have improved clearing consistency, but removals still routinely stop the line for a spell. Removing an unusable case is the core delay this need addresses, and manual removal still causes noticeable line stoppage. Quick fault clearing depends on operator familiarity with access points, so speed varies with experience. The lost throughput dominates; the scrapped case itself is a minor material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of verifying a case against the wrong specified quantity for the current production run
rows.imp_rat: Verifying a case against the wrong specified quantity is a quality-check error of limited centrality to the core casing job (relevance) with correctable consequences (severity). Bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-linked count targets make the correct spec usually load automatically, though manual override or stale recipes leave occasional mismatch risk. Pulling the correct target quantity for verification is a quick recipe lookup that rarely delays the run. Ensuring the verification spec matches the current run is a procedural cross-check within reach of trained operators. Verifying against the wrong quantity would pass or reject cases wrongly, but errors are usually caught before large loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discharging a case before its required quantity has been verified
rows.imp_rat: Discharging a case before quantity verification is a process-sequencing error of moderate relevance; the consequence is a downstream quality miss that is typically recoverable, so both proxies stay low under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge interlocks tied to count confirmation are established practice, so premature discharge is uncommon where sensors function correctly. Interlocked discharge gates hold cases only momentarily for verification, imposing negligible delay. The verify-before-discharge interlock is automated, requiring little operator judgement once configured. Preventing premature discharge avoids shipping unverified cases and the associated rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of incoming product accumulating at the caser while it is stopped
rows.imp_rat: Incoming product accumulating at a stopped caser is a flow/backup concern (moderate relevance) that is usually cleared without lasting damage (moderate severity), kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard accumulation tables and back-pressure controls handle most stops reliably, with overflow only in prolonged outages. Accumulation while stopped is absorbed by conveyors and does not itself add delay until buffers fill. Managing infeed accumulation is largely automatic via accumulation conveyors, requiring little operator skill. Backed-up product risks damage, spoilage or diversion, the main resource loss this need concerns.

rows.source_job: Unload completed cases from caser outfeed
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of caser stoppages caused by completed cases accumulating at the outfeed
rows.imp_rat: Caser stoppages from outfeed accumulation reduce throughput (moderate relevance) but are recoverable interruptions (moderate severity), keeping importance within the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Outfeed accumulation conveyors and interlocks are mature and keep such stoppages relatively infrequent. Outfeed accumulation causing caser stoppages is a throughput-limiting delay when discharge backs up. Discharge buffering is largely automated, so preventing outfeed backups needs minimal operator intervention. The cost is chiefly lost run time; the accumulated cases themselves are usually undamaged.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mechanical joints left loose or incompletely secured after assembly
rows.imp_rat: Loose or incompletely secured joints after assembly can lead to later failures (moderate relevance and severity), but are an assembly-time defect usually caught in inspection, held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Torque specs, marking paint and sign-off checklists reduce loose joints, but a few are still occasionally missed on complex assemblies. Torquing and checking joints adds routine time that crews build into assembly. Proper joint securing is basic fitter skill, though it depends on consistent attention across many joints. Loose joints rarely waste material but can cause failures requiring rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of misalignment among the caser's frame, conveyor sections, and case-forming components after assembly
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment among frame, conveyor, and case-forming components after assembly can degrade casing (moderate relevance), but is corrected during commissioning (moderate severity), capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment procedures and reference datums are mature, so post-assembly misalignment is generally kept within acceptable bounds. Aligning frame, conveyors and forming components takes iterative adjustment time during assembly. Achieving alignment relies on experienced millwrights using levels, strings and dial indicators. Misalignment causes wear and jams downstream, but alignment labor itself is modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that existing facility elements obstruct the designated caser footprint when placement begins
rows.imp_rat: Facility elements obstructing the caser footprint is a one-time installation/placement error, only peripherally relevant to ongoing case forming, and its consequence is a delay that is resolved before operation begins. Low on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Site surveys and layout drawings catch most obstructions, but undocumented existing elements still cause recurring surprises. Confirming a clear footprint before placement is a quick pre-check when a survey was done. Comparing layout to actual site conditions needs basic spatial judgement, not deep expertise. Discovering obstructions at placement forces costly rework or relocation of utilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed format components left insufficiently secured before the production run
rows.imp_rat: Insufficiently secured format components before a run is a setup-prep lapse of moderate relevance that risks minor faults or rework (severity). Kept within the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quick-lock designs and pre-run checks help, but insufficiently secured format parts still occasionally slip through. Securing format components is a quick step folded into changeover. Locking format parts correctly is routine operator work needing modest attention. An unsecured part can loosen and damage the machine, but securing itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shipping cases produced with incorrect carton counts before stable packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton counts before stable packing after startup is a startup defect relevant to grouping; usually caught, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Startup miscounts recur until settings stabilize, a predictable but incompletely controlled loss. Producing and correcting wrong-count cases at startup costs time. Reducing startup miscounts depends on operator setup verification. Wrong-count startup cases waste product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of closed shipping cases with required markings obscured by closure material
rows.imp_rat: Markings obscured by closure material is a labeling defect relevant to case completion; usually catchable, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized closure positions keep markings visible in most cases, with occasional obscuring. Adjusting closure to avoid obscuring markings adds minor setup time. Placing closure clear of markings depends on machine setup. Obscured markings can force costly relabeling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of shipping cases rendered unusable during forming or loading
rows.imp_rat: Cases rendered unusable during forming is material waste, relevant to loading; consequences are cost/scrap, recoverable, so lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Case erectors form most cases well, but a recurring spoilage rate persists from setup drift and jams. Replacing unusable cases during forming adds minor time. Reducing spoilage during forming depends on machine setup and operator care. Cases spoiled during forming or loading are wasted blanks and material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of case blanks consumed before acceptable packing is achieved after startup or changeover
rows.imp_rat: Case blanks consumed before acceptable packing after startup is waste, relevant to loading; consequences are scrap cost, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Startup scrap is an accepted recurring cost that setup skill only partly limits. Startup trial runs consume time until packing stabilizes. Minimizing blank consumption at startup depends on operator setup expertise. Blanks consumed before acceptable packing are direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of staged cases, dividers, or labels with damage or visible contamination
rows.imp_rat: Staged cases/dividers/labels with damage or contamination is moderately relevant; consequences usually caught downstream, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual checks catch obvious damage, but subtle staged-material defects recur. Inspecting staged cases and labels adds minor time. Spotting damage or contamination depends on operator attention. Damaged staged materials waste consumables.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packaging material units with missing or illegible identity, lot, or status markings
rows.imp_rat: Illegible material identity markings undermines identity control, moderately relevant; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Most supplier markings are legible, but missing or unclear markings recur at a low but persistent rate. Checking markings legibility adds brief time. Reading identity/lot/status markings depends on operator attention. Illegible markings force discard of otherwise good materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional damaged or misloaded units caused by diagnostic adjustments
rows.imp_rat: Additional damage from diagnostic adjustments is a defect relevant to loading; limited-scope consequences, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Trial-and-error diagnosis routinely produces additional misloads with little safeguard. Diagnostic trial adjustments add cycles during troubleshooting. Avoiding collateral damage during diagnosis needs skilled restraint. Extra damaged units during diagnosis are added scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same stoppage or misload recurring after case packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Recurring stoppage/misload after resume is reliability relevant to loading; consequences repeated but recoverable, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without formal root-cause practice at the station, the same faults commonly recur after resume. A recurring stoppage repeatedly consumes clearance time. Preventing recurrence requires root-cause skill many operators lack. Repeat misloads keep generating scrap until truly resolved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged during grouping or case loading after changeover
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned units damaged during grouping after changeover is a defect relevant to loading; product loss but caught, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Post-changeover handling damage recurs during the unstable startup period. Damage during grouping/loading after changeover is discovered during runs, adding sorting time. Minimizing it depends on experienced setup and handling. Damaged cartoned units are lost finished goods, a costly waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of case-packer unavailability during format changeover
rows.imp_rat: Changeover unavailability is a throughput delay, peripherally relevant to the core loading job; consequences are recoverable, so lower severity within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: SMED practices and change-part kits give repeatable changeover on mature lines, but variability remains high. Changeover unavailability is the headline downtime metric operators are constantly pressed to shorten. Faster changeover leans on experienced operators; novices take substantially longer. Downtime during changeover is lost production capacity with direct cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton-grouping jams or case-loading faults after maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Jams/faults after maintenance is reliability relevant to loading throughput; consequences recoverable stoppages, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Return-to-service after maintenance frequently surfaces new faults, indicating inconsistent restoration. Post-maintenance jams and faults each cause stoppages during restart. Avoiding them depends on the maintainer restoring settings correctly. Faults damage cartons and cases, adding scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of case material consumed before acceptable case formation and loading
rows.imp_rat: Case material consumed before acceptable formation is waste relevant to loading; cost consequences, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Startup scrap is expected and only loosely minimized. Trial cases before good formation add setup time. Fewer wasted cases depend on skilled quick setup. Case material consumed during setup is direct startup waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of case blanks damaged during feeding or forming at the case packer
rows.imp_rat: Blanks damaged during feeding/forming is waste relevant to loading; recoverable scrap, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Magazine and feed mechanisms damage a recurring fraction of blanks despite tuning. Damaged blanks during feeding cause brief interruptions. Feed setup skill affects blank damage but variation limits control. Damaged blanks are direct case-material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons or shipping cases damaged by grouping and loading jams
rows.imp_rat: Cartons/cases damaged by jams is waste relevant to loading; scrap-level consequences, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Jam-related damage is a persistent byproduct of imperfect setups with limited preventive control. Clearing damaged cartons and cases from jams adds cleanup time. Reducing jam damage relies on skilled setup more than the cleanup itself. Damaged cartons and cases are direct, tangible material losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shipping cases broken down because the carton grouping pattern differs from specification
rows.imp_rat: Breaking down cases for wrong grouping is rework, relevant to correct grouping; consequences are recoverable rework, so lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pattern errors are usually caught before shipment, but breakdown volume stays significant when setup is imperfect. Breaking down mispatterned cases interrupts flow and adds rework time. Recognizing pattern deviation requires trained inspection. Each broken-down case wastes case material and labor, a direct and visible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipping cases rejected because their closures are incomplete or damaged
rows.imp_rat: Cases rejected for incomplete closures is waste relevant to loading; scrap/rework consequences, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Closure defects are usually caught at inspection but variation keeps rejection rates nonzero. Rejecting bad-closure cases adds handling time. Identifying closure defects needs modest trained attention. Rejected cases waste material and require repacking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of case blanks failing to form because of dimensional or material variation
rows.imp_rat: Blanks failing to form from variation is waste relevant to loading; scrap consequences, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Forming faults from dimensional/material variation are common and only partly buffered by vacuum/forming adjustments. Blank forming failures stop the machine briefly each time. Blank quality is largely a supplier/material issue beyond operator skill. Failed blanks are wasted case material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the identified fault recurs after the case packer restarts
rows.imp_rat: Identified fault recurring after restart is reliability relevant; recoverable stoppage consequences, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quick-fix culture means restarts often occur before the cause is confirmed, so recurrence is common. Fault recurrence after restart means repeated downtime cycles. Confirming a fault is truly fixed demands diagnostic expertise. Recurring faults waste product and maintenance effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged during fault correction or restart
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during fault correction/restart is a defect relevant to loading; contained product loss, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Restart damage is a recurring, poorly-buffered occurrence. Damage during correction/restart occurs in the unstable window before stability. Skilled restart reduces this damage. Units damaged during correction are direct product loss.

rows.source_job: Verify pack quality before run release
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of pack quality shifting after verification as the run stabilizes
rows.imp_rat: Pack quality shifting after verification is a stability defect relevant to loading; drift is usually monitored, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Single-point verification doesn't capture stabilization drift, a known blind spot. Post-verification quality drift shows up later, requiring re-checks. Anticipating stabilization drift requires experienced judgement. Late drift produces defective cases that are wasted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of guards or access points left unsecured when readiness is declared
rows.imp_rat: Unsecured guards at readiness is primarily a safety issue, peripheral to loading job function; consequences can be severe but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guard interlocks prevent startup with open access points on modern machines, making this fairly well controlled. Checking guards and access points at readiness is quick. Securing guards requires little skill, mainly discipline to complete the walk-around. Unsecured guards cost nothing directly but risk injury and stoppage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of shift in format-critical positions between readiness verification and operation start
rows.imp_rat: Shift in format-critical positions before start is a setup-instability issue, moderately relevant; often caught before run, so lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without position feedback, shifts between verification and start go largely undetected until faults appear. Rechecking positions between verification and start adds a brief but often-skipped step. Detecting subtle positional shift requires experienced perception of critical settings. Undetected shift causes early jams and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons or product packages damaged during case-packer intervention and restart
rows.imp_rat: Packages damaged during intervention/restart is a defect relevant to loading; contained product loss, lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Some intervention damage is unavoidable with manual clearing, so it recurs at a low steady rate. Intervention damage is found after the fact, adding rework time. Careful handling during clearing depends on operator technique. Cartons and packages crushed during clearing and restart are directly scrapped, the primary loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of active product, carton, case, and pack-pattern configurations outside current validation coverage
rows.imp_rat: Configurations outside validation coverage is a compliance gap, less central to physical loading; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validation matrices exist but proliferating configurations frequently drift outside current coverage. Validation coverage checks are periodic, not per-run. Mapping configurations to validation coverage requires expert tracking. Out-of-coverage configurations risk regulatory and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case-packing batch-record entries that are missing or inaccurate at lot review
rows.imp_rat: Missing/inaccurate batch-record entries is a records defect, less central to physical job; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record review catches many errors, but missing or inaccurate entries recur in manual records. Correcting record entries at review adds rework time. Accurate entries depend on operator documentation discipline. Missing entries drive review delay and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a disposition decision uses evidence belonging to a different packed lot
rows.imp_rat: Disposition using another lot's evidence is a serious traceability error but less central to physical loading; consequences notable, capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Lot-linked electronic records make using wrong-lot evidence uncommon though manual dossiers add residual risk. Evidence retrieval for the correct lot is a defined step. Correct lot linkage relies on disciplined record identification. Wrong-lot evidence risks costly misrelease.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packed lots presented for disposition with required evidence missing
rows.imp_rat: Packed lots presented with missing evidence is a compliance defect, less central to physical job; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Review checklists reduce missing evidence, but incomplete dossiers still reach disposition periodically. Assembling required evidence extends disposition preparation time. Completeness relies on reviewer thoroughness against checklists. Missing evidence causes hold time and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shipping cases with incomplete or conflicting genealogy and quality-status records
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete/conflicting genealogy records is a traceability defect, less central to physical job; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES/ERP records help, but conflicting or incomplete genealogy entries remain a recurring finding. Genealogy record checks add review time at closure. Reconciling conflicting records requires diligent quality review. Conflicting genealogy drives investigation and delay costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that deviation scope excludes affected cases, lots, or shifts
rows.imp_rat: Deviation scope excluding affected cases/lots risks under-scoped recall, less central to loading; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scoping practices exist but under-scoping affected cases, lots or shifts is a recurring reliability gap. Scoping deviations correctly can extend investigation time. Correct scope determination demands experienced investigator judgement. Excluding affected units risks expensive escapes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of loaded shipping cases with unverifiable carton-to-case identity associations
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable carton-to-case identity associations undermine traceability, moderately relevant; severity somewhat lower as it is a records-linkage gap capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization aggregation systems are mature where deployed, making unverifiable links uncommon in compliant lines. Verifying carton-to-case associations adds scanning and reconciliation time. Setting up aggregation and verification requires serialization-system competence. Unverifiable associations risk holds and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intermittent grouping and case-loading failures go undetected during equipment evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Intermittent failures undetected during evaluation is relevant to reliability; caught in operation, so lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent failures are notoriously hard to catch and the status quo often lets them go undetected. Detecting intermittent failures may require extended observation windows that lengthen evaluation. Catching intermittent grouping and loading faults demands vigilant, experienced observers. Intermittent faults escaping detection cause sporadic costly rejects downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intermittent grouping, loading, or data-flow failures remain undetected during validation
rows.imp_rat: Intermittent failures undetected during validation is relevant to reliability; caught later or during runs, so lower severity within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because typical validation runs are short and use nominal conditions, sporadic failures frequently go undetected, leaving this a chronic and poorly served weakness. Intermittent faults by nature appear unpredictably and often only over long observation, so short validation windows rarely expose them in time. Diagnosing intermittent grouping or data-flow failures demands deep troubleshooting expertise and is highly dependent on a few experienced engineers. Undetected intermittent faults reaching production can cause costly recalls, but the validation-stage cost is mostly investigation time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that rejected or reworked cartons retain obsolete shipping-case associations
rows.imp_rat: Rejected cartons retaining obsolete case associations risks wrong-lot traceability, less central to loading; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Rework and reject handling is a notorious weak spot where obsolete case associations often persist because deaggregation logic is incompletely automated, leaving this poorly served. Clearing associations on rejected cartons happens at reject time so latency is minor. Correct deaggregation on rework demands careful operator procedures and is a frequent source of human error. Retained obsolete associations can cause shipment holds or scrap, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of aggregation-state loss during packaging interruptions and restarts
rows.imp_rat: Aggregation-state loss during interruption is a serialization/traceability risk, less central to physical loading; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Persistence and checkpointing of aggregation state across restarts is inconsistently implemented, so interruptions frequently corrupt or lose state, making this poorly served. Recovery after an interruption can extend restart time, but the state loss itself is the primary damage. Correctly recovering aggregation state after a stoppage often needs experienced operators or engineers to reconcile partial cases manually. Lost aggregation state can scrap in-process cases, a meaningful material and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement in shipping-case status and association data across packaging systems
rows.imp_rat: Data disagreement across systems is a records-integrity issue, less central to physical loading; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point integrations without a single source of truth commonly leave case status and association data disagreeing between systems, a persistent and hard-to-eliminate problem. Synchronizing status across MES, serialization and warehouse systems can lag, but the disagreement rather than the delay is the felt loss. Resolving cross-system data disagreements requires integration architects who understand each system's data model, a scarce and demanding skill. Data reconciliation labor and shipment holds create cost, though not primarily material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of aggregation and deaggregation events lacking required audit evidence
rows.imp_rat: Aggregation/deaggregation lacking audit evidence is a compliance defect, less central to physical job; consequences notable but capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: GMP-driven serialization systems are designed to capture aggregation/deaggregation audit records, so evidence is usually present, though gaps appear in manual deaggregation and rework scenarios. Audit evidence is logged as events occur so timeliness is generally adequate. Configuring complete audit capture requires validation and IT expertise, but once set it runs with little ongoing skill. Missing audit evidence creates compliance cost and potential rework rather than material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of treating invalid or out-of-scope supplier certifications as qualifying for the proposed cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Treating invalid supplier certifications as qualifying is a verification error affecting selection quality, but downstream checks limit severity. Bounded by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier-qualification reviews usually validate certificates, but out-of-scope or expired certifications occasionally pass unchecked. Verifying certificate scope and validity is a modest-time step in supplier qualification. Judging whether a certification is in-scope and valid for the proposed machinery requires trained QA reviewers. Accepting an invalid certification is low-cost until it triggers costly compliance failure later.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machinery assessment relies on superseded regulations or standards
rows.imp_rat: Relying on superseded standards in the assessment risks compliance gaps but is caught in review with recoverable consequences; relevance moderate, severity low. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory affairs functions track standard revisions reasonably well, so use of superseded standards is uncommon but not impossible. Checking current regulation and standard versions is a quick step within a documented review. Knowing which regulations or standards have been superseded requires ongoing regulatory-intelligence expertise. Relying on superseded standards can force costly rework, but this is relatively infrequent.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate cartoning machinery configurations lacking documentation needed to verify compliance
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance-verification documentation on candidate configurations is a due-diligence gap, relevant to selection but low in immediate severity. Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely quote configurations with documentation gaps that only surface during later verification, so buyers frequently discover missing compliance evidence too late to correct cheaply. Assembling and cross-checking compliance documentation from multiple suppliers during acquisition is slow and stretches evaluation timelines considerably. Judging whether a configuration's documentation is complete enough to verify GMP compliance demands scarce regulatory and validation expertise that few procurement teams carry in-house. Chasing missing documentation adds administrative cost but rarely dominates the acquisition budget.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking concealed wear, damage, or incomplete refurbishment when assessing machinery condition
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking concealed wear or incomplete refurbishment risks later failures, a genuine reliability concern, but the ceiling forces low scores here.
rows.sat_rat: Concealed defects are inherently hard to surface and inspections are inconsistent, so this remains a chronic, poorly served risk. Thorough condition inspection of used or refurbished machinery is time-consuming and often compressed under deal pressure. Detecting concealed wear or incomplete refurbishment demands rare hands-on mechanical inspection expertise that most buyers lack. Undetected condition problems lead to major post-purchase repair and downtime costs.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which total acquisition cost is underestimated after including transport, installation, integration, qualification, parts, and downtime
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating total acquisition cost is a budgeting error with financial but not functional job consequences; relevance to running the machine is low. Held within cap.
rows.sat_rat: TCO practice exists but estimates are consistently optimistic, so cost surprises during the project are common. Building a full total-cost-of-ownership estimate takes time that acquisition schedules rarely allow fully. Estimating transport, install, integration, qualification, parts and downtime demands broad cost-engineering experience. Total acquisition cost is routinely underestimated because hidden qualification and downtime costs are hard to quantify and often excluded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of drilling dust and debris released beyond the controlled work area
rows.imp_rat: Drilling dust escaping the work area is an installation-phase contamination concern that is peripheral to routine cartoning operation; severity moderate but occurrence is one-off. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Dust escape beyond the work area is a common, hard-to-contain problem where standard shrouding only partly succeeds. Erecting dust containment and cleaning after drilling extends the setup timeline noticeably. Effective containment relies on installers' diligence rather than deep expertise, so competent crews can manage it. Vacuum drill shrouds and barrier materials add modest but real consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of installation debris introduced into the sterile fill-finish environment while setting up the cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Installation debris in a sterile fill-finish environment risks contamination (relevance to product safety), but this occurs only at setup and the ranking caps severity below higher-priority items.
rows.sat_rat: Despite protocols, contamination excursions from installation debris remain a recurring risk that is hard to fully eliminate in practice. Cleanroom-compatible installation staging and cleaning add significant time to setup in fill-finish environments. Keeping installation debris out of a sterile zone demands trained aseptic-conscious technicians who are scarce and expensive. Temporary containment, gowning, and post-install cleaning consume real material and labor resources.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning-line unavailability caused by calibration after installation, format changeover, or scheduled maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Line unavailability from post-install or changeover calibration is a downtime error somewhat peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance); severity moderate due to lost availability, but ranking caps it at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration outcomes are fairly consistent, but the duration remains stubbornly long and variable across format changes. Calibration downtime after installation or changeover is a well-known chronic drain on line availability with only partial mitigation. Fast calibration still leans heavily on experienced technicians whose availability constrains turnaround. Lost production hours during calibration downtime carry substantial opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preventive-maintenance tasks omitted from the initial schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required PM tasks from the initial schedule is a planning defect peripheral to immediate cartoning function (low relevance) that could contribute to later failures (moderate severity), but the ranking places it below the batch above.
rows.sat_rat: OEM manuals and templates guide PM schedules, but tasks are still routinely omitted from initial setups. Building the initial PM schedule is time-bounded but rushed schedules omit tasks. Complete PM schedules depend on maintenance planners' knowledge of OEM recommendations. Omitted PM tasks lead to failures whose remediation cost far exceeds the setup effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a maintenance due point is based on the wrong commissioning date or usage trigger
rows.imp_rat: Basing a maintenance-due point on the wrong commissioning date or usage trigger is a scheduling-data error that only indirectly affects cartoning output (low relevance); a mis-timed task can lead to premature or late service (moderate severity), still constrained under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS setup often carries over incorrect commissioning dates or triggers, making this a recurring reliability gap. Setting maintenance triggers is quick once the correct date or usage basis is known. Selecting the right trigger requires understanding of the asset's commissioning and usage that planners sometimes lack. Wrong triggers cause either premature or missed maintenance with associated cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of carton or insert stock being depleted before the scheduled run is complete
rows.imp_rat: Running out of carton or insert stock before run completion stops output (moderate relevance), but it is a plannable supply issue with low intrinsic consequence severity within this ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level magazine sensors and reload alerts are mature and widely deployed, yet timing gaps and human response lags still cause occasional mid-run stock-outs. Low-level sensors and lookahead prompts give operators time to reload before depletion, though busy lines still hit surprise run-outs. Anticipating stock depletion depends on operator vigilance and material-planning experience that varies widely between shifts. Unplanned run-outs cause line stoppages and scrap, but the sensing hardware to avoid them is cheap and standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prior-SKU cartons or inserts left in the machine after changeover
rows.imp_rat: Prior-SKU cartons or inserts left after changeover risks mispackaging, yet detection and clearing are routine and consequences limited; relevance moderate, severity low. Bounded by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance checks are the norm, yet reliance on manual inspection means stray blanks and inserts occasionally remain. Manually clearing prior-SKU cartons and inserts adds changeover time, especially in complex magazines and tracks. Knowing every hiding spot for stray cartons demands machine familiarity that not all operators possess. Leftover prior-SKU material causes mixed product and recalls, but the clearing itself consumes little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine unavailability caused by format-changeover complications
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from changeover complications reduces availability (moderate relevance), yet it is a planned-activity delay with recoverable impact (low severity), held low by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Tool-less change parts and recipe recall help newer machines, but changeover complications are still common and inconsistently resolved. Format changeovers remain a major source of downtime, with complications routinely extending planned changeover windows. Clean changeovers depend on skilled setters, and tribal knowledge gaps make complications frequent on less-experienced shifts. Extended changeover downtime is costly in lost throughput and sometimes scrapped setup cartons.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons damaged or rejected at equipment transfers because of mismatched timing
rows.imp_rat: Cartons damaged at transfers due to timing mismatch cause direct product loss (relevance to the packing job), and rejects carry cost, but the ranking caps consequences so severity stays modest.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-coordinated transfers greatly reduce timing-driven damage, but mismatches during speed transitions still cause some rejects. Transfer jams from timing mismatch cause short stoppages when they occur. Setting proper transfer timing at commissioning takes engineering skill more than operator skill. Cartons damaged at transfers are direct scrap, a recurring small loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machine desynchronization after connected equipment changes pace
rows.imp_rat: Desynchronization duration after speed changes impacts line flow (moderate relevance), but it is transient and recoverable (low severity), keeping importance under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern line control with electronic camming resynchronizes reliably, though legacy or poorly integrated lines still desync noticeably. When connected equipment changes pace, resynchronization is usually fast on integrated lines but can lag on loosely coupled ones. Line integration reduces the need for manual speed adjustment by operators. Brief desync causes minor throughput loss and occasional scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected cartoning pace conflicts with upstream or downstream capacity
rows.imp_rat: A cartoning pace conflicting with line capacity affects throughput coordination (moderate relevance), yet the mismatch is usually correctable with limited harm (low severity), and the ranking holds it low.
rows.sat_rat: Line-balancing and PLC coordination handle steady-state well, but pace conflicts still arise during changes or product changeovers. Mismatched pace shows up as brief starving or backups rather than a hard stop. Setting a compatible line speed requires understanding upstream/downstream capacity. Pace conflicts mainly cost throughput efficiency rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of adjusted settings shifting when drive assemblies are secured or returned to service
rows.imp_rat: Setting shifts when drives are secured cause minor quality drift that is re-adjustable; relevance to core running is modest and severity low. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Torque procedures and lock features reduce shift, but returning assemblies to service still occasionally moves adjusted settings. Re-checking settings after securing drives adds minor time to the task. Preventing setting shift on tightening relies on technique like counter-holding that experienced fitters apply. Shifted settings cause scrap and re-adjustment, a small recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of packaging components or batch materials remaining in the cartoning machine after line clearance
rows.imp_rat: Leftover packaging components or batch materials after line clearance is a real cross-contamination and mislabeling risk (severity moderate), and clean changeover is fairly central to cartoning; but the ranking caps this batch, so relevance and severity are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Formal line-clearance procedures with sign-off are highly mature in GMP environments and reliably catch residual materials. Line-clearance checks are a quick, routine step before machine removal in pharma packaging. Line clearance is a well-trained standard operator task requiring modest judgement. Leftover packaging components pose product mix-up and audit risk more than direct cost.

rows.source_job: Erase proprietary data from machine controller
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that proprietary recipes, credentials, or operational data remain recoverable after erasure
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable proprietary recipes or credentials after erasure is a data-security risk (moderate severity) but peripheral to the physical cartoning job; ceiling caps both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard, verifiable sanitization practice for legacy PLCs/HMIs, so recoverable data frequently remains after disposal. Thorough data sanitization of controllers is often skipped or rushed because it competes with removal schedules. Securely erasing proprietary recipes and credentials from industrial controllers requires OT-security skills rarely present on disposal crews. Failing to erase risks IP loss and breach liability, but the erasure effort itself is cheap.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lubricant, hydraulic oil, and adhesive residue remaining inside the cartoning machine after drainage
rows.imp_rat: Residual lubricant or adhesive after drainage is a maintenance-cleanliness concern moderately tied to product integrity, but the consequence is typically contained (low severity) within this ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard drain-down procedures remove most fluid, but trapped adhesive and dead-legs routinely leave residue that is hard to fully eliminate. Draining lubricants, hydraulic oil and adhesive takes established but non-trivial time, especially for cured adhesive lines. Thorough flushing and clearing of residual adhesive requires technicians familiar with the machine's fluid paths. Residual fluids left inside become disposal liabilities and can contaminate scrap value, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of captured production data being lost before production reporting
rows.imp_rat: Losing captured production data before reporting harms record accuracy (moderate severity) but does not stop cartoning; the ceiling keeps proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: PLC historians and MES buffering make data loss uncommon, though gaps occur with network or storage failures on less-integrated lines. Automated data capture and logging happen continuously without adding operator time. Data retention requires little operator skill once the collection system is configured. Lost data rarely wastes material, though it can force costly re-runs of reporting effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machine fails to reach the ready state during scheduled startup
rows.imp_rat: Failure to reach ready state at startup delays production (moderate relevance to uptime), but startups are retried and the consequence is delay rather than harm (low severity) under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Modern PLC startup interlocks and self-checks make successful startup common, but sensor faults and setup errors still cause intermittent no-ready conditions. Startup sequences with guided checks reduce time to ready, though failed startups can add significant diagnostic delay. Reaching ready state often requires an experienced operator or technician to interpret why the machine will not sequence up. Failed startups waste labor and delay the schedule but rarely consume material directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of materials from the previous run remaining in the cartoning area at startup
rows.imp_rat: Leftover materials from the prior run at startup is a real contamination concern but here it is a lower-ordered residual-check error with recoverable consequences. Kept within the 2.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures are standard in regulated packaging, but they are manual and human oversight still lets residual material slip through. Clearance checks add time at startup but are usually brief when a checklist is followed. Detecting leftover material relies on operator diligence and a trained eye rather than automated verification. Missed leftover materials can cause mispacks or contamination that waste product and require rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of jog movement being blocked by an unmet machine-state prerequisite during troubleshooting or setup
rows.imp_rat: Jog movement blocked by an unmet state prerequisite during troubleshooting/setup is a peripheral usability friction (low relevance to core cartoning throughput), and its consequences are minor delays easily worked around (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: HMIs increasingly display the unmet condition, making the blockage more consistently understandable than in the past. A blocked jog during troubleshooting wastes time until the prerequisite is identified. Knowing which machine-state condition blocks the jog requires familiarity with the control logic, a real skill gap for less-experienced staff. The delay costs labor time but little material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of packaging debris or leaked product remaining in the machine after shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Packaging debris or leaked product after shutdown is a cleanliness concern that is routine to address with limited consequence; relevance moderate, severity low. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning SOPs exist but are manually executed with no automated verification, so completeness varies run to run. Post-shutdown cleaning is time-consuming and often compresses under schedule pressure, leaving debris behind. Thorough cleaning of debris and leaked product depends on operator conscientiousness and knowing all accumulation points. Residual debris and leaked product can foul the next run and force rework, wasting material and cleaning supplies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a rejected carton becomes mixed with acceptable output after removal
rows.imp_rat: Mixing a rejected carton back into acceptable output undermines the quality-assurance role of cartoning (relevance modest), and consequences can reach the customer, but the ranking caps this below higher batch items so severity stays low.
rows.sat_rat: Locked reject bins and diverter confirmation are mature practices that make reject commingling uncommon. Segregating rejects is a quick automated diversion needing little added time. Once reject bins/conveyors are set, minimal operator skill is needed to keep separation intact. A commingled reject reaching customers risks costly recalls, but incidents are rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of glue, tape, adhesive, or carton blanks being depleted before replenishment during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Depletion of glue or blanks mid-run halts cartoning (moderate relevance), yet replenishment is a manageable interruption with limited lasting harm (low severity) under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level warnings and glue-level monitors are widely deployed and make surprise depletion fairly uncommon. Level sensors give advance warning so refills can be scheduled without unplanned delay. Monitoring consumable levels requires only basic attention with modern low-level alerts. Running out mid-run risks scrap and rework, but proactive refill mostly avoids that spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine stoppage caused by replenishing consumables during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Stoppage to replenish consumables reduces uptime (moderate relevance to the job), but such stops are routine and short (low severity), and this batch ranking holds it low.
rows.sat_rat: Many modern cartoners offer splice tables or low-level refill, but plenty still require a stop, so results vary by machine generation. Replenishing glue, tape or blanks often forces a brief stop unless the machine supports on-the-fly refill. Loading consumables is routine and needs little skill. The stoppage costs throughput but the consumables themselves are budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of carton blanks damaged or misoriented during replenishment
rows.imp_rat: Blanks damaged or misoriented during replenishment cause minor scrap and feed issues (moderate relevance), but the loss is small and correctable (low severity) under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Keyed magazines and orientation guides help, but manual replenishment remains error-prone and outcomes vary by operator. Careful manual loading into magazines slows replenishment, and a jam from a misfed blank costs additional stoppage time. Correct orientation and gentle handling of blanks depends heavily on operator technique, and untrained staff routinely nick or misload stock. Damaged or misoriented blanks become scrap and can trigger jams that waste further material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required lubrication points left untreated during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Untreated lubrication points during maintenance can eventually cause wear, a moderate reliability concern, but the batch ranking forces low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Documented lube schedules and checklists make most points get treated, but manual rounds still miss awkward or undocumented points occasionally. Lubrication rounds are scheduled tasks that take predictable time when a checklist is followed. Following a lube-point checklist is a low-skill task, though knowing all hidden points requires familiarity with the machine. A missed lubrication point risks component wear costs, but consumable cost itself is minor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine maintenance history is unavailable during a compliance review or troubleshooting activity
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance history during a compliance review is largely administrative, so its centrality to actually cartoning product is low (relevance); consequences can touch compliance standing but are typically recoverable (moderate severity), and the ranking holds it below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: GMP-driven CMMS and controlled logbooks make history reliably available in regulated cartoning environments, though paper-heavy sites still risk misfiling. Retrieving maintenance history during a review is fast when a CMMS or validated logbook is in place. Pulling history records is a low-skill lookup task in most systems. Unavailable history during audit risks findings, but the record systems themselves are already budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machinery unavailability caused by pneumatic servicing
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from pneumatic servicing reduces availability somewhat, a moderate relevance to throughput, but the batch ranking forces low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard pneumatic maintenance is well understood, so servicing rarely causes unexpected extended unavailability. Pneumatic servicing is generally quick with modular fittings, so unavailability from it is short and predictable. Replacing pneumatic components is a routine skill most maintenance techs hold. Downtime for pneumatic service has moderate cost, largely contained by planned scheduling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required control-data elements omitted from each backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting control-data elements from backups risks recovery gaps, but rarely halts cartoning production; relevance and severity are minor and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Automated backup routines capture most data, but incomplete backup scoping and untested restores still leave gaps discovered only during recovery. Backups run quickly once configured, so time is rarely the limiting factor for capturing control data. Ensuring every required control-data element is included in a backup requires knowing the full configuration scope, a specialist concern. A backup missing critical elements only costs when recovery is needed, so day-to-day cost impact is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intermittent stoppage does not recur while the cartoning machinery is being observed
rows.imp_rat: The error of an intermittent stoppage not recurring during observation is a diagnostic annoyance, only peripherally central to the cartoning job (low relevance); when it occurs it delays troubleshooting but causes no irreversible harm (modest severity), and the batch ceiling caps it regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent faults notoriously fail to reproduce under observation, and current logging rarely captures enough transient detail to confirm root cause. Waiting for an intermittent stoppage to recur while observing can waste large, unpredictable amounts of technician time. Catching an intermittent fault demands an expert's patient observation and interpretation, a scarce skill. Observation time and repeated interventions for elusive faults are costly with no guaranteed result.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning line idling while the fault source is diagnosed
rows.imp_rat: Line idling during fault diagnosis is a downtime-duration error somewhat tangential to the core packing outcome (low relevance); severity moderate given lost throughput, but the ranking limits it to 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Onboard diagnostics and alarm logs help but rarely pinpoint root cause reliably, leaving diagnosis inconsistent between events and technicians. Diagnosing the fault source on an idled cartoner still relies heavily on technician trial-and-error, so idle time varies widely and is often long. Fast diagnosis depends on scarce senior technicians who know the machine's failure patterns, a heavy skill dependency today. Every minute of line idling burns production capacity, and slow diagnosis directly inflates downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of machine surface, conveyor, and product contact zone area with residue remaining after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residue remaining after cleaning has contamination and quality implications (moderate severity) and cleaning is relevant to changeover, but the ceiling holds it to low scores.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning SOPs and visual/swab checks catch most residue, yet manual cleaning still leaves inconsistent residue in hard-to-reach zones. Cleaning contact zones follows defined SOPs with allotted time, though thorough residue removal can extend the window. Effective cleaning follows a procedure but consistent residue-free results depend on operator diligence. Cleaning consumes labor and cleaning agents but is a routine, budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine components damaged during daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during daily cleaning cause maintenance cost and minor downtime, a low-severity operational error peripheral to core output. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning SOPs and approved tools reduce damage, but manual daily cleaning still causes occasional harm. Careful cleaning to avoid damage adds modest time to daily work. Avoiding component damage during cleaning depends on operator care and product knowledge. Damaged components during cleaning generate recurring replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while cleaning machine surfaces and conveyors
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury while cleaning carries genuine severity yet is ancillary to the core packaging function and ranks below the prior batch. Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO, guarding, and cleaning SOPs are mature and widely enforced, making injuries during cleaning uncommon. Lockout and safe-cleaning steps add minor time to the daily routine. Injury avoidance depends on established safety training rather than specialist skill. Injuries incur significant cost but mature safety practice keeps them rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator contact with hot adhesive during daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact with hot adhesive during cleaning is a safety concern with real severity, but it is peripheral to the cartoning job itself and lower-ordered here. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard lockout, cool-down steps, gloves, and guarding make hot-adhesive contact uncommon in disciplined shops. Waiting for adhesive to cool before cleaning adds a small delay to the routine. Avoiding hot-glue contact relies on trained handling and PPE habits that are well established. Burn incidents carry injury and downtime cost but are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of adhesive deposits remaining on nozzles and applicator heads after daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Adhesive deposits left on nozzles after cleaning can cause carton-sealing defects (moderate severity) and sealing is core, yet the batch ranking caps both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual daily cleaning removes most deposits, but hardened adhesive in nozzles recurs and is inconsistently cleared between technicians. Daily adhesive-head cleaning is a short scheduled task within the shift routine. Cleaning nozzles and applicator heads properly requires knowing how to avoid damaging fine components, a moderate skill need. Residual adhesive causes clogging and defect costs, partly offset by preventive cleaning consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatibility among the installed machine controller, PLC, and HMI versions
rows.imp_rat: Controller/PLC/HMI version incompatibility is a configuration error tangential to the moment-to-moment cartoning job (low relevance); severity moderate since it can halt the machine, but held at 2.5 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: OEM compatibility matrices and change-control practices reduce mismatches, but ad hoc field updates still introduce incompatible version combinations. Verifying controller, PLC, and HMI version compatibility takes time during upgrades but is a bounded, planned activity. Confirming version compatibility requires controls-engineering expertise and OEM documentation not held by every site. Incompatibility that halts the line or forces rollback is expensive, and current version tracking only partly prevents it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machinery unavailability beyond the approved maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Cartoning unavailability beyond the approved maintenance window affects throughput and is somewhat relevant to keeping the line running (low-moderate relevance) with real production-loss consequences (moderate severity), yet it must stay at or below the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Even with planned windows, discovered defects and parts delays routinely cause unpredictable overruns. Overruns beyond the approved maintenance window are common when unexpected findings extend the job. Staying within the window depends on skilled techs estimating and executing accurately, which varies. Window overruns push into production time, incurring lost-output cost that current planning only partly controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that condition indicators misrepresent the machine's actual condition
rows.imp_rat: Misleading condition indicators can hide machine problems, moderately relevant to reliable operation, but the direct consequence is delayed maintenance rather than immediate failure, so severity is low within this capped batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor drift, poor calibration, and uncalibrated condition flags mean indicators fairly often misrepresent actual machine state. Chasing a misleading indicator wastes diagnostic time before the real fault is found. Distinguishing a false indicator from a true condition demands experienced troubleshooting judgement. Unnecessary part swaps prompted by bad indicators add avoidable material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time to identify a critical machine alarm among simultaneous notifications
rows.imp_rat: Time to identify a critical alarm among many is an alerting-latency error not central to cartoning production itself (low relevance); severity moderate because a missed critical alarm can escalate, but held at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm prioritization and shelving practices exist but poorly configured alarm systems still bury critical notifications among nuisance alarms. Modern HMIs prioritize and time-stamp alarms so operators can find the critical one faster than legacy stacks, though alarm floods still slow identification. Interpreting which simultaneous alarm is the true root cause still requires trained judgement rather than being obvious to any operator. Delayed identification of a critical alarm prolongs stoppage cost, only partly contained by current alarm management.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a scheduled maintenance window conflicting with sterile fill-finish production commitments
rows.imp_rat: A maintenance window conflicting with fill-finish commitments is a scheduling coordination error, not central to the machine's cartoning function (low relevance); severity is moderate since it can disrupt a production commitment, but capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established shutdown-scheduling practice and production coordination meetings reduce most conflicts, but unplanned faults and shifting batch priorities still create recurring clashes. Maintenance windows are scheduled around production calendars via CMMS and shutdown planning, which usually finds a slot but not always without contention. Coordinating maintenance with sterile fill-finish commitments demands experienced planners who understand both production and validation constraints. Scheduling conflicts that force overtime or lost sterile batches are costly, and current planning only partially mitigates them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required personnel, replacement parts, or machine access are unavailable during a scheduled maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Missing personnel, parts, or access during a scheduled window delays maintenance but consequences are schedule slippage rather than catastrophe; relevance and severity both low-moderate. Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS scheduling coordinates resources reasonably well, but conflicting production demands still cause window shortfalls. Missing people or access wastes the booked maintenance window and pushes work later. Coordinating skilled staff, parts, and access needs planning competence that varies by site. A wasted window means paid labor and lost production, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical spare part is unavailable when maintenance is required
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable critical spare during maintenance extends downtime but is a supply-logistics error with moderate, recoverable impact. Capped below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Min/max spare policies exist but under-stocking of low-frequency critical parts remains a frequent recovery blocker. A missing critical spare stalls the whole repair while it is sourced, sometimes for days. Deciding which parts are truly critical requires reliability-engineering judgement many sites lack. Holding critical spares ties up capital, so stocking is often trimmed too far.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a maintenance entry is attributed to the wrong warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: A maintenance entry attributed to the wrong caser is an administrative record error (low relevance) with limited operational consequence (severity). Below the cap.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS asset tagging and barcode scanning reduce errors, but manual entry on similar casers still causes occasional misattribution. Correctly identifying the asset for a log entry is quick when tags and CMMS are clear. Attribution relies on the technician selecting the right asset ID, an attention-dependent step. Misattributed records cost little immediately but distort maintenance history and planning.
