rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of damage to facility structures during caser movement and loading
rows.imp_rat: Damage to facility structures during caser movement is largely a logistics/handling error (low relevance to core casing) with repairable property consequences (severity). Below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard path protection reliably limits structural damage, though scrapes and floor gouges still occur in tight spaces. Protecting structures during movement adds only modest planning time. Riggers routinely plan paths and protect door frames, floors, and structures using established methods. Structural repairs are costly, so protective blocking and floor plates are worthwhile inexpensive precautions.

rows.source_job: Inspect completed cases for packing quality
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of inspection findings being attributed to the wrong case or production lot
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing inspection findings to the wrong case or lot is an administrative record-keeping error (low relevance) with modest traceability impact (severity). Below the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Date/lot coders and serialization are mature and widely deployed, so misattribution is uncommon but still occurs at changeover boundaries and when codes are misread. Manual lot reconciliation after an inspection finding costs meaningful time, but automated coding systems apply timestamps and lot codes without slowing the line. Correct traceability depends heavily on operators disciplined about changeover coding and record-keeping, which introduces judgement-based error. Mislabeling a finding to the wrong lot can trigger costly over-broad recalls or scrapping of good product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that machine evaluations overlook available floor space, utility, and line-interface constraints
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking floor space and utility constraints during evaluation is a selection-phase error not central to running the cartoning job (low relevance); severity is moderate since it forces costly reselection, but ranking holds it below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established practice includes floor-plan and utility reviews, but ad hoc execution means constraints are still occasionally missed. Site surveys and constraint checks take time but are a routine part of the evaluation schedule. Layout and utility interface assessment relies on plant engineers whose availability and attention vary, leaving some constraints unexamined. Overlooked constraints translate into costly late modifications, though the check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile fill-finish compatibility requirements omitted from machine evaluations
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile fill-finish compatibility requirements from machine evaluations is relevant to selection but peripheral to the cartoning machine's core running job; when it occurs, consequences are contained to evaluation rework. Capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-specific checklists help, but some compatibility requirements are still omitted from machine evaluations. Ensuring every sterile fill-finish compatibility requirement is in the evaluation adds time to protocol design. Identifying the full sterile compatibility requirement set requires specialized aseptic and cleanability knowledge. Omitted requirements surface as costly rework in a sterile environment where remediation is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of quoted line items based on incorrect sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.imp_rat: Quoting line items based on incorrect sterile fill-finish requirements is a pre-purchase procurement error peripheral to the machine's cartoning job (low relevance); its consequences are contained to rework of a quote rather than production harm, but capped at 2.5 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement miscommunication between buyer and supplier is common, so quoted line items frequently reflect the wrong sterile basis until caught in review. Correcting quotes built on wrong sterile requirements forces re-quotation cycles that lengthen acquisition. Specifying sterile fill-finish requirements correctly upfront depends on scarce specialist judgement that is often not fully transmitted to suppliers. Line items priced against incorrect requirements produce misleading budgets and later change-order costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate configurations documented without critical sterile fill-finish suitability information
rows.imp_rat: Candidate configurations lacking sterile fill-finish suitability info is a documentation completeness error, relevant to niche evaluations but low-severity and recoverable. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor documentation is often written for general packaging and omits critical sterile-suitability parameters, so this gap recurs consistently across candidates. Extracting sterile fill-finish suitability details from generic vendor datasheets requires repeated back-and-forth that delays candidate shortlisting. Recognizing which sterile-specific attributes are missing needs deep aseptic-process knowledge that general buyers seldom possess. Missing suitability information carries little upfront cost but drives expensive rework if a wrong machine is pursued.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatibilities between the candidate machinery and site space, utilities, and adjacent packaging stages
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibilities between candidate machinery and site space/utilities are a pre-install fit error, tangential to the core cartoning function (low relevance); severity moderate due to installation rework, but held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility checks are standard yet frequently incomplete for line-interface details, so incompatibilities still slip through with some regularity. Verifying compatibility with site space, utilities, and adjacent stages adds evaluation steps but is normally scheduled in. Detecting subtle incompatibilities with adjacent packaging stages requires integration know-how that is unevenly available. Incompatibilities found late trigger expensive retrofits, so the modest checking cost is well justified.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a mismatch between candidate machinery electrical demand and available site utility capacity
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking an electrical-demand vs site-capacity mismatch is a procurement/utility planning error peripheral to cartoning operation (low relevance); severity moderate since it delays commissioning, but constrained by the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Utility demand data is usually documented and checkable, making mismatches comparatively rare relative to other gaps. Comparing machine electrical demand against site capacity is a quick check when nameplate data is provided. Electrical load assessment is a standard facilities-engineering competency generally on hand. An overlooked utility shortfall forces costly infrastructure upgrades, so the cheap verification has high leverage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a footprint or service-clearance collision after candidate machinery selection
rows.imp_rat: Discovering a footprint or service-clearance collision after selection is a late-stage siting error with limited bearing on the cartoning job itself (low relevance); severity is moderate given rework cost, capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Where dimensioned drawings and layouts are used the risk is well controlled, though service-clearance surprises still occur when data is incomplete. Clearance and footprint verification is a defined pre-selection task that fits within normal timelines. CAD layout overlays and clearance checks are routine engineering skills widely available in acquisition teams. A late footprint collision is costly to remedy, making the modest upfront layout effort clearly worthwhile.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect a mismatch between candidate machinery throughput and existing packaging line product flow
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect a throughput mismatch versus line flow affects planning relevance but its consequences surface later as tunable bottlenecks rather than catastrophic failure. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line-balancing reviews catch many mismatches, but interactions with upstream/downstream flow are imperfectly modeled and sometimes missed. Detecting throughput-to-line-flow mismatch requires line-balancing analysis that extends evaluation time. Spotting a flow mismatch against existing line rates requires experienced line-balancing engineers. An undetected mismatch creates bottlenecks that waste capacity and money after installation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required carton, leaflet, product, and case configurations absent from the specified operating envelope
rows.imp_rat: Missing carton/leaflet/product/case configurations from the operating envelope can cause specification gaps, but this is a scoping error whose severity is moderate and recoverable. Ranking below prior batch caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration matrices in the URS usually capture most cases, yet occasional formats are left out of the specified envelope. Cataloguing all carton, leaflet, product and case configurations for the operating envelope is a detailed, time-consuming task. Defining a complete operating envelope needs packaging engineers who know the full configuration range. A configuration absent from the envelope means later change parts or re-spec costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of essential changeover, cleaning, inspection, maintenance, ergonomics, and safe-access conditions omitted from the machinery specification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting changeover/cleaning/access conditions from the spec is a documentation-completeness error, relevant to procurement but low in immediate severity. Bounded by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: These operability requirements are frequently under-specified because operations and maintenance voices are added late, so gaps recur. Specifying changeover, cleaning, inspection, maintenance and access conditions thoroughly adds significant spec-writing time. Anticipating all operational and ergonomic access needs requires experienced operations and maintenance input often underrepresented in buying teams. Omitted access or cleaning provisions cause costly retrofits and lost uptime later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required safety evidence absent from the warehouse caser acceptance package
rows.imp_rat: Missing safety evidence from the acceptance package is a documentation-completeness rework error, peripheral to the operational packing CFJ; consequences are delayed sign-off, so both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence packages are routinely incomplete because vendors deliver partial documentation, so absent safety evidence remains a recurring gap despite checklists. Assembling the complete safety evidence package is a document-collection effort that commonly delays acceptance. Knowing exactly which safety evidence is required takes a compliance specialist, and gaps appear when that knowledge is missing. Missing evidence can stall commissioning at high cost, while compiling it is mainly labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required validation results lacking traceable evidence of the tested condition and observed response
rows.imp_rat: Validation results lacking traceable evidence is an administrative/documentation rework error, peripheral to the caser physically packing cases; consequences are audit gaps, keeping relevance very low and importance capped.
rows.sat_rat: Paper-based checklists and inconsistent screenshot habits leave many validation results without linked evidence, a chronic recurring gap. Assembling traceable validation evidence during preparation adds significant documentation time that crews often defer or rush. Capturing tested-condition-and-response evidence properly demands trained commissioning personnel who understand what constitutes acceptable proof. Missing evidence forces costly re-testing or re-qualification later, but the direct material cost of documenting is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between cartoned units recovered and cartoned units recorded after stoppage clearance
rows.imp_rat: Recovered-vs-recorded unit mismatch after stoppage is a reconciliation issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual reconciliation after stoppages routinely produces mismatches, a persistent weak area. Reconciling recovered vs recorded units after stoppage costs time. Accurate recovery reconciliation demands careful operator counting. Mismatches distort yield and reconciliation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration packed shipping cases wait for closure at the tertiary packaging station
rows.imp_rat: Packed cases waiting for closure is a flow delay, peripheral; recoverable throughput consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: In-line sealers keep closure timely, so wait-for-closure is a modest recurring issue. Packed cases waiting for closure adds delay and station congestion. Managing closure flow depends on operator pacing. Closure waiting wastes throughput but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeated closure attempts on the same packed shipping case
rows.imp_rat: Repeated closure attempts is minor rework, peripheral to core grouping/loading; low consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Maintained sealers usually close in one pass, so repeated attempts are relatively occasional. Repeated closure attempts directly waste time at the sealer. Reducing repeat attempts depends on correct machine setup rather than heavy skill. Repeated attempts waste sealing material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the applicable case-pattern specification at the tertiary packaging station
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in case-pattern spec is a definition issue, indirectly relevant; consequences contained before run, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized work instructions reduce ambiguity, but unclear specs still occasionally cause inconsistent interpretation. Clarifying an ambiguous pattern spec costs time before packing. Interpreting ambiguous case-pattern specs demands operator judgment and experience. Ambiguity-driven errors waste materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing the active tally for a partially filled case after an interruption
rows.imp_rat: Losing tally for a partially filled case after interruption is a count-recovery issue, peripheral to core loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual partial-case tallies are easily lost after interruptions, a recurring failure with weak safeguards. Reconstructing a lost tally after interruption costs time. Preserving the tally through an interruption depends heavily on operator memory or notes. Lost tallies cause miscounts and waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of obstruction to safe Case Packer access caused by staged materials
rows.imp_rat: Obstruction to safe access from staged materials is a safety/housekeeping issue, peripheral to loading function; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: 5S practices keep access reasonably clear, though clutter recurs during busy periods. Clearing obstructing staged materials adds minor time. Keeping access clear depends on operator housekeeping habits. Obstruction has little material cost but raises safety and delay risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of shipping-case unavailability while completed carton groups await loading
rows.imp_rat: Case unavailability while groups await loading is a flow delay, peripheral to loading; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Case magazines usually keep supply flowing, so wait events are relatively infrequent. Waiting for cases while groups are ready wastes packing time. Anticipating case supply requires operator monitoring. Idle waiting wastes labor and throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of Case Packer surfaces retaining product residue or debris after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Surfaces retaining residue after cleaning is a cleanliness issue, peripheral to physical loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning procedures leave surfaces mostly clean, but residue on complex surfaces recurs. Verifying surface cleanliness adds inspection time. Judging residue removal on machine surfaces depends on operator inspection. Residual debris can force recleaning, wasting time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of corrugated fiber and dust accumulating at the station during case packing
rows.imp_rat: Fiber/dust accumulation is a housekeeping/contamination issue, peripheral to physical loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Corrugated fiber and dust continually accumulate and are only periodically controlled, remaining a chronic nuisance. Cleaning up fiber and dust adds recurring housekeeping time. Managing corrugated dust requires routine but low-skill cleaning. Dust accumulation is inherent to corrugated handling and a persistent cleanliness cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of operator-borne contamination transferring to station surfaces or packaged units
rows.imp_rat: Operator-borne contamination transfer is a contamination issue, peripheral to core loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Gowning and hygiene practices control most operator-borne contamination, with occasional lapses. Hygiene practices add minor time. Preventing operator-borne contamination depends on hygiene discipline. Contamination events are costly but infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cleaning-agent residue remaining on station surfaces when packing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-agent residue at resume is a contamination-housekeeping issue, peripheral to loading function; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning procedures usually remove residue, so residue-on-resume is relatively uncommon. Verifying residue removal adds brief time before resuming. Ensuring residue removal follows cleaning procedures rather than special skill. Residue rework wastes cleaning agent and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between documented cleanliness status and the station's physical condition
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between documented and physical cleanliness is a records-accuracy issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning logs and checks align mostly, but record-to-reality discrepancies still occur. Reconciling documented vs actual cleanliness adds verification time. Matching records to physical state requires operator diligence. Discrepancies can trigger costly re-cleaning or holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of packing interruptions caused by packed cases accumulating downstream
rows.imp_rat: Interruption from downstream accumulation is a flow delay, peripheral; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation conveyors buffer surges, so backup-driven pauses are moderately controlled. Downstream accumulation backs up and pauses packing, costing time. Managing accumulation requires operator coordination with downstream. Accumulation stoppages reduce throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of carton-packing interruption during a case-format transition
rows.imp_rat: Carton-packing interruption during format transition is a delay, peripheral to core loading; recoverable throughput consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Format changeovers are a predictable but lengthy recurring interruption with limited quick-change tooling. Case-format transitions cause notable packing interruption while changeover is performed. Fast format changes depend on operator changeover expertise. Downtime during transitions costs throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among active batch requirements available at the station
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among batch requirements at station is an information-ambiguity issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled documentation reduces inconsistency, but conflicting station information still recurs. Resolving inconsistent batch requirements costs time. Reconciling conflicting requirements requires operator or supervisor judgment. Acting on inconsistent requirements causes rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from a station stoppage or misload to identification of its initiating cause
rows.imp_rat: Time to identify stoppage cause is a diagnostics delay, peripheral to core loading; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fault codes point to symptoms but true initiating causes remain uncertain without expert interpretation. Time to identify a stoppage's root cause is often long due to manual diagnosis. Fast diagnosis depends heavily on scarce experienced troubleshooters. Prolonged diagnosis extends downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case-packer maintenance tasks left incomplete at release
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete maintenance tasks at release is a maintenance-compliance issue, peripheral to loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance checklists and CMMS sign-off give decent control but incomplete tasks still slip through. Verifying all maintenance tasks are complete adds a checkout step. Completeness depends on maintainer discipline and knowledge. Incomplete tasks cause later failures with cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation of critical case-packer alignments from approved limits after maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Alignment deviation after maintenance is a setup variability issue, indirectly relevant; usually verified, moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment restoration is often done by feel rather than measurement, so deviations recur. Re-verifying alignments after maintenance takes setup time. Restoring alignments within limits requires skilled measurement. Out-of-limit alignment causes jams and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serviced case-packer components or fasteners reassembled incorrectly
rows.imp_rat: Components reassembled incorrectly is a maintenance defect, peripheral to core loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without robust reassembly verification, incorrectly refitted components are a known intermittent problem. Reassembly errors surface at restart, causing rework time. Correct reassembly depends on maintainer skill and reference documentation. Misassembly causes damage and repeat work.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of case-packer downtime after the fault is identified
rows.imp_rat: Downtime after fault identification is a delay, peripheral to core loading; recoverable throughput consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Once identified, common faults have known fixes, giving reasonably predictable repair times. Downtime after fault identification is the repair duration operators want minimized. Repair speed depends on maintainer skill and parts availability. Extended downtime is lost production cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which restoration adjustments exceed approved case-packer setup limits
rows.imp_rat: Restoration adjustments exceeding setup limits is a variability issue, indirectly relevant; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustments beyond approved limits happen when done by feel, a recurring control weakness. Restoration overshoot forces additional readjustment time. Keeping adjustments within limits demands disciplined skilled work. Out-of-limit adjustments cause faults and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case-packer conditions omitted from safeguard verification
rows.imp_rat: Conditions omitted from safeguard verification is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Verification coverage is inconsistent and conditions are commonly omitted without formal test plans. Comprehensive safeguard verification takes time that is often abbreviated. Knowing every condition to verify requires specialized safety knowledge. Omitted checks risk undetected hazards rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of safeguard responses differing across equivalent case-packer states
rows.imp_rat: Safeguard responses differing across states is a consistency issue, peripheral to core loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Safeguard behavior variation across equivalent states is a subtle, under-addressed reliability gap. Inconsistent safeguard responses cause confusing extra stops. Understanding why responses differ needs controls expertise. Inconsistency mostly costs time, not material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false safeguard activations during representative carton and case flow
rows.imp_rat: False safeguard activations is a nuisance-reliability issue, peripheral to core job; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor drift and marginal setups cause recurring false activations that are hard to eliminate. False safeguard trips stop the line and waste recovery time. Diagnosing nuisance trips requires controls knowledge. Nuisance stops lose throughput but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in required safeguard behavior and pass criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in safeguard behavior/pass criteria is a definition issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vague safeguard criteria produce inconsistent verification outcomes. Ambiguity forces operators to spend time interpreting expected behavior. Unclear pass criteria push reliance onto expert judgement instead of clear standards. Ambiguity costs mainly time and misjudgement, not direct material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that safeguard verification alters established case-packer settings
rows.imp_rat: Safeguard verification altering settings is a rework/config-drift issue, peripheral to core loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Verification occasionally perturbs established settings with no systematic guard against it. If verification disturbs settings, re-setup adds time. Avoiding setting changes during safeguard tests needs careful skilled handling. Altered settings cause rework and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among the packaging schedule, grouping requirement, and case specification
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among schedule/grouping/spec is a planning ambiguity, indirectly relevant; consequences contained by pre-run resolution, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation is largely manual across separate documents, so conflicts recur and are caught inconsistently. Resolving schedule, grouping, and spec conflicts before running can delay startup while documents are reconciled. Spotting contradictions among documents requires experienced cross-checking. Unresolved conflicts cause wrong-configuration runs that waste material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of operator understaffing relative to packing workload during demand peaks
rows.imp_rat: Understaffing at demand peaks is a capacity issue, peripheral to core loading function; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Static staffing plans handle average load but consistently under-serve genuine peaks. Peaks can be short-lived, and reacting to them lags the demand spike. Meeting peaks requires enough qualified operators, and the qualified pool is the binding constraint. Understaffing during peaks loses throughput while overstaffing to hedge wastes labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required packing positions assigned to unqualified operators
rows.imp_rat: Positions assigned to unqualified operators is a training/compliance issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Skills matrices exist but under staffing pressure unqualified operators are still assigned, so adherence is imperfect. Verifying qualification before assignment adds little delay. This need is fundamentally about matching scarce qualified operators to positions, the core skill-supply constraint. Unqualified assignment causes defects and rework, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required packing positions remaining uncovered after unplanned absences
rows.imp_rat: Uncovered positions after absences is a staffing delay, peripheral to loading function; consequences are throughput, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cover depends on ad-hoc call-ins so coverage speed and quality are inconsistent. Covering an unplanned absence takes time to locate and deploy a qualified replacement, leaving positions open. Replacements must be qualified, and the qualified pool is often thin, constraining fast cover. Uncovered positions either idle the line or force overtime, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time staffing remains mismatched after a packing-workload change
rows.imp_rat: Staffing mismatch after workload change is a capacity-timing delay, peripheral; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustments happen eventually but the lag and its length are inconsistent shift to shift. Rebalancing staff after a workload change is slow because reassignment is manual and reactive. Effective reassignment requires supervisory judgement about who can move where. The mismatch period wastes labor or loses output until corrected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required packing positions remaining uncovered during breaks and shift handoffs
rows.imp_rat: Positions uncovered during breaks/handoffs is a staffing delay, peripheral to loading function; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Staggered-break and handover routines are standard practice and cover most gaps reliably. Break and handoff gaps are predictable and usually bridged by rotation schedules, so uncovered time is short. Rotation relies on cross-trained relief operators being available. Relief staffing carries modest labor cost that is normally planned for.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of mid-shift operator reassignments caused by initial staffing mismatches
rows.imp_rat: Mid-shift reassignments from staffing mismatch is rework/capacity, peripheral to loading job; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because initial plans are often rough, reactive reassignments recur predictably. Mid-shift reassignments disrupt flow and cost setup time each time they occur. They stem from imperfect initial skill/workload matching, a planning-judgement shortfall. Churn wastes labor and productivity with each move.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of case-packing changes implemented without a resolved validation impact
rows.imp_rat: Changes without resolved validation impact is administrative/procedural, peripheral to the physical loading job; consequences moderate and capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems catch most, but informal or minor changes sometimes bypass validation-impact resolution. Change-control review before implementation adds lead time. Assessing validation impact requires specialist judgement. Unvalidated changes risk costly nonconformance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that validation-impacting case-packing deviations remain open without substantiated disposition
rows.imp_rat: Deviations open without disposition is a delay/compliance issue, peripheral to physical loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tracking systems exist but aging open deviations remain a persistent problem. Open validation-impacting deviations frequently linger without timely disposition. Timely closure depends on scarce investigator and SME availability. Prolonged open deviations extend holds and carry inventory cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained cartoned-unit reconciliation discrepancies at lot closure
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained reconciliation discrepancies at lot closure is a records ambiguity, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Count reconciliation catches most, but small unexplained variances persist and require investigation. Reconciliation at lot closure is a defined time step. Explaining discrepancies requires investigative attention. Unexplained discrepancies force costly rework and holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting release-criteria interpretations for the same packed-lot evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting release-criteria interpretations is a procedural ambiguity, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Written criteria help, but ambiguous cases still produce divergent interpretations across reviewers. Resolving interpretation conflicts adds review delay. Consistent interpretation depends heavily on scarce senior QA judgement. Conflicting interpretations cause rework and inconsistent decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required case-packing records that are incomplete, missing, or illegible when reviewed
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete/illegible records at review is a documentation defect, peripheral to loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Good documentation practices are enforced, yet illegible or incomplete records remain a common review finding. Chasing incomplete records lengthens review time. Legibility and completeness depend on operator recording habits. Incomplete records force costly re-review and investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved case-count discrepancies across packaging and inventory records
rows.imp_rat: Case-count discrepancies across records is an inventory-reconciliation issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: System reconciliation resolves most case-count discrepancies, with residual mismatches between packaging and inventory records. Cross-record reconciliation adds closure time. Resolving count conflicts relies on record-management diligence. Discrepancies drive investigation labor and inventory write-offs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable market requirements omitted from the packaging configuration criteria
rows.imp_rat: Market requirements omitted from config criteria is a specification-completeness issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory intelligence exists but requirements are fragmented, so omissions in configuration criteria persist. Gathering all market requirements upfront lengthens criteria development. Knowing all applicable market/regulatory packaging requirements demands regulatory-affairs expertise. Missed market requirements cause rejected shipments and rework abroad.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required packaging configurations and distribution conditions excluded from compliance evidence
rows.imp_rat: Configurations excluded from compliance evidence is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance documentation practices exist but coverage gaps for distribution conditions recur. Assembling compliance evidence across configurations is time-consuming. Determining which configurations and conditions need evidence needs compliance expertise. Excluded configurations cause audit findings and remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria lacking objective acceptance thresholds
rows.imp_rat: Criteria lacking objective thresholds is a definition ambiguity, indirectly relevant; consequences moderate and capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many criteria still lack objective acceptance thresholds, so pass/fail judgement varies. Defining objective thresholds for each criterion adds specification time. Converting subjective criteria into measurable thresholds needs metrology and standards expertise. Vague thresholds cause disputes and rework that objective limits would avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between standardized criteria and expected carton, case-material, and pack-pattern variation
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch of standardized criteria to variation is a definition/standards gap, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardization efforts exist but often lag real-world variation, leaving recurring criteria mismatches. Aligning standardized criteria to expected variation takes upfront analysis time. Anticipating material and pattern variation requires broad packaging experience. Mismatched criteria cause false rejects or missed defects, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of expected carton, case, and line conditions omitted from validation trials
rows.imp_rat: Conditions omitted from validation trials is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; consequences contained, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols exist but rarely span all carton/case/line conditions, so gaps remain common. Broadening validation trials to cover more conditions extends validation timelines. Selecting the full range of conditions to trial requires strong DOE and packaging expertise. Omitted conditions produce field failures costlier than the added trials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for the proposed case-loading configuration
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for loading config is a definition issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Templates help but acceptance criteria for new configurations still carry interpretive ambiguity. Removing ambiguity in acceptance criteria takes review and negotiation time. Writing unambiguous acceptance criteria requires disciplined technical-writing skill. Ambiguous criteria cause rework and disputes with real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that upstream or downstream line disturbances are misattributed to the case packer during risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing line disturbances to the case packer is an assessment-accuracy issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause practices exist but cross-unit misattribution remains common and poorly controlled. Disentangling line disturbances during risk assessment can add investigation time. Correctly attributing disturbances to upstream/downstream versus the case packer takes strong diagnostic skill. Misattribution wastes engineering effort chasing the wrong unit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that planned samples and repeated runs are insufficient to substantiate reproducible case packing
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient samples/runs to substantiate reproducibility is a validation-rigor issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans are standardized but are still sometimes too thin to substantiate reproducibility. Sizing sample plans and repeated runs is routine and does not drive delay. Determining statistically sufficient sampling requires statistical competence not always resident on the floor. Insufficient sampling leads to reruns and re-qualification costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reproducible case packing remains unsubstantiated because repeated qualification evidence is insufficient
rows.imp_rat: Reproducibility unsubstantiated from insufficient evidence is a validation-rigor issue, peripheral; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Replicate-run practice exists but reproducibility often remains under-substantiated, a recurring gap. Repeating qualification to substantiate reproducibility extends timelines when evidence is thin. Judging whether repeated evidence is sufficient requires validation expertise. Insufficient repeated evidence forces costly additional runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification source evidence is unavailable during internal review, supplier evaluation, or regulatory inspection
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence unavailable at inspection is a records-availability issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document management systems make source evidence generally available, so this need is reasonably well served. Retrieving qualification evidence during inspection can be slow when records are dispersed. Locating and interpreting source evidence needs documentation familiarity more than deep expertise. Missing evidence during inspection risks findings whose remediation is costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that periodic review fails to identify adverse trends, recurring deviations, or changes affecting the case-packer qualified state
rows.imp_rat: Periodic review missing adverse trends is an oversight process issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic review and trending programs are established, though they inconsistently catch slow adverse trends. Periodic review is a scheduled activity that does not itself impose urgent time pressure. Spotting adverse trends and subtle changes in qualified state requires analytical review skill. Missed trends lead to costly drift but review itself is relatively inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case-packer claims are accepted under conditions unrepresentative of sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Claims accepted under unrepresentative conditions is a qualification-rigor issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Claims are often accepted on convenient rather than representative conditions, so confidence in real-world performance is uncertain and inconsistently governed. Setting up representative sterile fill-finish conditions for testing takes time to arrange. Judging whether test conditions truly represent sterile production requires deep process and regulatory expertise that few possess, making this heavily skill-bound. Representative testing consumes sterile-grade materials and constrained resources at notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required carton formats, line states, and production scenarios omitted from validation
rows.imp_rat: Formats/scenarios omitted from validation is a coverage gap, indirectly relevant; consequences contained, capped within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage gaps mean untested conditions may fail in production, but the root issue is the difficulty of scoping complete test matrices. Building broad validation coverage across formats and scenarios lengthens the validation program considerably. Deciding which carton formats, line states and scenarios must be tested is a judgement-heavy task depending on experienced validation engineers, and omissions stem directly from that scarce expertise. Extra scenarios cost test materials and labor, a budgeted but real expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the rate of cartoned-unit grouping and case loading below the claimed output under representative product mix
rows.imp_rat: Grouping/loading below claimed output is a throughput-shortfall reliability issue, moderately relevant; recoverable consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Performance qualification runs test rate reasonably well for nominal products, but representative mixed-product output is often under-tested so shortfalls appear later, leaving a moderate gap. Throughput shortfalls under representative product mix are the essence of this need, but the loss is missed rated output rather than pure delay. Assessing achievable rate across product mix requires performance-qualification expertise to design and interpret runs. Underperformance against claimed output erodes capacity value, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between shipping-case completion and record availability to upstream line control
rows.imp_rat: Delay in record availability to line control is a data-timing issue, peripheral to physical loading; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Message queues occasionally back up or drop, but data does become available upstream consistently under normal operation. The core need is latency between case completion and record availability upstream, which real-time or near-real-time messaging in current systems handles reasonably but not instantly. Once interfaces are configured, minimal operator skill is needed to maintain the data push. Latency imposes little direct material or money cost beyond occasional queuing delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aggregation events recorded outside their actual packaging order
rows.imp_rat: Aggregation events recorded out of order is a data-sequencing issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Buffering, asynchronous messaging and timestamp drift regularly record events out of their true packaging order, and this is not robustly guarded against in typical deployments. Event ordering is captured in real time so delay is not the issue. Ensuring correct event sequencing under buffering requires careful controls configuration that is error-prone. Out-of-order events rarely cause direct material loss but can invalidate audit trails.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification-critical line-control or data-flow risks remain undiscovered until recommendation review
rows.imp_rat: Qualification risks undiscovered until review is a process-timing issue, peripheral to physical job; moderate consequences within ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether unit-to-case traceability is complete at compliance sign-off
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about traceability completeness at sign-off is an ambiguity, indirectly relevant; consequences contained, capped within ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of underestimation in installation, commissioning, and validation costs for each candidate machine
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating installation/commissioning/validation costs is a procurement-budgeting error remote from cartoning operation (low relevance) with financial overrun impact (low-moderate severity), held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Estimates rely on optimistic vendor inputs and thin historical data, so underestimation recurs predictably. Building realistic install, commissioning, and validation cost estimates requires data-gathering that stretches the evaluation. Accurate estimation of commissioning and validation effort demands experienced project engineers who are frequently unavailable during acquisition. These downstream costs are chronically underestimated, causing budget overruns after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unplanned machinery downtime cost omitted from each lifecycle cost estimate
rows.imp_rat: Downtime cost omitted from lifecycle estimates is an estimation error affecting financial planning, not core cartoning function; consequences are financial and limited. Capped below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Because downtime cost is inconsistently included, lifecycle estimates give an unreliable basis for machine selection. Downtime cost modeling is rarely done up front and is skipped when acquisition timelines are tight. Quantifying unplanned downtime cost demands reliability-engineering judgement seldom applied at acquisition. Lifecycle estimates routinely omit downtime cost, badly understating true cost of ownership for cartoners in sterile lines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance, performance, or delivery provisions open to conflicting interpretation
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous acceptance/performance provisions can create contract disputes, but relative to running the cartoning job this is a peripheral contractual error (low relevance) with disputes usually resolvable (moderate-low severity), capped low by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Provisions are frequently left open to interpretation, and ambiguity commonly survives into signed contracts. Tightening acceptance, performance, and delivery provisions requires negotiation cycles that add time. Drafting unambiguous provisions takes experienced contracts and validation expertise that is scarce. Conflicting interpretations later drive dispute and rework costs.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compliance assessment is completed after a purchase commitment for cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Late compliance assessment relative to purchase is an administrative/procurement timing error, largely peripheral to running cartoning (low relevance) with limited operational consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature capital-approval workflows in regulated firms reliably place compliance review before commitment, so late assessment is fairly rare. Stage-gate procurement processes normally schedule compliance assessment before purchase commitment, so timing is usually right. Sequencing the assessment ahead of commitment is a process-discipline matter more than an expertise gap. Assessing after commitment risks locked-in noncompliance costs, but gated processes largely prevent this.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among engineering, compliance, and suppliers about which requirements apply to the cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among engineering, compliance, and suppliers about applicable requirements is an administrative alignment issue peripheral to the machine actually forming and sealing cartons (low relevance), and while it can cause rework its consequences are recoverable (moderate-low severity). Capped at 2.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Without a single authoritative requirements baseline, disagreement over applicable requirements recurs frequently and is only partly mitigated by review meetings. Reconciling differing requirement interpretations across engineering, compliance, and suppliers drags out the specification phase. Aligning cross-functional views requires senior facilitation and regulatory judgement that is in short supply. Requirement disputes cause rework and scope changes that inflate acquisition cost.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transit damage remaining undetected during the delivery inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage at inspection is a delivery-QA defect peripheral to running operation, with consequences that surface later but are recoverable, capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Shock indicators and visual checks catch obvious damage but internal or latent damage frequently escapes the delivery inspection. Delivery inspection windows are short, pressuring inspectors to sign off before hidden damage is found. Detecting concealed transit damage on precision cartoners requires experienced eyes that are not always present at receiving. Undetected damage becomes a costly dispute later when the claim window has closed.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of missing or incorrect machine components left unidentified after comparison with the accepted order
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incorrect components left unidentified after order comparison (relevance ~2) is a receiving-inspection error peripheral to running production; severity (~2) since it is caught and remedied via reconciliation rather than causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list checks catch most discrepancies, though some missing or wrong components still go unnoticed at receipt. Component reconciliation against the order at delivery is a defined receiving step done reasonably promptly. Matching delivered components to the order needs a knowledgeable receiver but is largely checklist-driven. Unidentified missing components cause commissioning delay costs if caught late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical running, adjustment, and troubleshooting actions operators perform incorrectly during competency assessment
rows.imp_rat: Operators performing critical actions incorrectly during a competency assessment is a training-evaluation defect, not live production, so its relevance to the running job is indirect (low relevance) and consequences are contained to the assessment (low severity). Capped at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Operators still perform critical adjustment and troubleshooting actions incorrectly during assessment at meaningful rates. Competency assessments take dedicated time on and off the line. This need is fundamentally about building operator skill, and current on-the-job training only partially guarantees correct critical actions. Assessment and retraining consume trainer and operator hours.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled hands-on cartoning-machine training displaced by production demands
rows.imp_rat: Hands-on training displaced by production demands is a scheduling/support error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance), reducing operator competence over time (low-moderate severity), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because production consistently wins over training slots, this displacement is a persistent, poorly mitigated problem. Scheduled hands-on training is chronically displaced by production demands, directly cutting training duration. When training time is squeezed, operators never build the intended competency, so the skill deficit persists. Deferred training trades short-term production capture for later error and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts in instructions for running, adjusting, and troubleshooting the applicable cartoning-machine configuration
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts in running/adjusting/troubleshooting instructions is a documentation error peripheral to the cartoning function (low relevance) with mild, resolvable consequences (low severity), under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation for specific machine configurations is frequently inconsistent, and reconciling it is a recurring, poorly solved pain. Reconciling conflicting instructions consumes troubleshooting time when operators must decide which document to trust. Detecting and resolving instruction conflicts requires experienced judgement that not all operators have. Conflicting documentation causes rework and misadjustment with modest resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of priority cartoning-machine fault scenarios omitted from hands-on troubleshooting practice
rows.imp_rat: Omitting priority fault scenarios from troubleshooting practice weakens operator readiness, a training-support error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with moderate downstream downtime impact but capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Priority fault scenarios are frequently omitted from hands-on practice, leaving operators unprepared for real faults. Setting up hands-on fault scenarios takes scarce line time to arrange. Practicing priority fault scenarios directly builds troubleshooting skill, but comprehensive scenario coverage is rarely achieved. Simulating faults may require sacrificing product or downtime, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical machine settings accessible to personnel without authorization
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized access to critical settings is a security/governance concern that is peripheral to the mechanical cartoning job (low relevance) with contained consequences here (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access levels are a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably keeps critical parameters behind authorization, though misconfiguration still occasionally exposes settings. Setting up password-protected access levels adds modest configuration time during preparation but is a routine part of commissioning. Modern PLC/HMI platforms ship with built-in multi-tier access control, so little specialized skill is needed to lock critical settings behind authorization. Access-control features are bundled into standard control packages at no meaningful added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning a user to a role that conflicts with the user's approved responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Assigning a user to a conflicting role is an access-governance issue with compliance consequences but is peripheral to the physical cartoning job; both proxies stay low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Access-management workflows and approval gates reduce mismatched role assignments, though manual assignment still lets occasional conflicts through. Role assignment through user-management systems is quick once configured. Assigning correct roles requires an administrator who understands both responsibilities and system permissions. Role management is a low-cost administrative task within existing systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in role permissions across the combination packer, case packers, and wrap-arounds
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent role permissions across packing equipment is a governance detail with limited bearing on producing cartons; relevance and severity both minor.
rows.sat_rat: Different machines and vendors use different permission models, so inconsistency across the line is a common, weakly addressed issue. Harmonizing permissions across multiple machine types adds configuration time during preparation. Aligning role permissions across combination packers, case packers, and wrap-arounds requires cross-platform expertise that is scarce. Reconciling permission schemes is mostly labor with little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine control accounts assigned to multiple personnel
rows.imp_rat: Shared machine control accounts weaken traceability and access control, an administrative/security matter peripheral to the cartoning job; low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Shared machine-control accounts remain common on the plant floor despite policy, so this need is only partly served in practice. Provisioning individual accounts takes slightly longer than sharing one during setup. Creating individual accounts needs only basic administrative skill. Individual named accounts add negligible cost.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved standards or representative format materials are unavailable when calibration begins
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable standards or format materials at calibration start is a preparatory logistics gap, peripheral to running the cartoner (low relevance) with easily-absorbed delay consequences (low severity); it sits firmly under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-staging checklists help but availability gaps for approved standards still occur with some regularity. Chasing missing standards or format materials at calibration start can stall the process. Ensuring materials are staged is a planning task requiring coordination rather than technical skill. Missing reference standards force idle time and expedited procurement costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for accepted commissioning documentation needed to establish baseline records
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for accepted commissioning documentation to establish baselines is a one-time onboarding delay that does not affect ongoing cartoning (low relevance) and is recoverable (low severity). Held within 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document approval workflows are consistent but slow, so the waiting duration remains a chronic issue. Waiting for accepted commissioning documentation is a well-known bottleneck that delays baseline record creation. Approval bottlenecks stem from process, not from any skill shortage in the preparers. Delayed baselines can hold up qualification and production, carrying schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed components lacking identification or revision details
rows.imp_rat: Missing component identification or revision details hampers traceability but the machine can still run (low relevance), and the impact is a slower troubleshoot rather than harm (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset tagging practices are established, yet components lacking full identification or revision detail still slip through commonly. Recording component IDs and revisions during install adds incremental time. Capturing identification details needs diligence more than expertise. Missing IDs cause costly delays later when sourcing spares, but capture itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the recorded component locations and interconnections
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in recorded component locations and interconnections slows service documentation but is peripheral to producing cartons (low relevance) with limited fallout (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation is frequently ambiguous or outdated, leaving interconnection records a persistent weak spot. Documenting locations and interconnections clearly takes deliberate effort during handover. Producing unambiguous location records requires experienced documenters familiar with the layout. Ambiguous records cost troubleshooting labor later rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting configuration entries across the commissioning report and asset register
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting entries across commissioning report and asset register are documentation inconsistencies with little bearing on cartoning output; low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Manual double-entry between commissioning docs and asset registers frequently produces conflicting entries that go undetected. Cross-checking commissioning reports against the asset register is a slow manual reconciliation. Spotting configuration discrepancies demands attentive, knowledgeable reviewers. Manual reconciliation costs labor and downstream rework when errors propagate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of packaged product accumulated between the cartoning machine and downstream equipment
rows.imp_rat: Product accumulation between machines is a buffering/flow concern that is peripheral to the core cartoning function (low relevance) with easily absorbed consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation tables and buffer conveyors are standard and reliably absorb short mismatches on most lines. Accumulation is a buffering mechanism, not a delay, so it rarely costs time directly. Managing accumulation buffers needs modest operator attention. Excess accumulated product ties up work-in-process and risks handling damage, a modest cost concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of downtime events omitted from the production record during a cartoning run
rows.imp_rat: Downtime events omitted from the production record are a reporting-completeness issue with limited operational consequence; both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Where logging is manual, micro-stops and short stops are systematically missed, and automated capture is not yet universal. Manual downtime logging competes with running the line, so short events go unrecorded under time pressure. Capturing every event depends on operator diligence rather than automation, making omissions routine. Omitted downtime events distort OEE and hide costly losses but spend nothing directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a machine-state change and its entry in the production record
rows.imp_rat: Latency between a machine-state change and its record entry affects data timeliness only, a minor administrative concern; low relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Directly interfaced machines timestamp changes reliably, but many lines still rely on delayed manual entry, so latency varies widely. Automated state logging is near-instant, but manual entry practices introduce lag between the event and its record. Prompt recording depends on operator habit rather than skill where entry is manual. Recording latency itself wastes no material, only degrading the timeliness of reporting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interruption events assigned an unclear or incorrect cause category
rows.imp_rat: Miscategorizing interruption causes degrades analytics quality only; it is peripheral to running the machine and low-severity, so both proxies are small.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, dropdown-based cause coding is the norm and produces widely unreliable, vague, or wrong categorizations. Assigning causes is often deferred and done after the fact, adding little run-time delay but slowing analysis. Correct cause categorization depends heavily on operator experience and honesty, making it inconsistent and error-prone. Miscategorized downtime causes misdirected improvement spend but consumes little in the moment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cartons selected for periodic inspection differ from discharged output across the production run
rows.imp_rat: Variability between inspected samples and discharged output weakens sampling validity, but it is a peripheral statistical concern to the core packing job (low relevance) with limited direct consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans exist but manual periodic pulls often fail to represent the full run's variation, leaving a real gap. Pulling representative periodic samples takes little time but manual selection can be inconsistent. Choosing a truly representative sample across the run depends on inspector discipline and understanding of sampling. Non-representative sampling risks quality escapes rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that cartoning-machine operating-status indications are unavailable or stale during an active production run
rows.imp_rat: Stale or unavailable status indications is a monitoring-freshness error tangential to physically forming cartons (low relevance) with modest, recoverable consequences (low severity), under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Modern HMIs and PLCs deliver reliable status most of the time, though comms hiccups and slow polling occasionally leave indications stale. HMI status refreshes are generally near-real-time, but network or sensor lags cause brief stale-indication windows. Interpreting whether an indication is stale takes some operator awareness but little specialized skill. Stale status rarely wastes material directly, its cost is mainly indirect through delayed reactions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among alarm states, output-quality observations, and throughput indications during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among alarm, quality, and throughput indications is a data-consistency error marginal to actual carton production (low relevance) with low, diagnosable consequences (low severity), within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Data from separate subsystems is poorly integrated on most machines, so conflicting indications are common and largely unmanaged. Reconciling disagreeing alarm, quality, and throughput signals costs operators time during troubleshooting. Resolving contradictory indications demands significant diagnostic expertise that is scarce on the floor. Acting on inconsistent data can cause bad-batch decisions that waste product, but the disagreement itself spends little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false or nuisance alerts obscuring actionable cartoning-machine conditions during a production run
rows.imp_rat: False or nuisance alerts obscuring real conditions is an operator-attention error peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance) with low, non-catastrophic consequences (low severity), well within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm flooding and poorly tuned thresholds are a chronic, largely unaddressed problem that obscures real conditions. Chasing nuisance alerts wastes operator time and can slow genuine fault response amid the noise. Distinguishing false alarms from actionable ones requires experienced judgement that varies by operator. Nuisance alerts drive unnecessary stops and lost throughput, though they rarely consume material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation from the specified lubricant quantity for each moving component
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified lubricant quantity affects component longevity somewhat, but consequences are gradual and non-catastrophic; both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual grease-gun application means quantity deviation is common and inconsistent, with automated lubrication systems still uncommon on cartoners. Applying the correct lubricant quantity adds little time when done by a diligent technician. Judging the specified quantity per component relies on technician feel and experience rather than precise metering. Over- or under-lubrication wastes lubricant or causes wear, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of periodic inspections postponed because the cartoning machinery remains in production
rows.imp_rat: Inspections postponed because the machine stays in production is a maintenance-scheduling delay tangential to the cartoning function (low relevance) that risks latent faults (moderate severity), capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Production-priority conflicts routinely override PM schedules, and while planned shutdown windows exist, they are often too short so postponement remains common. Inspections deferred because the line is running accumulate and compress into risky catch-up windows. Deciding what can safely wait requires planner judgement about condition and risk. Postponement avoids short-term production loss but risks costlier unplanned failures later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection points assessed without current wear limits or baseline condition records
rows.imp_rat: Inspection points assessed without current wear limits is a maintenance-documentation gap tangential to the core function (low relevance) that can let degradation go unnoticed (moderate severity), capped by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: OEM manuals provide some limits, but real-world baseline and wear-limit records are frequently incomplete or not maintained per machine. Missing wear limits force inspectors to improvise, slowing the inspection and any resulting decision. Without documented baselines, inspection quality relies heavily on the individual technician's memory and judgement. Absent limits lead to either premature replacement or run-to-failure, both wasteful.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed service, repair, and inspection events missing from the machine maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Completed events missing from the maintenance history is a record-keeping defect, only peripherally tied to the cartoning job (low relevance) with mostly administrative, recoverable consequences (low severity); it is held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: GMP work-order discipline captures most events, but rushed or paper-based recording still lets some events go unlogged. Logging each service event takes minor time and is usually done at task close-out. Recording completed events is a low-skill clerical task once procedures require it. Missing history entries cost mainly during audits, not day-to-day operation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance entries containing inaccurate dates, work performed, parts replaced, or inspection results
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate maintenance-record entries are a documentation-quality defect that does not directly stop carton production (low relevance) and their consequences accumulate slowly rather than causing acute failure (low severity). Within the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured CMMS fields and review steps improve accuracy, yet manual transcription of dates, parts, and results still introduces recurring errors. Entering accurate details takes marginally longer than sloppy entry, a minor time factor. Accurate recording of parts and results needs the doer's attention and knowledge but not scarce expertise. Inaccurate entries cost through rework and audit findings rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the machine maintenance history and supporting service, repair, or inspection records
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between maintenance history and supporting records are a records-reconciliation defect that does not impede active cartoning (low relevance) and produce recoverable audit friction (low severity). Kept within the 2.0 limit.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic reconciliation and change control reduce mismatches, but parallel paper and electronic records still diverge frequently. Reconciling history against supporting records is a time-bounded but occasionally lengthy cross-check. Spotting discrepancies between systems requires quality-review familiarity rather than deep expertise. Discrepancies mainly cost investigation labor and audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of compressed air lost through pneumatic leaks remaining after service
rows.imp_rat: Residual pneumatic leaks after service waste compressed air and energy, a modest efficiency loss rather than a job-blocker; proxies held low.
rows.sat_rat: Leaks rarely stop production so they persist, and current practice seldom verifies leak-free state after servicing. Finding and sealing pneumatic leaks during service is time-consuming and often deprioritized when the line still runs. Locating small leaks requires deliberate testing technique that many technicians skip without ultrasonic tools. Compressed air is expensive to generate and leaks left after service waste energy continuously, a chronic overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that fault history and machine-state evidence are missing when diagnosis begins
rows.imp_rat: Missing fault history at diagnosis slows repair but does not directly affect the running cartoning function (low relevance), and its consequence is delay rather than damage (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern PLC/HMI fault logging is widespread but limited buffer depth and unconfigured logging still leave evidence gaps. Missing fault history forces re-creation of the failure before diagnosis can proceed, extending downtime. Reconstructing lost machine-state evidence requires experienced inference rather than reading a record. Logging capacity is inexpensive, so gaps stem from configuration not cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of nozzles or applicator components damaged during daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Nozzle or applicator damage during cleaning is a narrow, easily-remedied maintenance error with low relevance to the overall job and low severity. Bounded by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Even with soft tools and SOPs, fragile nozzles are frequently nicked or clogged during daily manual cleaning. Gentle handling of delicate nozzles slows the cleaning routine somewhat. Delicate applicator nozzles are easily damaged without careful, skilled handling. Nozzles and applicator heads are costly, so damage during cleaning is a real expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applicator components being misaligned after daily cleaning and reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Applicator misalignment after cleaning is a rework/setup concern that is peripheral to core packing (low relevance) and normally caught quickly (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Keyed fittings and documented reassembly help, but daily manual teardown still yields intermittent misalignment. Realigning applicator components after cleaning is a repeatable daily task that fits the shift routine. Correct reassembly depends on operator care and familiarity, so alignment quality varies by person. Misalignment causes glue defects and rework, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machinery unavailability caused by the required replacement component being unavailable when maintenance begins
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from an unavailable replacement part touches uptime but is framed here as a spares-logistics delay rather than the core forming function (low relevance) and is a recoverable production loss (low severity). Held to the 2.0 ceiling per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Stockout events remain unpredictable because failure timing is hard to forecast, so the shortfall recurs despite min-max policies. When the needed part is not on hand, expedited freight and emergency ordering still leave the machine down for hours to days, and this is a common cause of extended downtime. Planners can usually identify the right part quickly, so the delay is procurement-driven rather than a skill gap. Emergency stocking and rush shipping inflate costs, and holding safety stock for every possible failure is prohibitively expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of packaging line interruption during calibration and verification
rows.imp_rat: Line interruption during calibration and verification is a routine downtime error with limited centrality and recoverable impact, held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled calibration programs with defined intervals and traceable standards are mature practice, giving fairly consistent, planned interruptions. Calibration and verification require the line to stop, and the duration is largely fixed by the procedure and instruments. Calibration needs qualified metrology or technician skills that not every shift has on hand. The lost production time during calibration is a recurring but predictable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning-line unavailability during device checks and adjustments
rows.imp_rat: Line unavailability during device checks is routine adjustment downtime, peripheral to the core job and with recoverable impact, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized check routines and adjustment procedures make these interruptions largely repeatable and well contained. Device checks and adjustments stop the cartoning line, though routine checks are usually short and predictable. Most device checks follow standard procedures that trained operators can perform without specialists. Brief planned stoppages carry modest, foreseeable production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent resolving discrepancies in the approved software and firmware versions
rows.imp_rat: Time spent resolving software/firmware version discrepancies is largely administrative overhead with minor direct impact on cartoning output; low relevance and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration management and validated-state records help, but incomplete change logs frequently leave version status ambiguous. Resolving discrepancies between approved and installed software/firmware versions is often a slow manual reconciliation against documentation. It takes controls specialists to interpret version records and determine the approved baseline, a skill bottleneck. Time spent chasing version discrepancies diverts skilled labor but is not itself a large material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable critical diagnostic data during machine operation
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable diagnostic data during operation hampers troubleshooting, a moderate reliability concern, yet the ceiling holds relevance and severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Onboard diagnostics are generally present, but data dropouts, unlogged parameters, and limited sensor coverage leave intermittent blind spots. Diagnostic data is usually available in real time on the HMI, minimizing wait during operation. Interpreting diagnostic data streams still requires trained personnel to be useful. Gaps in diagnostic data raise the cost of blind troubleshooting but the data infrastructure itself is a sunk investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among diagnostic indicators used to assess the machine's condition
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting diagnostic indicators complicate troubleshooting but are a secondary diagnostic-support concern (low relevance) with recoverable impact (low severity), ranked below higher batches.
rows.sat_rat: Independent, uncorrelated diagnostic sources commonly disagree, leaving diagnosis dependent on interpretation. Resolving conflicting diagnostics extends the time to reach a confident diagnosis. Reconciling contradictory indicators is precisely where scarce expert judgement is relied upon today.
