Conflicting signals lead to trial-and-error part replacement that wastes money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of preventive maintenance tasks scheduled after the applicable service threshold is reached
rows.imp_rat: Scheduling PM tasks after the service threshold is a maintenance-timing administrative error only loosely tied to producing cartons (low relevance); its consequences are usually absorbable slippage (low severity), well under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS due-date alerts help keep most PMs on time, but production pressure still pushes some past threshold. The core issue is PM tasks slipping past their due threshold, i.e., being done late. Scheduling adherence depends on planner discipline more than technical skill. Overdue PMs raise breakdown risk and its associated costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of preventive maintenance due dates calculated from inaccurate operating-hour, cycle-count, or component-condition data
rows.imp_rat: PM due dates from inaccurate operating-hour data is a maintenance-calculation error marginal to the cartoning job (low relevance) with mild, recoverable consequences (low severity), consistent with the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many PMs still run on calendar estimates rather than validated operating-hour or condition data, so due dates are often miscalculated. Bad interval data doesn't slow a task directly but drives mistimed scheduling. Setting accurate hour/cycle-based intervals requires engineering judgement often absent. Inaccurate triggers cause either premature part replacement or late-failure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine unavailability caused by poorly sequenced preventive maintenance tasks
rows.imp_rat: Poorly sequenced preventive maintenance causing downtime is a peripheral scheduling issue for cartoning; its consequences are modest lost uptime, capped low by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS job plans and standard PM procedures give reasonable sequencing for routine work, but complex overhauls still suffer from ad-hoc ordering. Poor task sequencing inflates downtime through idle waiting and rework of already-completed steps. Good sequencing depends on an experienced planner or lead who understands task dependencies. Wasted labour hours and extended line stoppage add cost when sequencing is poor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stocked spare parts incompatible with the intended cartoning machine
rows.imp_rat: Stocking spare parts incompatible with the machine is an inventory-quality defect that can delay a repair but does not directly halt normal cartoning (low relevance) and is recoverable (low severity). Held within the 2.0 limit.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS BOMs and OEM part catalogs help, but machine revisions and undocumented modifications still let incompatible spares slip into stores. Discovering an incompatible part only at install adds a fresh procurement delay to the outage. Correct cross-referencing of part numbers to specific machine builds demands experienced storekeepers or engineers who are not always available. Wrongly stocked parts tie up capital and often become obsolete write-offs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a critical spare part remains below its required stock level after withdrawal
rows.imp_rat: Stockout duration for a critical spare after withdrawal is a supply-chain replenishment error peripheral to cartoning operation (low relevance); it may delay a repair (moderate severity) but is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated reorder triggers in modern CMMS reduce the risk, but manual withdrawals and lagging paperwork still create silent shortfalls. After a withdrawal, replenishment lead times mean critical parts can sit below reorder level for days or weeks. Setting appropriate reorder points requires analytical judgement that many maintenance stores lack. Faster replenishment or higher safety stock raises carrying and expediting costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion on critical machine surfaces during extended shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion during extended shutdown is a storage-preservation defect peripheral to active cartoning; consequences are repairable degradation, capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Rust preventives and covers are standard, but during extended shutdowns humidity control and coverage lapses still let corrosion appear. Corrosion prevention is applied before shutdown, so it adds little to active downtime. Effective preservation of critical surfaces requires knowledge of correct inhibitors and coverage that is not universally held. Preservation materials are cheap relative to the cost of a corroded machine after restart.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical machine surfaces or mechanisms omitted from preservation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting machine surfaces from preservation matters mainly at end-of-life storage, not core cartoning throughput; consequences are contained and recoverable. Held at or below 2.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without enforced preservation checklists, some surfaces or internal mechanisms are routinely missed, making omissions a recurring problem. Preservation work is done ahead of storage and does not extend operational downtime. Ensuring every critical surface and mechanism is covered depends on a thorough checklist and diligent execution that varies by person. Omitting preservation saves negligible short-term cost but risks expensive restoration later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine exposure to damaging storage conditions
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to damaging storage conditions is a preservation concern peripheral to active cartoning; consequences are recoverable degradation, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Best-practice guidance exists, but machines are frequently left in whatever space is available, so damaging exposure remains common. Moving or protecting a machine from bad storage conditions is a one-time effort that does not affect ongoing downtime. Choosing suitable storage environments requires some engineering judgement but is not highly specialized. Climate-controlled or protected storage carries facility cost that is often minimized, exposing machines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production interruption during drive-component adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish interruption during drive-component adjustment is tangential to the cartoning job itself and yields recoverable production loss, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented setup procedures exist, but achieving repeatable adjustment without breaching the sterile envelope remains difficult and variable. Drive-component adjustment on sterile fill-finish lines interrupts production and, given aseptic requirements, restart validation extends the outage considerably. Adjusting drives while protecting sterility demands highly trained technicians, who are scarce. Sterile-line downtime and any batch impact are extremely costly per hour.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required ownership, maintenance, and validation records missing at handover
rows.imp_rat: Missing ownership/maintenance/validation records at handover is a documentation gap peripheral to cartoning throughput, generally correctable. Held at or below 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Handover packages routinely omit some maintenance or validation records due to fragmented storage. Gathering ownership, maintenance, and validation records at handover can slow the transfer. Knowing the full required record set for a regulated cartoner takes documentation expertise. Missing validation records reduce resale value and may force revalidation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the intended recipient cannot accept or operate the cartoning machine because of space, utility, or capability constraints
rows.imp_rat: Recipient inability to accept the machine due to space/utility constraints is a transfer-logistics issue, not central to the cartoning job itself; effects are limited and detectable. Held under 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-transfer site checks are inconsistent, so capability mismatches are discovered too late fairly often. Verifying recipient capability upfront avoids late relocation delays. Assessing space, utility, and capability fit requires engineering evaluation the recipient may not perform. A machine that can't be accepted incurs return shipping and storage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required ownership, decontamination, or transfer records missing at handoff
rows.imp_rat: Missing ownership/decontamination/transfer records at handoff is a records-completeness issue at transfer, peripheral to producing cartons, with recoverable consequences. Ceiling enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Document packages at handoff are frequently incomplete because no single owner controls the set. Assembling ownership, decontamination, and transfer records at handoff adds delay when files are scattered. Coordinating cross-functional records demands administrative diligence often under-resourced at disposal. Missing records delay transfer and may require re-doing work, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage sustained by the cartoning machine during removal or transport to the recipient
rows.imp_rat: Damage during removal or transport is a decommissioning/logistics error peripheral to the cartoning CFJ (low relevance); it can harm asset value (moderate severity) but is ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Established rigging and packaging practice keeps damage uncommon, though incidents still occur with awkward or delicate assemblies. Careful rigging and crating for transport adds time but is standard for machinery moves. Protecting the machine during removal relies on competent riggers, a widely available skill. Transport damage erodes resale or transfer value, a meaningful cost incentive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of machine damage during disconnection, loading, or return transit
rows.imp_rat: Machine damage during disconnection/loading/return transit is an end-of-life logistics error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance); it can degrade the asset (moderate severity) but ranks below batch 3.
rows.sat_rat: Mature handling and securement practice makes serious transit damage relatively rare but not eliminated. Protective disconnection, loading and securing procedures add manageable time. Standard riggers and drivers cover this with common competencies. Damage in transit reduces recovered value and may incur claims, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish packaging disruption while the machine is removed for return
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish disruption during machine return is a removal-logistics delay, peripheral to the cartoning job and typically planned/absorbable. Held under 2.0 per batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: With no ready backup, disruption to sterile packaging during machine removal is prolonged and hard to bound. Sterile fill-finish lines cannot easily absorb the downtime when a cartoner is removed for return, and interim capacity is scarce. Managing removal without breaching sterile-area constraints needs specialized aseptic and validation expertise. Sterile-line downtime is extremely costly per hour.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine energy feeds left connected because their isolation points were not identified
rows.imp_rat: Leaving energy feeds connected due to unidentified isolation points is a safety concern, but within the cartoning CFJ it is a peripheral shutdown/service error (low relevance) and though consequences could be serious, ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO energy-isolation procedures are mature, but incomplete drawings and hidden feeds still leave the occasional connection unidentified. Locating every energy isolation point can be slow when documentation is outdated. Identifying all feeds relies on skilled techs interpreting drawings and the physical machine, a real dependency. A missed live feed risks safety incidents and rework, with limited direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required controller access is unavailable before decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable controller access before decommissioning (relevance ~2) is a narrow procedural prerequisite in the transition phase, not the operating job; severity (~2) capped, as delays are recoverable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Access is usually available but lost passwords or expired vendor support still leave controllers inaccessible at decommissioning. Missing controller access blocks decommissioning until credentials or vendor support are obtained, adding delay. Regaining controller access needs specific PLC/HMI expertise and vendor relationships. Locked-out controllers may require paid vendor intervention.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of production or decommissioning disruption caused by data extraction
rows.imp_rat: Minimizing data-extraction disruption during decommissioning is peripheral to the core job of cartoning production; severity is low as such delays are typically absorbable. Ranking caps this below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Planned extraction windows generally succeed but occasional access issues extend disruption. Data extraction can be scheduled during planned downtime to minimize disruption. Extracting controller and historian data needs a technician familiar with the specific systems. Extended extraction ties up lines and staff, adding modest cost.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricants, hydraulic oil, and adhesive residue being combined into an incompatible waste stream
rows.imp_rat: Combining lubricants, hydraulic oil, and adhesive into an incompatible waste stream is a waste-handling error not part of the cartoning function (very low relevance); severity moderate for reaction/compliance risk, but limited to 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established container-and-label practice usually keeps streams distinct, though shared drain pans and haste occasionally cause commingling. Keeping oil, hydraulic fluid and adhesive in separate containers costs little extra time under normal drainage. Knowing which of these fluids are chemically incompatible needs basic hazmat training that most disposal crews possess. Combined incompatible fluids often become higher-cost regulated waste, so separation saves money.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of recovered components contaminated by product, cleaning-agent, or lubricant residues
rows.imp_rat: Residue contamination of recovered components is a decommissioning-hygiene concern marginal to core cartoning throughput; consequences are containable, capped low per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning protocols exist in regulated plants but residual contamination in hard-to-reach areas still occurs. Achieving residue-free recovery adds cleaning cycles that extend the decommissioning timeline. Effective residue removal from complex cartoner geometries requires trained cleaning technicians. Contaminated components lose salvage value and may require disposal as waste.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption between retirement of the former cartoning machine and stable output from the replacement line
rows.imp_rat: Production interruption during changeover (relevance ~2 under the ceiling) touches output continuity but is a one-time transition event, not the ongoing cartoning job; severity (~2) is capped though real downtime costs exist, ranked below prior batch items.
rows.sat_rat: Even with planning, replacement lines rarely reach stable output immediately, so interruption persists. The gap between retiring the old line and stable replacement output is a well-known but hard-to-shrink downtime. Ramping a replacement to stable output needs experienced commissioning engineers. Extended interruption means lost production revenue.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartons produced during output validation fail approved packaging specifications
rows.imp_rat: Cartons failing spec during output validation is nearer the core job but bounded to the validation stage; caught before production, so consequences are limited. Capped by ranking at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols catch failures, but initial-run spec misses during commissioning are still common. Failed validation cartons force reruns that extend the qualification window. Achieving spec-compliant output requires skilled setup and adjustment technicians. Failed cartons waste materials and consume rework time.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned stoppages caused by mismatches between the replacement cartoning line and adjacent production stages
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages from line-mismatch (relevance ~2 under ceiling) affects throughput but is an integration issue rather than the intrinsic cartoning function; severity (~2) capped despite genuine downtime impact, as ranking places it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Integration testing helps but adjacent-stage mismatches still trigger recurring unplanned stoppages after changeover. Interface mismatches cause repeated stoppages that extend the ramp period. Diagnosing and resolving line-integration mismatches requires cross-stage integration expertise. Unplanned stoppages waste throughput and incur troubleshooting cost.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved packaging materials are unavailable when output validation begins
rows.imp_rat: Approved packaging materials unavailable at validation start is a scheduling error affecting a validation step, not the ongoing cartoning function, and is recoverable. Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material staging practices are established, though occasional supply gaps still stall validation. Approved packaging materials can usually be staged in advance of output validation. Procuring correct approved materials requires materials-management coordination that is routine. Unavailable materials idle validation crews, wasting labor.

rows.source_job: Disconnect the machine from facility utilities
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, water, or data connections left attached after disconnection work
rows.imp_rat: Connections left attached after disconnection work is a decommissioning safety error peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance); could cause equipment damage or hazard (moderate severity) but capped by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and walkdowns catch most connections, but a stray line is still occasionally missed before the machine is moved. Disconnecting all utility and data connections is routine work that adds predictable time. Standard techs handle multi-utility disconnection, though data lines occasionally need specialist attention. A leftover attached connection can cause damage on lift, a moderate cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of condition-critical maintenance and decommissioning records that are missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Missing condition-critical maintenance/decommissioning records affects verification, not live cartoning output; consequences are contained and correctable. Ceiling enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Condition-critical decommissioning records are often missing or incomplete because capture wasn't enforced during service life. Filling gaps in condition-critical records at disposal takes time that is rarely budgeted. Judging which condition records are critical requires maintenance-engineering knowledge. Missing condition data undercuts salvage decisions and warranty/liability positions.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among regulatory, safety, and contractual cleanliness criteria before decontamination begins
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting cleanliness criteria before decontamination is a documentation-alignment concern peripheral to core cartoning throughput; consequences are procedural delays, capped low per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard framework reconciling regulatory, safety, and contractual cleanliness demands, so conflicts routinely surface late. Reconciling conflicting cleanliness criteria stalls the start of decontamination while parties negotiate. It takes regulatory and contractual expertise to spot and harmonize divergent criteria, a scarce combined skill set. Unresolved conflicts force rework or over-cleaning, wasting agents and labor.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required decontamination records that are incomplete at transfer approval
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decontamination records at transfer approval is a records-completeness defect peripheral to cartoning operation, with recoverable compliance consequences, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists exist but gaps at transfer approval remain common because record capture is manual and inconsistent. Chasing down missing decontamination records at transfer approval delays handoff. Compiling complete records needs disciplined documentation practice that decommissioning crews often lack. Incomplete records can force re-decontamination or hold shipments, adding cost.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulated waste removed from the cartoning machine is routed to an unauthorized disposal or treatment channel
rows.imp_rat: Routing regulated waste to an unauthorized channel is a compliance/disposal error peripheral to producing cartons (very low relevance); severity moderate given regulatory exposure, but held under the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manifested hazardous-waste tracking and licensed haulers are a mature system that reliably prevents misrouting in well-run plants. Routing regulated waste through licensed channels follows a set manifest process that does not materially slow disposal. Selecting authorized treatment channels leans on EHS/waste-broker expertise that regulated facilities routinely retain. Proper channeling avoids fines and cleanup liability far exceeding the modest handling cost.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible waste streams from the cartoning machine being mixed during segregation or storage
rows.imp_rat: Mixing incompatible waste streams is a downstream disposal error largely disconnected from the cartoning job (very low relevance); severity moderate for safety/compliance risk, but capped at 2.5 by the batch order.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled segregated containers and hazardous-waste protocols are mature and generally prevent mixing, with occasional lapses under time pressure. Segregating incompatible waste streams adds sorting time that is well absorbed into normal decommissioning workflow. Correct segregation relies on trained EHS staff reading SDS and compatibility charts, a common competency in regulated plants. Mixing incompatibles can trigger costly reclassification of the whole container, an incentive that keeps practice disciplined.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of materials removed from the cartoning machine that are assigned an incorrect waste classification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect waste classification of removed materials is a disposal-administration error disconnected from the cartoning job (very low relevance); severity moderate for regulatory consequences, but capped at 2.5 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Classification guidance exists but ambiguous or mixed materials from a machine still produce a real rate of misclassification. Classifying each removed material takes review time but is embedded in the waste-manifest workflow. Accurate waste classification demands specialist knowledge of codes and thresholds, a genuine skill bottleneck. Misclassification either overspends on disposal or risks fines, creating cost exposure both ways.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preventive maintenance for the warehouse caser is scheduled after the required due date
rows.imp_rat: Late preventive maintenance scheduling for the caser is peripheral to the core casing job (low relevance) and its consequences are typically absorbed via buffer windows (low severity), so it sits within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated triggers and compliance dashboards make on-time PM largely dependable, though backlog and resource crunches still push some tasks past due. CMMS-driven PM scheduling with due-date alerts is mature and directly addresses on-time execution. Scheduling requires little specialized skill once the PM plan is loaded into the system. Overdue PM can escalate into costly failures, but scheduling itself consumes minimal resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the warehouse caser maintenance window conflicts with a sterile fill-finish production run
rows.imp_rat: A maintenance window conflicting with a sterile fill-finish run affects scheduling coordination, but relevance to the casing job and its recoverable consequence are both held low here.
rows.sat_rat: Production and maintenance schedules are often integrated in the CMMS/MES, yet last-minute production changes still create conflicting windows. Coordinating maintenance around sterile production runs is fundamentally a scheduling-window problem. Aligning windows needs planning coordination rather than deep technical skill. A conflict can force lost production time, which is costly, but avoidance is mostly planning effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualified maintenance personnel are unavailable during the scheduled warehouse caser maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable qualified maintenance personnel during the caser maintenance window is a scheduling-resource error peripheral to the running job (low relevance) with delay-only consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Whether the qualified person shows up for the window is inconsistent, and depth-of-bench planning is often thin. Staffing gaps directly extend downtime while a qualified technician is sourced or waited for. Qualified caser technicians are a scarce, cross-trained resource, and coverage during a specific window is chronically hard to guarantee. Idle equipment and overtime or contractor call-ins impose real cost when the right person is missing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the warehouse caser maintenance window omits required line-clearance and sterile-area activities
rows.imp_rat: Omitting line-clearance/sterile activities from a maintenance window has cross-contamination stakes, but as a maintenance-planning error ranked below prior items both proxies are capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Whether the window fully incorporates required sterile activities depends on checklist rigor that is inconsistently applied across events. Omitting line-clearance and sterile-area steps saves time upfront but forces costly rework or holds later. Knowing which sterile-area activities must be embedded in the window requires GMP and process expertise. A missed line clearance can scrap product batches, a very high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required warehouse caser replacement parts are unavailable on the scheduled maintenance date
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable replacement parts on the scheduled maintenance date is a parts-supply timing error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) causing deferrable delay (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Long-lead and obsolete parts for casers are a chronic availability problem, and just-in-time ordering frequently misses the scheduled date. A missing part on the maintenance date forces reschedule and extends downtime substantially. Correctly identifying and pre-kitting the right part needs technician knowledge of the specific machine configuration. Carrying spares for every part is expensive, so most sites under-stock and eat expedite fees.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a warehouse caser safety control remains bypassed or incorrectly reset after testing
rows.imp_rat: A bypassed or incorrectly reset safety control carries genuine harm potential (severity), yet as a testing-phase error it is ranked lower here and constrained to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO and post-work checklist practices catch most cases, yet human-dependent reset steps still leave a real recurring gap where a bypass is forgotten. Lockout/tagout verification adds only minutes to a maintenance job, so time is not the binding constraint here. Correctly restoring safety controls after testing depends heavily on technician attention and discipline, which varies across shifts. A missed reset carries little direct material cost until an incident occurs, but the potential liability is large.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required replacement component is unavailable when warehouse caser maintenance begins
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable replacement component at maintenance start delays repair but is a parts-logistics matter ranked below prior items; relevance and severity are both held low.
rows.sat_rat: Component availability at job start is frequently unreliable due to lead times and imperfect kitting, closely mirroring the broader parts-availability gap. A missing replacement component at the start of maintenance stalls the whole job. Anticipating the right component to kit requires diagnostic knowledge of likely failure modes. Full spares inventories are costly, so many components are ordered reactively.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser cannot be placed in a safe state for adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Inability to place the caser in a safe state for adjustment carries a real safety consequence, but per the batch ceiling both proxies stay at 2, reflecting its lower combined-importance rank.
rows.sat_rat: Machine-safety standards and documented isolation points make achieving a safe state routinely dependable, with occasional gaps on older or modified equipment. Placing the caser in a safe state via LOTO adds only minor time to the adjustment task. Safe-state procedures are standardized and require basic trained competence rather than scarce expertise. Safe-state isolation consumes no meaningful resources beyond a brief pause.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required utilities or controls are unavailable for the pre-shift functional check
rows.imp_rat: Missing utilities or controls for the pre-shift functional check delays startup checks (low relevance) but is recoverable before production (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: In established facilities utilities are normally present, though unannounced maintenance or lockouts occasionally leave a functional check unable to proceed. A quick pre-shift check confirms utilities are present with minimal time cost when facilities are stable. Verifying air, power, and controls availability needs only basic operator competence. Utility unavailability wastes a shift start but rarely consumes material resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the warehouse caser's recorded maintenance history is unavailable when traceability is required
rows.imp_rat: Missing recorded maintenance history when traceability is required is an administrative/compliance gap with low centrality to actually casing product, and its consequences are mostly documentation-related rather than operational. Held at or below the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS records make most history retrievable, but gaps, paper stragglers, and inconsistent entry mean traceability sometimes falls short when audited. Retrieving history from a CMMS is fast when records were entered correctly. Pulling maintenance records requires only basic system familiarity. Digital record storage is cheap and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required adhesive, tape, or case blanks are unavailable when the warehouse caser needs replenishment
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable adhesive/tape/case blanks at replenishment can stall casing, but it is a supply-scheduling matter that is lower-ranked here; severity is a recoverable short pause, relevance modest.
rows.sat_rat: Standard reorder practices handle common supplies, though these fast-moving items still occasionally run short at the point of need. Running out of adhesive, tape, or blanks halts replenishment until stock arrives. Tracking these commodity supplies is a clerical inventory task, not a skilled one. These materials are inexpensive and easy to buffer, so overstocking is affordable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser or its detached components arrive damaged or missing after return transit
rows.imp_rat: Damaged or missing components after return transit affects asset condition (severity) but is a logistics event outside routine casing operation (relevance), held at 2.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Manifests and crating reduce loss, but detached components arriving damaged or missing after transit remains a recurring issue. Packing and inventorying detached components adds handling time before transit. Proper crating and securing of detached parts is a learnable, common logistics skill. Lost or damaged components mean replacement or recovery costs at the recipient end.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an approved processor refuses a scheduled caser-material load because its acceptance requirements do not match the load
rows.imp_rat: A processor refusing a scheduled caser-material load for mismatched acceptance requirements is a peripheral upstream scheduling error rather than the caser's core packing job, so relevance is low; consequences are recoverable rescheduling, keeping severity modest. Held at or below 2.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-acceptance paperwork and waste profiles usually align loads correctly, but mismatches still occur when profiles are stale or incomplete. Confirming acceptance criteria before scheduling adds coordination time but avoids costly return trips. Matching a load to processor specs requires familiarity with each facility's requirements, a knowledge burden that falls on procurement or EHS staff. A refused load means wasted transport and rescheduling, a moderate but avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking critical internal mechanical, electrical, or structural damage during the caser assessment
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking internal damage during assessment could affect reliability, but as an assessment-phase error ranked low, its relevance to active casing and its consequence are both kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized teardown inspection protocols, critical hidden damage is inconsistently caught depending on who inspects. Thorough internal assessment takes time, and overlooking damage causes later rework, but the inspection itself is not the bottleneck. Spotting concealed mechanical, electrical, or structural damage relies heavily on inspector expertise and attention, which varies widely. Missed damage lowers salvage or resale value and can cause failures downstream at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required change parts or setup specifications being unavailable when changeover begins
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable change parts or specs at changeover delays setup (relevance) with recoverable schedule impact (severity), constrained under the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Changeover kits and setup documentation are common but not universally maintained, so missing parts still surface with real frequency. Missing change parts at changeover extend downtime while they are located. Knowing which parts and specs are needed relies on documented setup sheets and experienced setters. Hunting for parts wastes labor and idle line time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual case misalignment after correction
rows.imp_rat: Residual case misalignment after correction leaves some positional error (relevance) that can cause minor downstream defects (severity), but it ranks below batch 2 and is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard aligners and squaring devices leave small residual offsets that are usually within tolerance but not eliminated. Residual misalignment usually doesn't slow the cycle unless it causes a downstream jam. Achieving tight alignment relies on correct guide and pusher setup by a skilled setter. Minor residual misalignment rarely wastes material unless it compounds into a jam.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case displacement recurring after correction
rows.imp_rat: Recurring case displacement after correction is central to reliable casing operation, but the batch ceiling limits both relevance and severity to 2 given its lower ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Corrections often address the symptom rather than the cause, so displacement recurs on the same run and consistency remains only moderate. Re-registering a displaced case usually happens within a cycle or two so time impact is modest. Preventing recurrence after a correction depends on an operator diagnosing the root cause of guide or timing drift, which is a judgement-heavy task. Repeated displacement corrections consume operator attention and cause occasional product jams that waste material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of corrected items remaining outside the required position at the warehouse caser infeed
rows.imp_rat: Corrected items remaining out of position at infeed directly affect casing accuracy (relevance) and can cause misfeeds (severity), but the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical correction leaves a small but persistent fraction still out of position, so this gap recurs regularly. Items left out of position may need a rejection cycle, adding minor time. Correction success depends on tuned mechanical guides, not exceptional operator skill. Items still out of position lead to jams or miscased product, a recurring waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a shifted item reaches the casing point before its position is corrected
rows.imp_rat: A shifted item reaching the casing point before correction directly disrupts caser accuracy (relevance) and can cause jams (severity), but the ranking places it below batch 2, capping it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Position sensors and guides catch most shifts before the casing point, but fast transient shifts still occasionally reach it. Detecting a shifted item before casing rarely costs time when sensors reject upstream. Catching shifted items early leans on sensor setup and occasional operator watchfulness rather than deep expertise. A miscased item from an uncorrected shift causes rework or a scrapped case, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of surrounding line disruption caused by the caser restarting out of sequence
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence restart disrupting surrounding line has real throughput impact, but the ranking caps both proxies at 2 as it sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Modern PLC line-control interlocks handle sequenced restarts reasonably well on integrated lines, though older or loosely coupled lines still stumble. An out-of-sequence restart disrupts surrounding equipment until timing is re-established, and this recovery window is the duration this need seeks to shorten. Getting the caser back in phase with adjacent machines takes operator knowledge of the line's handshake sequence. Disruption from a bad restart can cause jams and product loss on neighboring stations.

rows.source_job: Position empty cases at loading station
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of fill-cycle delay caused by empty cases being unavailable at the loading station
rows.imp_rat: Fill-cycle delay from unavailable empty cases stalls output (relevance) with modest recoverable cost (severity), but ranks below batch 2 and holds at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Case magazines and buffers usually maintain supply, so starvation is intermittent rather than chronic where buffering exists. Empty-case starvation stalls the fill cycle directly, a common throughput bottleneck. Keeping empties supplied is a logistics/staffing task rather than a skilled one. Idle line time from starvation wastes capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product packages damaged while verifying the case quantity
rows.imp_rat: Package damage during case-quantity verification is a narrow inspection-side error (low relevance) with limited, mostly cosmetic consequences (low severity), constrained by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Non-contact counting (sensors, vision) is common and rarely damages product during verification. Damage during quantity verification rarely adds meaningful time. Automated count verification removes manual handling that would require care. Any package damaged during verification is wasted product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejecting a case that contains the specified product quantity
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting a correctly-filled case is a false-reject/rework error, but the batch ceiling ranks it below prior items, so relevance and severity are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Modern count verification keeps false rejects low, though threshold drift still causes occasional wrongful rejections. False rejects don't slow the line but trigger unnecessary rework handling. Reducing false rejects depends on correctly calibrated counting/inspection thresholds set by skilled techs. Rejecting a good full case wastes finished product and rework labor.

rows.source_job: Unload completed cases from caser outfeed
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed cases damaged while being moved from the outfeed to the downstream station
rows.imp_rat: Damage to completed cases moved from outfeed to the downstream station is a handoff-boundary error only loosely tied to the caser's core job (low relevance), with recoverable single-case loss (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Outfeed conveyors and case handling are mature, so damage in transfer is relatively uncommon. Damaging cases at outfeed transfer can trigger cleanup stops, adding time. Clean transfer is mostly conveyor engineering, not skilled labor. A damaged completed case is a full unit of lost finished goods, expensive.

rows.source_job: Unload completed cases from caser outfeed
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed cases delivered to the wrong downstream station
rows.imp_rat: Cases delivered to the wrong downstream station is a routing defect with genuine consequences, yet the batch ceiling holds both relevance and severity at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/scanner-driven divert logic on modern lines routes cases dependably, making wrong-station delivery relatively uncommon. Misrouted cases are usually detected and redirected quickly, so lost time is limited. Correct routing is largely automated via diverts and barcode reads, reducing dependence on operator vigilance. Wrong-station delivery causes occasional handling rework but little material waste.

rows.source_job: Inspect completed cases for packing quality
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packing defects in completed cases that pass inspection undetected
rows.imp_rat: Undetected packing defects passing inspection would normally matter, but per the batch ceiling this item ranks below the prior batch, so relevance and severity are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection catches many but not all packing defects, and escapes remain a persistent recognized gap. Escaped defects don't cost line time until a downstream complaint forces investigation. Reliable defect detection depends on inspection setup or attentive manual checking, both error-prone. A defect passing undetected can cause costly downstream rejects or customer returns.

rows.source_job: Track caser cycle count during production
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the running count and actual completed caser cycles during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between running count and actual caser cycles is a data-tracking annoyance rather than central to erecting cases; its consequence is minor reconciliation effort. Both relevance and severity capped low per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: PLC cycle counters track actual caser cycles closely, though sensor miscounts on double-feeds or jams introduce occasional drift. The running count updates essentially live so lag is not the primary concern. The counter operates without operator input, so little skill is required to keep it accurate. Count drift misstates output but wastes no material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of purchase order fields inconsistent with the approved machinery configuration and commercial terms
rows.imp_rat: Purchase-order fields inconsistent with approved configuration is an administrative procurement error, peripheral to running cartons, with correctable consequences. Capped by ranking position.
rows.sat_rat: ERP templates and approval workflows catch many mismatches, yet configuration-to-PO discrepancies still slip through. PO creation is quick, but verifying each field against the approved configuration adds review cycles. Cross-checking commercial and technical PO fields needs someone fluent in both, which is a recurring staffing gap. Field errors cause change orders and rework whose administrative cost is real but bounded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved scope and acceptance-term discrepancies across the purchase documents
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved scope and acceptance-term discrepancies in purchase documents are contractual matters peripheral to the machine's function (low relevance), risking disputes but not operational harm (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Purchase document sets are commonly assembled from mismatched templates, so scope and acceptance discrepancies persist unless a rigorous review catches them. Reconciling scope and acceptance-term discrepancies across purchase documents requires multiple review rounds that extend closing. Spotting cross-document scope conflicts demands careful contract-review skill that is unevenly applied. Unresolved scope discrepancies become disputed change orders that raise final cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of submitted cartoning machinery specifications that conflict with the authorized configuration
rows.imp_rat: Submitted specifications conflicting with authorized configuration is a documentation-consistency error, only indirectly affecting cartoning output, and typically caught in review. Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal spec-review gates help, but a real percentage of submitted specs still conflict with the authorized configuration. Specification review against the authorized configuration is a defined step but often compressed near award. Spotting spec conflicts requires technical reviewers who understand the authorized cartoner configuration in detail. Undetected spec conflicts drive costly late redesigns and rejected equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of committed cartoning machinery spending above the authorized value
rows.imp_rat: Committed spending above authorized value is an administrative budgeting error with limited bearing on the machine's core cartoning function and modest financial consequences, held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: ERP-enforced spend limits and approval hierarchies are widely deployed and reliably block committing above authorized amounts. Purchase-order authorization limits are enforced quickly through standard sign-off systems. Enforcing spend against an authorized value is a routine controls task requiring little specialist skill. Authorization ceilings and PO controls directly prevent spending above the sanctioned value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree the demonstrated machinery setup differs from the intended packaging formats and production conditions
rows.imp_rat: A demonstration setup diverging from intended formats/conditions is an acceptance-testing fidelity error, moderately relevant to validating the CFJ but ranked below batch 3, so both proxies held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance demonstrations are common practice but often run under idealized rather than production-representative conditions, so gaps between demo and reality persist. Arranging FAT-style demonstrations under representative formats and conditions takes scheduling effort and time. Designing a demonstration that truly mirrors intended production conditions requires seasoned process and packaging judgement. A demo that misses real conditions leads to costly post-installation surprises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier-selected references omit unfavorable experiences with machinery reliability, delivery, or after-sales support
rows.imp_rat: Omission of unfavorable references (relevance ~2) is an administrative due-diligence gap far from the machine's core cartoning function; severity (~2) is low because it only mildly biases purchase decisions and is correctable through independent verification.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers self-select favorable references, so negative reliability, delivery, or support experiences are routinely hidden. Uncovering the reliability or support problems suppliers omit takes lengthy independent digging. It requires savvy, well-networked buyers to surface unfavorable experiences suppliers withhold. Acting on curated references risks a costly wrong purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machinery is damaged during freight, unloading, or rigging
rows.imp_rat: Damage during freight or rigging is a logistics error marginal to the operating job, with consequences that are repairable or covered, held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established freight and rigging practices make serious damage uncommon, though mishandling incidents still occur. Coordinated rigging schedules exist but rushed unloading windows still raise handling risk. Specialized riggers and packaging engineers substantially reduce damage when properly engaged, but their availability varies. Proper crating, air-ride freight, and professional rigging cost more but are commonly specified for high-value cartoners.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configured values are not retained or applied by the caser
rows.imp_rat: Configured values not being retained forces re-entry and undermines caser setup, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, so relevance and severity are capped low despite the operational friction it causes.
rows.sat_rat: Modern PLC/HMI storage retains configured values reliably, with rare loss on power or firmware faults. Saving and recalling settings is near-instant on current controllers. Confirming that values were actually stored and applied requires only basic operator verification. Lost settings cause re-entry effort but little material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination to case materials during loading
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced to case materials during loading carries real quality risk, but the batch ceiling caps both relevance and severity at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Clean handling practices make loading contamination an uncommon event. Contamination checks add negligible time during loading. Keeping case materials clean is basic hygiene not requiring special skill. Contaminated materials are cheap to discard but rarely need to be.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loaded case materials being exhausted before the scheduled batch is complete
rows.imp_rat: Running out of loaded case material mid-batch causes a delay but is a manageable replenishment event; relevance to the core casing job and severity of consequence are both held low here.
rows.sat_rat: Level sensors and pre-stocking usually prevent runouts, with lapses during understaffing. Running out mid-batch forces an unplanned stop and refill that delays completion. Monitoring material levels requires only routine attention. Exhaustion wastes little material, mainly costing downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of loaded case blanks that are crushed, warped, or stuck together
rows.imp_rat: Crushed or warped case blanks disrupt loading quality, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, holding both proxies at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Good storage and handling keep most blanks usable, with occasional damaged stacks. Sorting out crushed or stuck blanks briefly interrupts loading. Proper blank handling and fanning is routine, low-skill work. Crushed, warped or stuck blanks are wasted material, the primary loss here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of case blanks or consumables being incorrectly seated in the caser's magazine, hopper, or feeder
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly seated blanks or consumables cause feed jams and rework, yet this ranks below the prior batch, so relevance and severity both stay low with a modest, correctable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Feeders tolerate most loading, but occasional misorientation still causes faults. Correctly seating blanks in the magazine is quick once learned. Proper orientation and seating in hoppers and feeders takes a bit of operator know-how. Misseated consumables cause minor jams and a little waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shipping damage remains undetected before installation
rows.imp_rat: Undetected shipping damage before installation risks later failures (severity) but is peripheral to operating the caser (relevance), and the cap keeps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Damage that only shows at install regularly slips past standard visual checks, so outcomes remain inconsistent. Visual inspection at receiving is quick but rushed, so latent transit damage is not caught within the unloading window. Detecting concealed shipping damage on a complex caser demands an experienced eye that most receiving crews lack. Undetected damage surfaces later as costly rework and downtime, but the up-front inspection itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine components damaged while being positioned or joined
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged while being positioned or joined harm the asset (severity) but occur during install rather than the casing job (relevance), constrained to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: With trained crews and rigging aids most positioning damage is avoided, though occasional mishaps persist. Careful rigging and positioning slows the install but the added time is planned for. Proper lifting and joining of heavy machine sections leans heavily on skilled millwrights and riggers. A dropped or mispositioned component is expensive to replace, driving investment in lifting gear.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the caser's installed position and its designated facility coordinates
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between the caser's installed position and facility coordinates is largely a layout/installation nicety with low relevance to core casing and mild consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Layout marks, lasers and datum references make coordinate placement a well-served, consistent task. Positioning the caser to facility coordinates from layout drawings is a routine placement step. Surveying to designated coordinates uses standard measuring practice well within crew capability. Minor position deviations are cheap to correct before anchoring but costly after.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of caser connection work delayed by an unavailable required utility service
rows.imp_rat: Delay from an unavailable utility service during connection is a setup-phase timing issue (relevance) with recoverable schedule impact (severity), and the batch ceiling holds it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utility readiness checklists exist but are inconsistently enforced, so waiting on services remains a common delay. Missing utility services frequently stall connection work while crews wait for facilities to deliver them. Little special skill is needed; the delay is a coordination and availability problem. Idle crew time during utility waits is a direct cost.

rows.source_job: Connect caser to plant control system
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of caser commands and status signals mapped incorrectly in the line-control system
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly mapped commands/status signals can misdirect the caser (relevance/severity), but this integration setup error sits below batch 2 and is held at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: I/O checklists and point-to-point testing catch many errors, yet manual mapping still leaves recurring incorrect signal assignments. Verifying command and status mappings adds point-to-point checkout time during integration. Correct signal mapping demands skilled controls integrators who understand both address maps. Mismapped signals cause misoperation and rework but little direct material waste.

rows.source_job: Connect caser to plant control system
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to existing line-control communications while connecting the caser
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to line-control communications during connection can briefly destabilize operations (relevance/severity), but as a transient install-phase error it stays within the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Segmentation and managed switches help, but tie-ins to live control networks still risk disrupting existing communications. Working around live line-control comms to avoid disruption can slow the connection considerably. Avoiding disruption to running communications requires experienced controls engineers who understand the live network. Unplanned disruption can halt an adjacent line, an expensive event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the machine setup area while installing format components
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination while installing format components could taint product in a food/pharma context (moderate severity), but for the caser's mechanical casing job it is a peripheral hygiene concern, so relevance stays low. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Basic housekeeping and hygiene protocols exist but are inconsistently followed, leaving contamination a periodic occurrence. Cleaning up contamination introduced during component installation adds rework time before the machine can run. Avoiding contamination requires disciplined handling habits that vary widely between operators and shifts. Contamination events can scrap product or trigger sanitation cycles, but for a warehouse caser the cost exposure is moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required guide rail, plunger, or folding plate is unavailable when setup begins
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable guide rail, plunger, or folding plate at setup does delay casing (moderate relevance), but it is a preparatory logistics issue that is quickly resolved by staging parts, so severity is low. Batch ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Format-part storage is typically informal shelving with no enforced kitting or check-out, so unavailability at setup is a common recurring surprise. When a guide rail or plunger is missing at setup, the whole changeover stalls waiting for the part to be located or fabricated. Knowing which format part belongs to which case size depends on tribal knowledge and experienced operators, not a foolproof system. Missing or misplaced format parts drive expedited replacement orders and idle labour, a recurring avoidable cost.

rows.source_job: Create and save case format recipes
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrect parameter values stored in each saved recipe
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect stored recipe parameter values could cause mispacks, but per the batch ranking this item sits below prior items, so relevance and severity remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual keying of values means transposition and unit errors persist despite HMI checks. Entering values is quick but verifying each one against spec adds time. Choosing correct parameter values relies on operator judgement and setup experience. Wrong values scrap trial cases and product before the error is noticed.

rows.source_job: Create and save case format recipes
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required case and product parameters omitted from a saved recipe
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required parameters from a saved recipe is a configuration-completeness error, capped low by the batch ranking on both relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: HMI templates prompt for many fields but optional or free-text parameters still get left blank. Filling all recipe fields takes little time when a template is used. Knowing every required parameter for a given format needs operator familiarity with the caser. Omitted parameters cause failed trials that consume product and materials.

rows.source_job: Create and save case format recipes
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a saved recipe is associated with the wrong case size or product format
rows.imp_rat: A recipe tied to the wrong case size could cause mispacks, but the batch ranking caps this below prior items, so both relevance and severity stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Naming conventions and dropdown selection reduce but do not eliminate wrong-format associations. Selecting or saving a recipe is fast on modern HMIs. Correctly tying a recipe to a case size and product format still depends on operator care and naming discipline. A misassigned recipe wastes trial materials and time but rarely damages the machine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product units damaged while being packed into cases during the trial run
rows.imp_rat: Product damage during trial-run packing harms units, giving it meaningful severity, but as a trial-phase error under the ceiling both proxies stay at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Trial packing damage occurs until settings converge but is expected and contained. Damaged units during the trial force re-runs that extend commissioning. Minimizing pack damage depends on skilled adjustment of grippers and timing. Damaged trial product is scrapped, a limited but real material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exhausting the supply of empty cases before the production run is complete
rows.imp_rat: Exhausting empty cases mid-run halts casing (relevance) and interrupts the run (severity), but the ranking places it below batch 2 and caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material planning and reorder practices make mid-run exhaustion uncommon but not impossible under supply hiccups. Running out of cases mid-run forces a stop, costing time to resupply. Preventing exhaustion is a planning task, low skill dependence. A shortfall wastes run time and can strand partially completed product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of line imbalance after upstream or downstream throughput changes
rows.imp_rat: Line imbalance after throughput changes affects flow but is transient and recoverable; the ranking keeps both proxies at 2 despite its relevance to line balance.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation conveyors buffer some mismatch but response to sustained changes is inconsistent and largely manual. Rebalancing a line after an upstream or downstream throughput change is the core delay this need targets, and today it drags on until an operator manually re-synchronizes rates. Restoring balance requires an experienced operator to judge accumulation and adjust speeds across machines. Prolonged imbalance idles equipment and can starve or back up neighboring stations, wasting capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption while changing the caser to a new SKU or case format
rows.imp_rat: Changeover interruption duration when switching SKU/case format only modestly affects the CFJ (low relevance) and produces recoverable throughput loss rather than catastrophic harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: SMED practices and tool-less adjustments reduce variability but changeover times still vary widely by operator. Changeover downtime for a new SKU is a major, well-known productivity loss. Fast changeover depends heavily on a skilled setter's speed and judgement. Every minute of changeover is lost production and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury from unexpected Case Packer movement during blockage clearance
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury from unexpected movement during blockage clearance carries genuine safety severity, but it is peripheral to the packing CFJ and the batch ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding, interlocks and lockout/tagout make injury from unexpected movement relatively well controlled. Lockout procedures add time to blockage clearance. Safe clearance depends on operator following lockout discipline. Injuries carry high human and financial cost, so controls are enforced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator pinch or crush injury during case-loading intervention
rows.imp_rat: Pinch/crush injury during case-loading intervention has real safety severity but is peripheral to the grouping/loading CFJ and the ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and safety procedures make pinch/crush injuries relatively uncommon, though intervention risk persists. Safe intervention practices add minor time. Avoiding pinch/crush injury depends on operator adherence to safe methods. Injuries are costly, driving strong controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of active tallies being assigned to the wrong shipping case during case transitions
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning active tallies to the wrong case during transitions is a traceability bookkeeping error, peripheral to the physical grouping/loading job (low relevance); consequences are recoverable via reconciliation, but ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Manual tally tracking makes misassignment during transitions a recurring problem. Reassigning misattributed tallies costs reconciliation time. Keeping tallies with the right case during transitions requires operator attention. Wrong-case tallies cause count errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of closure material applied beyond the shipping-case sealing requirement
rows.imp_rat: Excess closure material is minor waste, peripheral to the loading job; trivial consequences kept low within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sealers meter closure material fairly consistently, so overuse is minor and occasional. Excess closure application takes marginally more time. Applying the right amount depends on machine setting rather than operator skill. Overapplied tape or glue is direct consumable waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch-specific packaging discarded because its identity or release status cannot be confirmed
rows.imp_rat: Discarded batch-specific packaging is minor waste, peripheral to loading; trivial cost consequences kept low within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Status controls limit unnecessary discard, but unconfirmable materials still get scrapped recurrently. Resolving unconfirmable identity costs time before discard. Deciding to discard depends on operator judgment about status. Batch-specific packaging discarded for unconfirmable status is direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production-record entries whose case quantities disagree with physically packed quantities
rows.imp_rat: Record-vs-physical quantity disagreement is a reconciliation defect adjacent to loading (modest relevance); correctable and capped, so severity stays minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Manual record entry leaves recurring disagreements between recorded and physical quantities. Resolving record-vs-physical count disagreements costs time. Accurate record entry depends on operator care. Count discrepancies trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packed cases leaving tertiary packaging without a completed integrity decision
rows.imp_rat: Cases leaving without a completed integrity decision is a quality-gate omission somewhat related to loading completion (low relevance); capped by the batch ceiling regardless of downstream risk.
rows.sat_rat: Procedural sign-offs mostly ensure decisions, but cases occasionally leave without a completed check. Confirming an integrity decision before release adds brief time. Ensuring each case has a completed decision relies on operator discipline. Cases released without decision risk costly downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt that a closed shipping case contains the specified carton quantity
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that a closed case contains the specified count touches the loading correctness (slight relevance) but is a confidence/assurance concern that the ceiling caps low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposure to hazardous case-packer motion while diagnosing a stoppage or misload
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous motion during diagnosis has real severity, but it is peripheral to physical loading and the batch ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnosing with guards defeated is common practice, leaving meaningful exposure risk. Exposure to hazardous motion during diagnosis can halt work for safety. Safe diagnosis needs trained energy-control judgement. The stake is injury, not material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while accessing or adjusting the case packer during changeover
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury during changeover access has genuine severity but is peripheral to executing the case load and held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and procedures make changeover injuries relatively rare, though pinch-point exposure remains. Injury during changeover access causes significant lost time. Safe access relies on trained procedure adherence. Injury cost dominates over material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected case-packer motion exposing the operator to injury during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected motion exposing operator during maintenance carries safety stakes, yet is tangential to the loading job and capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO is a mature, enforced practice that makes unexpected-motion injury during maintenance uncommon. Injury from unexpected motion during maintenance causes major downtime. Safe maintenance depends on trained lockout/tagout practice. Injury cost far exceeds material considerations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while correcting the identified case-packer fault
rows.imp_rat: Injury while correcting a fault carries safety severity but is marginal to the grouping/loading CFJ and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Fault correction often tempts bypassing safeguards, keeping residual injury risk moderate. Injury during fault correction extends downtime substantially. Safe fault correction requires trained hazard awareness. Injury cost dominates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a hazardous safeguard failure remains undetected during pre-production verification
rows.imp_rat: Undetected hazardous safeguard failure has real safety stakes (slightly higher severity) but is a pre-production verification reliability issue not central to the loading CFJ, and the ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Without rigorous functional safety testing, hazardous failures can remain undetected before production. Full pre-production safety verification takes time that is sometimes shortened. Detecting hazardous failures needs specialized safety testing skill. An undetected hazardous failure risks severe injury and liability, not just material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected case-packer motion during reset after safeguard actuation
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected motion during safeguard reset poses safety severity, but it is peripheral to the grouping/loading CFJ and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reset-after-safeguard logic is generally engineered but unexpected motion incidents still occur. Unexpected motion during reset causes emergency stops that lose time. Safe reset requires trained understanding of stored energy and reset logic. The cost is injury risk, not material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packaging configuration change is implemented without required regulatory assessment or approval
rows.imp_rat: Implementing a config change without regulatory assessment is a compliance-governance error, tangential to the physical CFJ of loading cartons (low relevance); severity capped despite potential regulatory exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems generally force regulatory review, making unassessed changes fairly rare in regulated firms. Regulatory assessment gates add lead time before change implementation. Determining when regulatory approval is required needs regulatory judgement. Unassessed changes risk compliance actions far costlier than the assessment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for grouping and loading cartoned units into shipping cases
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for grouping/loading touches the CFJ definition (modest relevance) but its consequences are indirect and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Template-driven criteria help, but residual ambiguity in grouping/loading criteria persists in practice. Clarifying acceptance criteria is upfront work that does not chiefly cost time. Writing unambiguous acceptance criteria for grouping and loading requires experienced protocol authors. Ambiguity causes rework rather than direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of finished cases with counts or grouping patterns outside validated acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Finished cases with counts/patterns outside criteria touches loading correctness (slight relevance) but as an outcome defect it is capped low by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Automated count verification and rejection are mature, so out-of-criteria finished cases are relatively rare. Count/grouping checks run inline without meaningful added time. Modern case packers automate counting so little operator skill is required. Out-of-criteria cases cause reject and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned units damaged during routine grouping and loading into shipping cases
rows.imp_rat: Cartoned units damaged during routine grouping/loading directly relates to load quality (slight relevance) but as an outcome defect the batch ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed case packers keep routine damage low, so this is comparatively well served. Damage during routine loading does not primarily cost time. Minimizing handling damage depends on equipment tuning and operator skill. Damaged cartoned units are direct scrap and material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in cost inclusions and exclusions across candidate quotations
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in cost inclusions/exclusions across quotations is a purely commercial comparability issue with no impact on the cartoning function (very low relevance) and only budget-estimation consequences (low severity). Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because vendors structure quotes differently, inconsistency in cost inclusions is the norm and only partially corrected by manual leveling. Normalizing inclusions and exclusions across quotes into a comparable basis is tedious manual work. Building an apples-to-apples cost comparison needs experienced estimators who can spot hidden scope differences. Inconsistent inclusions distort total-cost comparisons and lead to selecting on misleading price signals.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of recurring-cost assumptions unsupported by sterile fill-finish operating evidence
rows.imp_rat: Recurring-cost assumptions unsupported by fill-finish operating evidence is a financial-modeling ambiguity remote from the carton job (very low relevance) with only budgeting-accuracy consequences (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recurring-cost figures are typically assumption-based with little sterile-specific operating evidence, so this need is poorly served. Gathering sterile fill-finish operating evidence to support recurring-cost assumptions is slow and often skipped under deadline. Validating operating-cost assumptions against real aseptic-process evidence requires specialist operational data few buyers can access. Unsupported recurring-cost assumptions produce total-cost-of-ownership errors that surface only during operation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material warranty, liability, or delivery provisions on which buyer and supplier positions remain irreconcilable
rows.imp_rat: Irreconcilable warranty/liability provisions is a contractual negotiation error remote from operating the machine (very low relevance) with potential financial exposure (low-moderate severity), capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contract negotiation resolves most such provisions, though a residual set of positions can remain deadlocked. Negotiating warranty, liability, and delivery provisions to closure can prolong the contracting phase. Resolving irreconcilable commercial positions needs skilled legal and procurement negotiators. Unresolved liability and warranty terms expose the buyer to later financial risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required compliance, validation, or documentation deliverables omitted from the contract
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance/validation deliverables from the contract is an administrative documentation gap (very low relevance to running cartons) though it could cause compliance exposure (low-moderate severity), held at 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deliverable lists are often incomplete and omissions recur, making this a consistently under-served risk in contracts. Compiling the full list of required compliance and validation deliverables into the contract is a slow, checklist-driven effort. Knowing which validation and documentation deliverables must be contractually mandated requires deep GMP procurement expertise. Omitted deliverables must be bought later at a premium or self-generated at cost.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evaluated options lacking sufficient maintenance, refurbishment, and compliance records to verify their history
rows.imp_rat: Evaluated options lacking history records is an evaluation-phase completeness error, remote from the running cartoning job, with modest consequences. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Records are frequently incomplete or unverifiable, so many evaluated options cannot have their history confirmed. Chasing maintenance, refurbishment, and compliance history for used or refurbished options takes significant elapsed time. Interpreting incomplete service and compliance records needs specialist scrutiny that is often unavailable. Missing history forces costly independent inspections or risks buying a poor-history machine.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among the purchase order, packing list, and acceptance criteria at delivery
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts among purchase order, packing list, and acceptance criteria (relevance ~1) are documentation-reconciliation errors quite distant from cartoning performance; severity (~2) is low since discrepancies are resolvable clerically at delivery.
rows.sat_rat: These documents are prepared by different parties and routinely conflict in detail at delivery. Cross-checking PO, packing list, and acceptance criteria at delivery adds receiving effort but is scheduled. Reconciling three documents demands attentive staff familiar with all of them. Document conflicts cause disputes and delayed payments with moderate cost impact.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the machinery and accompanying components inaccessible for physical examination at delivery
