rows.imp_rat: Duration to benefit realization is a value-timing delay tangential to the CFJ; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit-realization timing is inconsistently managed, so lags recur. The gap between deployment and benefit realization is the core delay this need targets and is often longer than planned. Accelerating benefit capture requires ramp-up and commissioning expertise not always coordinated. Delayed benefits defer return on deployed capital, effectively a carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of planned product and shipping-case formats that the authorized case-packing capability cannot accommodate
rows.imp_rat: Product/case formats the capability cannot handle limits the job, yet the batch ceiling forces both to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Product/case format coverage is inconsistently validated against the future portfolio. Verifying product and case-format coverage adds specification time. Confirming format compatibility needs packaging and product-portfolio expertise. Unaccommodated formats drive expensive change or new capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that demand falls below funded case-packing capacity before deployed capital is recovered
rows.imp_rat: Demand falling below funded capacity before payback is an investment-recovery concern distant from grouping cases; low consequence per ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Long-horizon demand uncertainty is inherently hard to contain and poorly mitigated in typical business cases. Demand risk plays out over years, well beyond any review timeframe to control. Judging recovery-period demand risk requires market and financial forecasting expertise. Demand falling below funded capacity leaves capital unrecovered — a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the authorized case-packing capability requires unplanned redesign or requalification before routine production
rows.imp_rat: Authorized capability requiring unplanned redesign is a project-rework concern distant from routine loading; low stakes under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scope-completeness checks are inconsistent, so requalification surprises remain a recurring risk in regulated fill-finish contexts. Unplanned redesign or requalification adds significant unbudgeted schedule before routine production. Anticipating redesign/requal needs demands validation and engineering foresight not always applied at authorization. Redesign and requalification generate substantial unplanned expenditure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the case-packer financial case fails the required investment-return hurdle
rows.imp_rat: The financial case failing the return hurdle is an investment-analysis concern distant from physical loading; low consequence per ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Hurdle evaluation is well-established, though its validity depends on assumption quality that varies. Hurdle-rate testing is a standard gated step that rarely adds delay once inputs are ready. Applying the investment-return hurdle is routine finance practice with clear templates. A failed hurdle signals capital that would be poorly returned, and the test guards against that.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay beyond the authorization date required by the site capacity strategy
rows.imp_rat: Delay beyond authorization date is a schedule error peripheral to loading cases; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Authorization timing is somewhat governed by capital calendars but still slips against strategy dates. Delay beyond the authorization date required by strategy is the direct schedule error targeted here. Meeting authorization deadlines needs governance-scheduling discipline. Late authorization can push cost escalation from delayed procurement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required case-packer acquisition expenditure omitted from the capital request
rows.imp_rat: Omitted acquisition expenditure from the capital request is a budgeting defect distant from packing itself; ceiling holds both at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Capital-request templates catch major items but ancillary costs are still routinely missed. Assembling a complete capital request takes scoping time. Ensuring nothing is omitted from the request requires thorough estimating discipline. Omitted acquisition costs surface later as unbudgeted spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that competing site investments displace the case-packer acquisition from the approved capital plan
rows.imp_rat: Competing investments displacing the acquisition is a capital-planning concern distant from grouping cases; low consequence per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Portfolio prioritization is unpredictable and the case-packer's place in the plan is not securely protected. Capital-plan prioritization cycles can stall the case-packer while competing projects are debated. Defending the acquisition against competing bids requires portfolio and business-case advocacy skill. Displacement delays benefit capture but is not itself a direct cash loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory sterile fill-finish compliance and validation requirements omitted from the authorization scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitted sterile compliance/validation requirements is a regulatory-scope defect; despite quality stakes the ceiling caps both at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory scope completeness is inconsistent, so mandatory requirements are still omitted from authorization scope. Compiling all mandatory compliance requirements adds scoping time. Knowing every sterile fill-finish validation requirement demands deep regulatory expertise. Omitted mandatory requirements cause costly late remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of material changes to production-volume or pack-format assumptions during capital review
rows.imp_rat: Material changes to volume/format assumptions during review is a planning-instability error distant from execution; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Assumption stability during review is weakly controlled, so material changes recur. Mid-review assumption changes reopen analysis and extend the cycle. Locking assumptions early requires forecasting discipline often not enforced. Late assumption changes can invalidate cost sizing already committed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in decision rights among site and corporate approvers
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in decision rights among approvers is a governance-administration matter peripheral to the physical case-packing job; consequences are procedural friction only.
rows.sat_rat: Standard authorization matrices only partially cover mixed site/corporate scenarios, so decision-right ambiguity recurs on non-routine acquisitions. Delegation-of-authority matrices exist but ambiguity over site-versus-corporate case-packer sign-off routinely drags approval cycles out. Resolving which approver owns a borderline capital decision demands seasoned governance judgement that few executors reliably possess. Unclear decision rights add rework and duplicated review effort but rarely destroy hard capital dollars directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage change in projected case-packer acquisition expenditure during capital review
rows.imp_rat: Change in projected acquisition expenditure during review is a budget-variability error remote from the job; ceiling forces 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Estimate stability is partly governed but early scopes still shift materially during review. Controlling cost change during review adds re-estimation cycles. Holding estimates stable requires disciplined estimating and vendor management. Percentage change in projected acquisition cost is the direct budget-drift error targeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether case-packer authorization will occur before the site capacity milestone
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about authorization timing is a planning-delay error distant from packing; relevance and severity at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of confidence erosion caused by conflicting financial, capacity, and timing signals
rows.imp_rat: Confidence erosion from conflicting signals is a reporting-ambiguity error distant from packing; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to provide stakeholders with a defensible case-packer funding status
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed without defensible funding status is an emotional/credibility error peripheral to the job; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the funded case-packer procurement appears indefensible under quality and validation scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Procurement appearing indefensible under quality scrutiny is a reputational/compliance error; ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt created by conflicting quality, validation, engineering, and finance assessments
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting assessments is a governance-ambiguity error remote from the CFJ; held at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of feeling personally exposed after unresolved case-packer scrutiny questions arise near an inspection milestone
rows.imp_rat: Duration of feeling exposed near inspection is an emotional-timing error peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether case-packer lifecycle economics fit within the approved capital envelope
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about lifecycle economics fitting the envelope is a financial-ambiguity error remote from execution; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of confidence erosion caused by conflicting case-packer lifecycle expenditure estimates
rows.imp_rat: Confidence erosion from conflicting lifecycle estimates is a reporting-ambiguity error peripheral to execution; relevance and severity at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes over unbounded case-packer lifecycle expenditure after funding commitment
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over unbounded lifecycle spend is an emotional/financial error peripheral to the CFJ; held at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a case-packer authorization falling short of pharmaceutical quality expectations is publicly attributed to the purchase executor
rows.imp_rat: Authorization publicly attributed to executor is a reputational/compliance error distant from packing; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with Quality and Validation leaders when the case-packer investment's compliance basis is challenged
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with quality leaders over compliance basis is a reputational error remote from execution; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the purchase executor is bypassed in future sterile fill-finish investment decisions because of the case-packer authorization
rows.imp_rat: Bypassed in future sterile investment decisions is a governance error distant from packing; relevance and severity at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that executive leadership attributes a case-packing capacity shortfall to the purchase executor's authorization judgment
rows.imp_rat: Capacity shortfall attributed to executor judgment is an accountability error remote from execution; held at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with executive leadership when the case-packer investment exceeds its approved capital envelope
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when investment exceeds envelope is a reputational/budget waste peripheral to the job; relevance and severity at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that executive leadership bypasses the purchase executor in future capital authorizations after the case-packer investment fails scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Bypassed in future authorizations after failure is a career/governance error peripheral to the job; both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of case debris released into the casing area during removal
rows.plain: Release as little loose material from unusable cases as possible while removing them from the casing area.
rows.imp_rat: Case debris released during removal is a housekeeping/contamination concern at end-of-life, peripheral to the ongoing casing job and low-consequence here, kept at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and catch trays contain most debris but scattered material still escapes into the casing area during removals. Cleaning debris released during case removal takes minor recurring time. Debris cleanup requires no particular skill. Released debris represents wasted packaging material and cleanup effort, the loss this need targets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of trainee contact with moving caser components during the demonstration
rows.plain: Keep trainees from touching moving parts of the machine that puts products into cases during the demonstration. This helps prevent injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Trainee contact with moving components is a safety concern but confined to demonstrations, not the core job; batch ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: OSHA-compliant guarding and interlock stops make contact with moving components uncommon, though a curious trainee reaching in remains a residual risk during live operation. Guarding interlocks and roped-off zones are standard and add little time once set up before the demo. Trainees rely on a knowledgeable demonstrator to keep them at safe distance, but fixed guarding and light curtains reduce dependence on constant supervisory vigilance. Machine guarding, interlocks and safety signage are already built into compliant equipment at no added demo-specific cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising pharmaceutical packaging cleanliness during the demonstration
rows.plain: Keep the medicine packaging clean while showing how the casing equipment works. Dirt or other contamination could make the packaging unsuitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Packaging-cleanliness risk during a demonstration is confined to a sales/demo context rather than routine casing (low relevance), and any contamination is isolated to the demo unit (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established GMP cleaning protocols and controlled demo areas keep most contamination risk in check, but the extra handling of a demonstration still introduces occasional cleanliness compromises. Gowning, cleaning and using validated packaging for the demo adds preparation time in pharma environments. Maintaining cleanliness during a live demo depends on operators and trainees adhering to GMP handling discipline, which requires trained personnel. Using cleanroom-grade materials and sacrificial packaging for demonstrations adds material cost that is generally accepted in regulated settings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the warehouse caser faulting before the demonstrated cycle is complete
rows.plain: Prevent the warehouse machine that puts products into cases from stopping before the demonstration is finished. This lets observers see the complete operation.
rows.imp_rat: A caser faulting mid-demonstration affects a demo event, not routine job output; relevance and severity are modest and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: A run-in period plus a rehearsed demo cycle materially reduces mid-cycle faults, yet caser jams and sensor faults still occasionally surface during the actual demonstration. Pre-demonstration warm-up cycles and dry runs add setup time but catch most faults before the witnessed run begins. Experienced FSEs and validation technicians know the machine's failure-prone stations and pre-stage them, though this expertise is not uniformly available. Spare parts kits and consumables are typically staged on-site, adding modest cost but preventing costly mid-demo failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required demonstration materials are unavailable at the scheduled start
rows.plain: Make sure all materials needed for the demonstration are ready when it starts. This prevents interruptions and allows the case-packing operation to be shown properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing demonstration materials at start is a scheduling error tied to demos, not core casing; low relevance and severity, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a rigorously maintained materials checklist, missing case blanks or wrong product samples still cause delays or aborted demonstrations at the scheduled start. Assembling cases, packaging, and product samples takes lead time and is often left to the last minute before a scheduled demo. Knowing exactly which case blanks, tapes and product formats the demo needs requires familiarity that a checklist only partly substitutes for. Demo materials are consumables of modest cost, but scrambling for last-minute substitutes or expedited shipments inflates spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which demonstrated operator actions differ from normal warehouse caser operation
rows.plain: Make sure the actions shown by the operator are close to the actions normally used to run a warehouse box-packing machine. This helps show that the demonstration reflects real work.
rows.imp_rat: Demonstrated actions differing from normal operation is a demo-fidelity variability issue with peripheral relevance and low severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Demos are frequently run slower or in a curated sequence to avoid faults, so demonstrated actions often deviate from normal operation, leaving this need only partially served. Running the demo in genuine production mode takes no longer than a scripted one, though setting up realistic conditions can add prep time. It takes a skilled operator to run the caser in true production sequence rather than a simplified staged routine that diverges from normal use. Demonstrating at real line speeds with real materials consumes more product and consumables than a token demo would.

rows.source_job: Record cases produced per production run
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production runs with an incorrectly transcribed completed-case total
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly transcribed completed-case totals is a clerical error with minimal direct impact on the casing job, so both proxies are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Hand transcription of counts remains common and is a well-known source of consistent error with weak verification. Transcription errors surface during later audits, delaying accurate reporting. Manual keying of totals demands careful attention, and fatigue makes errors likely. A mistyped total misstates yield but consumes no material.

rows.source_job: Record cases produced per production run
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed-case totals assigned to the wrong production run
rows.plain: Make sure the number of cases completed is linked to the correct production batch. This keeps production totals accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Case totals assigned to the wrong production run is an administrative record-keeping defect, low in relevance to the core job and low in consequence under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where run totals are logged manually against run IDs, misassignment happens regularly and is caught only in reconciliation. Reassigning a misattributed total requires later manual investigation, adding delay to reporting. Correct assignment depends on the operator or clerk tying the count to the right run identifier, an error-prone manual link. Wrong assignment corrupts production records but wastes little physical resource.

rows.source_job: Record cases produced per production run
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production runs whose recorded total includes cases from an adjacent run
rows.imp_rat: Recorded totals including cases from an adjacent run is a bookkeeping error peripheral to the physical casing job, warranting low relevance and severity within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Run-boundary demarcation is often manual and loosely enforced, so bleed-over between adjacent runs is a frequent and poorly guarded error. Contamination of a run's count by adjacent-run cases is often discovered only after the fact, delaying reconciliation. Keeping run boundaries clean depends on operators correctly signaling changeovers, an attention-dependent step. Miscounts across runs distort yield accounting rather than consuming material directly.

rows.source_job: Record cases produced per production run
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between production-run completion and completed-case total recording
rows.plain: Record the total number of completed cases as soon as a production run ends. This keeps production records accurate and current.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in recording completed-case totals is a reporting-timeliness issue peripheral to casing (low relevance) with easily-absorbed consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual end-of-run logging is inconsistently prompt, so recording latency varies widely. The delay between run completion and recording the total is often significant where operators log totals manually at end of shift. Timely recording depends on operator diligence rather than any automated capture. Late recording delays reporting but costs no material.

rows.source_job: Inspect completed cases for packing quality
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration completed cases remain at the caser outfeed awaiting inspection
rows.plain: Inspect completed packages soon after they leave the case-packing machine. This helps find packing problems without leaving finished packages waiting for long.
rows.imp_rat: Completed cases dwelling at outfeed awaiting inspection is a minor flow-timing delay of limited severity, ranked below the prior batch and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Inline vision inspection keeps most cases moving without a wait, though manual-inspection stations still create queues. The dwell of completed cases at outfeed awaiting inspection is the delay this need targets, and inline inspection has shortened it considerably on modern lines. Automated inline inspection reduces reliance on an inspector being present, lowering skill dependence. Cases waiting at outfeed tie up buffer space with little direct material cost.

rows.source_job: Inspect completed cases for packing quality
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between inspectors applying packing-quality acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure inspectors judge the quality of packed boxes in the same way. This creates a fair and consistent decision about whether the packing is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Inspector disagreement on packing-quality acceptance criteria affects consistency but is peripheral to the core casing function and low-consequence here, capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Written criteria and sample boards reduce but do not eliminate subjective variation between inspectors. Inspector disagreement does not primarily cost time but rather produces inconsistent decisions. Consistent application of packing-quality acceptance criteria depends heavily on trained human judgement, and inter-inspector variation is the core issue here. Disagreement causes some good product rejection and defect passage, a modest material cost.

rows.source_job: Track caser cycle count during production
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the running cycle count is unavailable to the operator during the production run
rows.plain: Keep the operator able to see the current number of machine cycles during production. This helps the operator monitor how much work the machine has completed.
rows.imp_rat: Running-cycle count being unavailable to the operator is an information-display gap marginal to physical casing throughput (low relevance) and transient (low severity); capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard HMI count displays are dependable, with unavailability limited to occasional screen or comms faults. Count displays are generally live on the HMI, so periods where the count is unavailable to the operator are short and tied only to display faults. Reading the count off the HMI requires no special skill. Brief unavailability has negligible resource cost.

rows.source_job: Track caser cycle count during production
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between completion of a caser cycle and its inclusion in the running count
rows.plain: Add each completed box-packing machine cycle to the running total as soon as it finishes. This keeps the production count accurate and up to date.
rows.imp_rat: Latency between cycle completion and count inclusion is a reporting-timeliness error peripheral to physical casing (low relevance) and trivially resolved (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic cycle sensing gives consistent, immediate increments with only rare missed pulses. The lag between a completed cycle and its appearance in the running count is essentially real-time on PLC-driven counters, so this need is largely met. No operator involvement is needed for the count to update, so skill is irrelevant to the latency. Near-instant counting incurs no added resource cost.

rows.source_job: Track caser cycle count during production
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexpected running-count resets before the production run ends
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected running-count resets affect count tracking rather than the physical casing throughput (low relevance) and are recoverable (low severity); the mandatory cap keeps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Modern counters hold state reliably through minor stoppages, so spurious resets are relatively rare but power or fault events still cause them. An unexpected reset forces recovery of the lost count, adding delay to the run. Diagnosing why a counter reset early requires some troubleshooting knowledge but is infrequent. A lost running count wastes reconstruction effort rather than material.

rows.source_job: Track caser cycle count during production
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cycle-count target and running count cover different production-run boundaries
rows.plain: Make sure the target number of machine cycles and the live count refer to the same production run. This keeps the reported count accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Cycle-count target and running count covering different run boundaries is a metric-definition ambiguity with negligible operational consequence and low relevance to casing; both minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Run-boundary and reset events are often manually initiated and imperfectly synchronized, so target and running count can straddle different runs. Boundary mismatch between target and running count is discovered at run reconciliation, delaying closeout. Aligning the count reset with the true run boundary depends on the operator triggering it correctly at changeover. Mismatched boundaries distort per-run accounting without material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of scheduled caser lubrication points left unlubricated
rows.plain: Make sure every planned lubrication point on the box-packing machine receives lubricant. This helps moving parts work properly and reduces wear.
rows.imp_rat: Unlubricated scheduled lubrication points affect maintenance, but the mandatory ceiling keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Paper or CMMS lube checklists exist and mostly work, but points get skipped when fittings are obscured or checklists are outdated, leaving recurring gaps. Lubrication rounds are quick to schedule but easy to defer under production pressure, adding little time burden either way. Following a greasing checklist is low-skill, though knowing every hidden fitting on a caser takes machine familiarity that newer techs lack. Grease and labor are cheap, so the cost of doing it right is trivial versus the bearing failures caused by missing a point.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of caser unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window due to lubrication work
rows.plain: Keep the case-packing machine from staying out of service longer than planned because moving parts still need oiling. This helps maintenance finish within its scheduled time.
rows.imp_rat: Caser unavailability beyond the maintenance window from lubrication work is a schedule-overrun of limited scope, low relevance and low severity, capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PM checklists make lubrication predictable, though occasional access difficulties or seized fittings still cause overruns. Lubrication tasks are well-established scheduled activities with defined durations, so overruns beyond the window are the exception rather than the rule. Lubrication is a routine, well-proceduralized task requiring only modest technician training. Lubricants and labor are low-cost, and centralized or auto-lube systems further reduce spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replenishments in which the operator loads an incorrect adhesive, tape, or case blank
rows.plain: Load the correct glue, tape, or box material each time supplies are refilled. This helps the machine package products properly and prevents wasted materials.
rows.imp_rat: Loading incorrect adhesive, tape, or case blank affects case quality, but the mandatory batch-5 ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling, keyed feeders and visual checks reduce mis-loading, though look-alike consumables still cause occasional errors. Loading the wrong consumable forces a stop-and-swap that costs time. Correct loading is a low-skill task but relies on operator attention. Wrong adhesive or blanks waste material and can spoil cases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of replenished consumables compromised by contamination
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated replenished consumables threaten product integrity, yet the mandatory ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed packaging and stocking practices keep most consumables clean, with contamination arising mainly from poor storage or open handling. Handling consumables cleanly adds negligible time to replenishment. Avoiding contamination requires basic handling discipline rather than scarce expertise. Contaminated consumables cause scrap and rework, but material waste per event is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of warehouse caser stoppage during consumable replenishment
rows.plain: Keep the box-packing machine stopped for as little time as possible while supplies are refilled. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Stoppage during consumable replenishment is a routine, expected pause with low incremental severity and modest relevance; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard replenishment procedures are dependable, though awkward loading points can still cause longer-than-needed stops. Consumable replenishment stoppages are typically brief and predictable, and quick-load designs keep them short. Replenishment is a low-skill operator task requiring minimal training. The stoppage consumes some production time but material and labor costs are low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incomplete or conflicting asset records prevent the purchaser from establishing clear ownership of the caser
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment records agree and are complete, so the buyer can clearly prove that they own the box-packing machine.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete asset records blocking clear ownership is a title/documentation error, marginal to operational casing (low relevance) with resolvable legal consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset registers and bills of sale usually establish ownership cleanly, with conflicts arising mainly for poorly-tracked legacy equipment. Assembling asset records to prove ownership can slow closing but is routine transaction work. Establishing clear title from asset records is standard administrative practice needing modest expertise. Record disputes can delay payment or reduce sale price, a contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the buyer rejects the ownership transfer after discovering an undisclosed caser condition
rows.plain: The buyer should know about the caser’s condition before accepting ownership. This prevents the buyer from rejecting the transfer after discovering an important problem that was not disclosed.
rows.imp_rat: Buyer rejecting ownership transfer over undisclosed condition is a transaction-disclosure error, tangential to casing performance (low relevance) with reversible deal consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard as-is disclosures and pre-sale inspections reduce surprise rejections, though undisclosed conditions still occasionally surface. Disclosure and inspection before transfer add negotiation time but are part of normal sale cadence. Preparing accurate condition disclosures requires assessment competence but follows established asset-sale practice. A rejected transfer means re-marketing cost and lost sale value, a moderate risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the ownership-transfer hold caused by incomplete financial, legal, or facility approvals
rows.plain: Shorten the hold on changing ownership while financial, legal, or building-related approvals are still missing. This helps the ownership change be completed sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Ownership-transfer holds from incomplete approvals are an administrative delay peripheral to the caser's operational job (relevance low), and impact is held at the batch ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Multi-party approval chains are unpredictable, so holds vary widely with no reliable clearing time. Ownership-transfer holds waiting on approvals are exactly the delay this need targets and they are often prolonged. Navigating financial, legal and facility sign-offs needs coordination skill spread across departments. Prolonged holds carry storage and opportunity costs on a stranded asset.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that ownership of the caser passes before the purchaser receives the agreed funds
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that ownership of the box-making machine changes hands before the seller receives the agreed payment. This protects the seller from handing over the machine without being paid.
rows.imp_rat: Ownership passing before agreed funds arrive is a payment-sequencing error in the transaction, tangential to casing (low relevance) with recoverable financial consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature commercial-transaction practice with escrow, lien clearance, and documented transfer of title makes premature ownership transfer uncommon. Escrow and payment-before-title practices add little delay to typical asset sales, so timing is rarely the felt loss here. Standard bills of sale and lien-release procedures are handled by procurement or legal staff without exotic expertise. Escrow or holdback arrangements carry modest fees relative to the caser's residual value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recipient site cannot accommodate or operate the donated caser
rows.plain: The receiving site should be able to fit, use, and run the donated machine. This avoids giving them equipment they cannot use.
rows.imp_rat: Whether a donated caser fits a recipient site is a disposition concern remote from the core casing function; the ceiling keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Donation matching is largely ad hoc with little standardized fit-checking, so mismatches are common and poorly mitigated. Pre-donation site assessments, when done, take coordination time between donor and recipient. Judging whether a recipient site can house, power, and operate the caser needs engineering assessment the recipient often lacks. A failed donation wastes transport and reinstallation spend and may leave the donor re-disposing the unit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required ownership, maintenance, or decommissioning records missing from the donation package
rows.plain: Include all needed records about ownership, upkeep, and taking the equipment out of service in the donation package, so the recipient can safely understand and manage it.
rows.imp_rat: Missing records in the donation package is an end-of-life documentation ambiguity marginal to the CFJ (relevance low), and its consequences stay at the mandated ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Donation packages routinely arrive with gaps because record custodianship is fragmented. Assembling a complete donation package is a time-consuming records hunt across systems. Knowing which ownership and maintenance records are required for donation needs experienced judgement. Missing records can void the donation or its tax value, a meaningful loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to nearby sterile fill-finish activities during caser removal
rows.plain: Remove the equipment without interrupting nearby work that makes sterile products and puts them into their final containers. This helps ongoing production continue while the equipment is removed.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to nearby sterile fill-finish during removal is an adjacent-process concern, not the caser's own job (low relevance), and the ceiling holds severity at 1 despite sterility stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Disruption duration is variable and hard to predict because unexpected contamination-control steps arise mid-removal. Removal near sterile fill-finish is typically scheduled into shutdown windows, but overruns that prolong disruption are common. Sequencing removal to minimize sterile-area disruption requires experienced planners who understand both rigging and aseptic constraints. Lost sterile production time during disruption is extremely expensive, pressuring but not eliminating delays.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the manufacturer rejects the caser return because warranty or recall records are incomplete
rows.plain: Make sure all records needed to support the caser's warranty or recall return are complete. Otherwise, the manufacturer may refuse to accept it.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete warranty/recall records causing a rejected caser return is a peripheral administrative concern (low relevance) to the core casing job, and its consequences are limited to a return process hiccup (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Warranty and recall documentation is commonly fragmented or lost over an asset's life, so record-based rejections are a frequent unresolved pain. Reconstructing missing warranty or recall records late in the lifecycle consumes unplanned time. Assembling complete warranty/recall provenance requires records-management diligence that is often lacking at disposal. Incomplete records force forfeited warranty credits or paid disposal that a valid return would have covered.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the caser is not shipped within the required warranty or recall return window
rows.plain: The caser should be shipped back within the time allowed for warranty or safety-related returns. This helps preserve the right to receive repair, replacement, or other support.
rows.imp_rat: Missing a warranty or recall return shipping window is a logistics/administrative delay peripheral to the core casing function (low relevance) with limited consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Deadline tracking exists but is inconsistently enforced, so late shipments still happen with real frequency. Meeting a fixed return window is fundamentally a scheduling race that disposal teams often start too late. Knowing the applicable window and initiating logistics is routine once someone is assigned to track it. Missing the window converts a free/credited return into a paid disposal, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of caser-waste disposals with incomplete or inaccurate regulatory records
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete regulatory records for waste disposal has compliance stakes, but the batch ceiling ranks it below prior items, so both proxies stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated waste manifests are a mature, audited practice, so records are fairly complete though still occasionally inaccurate. Compiling regulatory waste records is time-consuming but usually built into the disposal workflow. Environmental compliance staff or licensed haulers handle the paperwork, so required skill is available where regulation bites. Incomplete regulatory records risk fines that far exceed the cost of doing them right.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the packaging line stoppage during the transition from the retired caser
rows.plain: Keep the packaging line from being stopped for longer than necessary while replacing the old case-packing machine. This helps products continue moving through packaging during the change.
rows.imp_rat: Line stoppage during transition from a retired caser is a one-time changeover event marginal to ongoing casing (low relevance) and temporary (low severity); held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Established changeover planning makes stoppage duration reasonably predictable, though overruns still happen. Packaging-line stoppage during caser changeover is planned into maintenance windows and is a familiar, bounded event. Line transition and equipment swap-out is standard maintenance-engineering work with well-understood procedures. Downtime costs are real but the transition is usually scheduled to minimize production loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incompatibilities with the vacated space or line interfaces block replacement-unit startup
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement machine fits the empty space and connects properly to the existing equipment, so it can start running.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibilities blocking replacement-unit startup could delay resuming operations, but the batch-5 ceiling caps this below prior items; relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-install interface surveys catch many issues, but incompatibilities still occasionally block startup. Interface mismatches surface at replacement startup and can stall commissioning for days. Reconciling the vacated line's mechanical, electrical, and data interfaces to a new unit needs integration engineering. Blocked startup extends downtime and may require costly retrofit adapters.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production lots affected by incorrectly transferred casing settings and production parameters
rows.plain: Fewer production lots should be affected by settings or operating instructions transferred incorrectly from the old equipment to its replacement. This helps the replacement produce the intended results.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly transferred casing settings can spoil production lots, but the batch ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Setting transfer is often manual and error-prone, though first-article checks catch many mistakes before large lot impact. Verifying transferred settings before production use adds modest checking time. Correctly migrating casing settings to a replacement demands process-engineering knowledge that is usually available. Bad-lot production from wrong settings wastes material and rework across affected lots.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transition work compromises controlled manufacturing conditions around the packaging line
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement work does not reduce the cleanliness or control of the production area near the packaging line. This helps protect the manufacturing process during the change.
rows.imp_rat: Transition work compromising controlled conditions is tangential to the caser's core casing function (low relevance); while contamination is serious in principle, the ceiling forces this below prior batch items, holding severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Transition work adjacent to controlled manufacturing frequently risks particulate or pressure excursions, and deviations here are a recognized, imperfectly-controlled risk. Protecting controlled conditions forces slow, staged transition work and containment setup that extends the project. Maintaining cleanroom/controlled-environment integrity during removal demands specialized contamination-control expertise beyond ordinary rigging crews. Containment, HEPA measures and requalification add significant cost to what would otherwise be simple removal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned replacement-unit stoppages after production resumes
rows.plain: The replacement machine or unit should not stop unexpectedly after production starts again. This helps production continue without unplanned interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned replacement-unit stoppages after resuming production do harm throughput, but the ceiling forces this error below prior items, so both proxies stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Post-startup instability is a known break-in phenomenon partly mitigated by ramp-up procedures but not eliminated. Unplanned stoppages after resumption cost cumulative downtime during the shakedown period. Stabilizing a fresh replacement demands experienced commissioning and tuning personnel. Each stoppage wastes production time and can spoil in-process product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required caser disposal fields left undocumented in the sustainability report
rows.plain: Record every required detail about how the box-packing machine was discarded in the environmental report, so its disposal can be accounted for.
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented disposal fields in the sustainability report is a reporting-completeness defect distant from the casing job (relevance low), and consequences stay at the batch-5 ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Sustainability reports commonly have blank disposal fields because upstream disposal data isn't captured in a report-ready form. Populating sustainability report fields is a periodic reporting task done under deadline pressure. Completing disposal fields needs familiarity with sustainability reporting taxonomies held by few staff. Undocumented fields risk compliance gaps rather than immediate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of caser disposal entries containing inaccurate disposed-material quantities
rows.plain: Make sure disposal reports state the correct amounts of each material thrown away. Accurate amounts are needed for trustworthy environmental reporting.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate disposed-material quantities in disposal entries is a data-accuracy defect peripheral to casing operations (relevance low), and consequences are constrained to the batch ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Manual quantity entry without automated capture leads to recurring inaccuracies in disposal entries. Quantity reconciliation is a modest logging step but is often deferred. Accurate quantity capture needs someone diligent at the scale or count, a routine but error-prone task. Inaccurate disposed quantities distort recovery credits and billing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the final disposition of caser components cannot be substantiated for sustainability reporting
rows.plain: Keep enough proof of what happened to the case-packing machine parts when they were retired or discarded. This supports accurate reporting about environmental and sustainability results.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to substantiate final disposition for sustainability reporting is a traceability unreliability marginal to the CFJ (relevance low), with consequences held at the mandated ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Once components leave the dock the paper trail routinely breaks, so the final fate of caser materials frequently cannot be verified with any consistency, making this a chronic unsolved gap. Chasing down downstream disposition proof from recyclers and scrap handlers stretches over weeks or months, and often the evidence never arrives at all. Substantiating final disposition demands scarce expertise in chain-of-custody documentation and vendor auditing that most warehouse disposal staff simply do not have. Certified downstream tracking and third-party disposition audits are expensive add-ons that most operations skip, leaving reporting exposed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completion of the caser's disposal and receipt of reportable disposal data by the Sustainability Officer
rows.imp_rat: Delay in the Sustainability Officer receiving reportable disposal data is a reporting-timing error peripheral to the core casing function (relevance low), and its impact is capped by the batch-5 ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Because transmission is ad hoc rather than systematized, data arrives incomplete or late often enough that officers must repeatedly follow up. Disposal completion data typically reaches the Sustainability Officer through manual emails, spreadsheets and batched monthly rollups, introducing lags of days to weeks. Compiling the reportable figures requires someone who understands both the disposal event and the reporting schema, a handoff that stalls when that person is unavailable. The delay itself costs little directly but consumes staff hours re-requesting and reconciling numbers that should flow automatically.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting caser disposal entries across facility and disposal-party records
rows.plain: Make sure the facility’s disposal record and the disposal company’s record give the same information about the case-packing machine. Matching records support accurate environmental reporting.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting disposal entries across facility and disposal-party records is a reconciliation ambiguity marginal to the casing job (relevance low), with consequences held at the mandated ceiling for batch 5 (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Facility and third-party systems rarely integrate, so conflicting entries are a common and persistent problem. Cross-checking facility and disposal-party records happens after the fact, so conflicts surface late. Reconciling two organizations' data formats requires careful clerical cross-referencing that is easy to botch. Conflicting entries trigger disputes and duplicate handling charges.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected maintenance, procurement, and operations teams omitted from the caser retirement notification
rows.plain: Make sure all affected maintenance, purchasing, and day-to-day operations teams are told when the warehouse equipment is being retired. This helps each team prepare and take the right follow-up steps.
rows.imp_rat: Teams omitted from the retirement notification is a communications defect tangential to the core function (relevance low), and its impact is held at the mandated ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Notification lists are often incomplete because there is no authoritative stakeholder map for a given caser. Sending retirement notifications is fast once the recipient list is known. Identifying every affected team relies on organizational knowledge that is unevenly held. Missed notifications cause misallocated planning effort rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between the caser's removal from service and notification of affected stakeholders
rows.plain: Notify the affected people as soon as the case-packing machine is taken out of service. This helps them adjust their work or make other arrangements.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between removal from service and stakeholder notification is a timing error peripheral to the core function (relevance low), with impact bounded by the mandated ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Without an automated trigger tied to decommissioning, notification timing is inconsistent. The delay between removal from service and stakeholder notification is exactly the error, and it is often days or weeks. Triggering timely notice requires someone attentive to close the loop, not deep expertise. Notification lag mainly costs through delayed downstream reactions rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended recipients who do not receive the caser retirement notification
rows.imp_rat: Intended recipients not receiving the retirement notification is a notification-delivery unreliability marginal to the casing job (relevance low), and consequences are held at the batch ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Broadcast notifications reach most recipients, but stale lists and filtering still drop some intended parties. Distribution of notifications is near-instant via email or system alerts. Sending to a distribution list needs little skill; accuracy of the list is the issue. Non-delivery costs are indirect, tied to uninformed downstream actions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance, procurement, and operations plans that still treat the retired caser as available after notification
rows.plain: After people are notified that the box-packing machine has been retired, make sure all maintenance, purchasing, and operating plans stop treating it as usable. This prevents people from planning work or purchases around equipment that is no longer available.
rows.imp_rat: Plans still treating the retired caser as available is a rework error only loosely connected to the CFJ (relevance low), with consequences capped for this lower-ranked batch (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Even when notified, plans frequently continue to treat the caser as available due to poor propagation. Updating plans after notification lags because plan revision cycles are periodic. Removing a retired caser from plans needs planners who correctly interpret the notice. Plans still assuming availability cause wasted procurement and scheduling decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent resolving the identity or disposal requirements of materials retained in the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Time resolving identity/disposal of retained materials is an administrative decommissioning error with negligible effect on the casing job and trivial consequences, so both proxies are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or ambiguous records make resolution unpredictable, sometimes fast and sometimes protracted. Chasing down the identity and disposal requirements of retained materials is a chronic time sink when documentation is missing. Resolving material identity needs someone versed in SDS, product records, and waste rules, which slows things when unavailable. Delays hold up disposal and staff hours, though the direct material cost is minor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty about the substances previously handled by the caser
rows.plain: Find out what substances the casing machine handled before, so it can be cleaned safely and thoroughly.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about substances previously handled matters for handling/cleaning but is peripheral to the casing throughput job for this scope, capped at 1 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Substance-history records are often incomplete or lost over an asset's life, leaving persistent uncertainty with few reliable workarounds. Establishing prior-substance history when records are thin delays safe handling decisions. Determining what was previously handled often requires investigative expertise and interviews when documentation is absent. Uncertainty forces treating equipment as worst-case contaminated, inflating decontamination and disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of caser release delay caused by incomplete decontamination records
rows.plain: Release the case-packing machine promptly when its cleaning and contamination-removal records are complete and correct. This prevents paperwork problems from keeping the machine unavailable.
rows.imp_rat: Caser release delay from incomplete decontamination records is a documentation-driven delay of modest centrality and consequence; batch ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Record completeness varies with how disciplined the decon process was, so delays recur unpredictably. Release is directly held up when decontamination records are incomplete, and closing documentation gaps is a common delay. Compiling compliant decontamination records requires QA-literate staff who know what evidence release demands. Delayed release ties up space and holding cost but the documentation itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of caser-assigned services that remain active after the warehouse caser leaves service
rows.plain: Stop all services assigned to a warehouse box-packing machine when that machine is no longer in use. This prevents paying for services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Services staying active after the caser leaves service is a billing-cleanup waste error tangential to the core job (relevance low), with consequences held at the ceiling for this lower-ranked batch (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a linked service registry, so orphaned active services persist unnoticed as a chronic failure. Deactivating assigned services requires coordinated action that often trails the physical retirement. Knowing every service tied to a caser demands institutional knowledge that no single role reliably holds. Services left active keep incurring charges for an asset that no longer exists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cancellation requests rejected because the purchaser lacks documented account authority
rows.plain: Avoid rejecting a request to stop a service because the buyer cannot prove they are authorized to close the account. Clear proof of authority helps the request be handled without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Cancellation requests rejected for lacking documented authority is an account-governance ambiguity distant from the core casing function (relevance low), with consequences capped for this lower-ranked batch (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Authority documentation is inconsistently maintained, so rejections happen regularly but not universally. Verifying account authority is quick when documentation exists but stalls the request when it doesn't. Confirming authority is a clerical check requiring modest procurement knowledge. Rejected cancellations cause rework loops rather than large direct costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that caser-assigned services remain billable after the warehouse caser leaves service
rows.plain: Stop charges for services assigned to the warehouse packing machine soon after the machine is taken out of use. This helps avoid paying for services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Services remaining billable post-retirement is a cost-leakage delay not central to casing function (relevance low), and its financial consequence is capped by the batch-5 ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Without automatic linkage between asset retirement and billing systems, over-billing recurs consistently. Billing continues for the lag between retirement and contract cancellation, and that lag is often long. Catching billable services requires cross-checking contracts that few disposal staff are equipped to do. Every extra billable day is direct wasted spend on a decommissioned caser.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that caser-assigned connectivity or monitoring services are terminated before final decommissioning activities are complete
rows.plain: Make sure internet connections and monitoring help remain active until all final shutdown and removal work is complete. Ending them too early could disrupt that work.
rows.imp_rat: Premature termination of connectivity/monitoring during decommissioning is an end-of-life sequencing concern, distant from the casing job; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Service termination and decommissioning are usually managed by separate parties with weak sequencing, so premature cutoffs recur. Aligning service-termination dates with decommissioning milestones requires lead time that is often mismanaged. Coordinating IT/vendor service teardown with physical decommissioning needs cross-functional coordination skill. Prematurely cutting connectivity can force costly re-provisioning or on-site data-recovery trips.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that remote access to the warehouse caser remains authorized after monitoring services end
rows.plain: Make sure remote access to the warehouse case-packing machine is turned off when monitoring services end. This prevents former service providers from continuing to access it.
rows.imp_rat: Remote access remaining authorized after monitoring ends is a security-hygiene issue at end of life, remote from casing; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Orphaned remote-access credentials after service end are a well-known, frequently missed gap in decommissioning practice. Revoking remote access is quick once someone remembers to do it. Access de-provisioning is routine IT work but depends on someone owning the offboarding task. Lingering access rarely costs money directly unless a breach occurs, but breach exposure can be large.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of selected reusable components entering the recycling or waste stream before removal from the retired warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Reusable components lost to waste before removal is an end-of-life waste error of low relevance and mild consequence; bounded to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: When pre-removal marking is skipped, selected reusable parts do get swept into scrap streams, a recurring gap. Tagging reusable components before teardown adds a preparation step but prevents lost recovery time. Identifying which components to preserve requires knowledgeable planning ahead of demolition. Reusable parts scrapped before removal are lost value, sometimes significant.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serviceable components damaged during removal from the retired warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Damaging serviceable components during removal from a retired caser affects salvage value, not the operating job (low relevance); consequences are limited to recovered-part loss (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With experienced crews damage is limited, but outcomes vary with crew skill and time pressure. Careful extraction of serviceable parts is slower than bulk demolition but is planned for. Damage-free removal depends on skilled technicians using proper tools and techniques, and skill shortfalls cause most damage. Components damaged in removal lose their recovery value, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of nonserviceable components mistakenly accepted for reuse after recovery from the retired warehouse caser
rows.plain: Do not approve broken or unusable parts for future use after removing them from the retired warehouse machine. This prevents unsuitable parts from being kept as usable spares.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting nonserviceable components for reuse is an end-of-life recovery defect peripheral to casing; low relevance and severity, held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without rigorous acceptance testing, marginal components get wrongly cleared for reuse fairly often. Verifying serviceability of recovered components adds inspection time to the recovery workflow. Judging whether a recovered component is truly reusable requires technical inspection skill that is unevenly applied. Accepting nonserviceable parts for reuse causes downstream failures and waste more costly than the part itself.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recovered components lacking clear identity or condition records after removal from the retired warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Missing identity/condition records on recovered components is a decommissioning-recordkeeping error, peripheral to casing (low relevance) with modest traceability consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Component records are frequently thin or missing after teardown, so identity/condition traceability is unreliable. Documenting recovered-component identity at removal adds time that is often skipped under schedule pressure. Accurate condition and identity recording needs staff who know what attributes matter for later reuse. Unidentified components can't be resold or reused efficiently, eroding recovery value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recyclable caser materials diverted to general waste because separated material categories are remixed during staging or transport
rows.imp_rat: Recyclable materials remixed during staging is an end-of-life sustainability waste with peripheral relevance and mild consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled bins and segregated staging are standard, but remixing during transport still diverts recyclables at a moderate rate. Keeping separated streams from remixing requires disciplined staging that adds handling time. Segregation is a low-skill task but depends on consistent worker attention rather than expertise. Remixed materials get downgraded to general waste, losing recycling value and incurring disposal fees.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of caser-material loads lacking required weight, destination, or recovery records
rows.plain: Make sure every load of machine material sent for recycling has the required weight, destination, and recovery information. These records show where the material went and what happened to it.
rows.imp_rat: Missing weight, destination, or recovery records on caser-material loads is a recordkeeping defect that is peripheral to the core casing job (relevance low), and the batch ceiling caps consequences here regardless of downstream reporting friction (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Manifest and weigh-ticket systems make these loads mostly well documented, with lapses mainly on ad-hoc loads. Weight, destination and recovery capture is a standard weigh-and-log step that adds little time. Scale readings and destination logging need minimal skill once the process is set up. Missing recovery records forgo material-recovery credits and invite compliance penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned interruption to adjacent material handling during caser decommissioning
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected interruptions to nearby material-handling work while the case-packing machine is being taken out of service. This helps nearby movement of materials continue as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interruption to adjacent material handling during decommissioning is a rare end-of-life event with only local disruption; both its centrality to ongoing casing and its consequences are low, consistent with the batch-4 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Unplanned interruptions to neighboring handling do occur but are usually contained by standard staging plans. Adjacent material-handling interruptions are typically short and managed through routing workarounds during decommissioning. Rerouting nearby material handling is routine logistics work requiring no special expertise. Brief adjacent interruptions carry modest cost and are easily absorbed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of decommissioning records that conflict with the caser's physical status
rows.plain: Make sure the written records match the actual condition of the machine that packs cases. This prevents confusion about whether the machine was really taken out of service as recorded.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning records conflicting with physical status is an end-of-life reconciliation issue, peripheral to active casing; capped at 1 for both.
rows.sat_rat: Records and physical status routinely drift because updates are event-driven and not systematically enforced at disposal. Reconciling records against physical status is a slow manual audit that often lags the actual decommissioning event. It takes someone who understands both the asset register and the physical yard to spot conflicts, and that dual knowledge is scarce. Stale records tie up ghost assets and waste reconciliation labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of stored caser data overwritten or deleted before extraction
rows.plain: Avoid losing or replacing important information stored in the case-packing machine before it is copied out. This ensures the needed operating records are available for use.
rows.imp_rat: Overwriting stored caser data before extraction risks data loss, but the batch ceiling requires relevance and severity to remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without an enforced extract-then-wipe gate, premature overwrites still occur with moderate frequency. Extracting data before overwrite adds a step that is easy to skip under schedule pressure. Ordering extraction before purge is procedurally simple but relies on disciplined sequencing. Overwritten data lost before extraction may be irrecoverable, forcing costly reconstruction or lost value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of exported caser files that are corrupted or unreadable
rows.plain: Reduce the share of exported files from the box-packing machine that are damaged or cannot be opened. This helps preserve the machine's operating information when it is moved or saved elsewhere.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted or unreadable exported files undermine data usability, but the batch ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Export tooling is generally stable with checksums, so file corruption is uncommon but not eliminated. Re-exporting corrupted files costs some rework time but is usually feasible while the unit is still powered. Detecting and re-running a bad export is routine technician work. Corrupted exports mainly cost re-handling effort rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operational, diagnostic, or configuration datasets omitted from the export
rows.plain: Include all needed operating, fault-finding, and setup information in the exported data, so people have the complete information they need for later use.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted datasets from export reduce diagnostic completeness, but ranking discipline forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Export scope is rarely governed by a validated checklist, so omissions of less-obvious datasets recur. Discovering omitted datasets after the unit is gone forces slow or impossible retrieval. Knowing the full set of operational, diagnostic, and config datasets to export requires system familiarity that is often incomplete. Missing datasets can force expensive reconstruction or reduce redeployment value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the administrator is denied access to stored caser data
rows.plain: The administrator should be able to access the saved case information when exporting it. This matters so the needed information can be retrieved and shared.
rows.imp_rat: Denied administrator access to stored data impedes management tasks, but this batch's ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Credential management is imperfect at end-of-life, but vendor recovery paths usually restore access eventually. Recovering administrator access when locked out can cause hours of delay awaiting vendor support. Denied admin access usually stems from lost credentials or vendor-locked systems, requiring scarce specialist intervention. Vendor unlock or password-recovery services carry fees but are bounded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that caser shutdown interrupts an export in progress
rows.plain: Make sure shutting down the equipment does not stop the copying of operating information before it is finished. Otherwise, some information may be lost or incomplete.
rows.imp_rat: A shutdown interrupting an export in progress is disruptive, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, so relevance and severity remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard shutdown checklists usually catch in-progress exports, though occasional overlooked jobs still get interrupted. Coordinating shutdown around export completion adds only brief scheduling delay. Operators generally know to quiesce active jobs before powering down, a familiar routine. An interrupted export costs re-run material and effort but is usually recoverable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that credentials, network configurations, operator profiles, or facility information remain recoverable after the purge
rows.plain: Make sure passwords, connection details, worker accounts, and facility information cannot be recovered after the saved information is erased.
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable credentials or facility data after a purge is a data-security concern at disposal, not core to casing; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitization standards exist but are inconsistently applied to embedded machine controllers, so recoverable residue is a real risk. Certified wipe or media destruction adds bounded, predictable time to disposal. Proper sanitization to a recoverable-proof standard requires security expertise that plant disposal crews often lack. Certified destruction or verified wipe tools cost money but are modest against breach liability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of regulated production or audit data unintentionally deleted during the purge
rows.plain: Keep required production and inspection records from being accidentally erased when stored information is removed. This preserves information that may be needed to prove what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Unintentional deletion of regulated production or audit data during purge carries compliance stakes, but the batch ceiling caps this below prior items, so both relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Purge scoping is often coarse, so unintended deletion of audit-required data is a recurring, weakly guarded risk. Distinguishing retain-versus-purge data during the wipe slows the operation. Separating regulated production/audit records from purgeable data requires domain and compliance judgement that is scarce. Accidentally deleting regulated data can trigger fines and reconstruction costs far exceeding the disposal budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control logic or validated machine parameters are altered while removing the targeted information
rows.plain: Make sure removing the selected information does not change the machine’s operating instructions or approved settings.
rows.imp_rat: Altering validated control logic while removing information could disrupt production, but the ceiling forces both proxies to 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Read/erase operations sometimes touch protected parameter areas, but access controls on validated logic offer partial protection. Working carefully around validated parameters slows the information-removal task slightly. Isolating targeted data without touching control logic requires controls expertise that is not always present at disposal. Altered validated parameters can force costly revalidation if the unit is redeployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that removed information reappears from connected or retained data stores before transfer or disposal
rows.plain: Make sure deleted information cannot come back from connected devices or saved copies before it is transferred or destroyed. This protects the information from being accessed after removal.
rows.imp_rat: Removed information reappearing from connected stores is a data-sanitization concern at end of life, remote from casing; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Backups and connected historians routinely retain copies that reappear after a local purge, a poorly controlled and common failure. Tracing and purging connected/retained data stores extends the purge timeline unpredictably. Mapping every backup, historian, and connected store that mirrors caser data demands deep data-architecture knowledge rarely on hand. Missed stores rarely add direct cost until data resurfaces, but remediation then is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer or disposal delay caused by inability to confirm purge completion
rows.plain: Shorten the delay in transferring or disposing of equipment when no one can yet confirm that leftover material has been fully removed. This helps the equipment move on or be discarded safely.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer/disposal delay from inability to confirm purge completion is a downstream housekeeping issue peripheral to the caser's central function, so relevance is minimal. The impact of the delay is minor and reversible, keeping severity low. The batch-5 ceiling caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established purge-verification procedures and certificates make confirmation reasonably dependable, with only occasional gaps causing genuine transfer delays. Waiting to confirm data purge before transfer or disposal creates real hold-ups, though standard IT decommissioning checklists usually clear this within a defined window. Confirming purge completion requires basic IT decommissioning competence that is fairly widely available in most operations. The confirmation step consumes some technician time and occasionally holds inventory, but the resource cost is contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of undocumented utility or facility connections discovered during caser uninstallation
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented utility connections found during uninstallation is a decommissioning-documentation error with little bearing on the casing job itself and only administrative consequences, so both proxies are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: As-built drawings for aging installations are notoriously inaccurate, so undocumented connections surface frequently and unpredictably. Discovering undocumented connections mid-uninstall stops work while they are traced and resolved. Recognizing and safely handling surprise connections demands experienced facilities staff on the spot. Unplanned discoveries trigger change orders and rework that inflate disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that final disposition documentation is linked to the wrong warehouse caser
rows.plain: Make sure the final paperwork is connected to the correct box-packing machine, so there is no confusion about which machine it describes.
rows.imp_rat: Final disposition documentation linked to the wrong caser is a recordkeeping error, not central to casing; capped below prior batch, both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/asset-tag scanning where deployed makes matches largely reliable, but manual entry and duplicate tags still cause recurring mismatches. Linking disposition docs to an asset ID is a quick lookup step, so time is rarely the bottleneck. Matching a physical caser to its record demands attentive clerical judgement that untrained staff frequently get wrong during rushed disposal. Correcting mislinked documentation later is cheap in dollars but the rework is the main cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the documented end-of-life state and the warehouse caser's actual state
rows.plain: Make sure the recorded end-of-life condition matches the actual condition of the machine that packs products into cases in the warehouse. This prevents incorrect records about what happened to the machine.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between documented and actual end-of-life state is a decommissioning accuracy issue outside core casing; held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a formal end-of-life verification checklist, documented and actual states diverge frequently and inconsistently. Verifying documented end-of-life state against actual state adds inspection time that is often skipped under pressure. Judging true end-of-life condition requires technical familiarity with the caser that generalist disposal crews lack. Discrepancies force re-inspections and disputed disposals that consume extra resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance events omitted from the warehouse caser's maintenance history summary
rows.plain: Make sure the final summary includes every maintenance event. A complete summary helps people understand the machine’s full service history when it leaves the warehouse.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted maintenance events from the history summary is a record-completeness defect peripheral to the casing job (relevance low), with consequences bounded by the batch-5 ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: CMMS-based histories are fairly complete, though undocumented or paper-era events still slip through. Pulling a maintenance history summary is fast where a CMMS exists. Reading maintenance logs is routine, but reconstructing missing events needs experienced interpretation. Omitted maintenance events mainly cost credibility and downstream rework rather than direct dollars.
