rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the warehouse caser's recorded disposition path lacks confirmed authorization
rows.plain: Make sure the warehouse worker’s recorded final handling decision has been properly approved. This helps ensure the item is handled in the authorized way.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition path lacking confirmed authorization is a records/governance concern outside core casing operation; the ceiling keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Asset-disposal workflows increasingly require documented authorization, though records sometimes still lack confirmed sign-off. Obtaining disposition authorization sign-off can add administrative delay to closure. Recording an authorized disposition path is clerical work needing only knowledge of the approval chain. Unauthorized disposition risks compliance penalties but has little direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in completing the warehouse caser's final disposition record
rows.plain: Complete the warehouse equipment’s final record without unnecessary waiting. This provides a clear record of what was done with it.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in completing the final disposition record is a peripheral administrative step for warehouse casers; the core casing job largely succeeds regardless, so relevance is minimal. Consequences of a late record are trivial and easily absorbed, keeping severity low. The batch-5 ceiling caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Records do eventually get completed under existing asset-management practice, but the timing is inconsistent and dependent on manual prompting. Final disposition records are commonly finished only after all vendor confirmations trickle in, so the record often sits open for weeks past the physical disposal. Closing the record correctly needs someone familiar with asset registers and disposal categorization, and their limited availability drags out completion. The administrative effort to keep records open and later finalize them wastes modest labor but rarely large sums.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of caser functions that cannot be verified under safe operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable caser functions under safe conditions is an unreliability error that, while it sounds operationally relevant, is ranked below the prior batch and thus capped (relevance and severity both held at the ceiling).
rows.sat_rat: Many functions simply can't be verified once the caser is partly decommissioned, leaving gaps. Verifying caser functions safely takes setup and test time that disposal timelines squeeze. Safe functional verification requires trained technicians who understand caser operation and lockout procedures. Test rigs and safe environments cost money that disposal budgets underfund.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the caser's condition caused by missing or conflicting maintenance and modification records
rows.plain: Make sure missing or contradictory records do not leave people unsure about the casing machine’s condition, including its upkeep and past changes.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty from missing maintenance/modification records affects condition assessment but is tangential to the casing job for this batch, kept at 1.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance and modification records for older casers are frequently missing or conflicting, so condition uncertainty here is a chronic, largely unmitigated pain. Chasing missing maintenance and modification records causes significant delays in condition assessment. Reconstructing an asset's true state from incomplete records demands experienced investigators who can infer from fragmentary evidence. Record gaps force conservative, costly assumptions or physical re-inspection to fill the void.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in applying disposition criteria to the observed caser condition
rows.plain: Use clear and consistent rules when deciding what to do based on the observed condition of the caser. This helps people reach the same decision when they inspect it.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in applying disposition criteria to caser condition is an inspection-judgment issue of limited centrality and limited harm, kept at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Disposition criteria are often qualitative, so different assessors reach different conclusions on the same caser, leaving real ambiguity. Ambiguous disposition decisions cause back-and-forth reviews that delay the disposal call. Applying disposition criteria to observed condition relies heavily on expert engineering judgement, and inconsistent judgement is the core problem. Wrong disposition calls waste money either by scrapping reusable assets or reworking unsound ones.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the responsible provider rejects the warehouse caser for noncompliance with applicable return terms
rows.plain: The provider should accept the machine when it is returned. This means the machine must meet the provider’s return rules.
rows.imp_rat: Provider rejecting a caser for return-terms noncompliance is an end-of-lease/return error, remote from casing operation (low relevance) with contained financial consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Return terms vary by provider and are frequently misread, so noncompliance rejections remain a recurring, partly-mitigated failure. Verifying return-term compliance before shipment adds review time that is often compressed near disposal deadlines. Interpreting provider-specific return terms demands attentive reading of contracts that disposal staff seldom specialize in. A rejected return triggers return-shipping, restocking, and re-handling costs that erode recovered value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to sterile fill-finish activities while the warehouse caser is isolated and removed
rows.plain: Spend less time stopping sterile filling and packaging work while the case-packing machine is separated and sent back to its supplier. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to sterile fill-finish during isolation and removal concerns adjacent processes rather than casing (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1 despite sterility exposure.
rows.sat_rat: The duration is unpredictable because isolation and cleanup contingencies frequently surface, so this remains poorly served. Isolating and removing the caser without prolonging sterile-line downtime is hard, and isolation steps routinely extend the outage. Clean isolation and extraction adjacent to aseptic operations needs scarce specialized coordination between engineering and QA. Every hour of sterile fill-finish disruption carries very high opportunity cost, driving tight but strained scheduling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the warehouse caser remains at the purchaser's site after it is ready for return because provider handoff is delayed
rows.imp_rat: Caser lingering at the purchaser's site due to handoff delay is a logistics-timing error only marginally tied to the CFJ (relevance low), with consequences bounded by the mandated ceiling (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Handoff timing depends on external providers, making delays common and hard to control. Provider handoff delays leave the caser sitting on site, and this waiting is the core error. Coordinating handoff logistics is routine scheduling work needing little specialized skill. Idle floor space and storage while awaiting pickup carry a real holding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of purchase and ownership cost elements omitted from the warehouse caser evaluation
rows.plain: Fewer buying and ownership costs should be left out when evaluating the machine that packs cases in the warehouse. This gives a more complete picture of what the machine will really cost.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting cost elements from the evaluation is a procurement-analysis defect unrelated to executing casing and only financial in consequence, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a standardized cost-element checklist, evaluations vary in completeness and omissions recur across buyers. Enumerating every purchase and ownership cost element for the evaluation lengthens the analysis phase. Distinguishing capitalizable purchase costs from ongoing ownership costs takes financial and technical literacy that is unevenly distributed. Installation, freight, training, and spares are frequently excluded from the evaluation, producing budget surprises after commitment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in scope, currency, tax, and payment assumptions across warehouse caser proposals
rows.plain: Make sure all proposals for the box-packing machine use the same assumptions about what is included, prices, taxes, and payment terms.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scope/currency/tax assumptions across proposals is a procurement-comparison error, marginal to the casing job (low relevance) with correctable commercial consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers quote on differing bases with no enforced template, so inconsistency across proposals is common and hard to eliminate. Normalizing scope, currency, tax, and payment assumptions across proposals is a manual effort that slows side-by-side comparison. Aligning heterogeneous proposals demands commercial judgement to interpret differing assumptions, which is a scarce buyer skill. Inconsistent tax and currency assumptions distort comparisons and can lead to selecting a truly costlier option.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that available financing terms conflict with the purchaser's budget, cash-flow, or approval constraints
rows.plain: Make sure the payment options fit the buyer’s budget, available cash, and approval rules. A payment plan that does not fit could prevent the purchase from being approved or paid for.
rows.imp_rat: Financing terms conflicting with budget or approval constraints is a procurement/administrative matter (low relevance to actually casing) with absorbable consequences (low severity). Held at the 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Finance review usually catches gross conflicts, but the fit between offered terms and internal constraints is often imperfect. Reconciling financing terms with budget and cash-flow constraints takes iteration with finance and the supplier. Aligning financing structures to approval and cash-flow limits needs financial-analysis skill not always in procurement. Mismatched financing directly affects cash outflow and total borrowing cost, the core money impact here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quoted prices or financing terms expire before the warehouse caser purchasing decision
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the quoted price or payment plan ends before a decision is made about buying the box-making machine. This helps keep the offer available while the purchase is being considered.
rows.imp_rat: Quoted prices/financing expiring before the purchase decision is a commercial-timing error, marginal to casing (low relevance) with re-negotiable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validity periods are stated in most quotes, so lapses are foreseeable, but tight windows still catch buyers out. The core risk is the decision cycle outrunning the quote's validity window, and approval timelines are often slow relative to price validity. Tracking expiry dates is straightforward clerical work needing little expertise. Expired pricing or financing forces re-quoting at potentially higher cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time to obtain complete, decision-ready cost and financing information for the warehouse caser
rows.imp_rat: Delay in obtaining decision-ready cost and financing information is administrative and peripheral to the physical casing job; both proxies stay at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Information arrives piecemeal and in inconsistent formats, so decision-ready completeness is achieved slowly and unevenly. Getting complete, decision-ready cost and financing information typically requires slow supplier back-and-forth that drags out the buy. Assembling decision-ready packages needs someone who knows what data to demand from suppliers. Delay in complete information can push into worse pricing or rushed decisions, but the gathering itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of supplier references or reviews with undisclosed commercial conflicts
rows.imp_rat: Undisclosed commercial conflicts in supplier references is a procurement-diligence concern remote from running casers; relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard practice to surface reference conflicts, so biased references pass through undetected regularly. Vetting references for hidden conflicts adds diligence time that is often skipped. Detecting undisclosed commercial conflicts requires investigative diligence skill that most buyers lack. Undetected conflicts skew supplier selection, risking poor-value purchases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of waiting for responses from supplier references
rows.plain: Reduce the time spent waiting for answers from people who have worked with the supplier. This helps the team evaluate the supplier sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting on supplier reference responses is a procurement-vetting delay far removed from the core functional job (relevance low), and its impact is minor and below-batch-ranked (severity capped).
rows.sat_rat: Reference responsiveness is entirely outside the buyer's control, so response timing is chronically uncertain. Waiting on supplier references to respond is a slow, unpredictable process that stalls diligence. Little skill is required, just persistent follow-up. Reference delays cost mainly opportunity time, not money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among reference accounts of Setzpacker reliability in sterile fill-finish use
rows.plain: Get consistent information from other customers about how dependable the packing machine has been when used to prepare sterile products.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among reference accounts of Setzpacker reliability is a pre-purchase information gap, not core to operating; capped at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Reference accounts for this narrow application are few and their accounts conflict, so consistent reliability evidence is rarely obtainable. Chasing reference accounts for Setzpacker reliability in sterile fill-finish takes weeks of outreach and often stalls. Interpreting whether references are comparable to sterile fill-finish use requires deep application expertise. Reference-checking is low direct cost but poor references risk an expensive wrong purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reference feedback lacking specific evidence about equipment failures and unplanned downtime
rows.imp_rat: Reference feedback lacking evidence about failures is a diligence data-quality issue peripheral to casing; held at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: References typically give generic praise without hard downtime data, so evidence-backed feedback is chronically lacking. Extracting specific failure and downtime evidence from references requires prolonged, often fruitless follow-up. Getting references to disclose specific failures takes skilled, probing interviewing that most buyers do not perform. Vague feedback is cheap to collect but leaves failure risk uncosted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approval is blocked by missing validation, quality, or regulatory evidence for the warehouse caser
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase approval includes all required evidence that the warehouse case-packing machine works properly, meets quality requirements, and follows applicable rules. Missing proof could block approval.
rows.imp_rat: Approval blocked by missing validation evidence is a procurement/compliance gating error, not the caser's operating job (low relevance), and merely delays acquisition (low severity); ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Missing validation or regulatory evidence frequently blocks approvals in regulated settings, and standard gate reviews only partly prevent it. Gathering validation, quality, and regulatory evidence for approval is a lengthy effort that often gates the timeline. Determining sufficiency of regulatory evidence requires scarce quality and validation experts. A blocked approval delays the whole project at real cost, though assembling evidence is mainly labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that allocated capital funding is unavailable or insufficient when the warehouse caser purchase reaches approval
rows.plain: Make sure enough approved money is available when the warehouse case-packing machine reaches the purchase-approval stage. This prevents the purchase from being delayed or stopped for lack of funds.
rows.imp_rat: Capital funding availability at approval is a procurement-finance concern, peripheral to the caser physically casing product (low relevance), and a funding shortfall merely delays purchase rather than causing operational harm (low severity). Ranking caps this below batch 3.
rows.sat_rat: Formal capital approval processes make funding surprises relatively uncommon, though reprioritization and estimate drift still occasionally leave shortfalls. Confirming funding availability at approval follows established budget-cycle timing, though funding gaps can delay purchase. Capital planning is a standard finance competence, requiring little specialized equipment expertise. The whole point is ensuring allocated capital is actually available and sufficient at approval, which mature capital-budgeting processes generally handle.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required procurement and management decisions on the warehouse caser purchase
rows.plain: Get the purchasing and management decisions needed to buy the box-packing machine as soon as possible. This prevents the purchase from being held up inside the company.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for procurement decisions is a delay peripheral to the caser's functional job; relevance and severity fixed at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Approval SLAs are frequently unenforced, so decision timing is unpredictable and slips are common. Waiting on procurement and management decisions is a well-known, often lengthy bottleneck for capital equipment purchases. Building a persuasive decision package to speed approval takes practiced procurement skill that not all requesters have. Decision delay ties up nothing material but can push the purchase into worse pricing windows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of approval-stage reroutings caused by unclear authority for the warehouse caser purchase
rows.plain: Make sure it is clear who has the authority to approve the warehouse equipment purchase. This prevents the request from being sent repeatedly to the wrong person or group.
rows.imp_rat: Reroutings from unclear purchase authority is an administrative procurement error, peripheral to caser operation; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delegation-of-authority matrices exist in most organizations and catch many cases, though ambiguity at thresholds still causes reroutes. Rerouting an approval because authority was unclear adds delay each time it happens. Knowing who actually holds sign-off authority for a capital caser purchase relies on institutional knowledge that is uneven across buyers. Misrouted approvals waste staff effort but rarely add hard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of approval stages repeated after changes to the warehouse caser scope, pricing, or commercial terms
rows.plain: Avoid repeating approval steps after changes to the warehouse equipment purchase, its price, or its business terms. This saves time while still allowing the updated purchase to be properly approved.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated approval stages after commercial-term changes is a procurement rework issue, tangential to the caser's core job; relevance and severity held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow tools track approvals, but change-triggered re-approval rules are inconsistently applied, so redundant re-loops recur. Every scope, price, or terms change typically forces a re-loop through approvals, adding days to weeks. Judging which changes truly require re-approval versus a minor amendment needs procurement-policy familiarity that varies by person. Repeated approval cycles consume staff hours but little direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contract terms concerning acceptance criteria and validation responsibilities for the warehouse casers
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contract terms on acceptance/validation are a procurement-negotiation error, not the operating job (low relevance), and resolvable in contracting (low severity); ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Contract templates cover many terms, but acceptance and validation responsibilities are often left ambiguous, so unresolved terms remain a recurring source of disputes. Negotiating acceptance-criteria and validation-responsibility terms adds contracting cycles that can extend closing. Drafting clear acceptance and validation clauses needs experienced contract and quality staff working together. Ambiguous terms lead to costly disputes over responsibility later, while negotiating them is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the purchase transaction delay caused by outstanding purchaser approvals and signatures
rows.plain: Shorten the delay in completing the purchase while the buyer is still getting required approvals and signatures. This helps the sale be finished sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay from outstanding purchaser signatures is a commercial-process issue distant from caser operation; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: E-signature and workflow platforms are mature and widely deployed, reliably moving approvals though signers' availability still causes occasional holds. Outstanding signatures still stall transactions, though e-signature routing has meaningfully shortened these delays. Little specialized skill is needed once the signature workflow is set up. Signature delay adds no direct cost beyond the opportunity cost of waiting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which the final warehouse caser purchase commitment exceeds the authorized project budget
rows.plain: Keep the final commitment to buy the warehouse case-packing equipment within the approved project budget. This prevents the purchase from costing more than allowed.
rows.imp_rat: Purchase commitment exceeding budget is a financial-governance error peripheral to casing operations (low relevance) with absorbable consequences (low severity), forced below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Budget-versus-commitment controls are standard, so large overruns are uncommon, though scope additions and change orders still cause modest exceedances. Keeping the commitment within budget is managed within the approval cycle without much added time. Cost estimating and budget control are routine finance and procurement skills. The need is directly about avoiding overspend beyond authorization, which purchase-order controls and quotes generally contain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the final contract and the agreed quotation concerning warehouse caser scope, deliverables, and commercial terms
rows.plain: Make sure the final contract matches the agreed offer for the warehouse packing machine, including what is included, what will be delivered, and the business terms. This helps prevent disagreements later.
rows.imp_rat: Contract-vs-quotation discrepancies on caser scope are an administrative procurement error, peripheral to the casing job itself (low relevance); consequences are commercial rework rather than operational failure (low severity), and the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established contract-review workflows and legal sign-off make gross discrepancies uncommon, though subtle scope mismatches on specialized casers still slip through. Contract-to-quote reconciliation adds days of legal and procurement review, but that time is routinely budgeted into capital acquisitions. Catching scope and commercial-term drift between quote and contract demands experienced procurement or contract counsel who are not always available for a niche capital-equipment buy. Standard redline and clause-mapping practices catch most commercial-term discrepancies before signature, limiting costly post-award disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier offer becomes unavailable before contract execution
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier’s offer is still available when the purchase agreement is signed. This helps prevent the price or terms from changing unexpectedly.
rows.imp_rat: A supplier offer lapsing before contract execution is a procurement-timing error, remote from casing performance (low relevance) with re-quotable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Written offer-hold or reservation commitments are common practice, making sudden unavailability uncommon but not guaranteed. Racing internal approval before an offer lapses pressures the decision timeline. Managing offer availability is a routine procurement-coordination task requiring modest skill. A lapsed offer can force renegotiation at worse terms, though holding an offer usually costs nothing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of spare-part inventory records that differ from physical stock
rows.plain: Make sure the inventory records match the parts that are physically in stock. This prevents people from selling parts that are missing or overlooking parts that are available.
rows.imp_rat: Inventory records differing from physical stock is a purely administrative record-accuracy error (very low relevance to cartoning) with trivial, easily reconciled consequences (very low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cycle counting and barcoding help, but informal 'grab a part' behaviour keeps record-to-physical mismatch a chronic issue in most stores. Record discrepancies waste time hunting for parts that the system says exist but are gone. Accurate transaction discipline depends on every user logging withdrawals correctly, which is a persistent human-behaviour weakness. Inaccurate records cause both duplicate purchasing and unexpected stockouts, wasting money on both ends.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical manufacturing audit findings with unresolved closure or unclear current standing
rows.plain: Avoid having serious findings from manufacturing inspections remain unresolved or with an unclear status. A clear current status shows whether the supplier has addressed the problems.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved audit findings are a compliance-standing ambiguity tangential to cartoning; the ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Audit findings frequently have ambiguous or unresolved closure status, and tracking systems are inconsistent across suppliers. Chasing the current standing of audit findings can stall qualification while responses are gathered. Interpreting audit findings and their closure status requires quality-engineering judgement that is not always available. Tracking findings is largely administrative and not a significant cost burden.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of financial records used in the supplier review that are outdated or incomplete
rows.plain: Use current and complete financial information when reviewing a supplier, so the supplier can be judged fairly and accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Outdated financial records in a supplier review are administrative and tangential to cartoning function (low relevance) with modest, recoverable consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0 by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Third-party credit reports help, but small or private machinery vendors often have stale or incomplete financials on file. Pulling financial records for supplier review is reasonably quick with credit databases and ERP data. Interpreting supplier financial health requires finance/credit judgement. Outdated records can mask supplier risk, exposing the buyer to costly default.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the qualification review remains stalled while required supplier evidence is unavailable
rows.plain: Checking a supplier should not stop while waiting for documents or other proof from the supplier. This helps confirm the supplier is suitable sooner.
rows.imp_rat: A stalled qualification review awaiting supplier evidence is an administrative delay with minor consequence relative to the core job, and the ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier evidence gaps stalling qualification are a recurring, poorly-controlled bottleneck in typical practice. Qualification reviews routinely stall while waiting on missing supplier evidence, extending timelines unpredictably. Knowing what evidence to demand and follow up requires qualification expertise. The stall itself costs little directly but delays capital deployment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required supplier cost inputs are unavailable before the acquisition review
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier's price information is available before the purchase review. This helps people estimate the equipment's full cost over the time it is used.
rows.imp_rat: Missing supplier cost inputs before review is a low-ranked delay with absorbable impact, held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Total-cost inputs frequently arrive incomplete, so reviews often proceed on partial data. Getting supplier cost inputs in time for review depends on vendor quotation turnaround, which is often slow. Knowing which cost inputs (install, tooling, validation) to demand takes procurement and engineering expertise. Missing cost inputs undermine budget accuracy and can lead to overspend surprises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of acquisition cost excluded from the agreed commercial terms
rows.plain: Leave out as little of the purchase cost as possible from the final agreed buying terms.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition cost excluded from agreed commercial terms is a purely financial-terms waste, peripheral to the job; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Commercial terms often omit ancillary costs, and surprise excluded charges are a common source of budget overrun. Uncovering excluded costs late causes renegotiation that delays deal closure. Identifying total cost of ownership items requires procurement expertise that many buyers lack. Hidden or excluded costs like installation, spares, and freight routinely inflate acquisition spend beyond the agreed figure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of contract value payable before corresponding delivery and acceptance milestones are achieved
rows.plain: Reduce the share of the purchase price that must be paid before the related delivery and approval steps are complete. This limits payment before the buyer receives and accepts what was promised.
rows.imp_rat: Contract value payable before milestones is a commercial-terms error, not central to running cartons; batch ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Milestone payment terms are common in capital equipment contracts and reliably limit advance payment risk when enforced. Negotiating milestone-linked payment schedules is a routine part of contract drafting that rarely extends timelines. Procurement and finance teams routinely structure progress payments tied to acceptance, a well-understood commercial skill. Standard practice ties payment tranches to delivery and acceptance milestones, directly protecting cash exposure.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an otherwise viable machinery option becomes unavailable before the purchaser completes the evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure a suitable used or refurbished machine is still available before the evaluation is finished. This prevents losing an option that could have met the need.
rows.imp_rat: A viable option becoming unavailable during evaluation is peripheral with modest consequence, held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Cartoning machinery is typically built-to-order rather than off-the-shelf, so outright unavailability during evaluation is relatively rare though lead times shift. Evaluation periods for capital machinery are long, increasing exposure to lead-time or availability shifts. Judging which option remains viable requires experienced evaluators. Losing a preferred option can force a costlier alternative.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, operations planner, logistics coordinator
rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of gaps in manufacturing and shipment status visibility
rows.plain: Avoid long periods when no one can see the machine’s manufacturing or shipping progress. This helps the team know where things stand and respond to delays.
rows.imp_rat: Gaps in status visibility are a logistics-monitoring nuisance, not central to machinery operation, and their consequences are limited. Score constrained to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because most OEMs lack live buyer-facing tracking portals, status arrives in sporadic manual bursts, leaving unpredictable and largely unmanaged gaps. The error itself is the length of the blind periods between vendor updates, which for custom cartoning equipment can stretch weeks with no visibility. Closing a visibility gap usually means an expeditor personally chasing the OEM by phone and email, a labor-intensive relationship skill rather than a systematic feed. Extended dark periods force conservative buffer scheduling and standby resources, quietly inflating carrying and coordination cost.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, operations planner, logistics coordinator
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a machinery delay becoming known and its notification to the facility
rows.plain: Tell the facility as soon as a delay in the machinery shipment becomes known. Early notice helps the facility prepare for the change.
rows.imp_rat: Time between a delay becoming known and its notification is a communication-latency issue tangential to the core job (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity); ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated status feeds, notification depends on someone remembering to relay it, so it is inconsistent. The lag between a delay becoming known and notifying the facility is often long because updates travel manually through the chain. Relaying a delay notice needs little skill, just prompt communication. Late notification wastes prepared labor and staging cost at the facility.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, operations planner, logistics coordinator
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reported manufacturing and shipment milestones that are inaccurate
rows.plain: Keep reports about when machines are made and shipped accurate, so people can trust the schedule and make the right plans.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate manufacturing/shipment milestone reporting is an administrative tracking error with low centrality to the cartoning function and easily-absorbed consequences. Held at 1.0 by the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cartoning machinery is largely built-to-order by OEMs whose self-reported build and ship milestones are notoriously optimistic and manually updated, so inaccurate milestones are a chronic, poorly-mitigated pain for the buyer. Milestone reports arrive on the vendor's cadence, but the concern here is not speed so much as whether the dates given actually hold. Interpreting whether a reported milestone is genuine or optimistic requires an experienced procurement or project engineer who knows the OEM's habits, and that judgement is scarce. Inaccurate milestones trigger costly downstream rescheduling of installation crews, line downtime and expedited freight, so the money exposure is real but indirect.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, operations planner, logistics coordinator
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies in estimated arrival information from involved parties
rows.plain: Make sure the expected arrival times reported by everyone involved agree with one another. This makes it easier to plan for the machinery's delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies in estimated arrival info are peripheral coordination noise relative to the core cartoning-machinery job; relevance is low and severity of an ETA mismatch is minor. Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple parties reporting different ETAs with no single source of truth make discrepancies routine and largely unresolved. Chasing down conflicting ETAs from carriers, riggers and vendors consumes coordinator time. Reconciling contradictory arrival estimates requires an attentive coordinator to arbitrate sources. ETA discrepancies cause misstaged resources and wasted standby cost.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, operations planner, logistics coordinator
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between machinery arrival at the facility and confirmation of delivery receipt
rows.plain: Confirm that the machinery was delivered as soon as it arrives at the facility. This provides a clear record that the delivery was received.
rows.imp_rat: The lag between arrival and delivery confirmation is a clerical receipt step, low relevance to the core job and trivial in consequence. Ceiling forces 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Carrier proof-of-delivery and ERP goods-receipt postings make arrival confirmation a well-established, reliably closed loop even if occasional paperwork delays occur. Confirming receipt of a delivered machine is a same-day dock and receiving activity with mature freight and warehouse practice, so the lag is typically short. Standard receiving-dock staff and inspection checklists handle machinery arrival confirmation without specialized expertise. Delivery confirmation uses existing receiving infrastructure and carrier POD systems, so incremental cost is minimal.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of packaging debris or transit contaminants entering controlled facility areas during the delivery inspection
rows.plain: Keep packing pieces and dirt from delivery out of protected facility areas during the inspection. This helps prevent the facility from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Debris entering controlled areas during delivery inspection has some contamination severity, but the ranking caps this item at 1 for both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Established GMP receiving practices exist but transit debris still occasionally breaches controlled areas when staging discipline lapses. Cleaning and de-boxing at a staging airlock adds only modest time to the receiving process. Facility receiving staff generally know contamination-control protocols but relies on consistent discipline rather than deep expertise. Dedicated uncrating areas and cleaning supplies are inexpensive relative to overall acquisition cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time competing-machine data remains unavailable beyond the evaluation deadline
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for information about other machines when that information is needed for an evaluation. This allows the machines to be compared before the evaluation deadline.
rows.imp_rat: Competing-machine data unavailable past the evaluation deadline is a research-timing issue peripheral to running cartoning machinery (low relevance), delaying evaluation only (low severity). Ceiling 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Data-gathering practices exist but timeliness is inconsistent and delays past deadlines still happen. Competing-machine data often trickles in slowly and can lag past evaluation deadlines. Sourcing competitor data requires research skill but is not the primary bottleneck. Acquiring competitive data is generally low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cartoning machinery scope omitted from the formal quotation
rows.plain: Include every required part of the packaging machine work in the formal price offer, so the buyer knows exactly what is included.
rows.imp_rat: Scope omitted from a formal quotation is a commercial-completeness defect, not core to cartoning, and capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard RFQ practices reduce omissions, but incomplete quotations with missing scope remain a frequent recurring issue. Scope gaps surface late and force requote cycles that extend the acquisition timeline. Building a complete scope statement demands experienced specification writers who know cartoning line requirements. Omitted scope leads to change orders and unbudgeted add-ons that inflate final cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from submission of complete requirements to receipt of the formal quotation
rows.plain: Spend less time between sending all the needed information and receiving the official price offer. This helps the customer decide sooner whether to proceed.
rows.imp_rat: Time from complete requirements to formal quotation is a minor timing error relative to the CFJ, held at the floor by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quote turnaround varies widely by vendor with no enforceable service level, making timing unpredictable. Formal quotations for configured cartoning machinery commonly take one to several weeks even after complete requirements are submitted. Little buyer skill affects the vendor's internal quoting speed once requirements are clear. Quotation delay carries little direct cost but extends the acquisition timeline.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of approval submissions returned because required compliance and purchase documentation is missing
rows.plain: Make sure each approval request includes all required proof of rule compliance and purchase documents. This helps prevent the request from being sent back for missing information.
rows.imp_rat: Approval submissions returned for missing documentation is administrative rework, low relevance to the job, and capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and templates help, but incomplete compliance packages returned for rework remain a common recurring problem. Missing documentation triggers resubmission cycles that add days or weeks to approval flows. Assembling complete compliance packages demands knowledge of many document requirements that varies across staff. Rework of submissions consumes staff hours but is not a major direct cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required internal approvals for the cartoning machinery purchase
rows.plain: Obtain the needed internal approvals for buying the packaging equipment without a long wait. This helps the purchase move forward on time.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting on internal approvals for the purchase is a low-stakes administrative delay, capped at 1.0 per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Approval routing tools give some visibility, yet bottlenecks and out-of-office approvers make timing unpredictable. Internal capital-approval workflows for machinery routinely stall in multi-level sign-off queues, and even with ERP approval routing they frequently wait days to weeks for busy approvers. Little specialized skill is needed to move an approval along, but knowing how to escalate and package a justification does require procurement savvy. Delays tie up no direct money but risk holding a budgeted purchase, an indirect opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transaction delay caused by outstanding capital, legal, quality, or procurement approvals
rows.plain: Shorten the purchase delay while money, legal, quality, or purchasing approvals are still outstanding. This helps the transaction finish sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Transaction delays from outstanding capital, legal, quality, or procurement approvals are peripheral to the machinery's functional job (low relevance) and produce recoverable schedule impacts (low severity). Ranking caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sequential multi-gate approval processes are notoriously slow and stalls are a chronic, largely unmitigated pain. Outstanding capital, legal, quality, and procurement approvals routinely stall transactions for extended and unpredictable periods. Navigating multiple approval bodies requires coordination skill but is not the core failure. Delays tie up capital and carrying costs but the direct cost of approving is minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required vendor qualification and payment documents missing at transaction execution
rows.plain: Reduce the number of supplier approval and payment documents missing when a purchase is completed. This helps finish the transaction correctly and avoid delays or disputes.
rows.imp_rat: Missing vendor qualification or payment documents at execution is a minor documentation error relative to the core job, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard vendor-onboarding checklists and ERP holds catch most missing documents, though gaps still slip through for unfamiliar or foreign suppliers. Assembling vendor qualification and payment documents is time-consuming but not the primary failure mode addressed here. Procurement clerks generally know the required document set, though onboarding a new machinery vendor demands attention to compliance specifics. Missing documents at execution rarely cost money directly but can trigger rework and payment delays.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the vendor offer expiring before both parties execute the purchase documents
rows.plain: Prevent the supplier’s offer from expiring before both sides sign the purchase papers. This helps ensure the planned purchase can be completed.
rows.imp_rat: A vendor offer expiring before execution is a minor timing error relative to the CFJ, held at the floor by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quote validity dates are known but rarely tracked systematically, so lapses happen when approvals drag. Offer expiry is a race against slow internal execution timelines that buyers often barely win. Tracking validity windows requires diligent buyer attention but no deep expertise. An expired offer can force renegotiation at higher prices, a real cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which cartoning machinery registration completion exceeds the manufacturer's warranty deadline
rows.plain: Finish registering the cartoning equipment before the manufacturer’s warranty deadline, rather than after it. This helps protect the equipment’s warranty coverage.
rows.imp_rat: Registration exceeding a warranty deadline is an administrative timing error (low relevance) with modest financial exposure (low severity); capped at 1.0 by the batch order.
rows.sat_rat: Registration is easily overlooked amid installation activity, so deadline overruns recur without automated reminders. Completing registration before the warranty deadline depends on manual follow-through that often lags the purchase. Registration is procedurally simple, needing coordination more than expertise. Missing the warranty window can forfeit valuable coverage, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machinery registration submission is rejected for missing required equipment or site records
rows.plain: The warranty registration should include all required information about the equipment and its location. This helps prevent the supplier from rejecting the registration.
rows.imp_rat: A registration submission rejected for missing records is a rework administrative loop (low relevance) with modest rework cost (low severity); ranking forces 1.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Without a validating checklist tied to the manufacturer's requirements, submissions are rejected for missing records fairly often. Rejection cycles add days of resubmission delay. Avoiding rejection requires knowing the exact required equipment and site record set, which is expertise-dependent. Rejections cost mainly rework time rather than money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of registered serial, configuration, owner, or sterile fill-finish site fields that conflict with accepted equipment records
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment registration details—such as its serial number, setup, owner, and site—match the accepted equipment records. Matching details help ensure warranty support is tied to the correct equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting registration field data is a records-hygiene issue peripheral to running cartoning (low relevance) with low-stakes correction cost (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual re-keying of serial, owner and configuration data across systems routinely produces conflicting records with no automated validation. Reconciling registration fields against equipment records is a manual, slow cross-check. Correctly matching serial, configuration and regulated site fields demands attentive, knowledgeable staff, especially for sterile fill-finish contexts. Field conflicts trigger rework and potential compliance penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unconfirmed warranty and service eligibility for the cartoning machinery after registration submission
rows.plain: Shorten the wait for confirmation that the packaging machine is covered by its warranty and service support after its registration is submitted. This helps the owner know what support is available.
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed warranty eligibility after submission is an administrative delay peripheral to cartoning output (low relevance) with limited impact (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manufacturers rarely provide prompt automated confirmation, leaving eligibility status uncertain for extended periods. Confirmation of warranty and service eligibility after submission can drag while the manufacturer processes registration. Chasing confirmation needs persistence but little specialized skill. Unconfirmed eligibility leaves the buyer exposed to uncovered service costs in the interim.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the purchaser's financing application is rejected during underwriting
rows.plain: Provide the lender with what it needs to approve the financing request. This helps prevent the application from being turned down during the lender's review.
rows.imp_rat: A purchaser's financing rejection during underwriting is a financial-transaction matter unrelated to the cartoning job (low relevance) with contained fallout (low severity); ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established equipment-finance lenders and pre-qualification reduce surprise rejections, though outcomes still hinge on buyer creditworthiness. Underwriting decisions on equipment financing typically take days to a couple of weeks. Preparing a fundable application benefits from finance expertise to preempt underwriter concerns. Rejection can force costlier financing or delay, a real financial hit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that approved financing proceeds remain unavailable after the supplier payment deadline
rows.plain: Make approved loan or funding money available sooner after the supplier’s payment deadline. This helps pay the supplier without a prolonged delay.
rows.imp_rat: Approved financing proceeds delayed past a supplier deadline is a treasury-timing issue peripheral to cartoning (low relevance) with recoverable impact (low severity); ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Funding timelines are reasonably predictable with established lenders, though misalignment with payment deadlines still happens. Gaps between financing disbursement and the supplier payment deadline arise from loan funding lags that are only partly controllable. Coordinating funding timing needs treasury attention but no rare skill. Late funds can incur late-payment penalties or lost early-pay discounts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of total financing cost above the approved machinery acquisition budget
rows.plain: Keep the total cost of borrowing money to buy the machinery within the approved budget. This includes the extra amount paid for financing, not just the amount borrowed.
rows.imp_rat: Financing cost above budget is a procurement-finance overrun tangential to the cartoning function (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity); ranking limits to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Total financing cost is fairly predictable once terms are locked, limiting surprise overages. Comparing financing offers takes some time but is manageable. Optimizing total financing cost requires financial acumen to compare rate structures. Competitive lending markets and rate shopping usually keep financing cost near budget, though overruns from rate movement still occur.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which collateral and covenant obligations exceed the purchaser's approved risk limits
rows.plain: Make sure the required security and loan conditions stay within the buyer’s approved level of financial risk.
rows.imp_rat: Collateral and covenant obligations exceeding risk limits is a financing-risk error unrelated to the cartoning job; the ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established credit-review practices generally keep obligations within risk limits, though complex deals occasionally breach them. Reviewing covenant terms against risk limits is a routine legal step that seldom delays the transaction materially. Legal and treasury specialists routinely assess collateral and covenant obligations against approved limits. Excessive covenant obligations can lock up collateral and raise financing cost if not controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies among the machinery quotation, acquisition scope, and financing documents
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment price offer, what is being purchased, and the financing papers all describe the same deal. This helps prevent disputes about the purchase or payment.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies among quotation, acquisition scope, and financing documents are procurement paperwork issues barely connected to the cartoning function (low relevance), and any consequence is correctable rework (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Documents are often prepared by different parties and inconsistencies among them are a common, poorly-controlled problem. Reconciling discrepancies among quote, scope, and financing docs adds delay before closing. Cross-checking multiple documents for consistency requires careful, expert review that is error-prone. Undetected discrepancies can create cost and financing gaps but the checking itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machinery order is submitted without valid purchasing authorization
rows.plain: The order for the cartoning machine should be submitted only with valid approval to make the purchase. This prevents an unauthorized order from being rejected or causing problems.
rows.imp_rat: Submitting an order without valid purchasing authorization is a lower-ranked control here; the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Modern ERP/purchasing systems enforce authorization matrices well, so unauthorized submissions are uncommon where such controls are deployed. Authorization checks are near-instant when embedded in the purchasing system, adding negligible delay. System-enforced authorization limits reduce dependence on individual judgement about who may approve. Preventing unauthorized orders avoids costly maverick spend and contract disputes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the authorized cartoning machinery order is submitted after the allocation or manufacturing deadline
rows.plain: Send the approved order for the packing machine before the money or manufacturing deadline passes.
rows.imp_rat: Late order submission after a deadline is a procurement-timing issue, peripheral to the cartoning function and with modest fallout. Kept at the mandated 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Capital-equipment PO authorization is a standardized, gated process, so on-time submission is fairly consistent but multi-level capex approvals introduce recurring, unpredictable delays. The error is submitting the authorized order late against allocation or manufacturing slot deadlines, and established PO approval workflows usually get it out on time but internal sign-off chains still cause slippage. Coordinating capital approval, specification finalization and vendor slot booking requires competent procurement staff but relies on routine, known process rather than rare expertise. Missing an OEM production-slot deadline can push delivery by months and incur price escalation, so late submission carries meaningful cost risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machinery supplier does not receive the submitted authorized order
rows.plain: Make sure the machinery supplier actually receives the approved order. This helps prevent delays or confusion about whether the order was placed.
rows.imp_rat: Risk that the supplier never receives the authorized order is a low-ranked reliability issue here; both proxies held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: EDI and email PO confirmations make non-receipt rare, though lack of guaranteed acknowledgement still allows occasional silent failures. Electronic order transmission is essentially instantaneous once submitted. Sending an authorized order requires minimal skill given standard PO systems. A lost order costs little directly but can cascade into schedule and expediting expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant cartoning machinery types, configurations, or suppliers omitted from the market scan
rows.plain: Include all important types, setups, and suppliers of machines that put cartons around products. This helps the buyer compare the full range of available choices.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting relevant machinery types or suppliers from a market scan is a research-coverage shortfall peripheral to cartoning operation (low relevance), affecting only breadth of options (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scans commonly omit relevant configurations or suppliers, leaving blind spots that are hard to detect. Broadening the market scan to capture all types and suppliers adds research effort and time. Comprehensive market scanning requires industry knowledge to know what to include. Additional scanning is inexpensive relative to the machinery purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that market research relies on obsolete specifications or supplier availability information
rows.plain: Use current information about machine features and supplier availability, so the search is not based on outdated information.
rows.imp_rat: Market research relying on obsolete specifications is a research-currency issue peripheral to cartoning operation (low relevance), affecting only decision quality (low severity). Ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Market research often draws on outdated specs or availability, and there is no reliable freshness check in typical practice. Verifying whether specs and availability data are current adds some research time. Judging whether market data is obsolete requires market familiarity that varies among buyers. Refreshing market research is low cost, so this is not a spending concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain critical supplier information for the acquisition project
rows.plain: Spend less time gathering important information from suppliers for a project to buy new equipment. Better information helps the buyer compare possible machines and make a sound choice.
rows.imp_rat: Delay obtaining critical supplier information is a peripheral timing issue with modest consequence, kept at 1.0 by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier data arrives inconsistently and in varied formats, so key details are often incomplete or ambiguous on first pass, forcing repeat requests. Obtaining critical supplier information for capital cartoning equipment still relies on back-and-forth emails, phone calls and vendor sales cycles that routinely stretch across weeks despite online catalogs. Buyers need technical judgement to know which specs to request and how to interpret vendor responses, so information-gathering leans on experienced engineering-procurement staff. The cost of chasing supplier data is mostly staff time rather than direct spend, so it is a modest but recurring drain.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier demonstration capacity is unavailable before the purchase decision deadline
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier can show the machinery working before the deadline for deciding whether to buy it.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable supplier demonstration capacity before a decision deadline is a pre-purchase scheduling issue peripheral to the cartoning function (low relevance), causing a delayed decision only (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers generally provide demo capacity, though occasional unavailability before deadlines still occurs. Scheduling supplier demonstrations ahead of the decision deadline usually works but can slip with tight timelines. Coordinating demo availability is a scheduling task requiring little specialized skill. Demonstrations carry travel and setup costs but these are modest and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that representative product samples are unavailable in the required condition on the demonstration date
rows.plain: Make sure suitable product samples are ready and in the required condition on the day of the demonstration. This allows the machine to be shown using realistic materials.
rows.imp_rat: Representative samples unavailable on the demonstration date is a trial-logistics issue tangential to the cartoning job (low relevance) that merely delays a demo (low severity). Ceiling 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Samples are usually available but occasionally arrive in the wrong format or condition, disrupting demonstrations. Preparing representative samples in required condition is usually done in time but can cause last-minute scrambles. Producing correct product samples for a cartoner demo requires product and packaging know-how. Sample preparation and shipping are minor costs within the acquisition budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restrictions on proprietary or regulated samples prevent delivery to the demonstration site
rows.plain: Make sure rules about restricted or legally controlled samples do not prevent the samples from reaching the demonstration site.
rows.imp_rat: Restrictions preventing sample delivery to a demo site is a logistics/compliance edge case remote from running cartoning machinery (low relevance) causing a delayed demonstration (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Restrictions occasionally block sample delivery to demo sites but surrogate materials are a common reliable workaround. Arranging clearances for regulated samples can add lead time but is usually anticipated. Handling proprietary or regulated sample restrictions requires regulatory and legal awareness. Compliance handling adds minor cost relative to the transaction.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mechanical interface dimensions verified from outdated or incomplete site data
rows.plain: Make sure measurements where the equipment will connect to the production line are checked using current and complete site information. Wrong measurements can prevent the equipment from fitting or working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Interface dimensions from outdated site data is a fit-verification ambiguity of limited centrality, and the batch ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Site data is often outdated or incomplete, and mechanical interface mismatches discovered late are a chronic acquisition problem. Re-measuring or reconciling stale site data delays interface verification during acquisition. Accurate interface verification requires engineering skill to interpret drawings against as-built conditions. Field re-surveys add modest cost but avert far costlier fit-up failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain feedback from reference customers
rows.plain: Get feedback from customers who already use the supplier more quickly. This helps the buyer judge whether the supplier is reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain reference-customer feedback is a pre-purchase due-diligence delay, minimally relevant to the machinery's core job and low in severity. Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: OEMs routinely supply reference lists so feedback is obtainable, but responses are curated toward satisfied customers and arrive inconsistently, limiting how dependable the signal is. Obtaining reference-customer feedback depends on scheduling calls or site visits with busy third parties, so turnaround is slow and variable during vendor evaluation. Knowing which references to ask and what probing questions to pose about cartoning uptime and service requires an experienced evaluator, adding a modest skill dependency. The effort is mostly staff time and occasional travel, a modest and generally accepted cost of due diligence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of collected references that lack concrete evidence about machinery reliability, delivery adherence, or after-sales support
rows.plain: Fewer collected recommendations should be missing specific proof about equipment reliability, on-time delivery, or support after purchase. This helps buyers judge suppliers using real experience.
rows.imp_rat: References lacking concrete evidence is a diligence-quality gap far from cartoning operation (low relevance), with the consequence being weaker decision support only (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Collected references commonly offer only vague impressions rather than concrete evidence, undermining decision confidence. Chasing concrete evidence from vague references extends the qualification period. Extracting hard evidence from references requires skilled, probing questioning many buyers do not apply. Gathering references is inexpensive, so the gap is not a cost issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among references about machinery reliability, delivery adherence, or after-sales support
rows.plain: Make sure customer references give a consistent picture of the machine’s dependability, whether deliveries arrive as promised, and how well the supplier helps after the sale. This helps the buyer judge the supplier’s reliability.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among references about reliability or support is a research-quality issue tangential to running cartoning machinery (low relevance), and its consequence is uncertainty in evaluation only (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: References frequently disagree about reliability, delivery, and support, and there is no standard method to reconcile them. Resolving conflicting reference accounts takes extra follow-up time before a decision. Weighing contradictory reference feedback requires judgement to separate signal from noise. Reference checking is low cost, so disagreement imposes little direct expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury while unloading or positioning the cartoning machinery
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that anyone is hurt while the box-making equipment is unloaded or placed where it will be used.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury during unloading is genuinely severe, but the batch-5 ceiling forces both relevance and severity to 1 regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Mature lifting standards, lift plans, and PPE practices make serious injuries during equipment receipt uncommon though never eliminated. Rigging and installation crews follow established lift plans that add little time and are routine for heavy machinery receipt. Certified riggers and OSHA-trained personnel bring proven expertise to unloading and positioning heavy cartoners. Proper lifting equipment and trained crews cost money but are a standard, budgeted line in machinery acquisition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the machinery or rigging crew waits because freight arrival, rigging availability, and receiving-site readiness are misaligned
rows.plain: Reduce waiting by coordinating the freight arrival, lifting crew, and receiving site so they are ready at the same time. This helps the machinery be delivered and installed without avoidable delays.
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned freight, rigging, and site readiness is an installation-logistics delay peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance) with manageable idle cost (low severity); ranking limits to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With three independent parties and no shared scheduling system, misalignment is common and hard to prevent. Aligning freight arrival, rigging crew and receiving-site readiness is a chronic coordination gap that leaves crews idle or freight waiting. Successful synchronization depends heavily on an experienced logistics coordinator juggling multiple parties. Idle rigging crews and demurrage are directly costly, magnifying the pain.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that rigging activity compromises controlled-area conditions at the sterile fill-finish facility
rows.plain: Make sure moving and securing the machinery does not make the controlled clean area unsafe or unclean.
rows.imp_rat: While rigging-related contamination at a sterile fill-finish facility could be severe, the ranking places this below the prior batch, so both proxies are held at 1 under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Containment protocols exist but rigging in or near sterile zones still poses a real risk of compromising controlled conditions. Coordinating rigging around controlled-area protocols extends move planning time. Protecting cleanroom conditions during rigging requires specialized contamination-control expertise that is not always present. A contamination breach forces costly recleaning and requalification of the controlled area.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among acceptance parties about the safety requirements applicable to the caser
rows.plain: Make sure everyone reviewing the box-packing machine agrees on which safety rules apply. This prevents important safety requirements from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among acceptance parties over safety requirements is an acceptance-alignment ambiguity; the ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without a shared, documented safety spec, acceptance outcomes vary case to case and disagreement recurs. Resolving safety-requirement disputes among acceptance parties drags acceptance out while parties argue applicable standards. Interpreting which machinery-safety codes apply to a caser demands scarce EHS and compliance expertise, and disagreement is frequent when that expertise is thin. Disputed safety scope can force rework, retrofits, or re-inspections that add unbudgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance history records that are missing, illegible, or internally inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Missing or inconsistent maintenance records is a documentation-quality error peripheral to active casing; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance logs are commonly incomplete, illegible, or inconsistent, particularly on secondary-market casers, so this need is largely unserved. Obtaining and combing through maintenance history, especially for used units, is slow and frequently dead-ends. Judging whether maintenance records are complete and internally consistent needs a reliability engineer's eye. Missing records are cheap to request but leave hidden refurbishment or failure costs unknown.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed modifications absent from the warehouse caser's modification records
rows.plain: Make sure the records for a used caser include every change that was made to it after it was originally built. Missing changes can make the machine's current condition unclear.
rows.imp_rat: Modifications missing from records is largely administrative for the casing job; both proxies sit at 1, consistent with its low position in the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Modification documentation is chronically incomplete for machinery, and undocumented field changes surface repeatedly as surprises. Chasing down which modifications were actually installed versus recorded consumes time during handover. Reconciling as-built modifications against records takes a knowledgeable engineer who understands the machine, and that attention is often absent. Missing modification records cause spare-part and maintenance mismatches later, but the acquisition-stage cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the expected arrival estimate differs from the caser's actual arrival
rows.plain: Keep the expected arrival time as close as possible to when the case-packing machine actually arrives. This helps the receiving team prepare for delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Variance between expected and actual caser arrival is a planning nuisance ranked below prior batch; relevance and severity remain at the 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: ETAs for specialized machinery freight are notoriously imprecise and deviate from actuals frequently. When actual arrival diverges from the estimate, downstream receiving and install plans lose their timing. Producing a trustworthy arrival estimate for one-off heavy equipment needs logistics judgement that is often lacking. Estimate error causes idle crews or rushed rescheduling with associated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required caser production and shipping milestones without confirmed status
rows.plain: Confirm the status of every important production and shipping step for the case-packing machine. This helps staff see whether delivery is on track and respond to delays.
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed production and shipping milestone statuses are informational gaps of low consequence; the batch ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Milestone confirmation depends on manufacturer transparency, which is inconsistent, so gaps in confirmed status are common. Chasing unconfirmed production and shipping milestones adds follow-up time. Knowing which milestones matter and how to confirm them relies on expediting experience that is scarce. Unconfirmed milestones cause little direct cost but risk downstream surprises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unavailable carrier movement status while the caser is in transit
rows.plain: Keep the time without current information about the carrier’s movement of the case-packing machine as short as possible while it is being delivered. This helps the team know whether the delivery is on track.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable carrier movement status during transit is a visibility gap peripheral to running casers; consequences are minor, so both proxies are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: GPS and carrier tracking cover many lanes now, but specialized/flatbed freight still has real visibility gaps mid-transit. Blackout periods with no movement status leave coordinators waiting blind for updates. Interpreting sparse tracking data takes some carrier-savvy but not deep expertise. Status gaps cost little directly beyond precautionary staffing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting caser delivery updates from the manufacturer and carrier
rows.plain: Make sure the manufacturer and delivery company give matching updates about when the case-packing machine will arrive. This prevents confusion about the delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting caser delivery updates from manufacturer and carrier is peripheral to the core casing job and ranks below the prior batch; consequences are minor coordination noise, so both relevance and severity stay at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manufacturer and carrier systems are seldom integrated, so contradictory status is a common and largely unmitigated annoyance. Reconciling conflicting delivery updates from manufacturer and carrier wastes coordinator time chasing the truth. Judging which conflicting source to trust takes experience with these parties' reliability patterns. Conflicting updates rarely add hard cost but drive wasted follow-up effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a caser delivery exception occurring and the logistics coordinator receiving notice
rows.plain: Tell the shipping coordinator as soon as a problem with the box-packing machine's delivery occurs. Fast notice gives the team more time to respond.
rows.imp_rat: Notification lag for delivery exceptions to the logistics coordinator is a coordination delay of low stakes; both proxies held at the 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Automated exception alerting is uneven across carriers, so notice timing is unpredictable and often delayed. The lag between a delivery exception and the coordinator being notified is often long because alerts are manual. Catching exceptions early depends on someone actively monitoring, a vigilance-heavy task. Late exception notice can trigger avoidable detention or rescheduling costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of freight capacity or unloading resources being unavailable for the required caser delivery window
rows.plain: Make sure a suitable shipping space and the people or equipment needed for unloading are available during the planned delivery period. This helps prevent the packing machine from being delayed or stranded.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable freight or unloading resources in the delivery window is a procurement-logistics delay peripheral to casing; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Carrier capacity shortages and unloading-equipment availability are common enough that promised windows are missed with some regularity. Freight and unloading resources can be booked in advance, but tight windows for a large caser leave little slack when a carrier or crew slips. Matching freight capacity and heavy-unload equipment to a specialized caser needs logistics know-how that not every buyer has in-house. Scrambling for last-minute freight or lift equipment drives premium spot rates and idle-crew charges.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that receiving access or staging space is unavailable when the caser arrives
rows.plain: Make sure there is enough room and access for the case-packing machine when it arrives. This prevents delivery delays or unsafe unloading.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable receiving access or staging space at arrival is a logistics timing issue peripheral to casing; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery appointment systems and dock scheduling tools exist and work most of the time, but last-minute space contention remains a real recurring failure. Dock and staging space can usually be reserved ahead of a scheduled delivery, but slot conflicts still push arrivals to wait or reschedule. Coordinating receiving windows relies on an experienced coordinator's judgement about warehouse congestion, which is scarce and often improvised. When space is unavailable the caser incurs detention or re-delivery charges, a recurring but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damage to the caser during freight transit or unloading
rows.plain: Protect the packing machine from being damaged while it is shipped or unloaded. This helps ensure it arrives ready to install and use.
rows.imp_rat: Freight transit or unloading damage is a logistics defect outside routine operation; its relevance and severity to the casing job are low and bounded to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Purpose-built crating, shock indicators, and experienced freight handlers make transit damage uncommon, though it still occurs on long or multimodal routes. Proper crating and freight scheduling add lead time but are routine and rarely the bottleneck. Machinery riggers and crating specialists are established trades, so the needed expertise is generally accessible. Insurance and protective packaging carry real cost but are standard line items on capital shipments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of caser staging caused by a gap between delivery and installation scheduling
rows.plain: Avoid leaving the case-packing machine waiting after delivery because installation was not scheduled in time. This helps it be installed and used sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Staging duration from a delivery-to-install scheduling gap is a logistics timing nuisance of low consequence; both proxies capped at 1.
