rows.sat_rat: Delivery and install schedules are frequently owned by different parties and poorly synchronized, so staging gaps recur. Gaps between delivery and installation scheduling routinely leave the caser staged and idle for extended periods. Tightly sequencing delivery to install readiness takes coordination skill that is often not applied. Extended staging ties up floor space and may incur storage or demurrage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of selected caser configuration requirements omitted or misstated in the formal quotation
rows.imp_rat: Omitted or misstated config requirements in the quotation is a pre-purchase paperwork error, peripheral to executing the casing job and with recoverable consequences, capped at 1.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Config-to-quote transcription for complex machinery is error-prone with only spec-sheet checklists as the common control, so omissions recur. Verifying that every configuration requirement made it into the quote is a manual line-by-line check that stretches the quoting cycle. It takes a specifier who understands both the caser's technical options and the RFQ language to spot an omitted or misstated requirement, and that dual fluency is scarce. A missed configuration item surfaces as a costly change order or retrofit after purchase, and no cheap safeguard fully prevents it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required acquisition cost excluded from the quoted total
rows.plain: Include every cost needed to buy the item in the quoted total. This prevents important expenses from appearing later as unexpected charges.
rows.imp_rat: Excluding acquisition cost from a quoted total is a peripheral quoting/administrative error with low relevance to the casing job itself and mild consequences; capped by batch ceiling at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Line-item quote formats make omitted acquisition costs somewhat visible, though inconsistent supplier formatting leaves gaps. Confirming the quoted total captures all required acquisition costs adds a modest reconciliation step. Reading a quote for excluded acquisition items like crating, commissioning, or duties needs a buyer familiar with equipment quotations. Quotes commonly break out or footnote exclusions, so buyers can catch most missing acquisition cost, but hidden exclusions still cause overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in delivery and support commitments in the formal quotation
rows.plain: Make sure the formal price quote clearly states when delivery will happen and what support is included. This prevents disagreements about what the seller promised.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in delivery/support commitments in a quotation is a contracting-clarity error, peripheral to the casing job (low relevance) with negotiable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard quotes often leave delivery windows and support scope loosely worded, so ambiguity persists absent firm SLA language. Clarifying delivery and support commitments requires back-and-forth with the supplier that adds days to the quote phase. Spotting vague support or delivery language takes a buyer experienced in equipment service contracts. Ambiguous commitments translate into uncosted service gaps discovered after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the formal quotation arriving after the internal approval package deadline
rows.plain: Avoid receiving the official price offer after the deadline for internal approval. Getting it on time allows the purchase request to be reviewed before the deadline.
rows.imp_rat: A quotation arriving after the approval deadline is a procurement timing error, peripheral to the caser job; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Quote turnaround depends on the supplier and is only loosely controlled, so late arrivals against internal deadlines happen fairly often. A late quotation compresses the time available to assemble the approval package before its deadline. Aligning supplier quote timing with internal deadlines requires buyer coordination skill that is inconsistently applied. A missed deadline may defer the purchase but does not itself waste money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of quotation validity expiring before internal approval is completed
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a supplier’s price offer expires before the company finishes approving the purchase. This helps keep the agreed offer available.
rows.imp_rat: Quotation validity expiring before internal approval is an approval-timing error, peripheral to casing (low relevance) with re-quotable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows and diarized expiry dates help, but slow sign-off chains still cause validity to lapse. Internal approval frequently lags the quotation validity period, and the whole point is to beat that clock. Coordinating approvers is process management rather than specialized skill. Expired validity can trigger re-pricing, though extensions are often granted at no cost.

rows.source_job: Inspect received caser and report discrepancies
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transit damage going unidentified during the receiving inspection
rows.plain: Make sure any damage from shipping is found during the inspection when the machine arrives. This allows the receiving team to report problems accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage at receiving is an inspection lapse with modest relevance to ongoing casing and bounded severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Shock/tilt indicators and inspection checklists help, but concealed internal damage frequently goes unnoticed until commissioning. A thorough receiving inspection takes hours and can delay acceptance if done rigorously. Identifying concealed transit damage on complex machinery requires an inspector who knows the equipment, and that skill is not always on the dock. Missed damage becomes an uninsurable or disputed claim later, and quick visual checks are cheap but incomplete.

rows.source_job: Inspect received caser and report discrepancies
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configuration, accessory, documentation, or quantity discrepancies not identified against the purchase order
rows.plain: Make sure the delivered machine has the right setup, extras, documents, and number of items listed in the order. Finding differences early makes them easier to correct.
rows.imp_rat: PO discrepancies not identified is a receiving/reconciliation error with peripheral relevance to casing and modest severity; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard three-way-match receiving practice catches quantity and accessory gaps well, but subtle configuration mismatches slip past non-specialist receivers. Checking configuration, accessories, docs, and quantities against the PO is a defined receiving step that fits normal dock time. Matching delivered items to a PO is routine warehouse work, though verifying technical configuration needs someone knowledgeable. PO-matching prevents paying for the wrong items, and packing lists make most discrepancies visible at low effort.

rows.source_job: Inspect received caser and report discrepancies
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict between the purchase order, shipping records, and delivered caser identification
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase order, shipping information, and delivered case-packing machine all identify the same item. This helps catch wrong or missing equipment before it is accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between purchase order, shipping, and delivered caser identity is a documentation-reconciliation error that is peripheral to the casing job itself (low relevance); consequences are administrative and correctable (low severity). Capped at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Serial-number and nameplate matching against documents is standard and usually consistent, so conflicts are relatively rare. Cross-checking PO, shipping records, and nameplate IDs is a brief confirmation step at receiving. Reconciling identifiers is clerical and requires little specialized skill beyond attention to detail. Serial and model verification is essentially free and prevents costly wrong-unit acceptance.

rows.source_job: Inspect received caser and report discrepancies
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of discrepancy reports lacking evidence needed for supplier review
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy reports lacking evidence for supplier review is a documentation gap of low operational consequence; capped at 1 for both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence capture at receiving is inconsistent, so many discrepancy reports arrive incomplete. Discrepancy reports lacking evidence force supplier back-and-forth that delays resolution. Documenting a discrepancy with the right evidence takes inspection know-how that receiving staff may not have. Weak reports can lose warranty or claim leverage, costing money.

rows.source_job: Inspect received caser and report discrepancies
rows.stmt: Reduce the time from discrepancy detection to supplier notification
rows.imp_rat: Delay from discrepancy detection to supplier notification is an administrative timing error only loosely tied to the caser's core function (relevance low), and its consequences are absorbable and ranked below the prior batch (severity capped at ceiling).
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier claim channels exist and work, but timeliness varies with how promptly receiving staff act. The lag from detecting a discrepancy to notifying the supplier is often extended by manual reporting steps. Little specialized skill is needed once a notification path exists. Delayed notification can jeopardize claim windows and add cost.

rows.stk: Engineering, procurement, or operations staff responsible for equipment selection.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of viable warehouse caser options overlooked across manufacturers, distributors, and secondary markets
rows.plain: Overlook as few suitable machines for putting products into shipping boxes as possible, including those sold by makers, dealers, and resale sources. This increases the chance of finding the best available choice.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking viable caser options across markets is a sourcing-search error, remote from the operational casing job (low relevance) with only opportunity-cost consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is no comprehensive catalog of caser sources, so viable options are routinely missed and coverage is inconsistent. Scanning manufacturers, distributors, and secondary markets thoroughly is time-consuming and often truncated under deadline pressure. Knowing the full universe of caser suppliers, including secondary markets, requires deep market knowledge few buyers possess. Overlooked options mean missed price or value alternatives, but broad search itself is cheap if pursued.

rows.stk: Engineering, procurement, or operations staff responsible for equipment selection.
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of acquisition and ownership cost omitted from candidate comparisons
rows.plain: Leave out as little of the cost of buying and owning each case-packing machine as possible when comparing the choices.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting ownership cost from candidate comparisons is a purchasing-analysis gap with no bearing on running the caser and only financial-estimate consequences, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: TCO templates exist but are inconsistently populated, so comparisons remain patchy and understate true cost. Assembling total-cost-of-ownership figures across candidates takes considerable time chasing energy, spares, and maintenance numbers. Building a credible TCO model requires cost-engineering judgement that many procurement teams lack for specialized casers. Suppliers rarely volunteer lifetime cost data, so ownership costs like spares, consumables, and downtime are routinely left out of comparisons and overspend follows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ownership and serial-information fields recorded inaccurately for the warehouse caser
rows.plain: Record the warehouse machine's owner and identifying number correctly, along with its warranty details. Accurate records make it clear who is responsible for the machine and what coverage it has.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate ownership and serial fields is a data-entry defect with low centrality and mild consequence; constrained to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual transcription of serials and ownership fields is error-prone and rarely verified, so inaccuracies persist. Correcting inaccurate serial or ownership fields later is a minor administrative task. Transcribing serial and ownership data accurately needs care but no expertise. Inaccurate records can complicate warranty or resale claims, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay beyond the supplier's caser-registration deadline
rows.plain: Register the equipment with the supplier before the supplier’s deadline. This helps protect ownership records and warranty rights.
rows.imp_rat: Delay past the registration deadline is an administrative compliance timing error with low direct relevance to casing; constrained to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is no enforced trigger to register on time, so missing the supplier deadline is a common outcome. Registration is often left until after the deadline, and the delay is the core error itself. Meeting a registration deadline is a scheduling matter, not a skill challenge. Late registration can void or shorten warranty coverage, a real cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable warranty entitlements left inactive after registration
rows.plain: Make sure every warranty benefit that applies is activated after the equipment is registered. This helps ensure the owner can use repair or replacement coverage when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Inactive warranty entitlements after registration is an administrative oversight with only financial-recovery consequences and no bearing on the casing job, held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Warranty registration is frequently overlooked or partially completed, so entitlements commonly remain inactive. Activating a dormant warranty entitlement later takes some administrative time but is not urgent to setup. Registering warranties is a clerical task requiring little specialized skill. Inactive warranty entitlements are a direct financial exposure when a covered failure must be paid out of pocket.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required service contacts missing from the supplier's caser record
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment case record includes all needed service contact details. This helps people know whom to contact for repairs or warranty support.
rows.imp_rat: Missing service contacts in the supplier record is a record-keeping defect with limited relevance and low consequence; constrained to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier records often capture only a primary contact, leaving gaps that surface at the worst time. Missing service contacts cost time chasing down who to call when an issue arises. Recording contacts is clerical and needs no special skill. Incomplete contacts mostly cost delay rather than money directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that authorized asset personnel lack active support credentials when caser service is needed
rows.plain: Make sure the people responsible for the equipment still have a valid support agreement when service is needed. Without it, they may not be able to get help under the agreed terms.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel lacking active support credentials is a service-access administrative gap with limited direct impact on the core casing job; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Credential management is typically informal and only checked when service is needed, so gaps are discovered too late. Discovering lapsed support credentials at the moment of need delays service until access is restored. Maintaining credentials is administrative and needs no technical expertise. Lacking active credentials can force paid emergency support or downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of packaging debris or moisture transferred onto caser surfaces during unpacking
rows.plain: Keep packaging scraps and moisture from getting onto the machine’s surfaces while it is being unpacked. This helps keep the machine clean and prevents possible damage.
rows.imp_rat: Packaging debris or moisture during unpacking is an installation-hygiene issue only loosely tied to the caser's casing job (low relevance) and correctable (low severity); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard unpacking and wipe-down practice handles most of this well, so residual transfer of debris is uncommon. Wiping down packaging debris and moisture from surfaces is a quick task that rarely delays setup significantly. Unpacking and cleaning caser surfaces needs no special expertise, just attentiveness. Debris and moisture rarely cause material loss unless they reach bearings or electronics, keeping cost impact low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of assembly decisions blocked by incomplete or conflicting installation documentation
rows.plain: Give the assembly team complete, consistent installation instructions so they can decide how to put the parts together. This prevents work from stopping because the instructions are missing or disagree with each other.
rows.imp_rat: Assembly decisions blocked by conflicting documentation is a peripheral installation-phase error to the ongoing casing job, warranting low relevance and low severity within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Installation manuals are often incomplete or contradictory, routinely blocking decisions. Blocked assembly decisions idle the crew while documentation is clarified. Filling gaps in installation docs depends on experienced installers improvising correctly. Stalled or reworked assembly from bad docs drives labor overrun.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of compressed-air leakage at the caser utility connection
rows.imp_rat: Compressed-air leakage at the utility connection is a minor energy-waste error with trivial consequence and little relevance to whether cases get erected, so both proxies are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice uses soapy-water or listen checks at commissioning but there is rarely systematic leak-down verification, so leaks recur inconsistently. Detecting and fixing an air leak at the utility connection during preparation adds only minor time when caught, but undetected leaks persist across shifts. Making a leak-free pneumatic connection is a routine fitter skill, though torqueing fittings and applying sealant correctly still relies on technician diligence. Compressed-air leaks are a well-known energy drain and small leaks silently waste money for long periods before anyone acts.

rows.source_job: Create and save case format recipes
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an existing approved recipe is overwritten unintentionally while saving another recipe
rows.plain: Make sure saving a new package-size recipe does not accidentally replace an already approved one. This protects the recipe that is already authorized for use.
rows.imp_rat: Unintentionally overwriting an approved recipe is a configuration-management slip somewhat removed from active casing (low relevance) and reversible from backups (low severity); held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Many HMIs give weak or no overwrite warnings and lack version control, so unintentional overwrites happen and go unnoticed. An accidental recipe overwrite costs the time to rebuild or restore the lost recipe, sometimes mid-changeover. Avoiding overwrites depends on the operator's care with save dialogs rather than any protective skill. A lost recipe rarely wastes material directly but can cause a bad run before it is noticed.

rows.source_job: Create and save case format recipes
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of saved recipes whose names do not uniquely identify the intended case size and product format
rows.plain: Give each saved recipe a name that clearly identifies the intended box size and product type. This helps workers choose the correct recipe and avoid using the wrong settings.
rows.imp_rat: Non-unique recipe names are a naming-convention error marginal to the casing job (low relevance) and correctable by inspection (low severity); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Naming is usually ad hoc with no enforced convention, so ambiguous recipe names are widespread and cause selection errors. Ambiguous recipe names cost operators time hunting for or verifying the correct recipe at each changeover. Interpreting cryptic recipe names relies on experienced operators who know the local naming conventions. Selecting the wrong recipe due to ambiguous naming can cause scrapped cases and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of baseline entries that differ from the as-installed Setzpacker configuration at commissioning completion
rows.plain: Make sure the documented starting setup matches the setup actually installed on the Setzpacker machine when commissioning is finished. This helps confirm that the records accurately describe the completed machine.
rows.imp_rat: Baseline entries diverging from as-installed Setzpacker config at commissioning is a one-time documentation error; low relevance to daily casing and low severity, held at the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Baselines are often captured from design intent rather than verified as-installed, so mismatches at commissioning are frequent. Discrepancies between baseline and as-installed config surface later as time-consuming detective work. Reconciling the recorded baseline against the actual machine requires careful expert verification rarely done thoroughly. A wrong baseline can drive incorrect settings and scrap during future setups, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configuration entries with conflicting values across commissioning records at handover
rows.plain: Make sure the machine’s recorded settings and details agree across all handover records. This gives the receiving team one reliable description of the machine.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting configuration values across commissioning records is a documentation-consistency defect, low in relevance to live casing and low in consequence, held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With paper forms, spreadsheets, and vendor sheets kept separately, cross-record conflicts routinely slip through at handover. Conflicting values across records cost time to arbitrate which record is authoritative. Spotting conflicts across multiple commissioning documents needs an experienced reviewer paying close attention. Conflicting records mostly waste engineering time rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of baseline entries lacking clear component identifiers, measurement units, or format references
rows.plain: Make sure the starting equipment information clearly names each part, explains how measurements are expressed, and points to the correct format. This gives people a clear and consistent reference for the machine's setup.
rows.imp_rat: Baseline entries lacking identifiers or units are a documentation-completeness error peripheral to casing (low relevance) and correctable (low severity); ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning documentation is frequently hurried and inconsistent, so missing units and references are common defects. Baseline entries missing identifiers or units force people to reconstruct meaning later, slowing troubleshooting and future setups. Filling in clear, complete baseline entries demands documentation discipline that many commissioning technicians lack. Incomplete baselines mostly cost future rework time rather than immediate material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between commissioning completion and availability of the baseline record for handover
rows.imp_rat: Time-to-availability of the baseline record for handover is a documentation-timing issue with no operational impact on casing, held at the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The record almost always arrives eventually, so the issue is latency more than outright absence. Baseline records are often finalized days or weeks after commissioning completes, delaying handover readiness. Producing the record promptly is more about workflow than skill, though it needs the commissioning engineer's availability. The lag mainly delays productive use rather than consuming direct resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between throughput information available to the operator and actual line throughput
rows.plain: Make sure the operator's information about how much the production line is making matches the line's actual output. This helps the operator set the feed rate correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between displayed and actual line throughput is an information-accuracy issue that only mildly affects operator decisions; ranked below prior batch, so relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Displayed throughput often diverges from actual due to averaging windows and uncounted micro-stops, leaving a persistent moderate discrepancy. Throughput readouts refresh frequently, so latency is not the main gap. Interpreting whether displayed throughput reflects reality requires operator experience with the line's quirks. Misleading throughput info leads to suboptimal adjustments rather than direct waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of post-update control defects that cause incorrect case handling
rows.plain: Fewer software changes should lead to mistakes in how cases are handled. This helps the system continue handling each case correctly after an update.
rows.imp_rat: Post-update control defects causing incorrect case handling do bear on the casing job, but the batch-5 ceiling caps this below all prior items, so relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Update validation testing catches many defects but coverage gaps let control errors reach production and mishandle cases. Post-update control defects surface during production and force emergency troubleshooting that halts the line. Detecting and fixing control-logic defects requires specialized automation-engineering expertise. Mishandled cases from defects cause product loss and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated caser settings altered or lost during the update
rows.imp_rat: Validated settings altered or lost during update affects operation, but the ranking caps this below the prior batch at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Backup-and-restore discipline around firmware/software updates is inconsistent, so validated settings are frequently altered or overwritten without notice. Reconfiguring or re-validating settings after an update adds meaningful downtime when they are lost. Restoring correct validated settings requires someone who knows the golden configuration, which is often tribal knowledge held by few. Lost settings force rework and revalidation cycles that consume labor and can scrap product runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish packaging disruption caused by the update
rows.plain: Spend less time stopping medicine packaging in a germ-free area when the machine’s operating software is changed. This helps packaging restart sooner after the change.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish disruption from an update could be serious in general, but within this batch's ordering it ranks below prior items and is constrained to 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Update rollouts in GMP sterile environments carry significant deviation risk and disruptions recur despite change-control processes. Updates that disrupt sterile fill-finish packaging can propagate downtime through the line, and coordinating windows to minimize this is often imperfect. Minimizing disruption requires cross-disciplinary planning between validation, IT and packaging that few teams execute smoothly. Sterile line disruption is extremely costly in lost product and revalidation, so the stakes are high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the warehouse caser remains out of service while required repair parts are unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Downtime awaiting repair parts affects availability, but the mandatory ceiling ranks it below prior items, so relevance and severity remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Spare-parts inventory for casers is frequently incomplete or poorly forecast, so critical parts are often unavailable when needed and downtime stretches out. Waiting for unavailable repair parts is a chronic, often multi-day downtime that is the worst-served time pain here. Parts availability is a stocking and procurement issue, not a skill gap. Emergency ordering, expediting, and lost throughput make stockouts very expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among acceptance criteria for the warehouse caser's expected safety-control response
rows.plain: Make sure everyone agrees on what a safe response should look like when the case-packing machine is tested. This avoids conflicting safety expectations.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among acceptance criteria for safety-control response is a specification-consistency error removed from active casing (low relevance) and reconcilable in review (low severity); capped by rank.
rows.sat_rat: Today acceptance criteria are often authored across separate documents with no formal conflict check, so contradictory requirements routinely slip through and undermine consistent safety verification. Reconciling conflicting acceptance criteria for safety-control responses adds slow back-and-forth reviews during maintenance sign-off. Resolving contradictions among safety criteria demands scarce controls-and-safety engineering judgement that most maintenance teams lack on-site. Ambiguity forces rework and revalidation cycles that consume engineering hours, but the direct material cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of functional test interruptions caused by the warehouse caser being released back to packaging production
rows.plain: Prevent safety testing from being interrupted because the case-packing machine is returned to production. This allows all safety checks to be completed in one planned period.
rows.imp_rat: Functional-test interruptions from premature release to production are a commissioning-workflow error, tangential to the core casing job (low relevance) with recoverable scheduling consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Production pressure frequently overrides test completion because scheduling controls to lock equipment during testing are weak, so interruptions recur. When the caser is pulled back into production mid-test, the functional test must be restarted later, wasting elapsed time. Preventing premature release requires coordination discipline more than technical skill. Interrupted and repeated tests waste labor but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of product-contact area retaining debris or product residue after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residual debris on product-contact area poses contamination risk, yet its rank below the prior batch caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Documented sanitation SOPs and visual checks handle most residue, but crevices and blind spots still routinely retain debris. Cleaning cycles are budgeted into maintenance windows, so time pressure only occasionally forces shortcuts. Thorough cleaning of contact areas depends on the cleaner knowing hard-to-reach spots, a modest skill gap. Cleaning agents and labor are low cost, so residue left behind is not primarily a spend issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of guides or conveyor components damaged or misaligned during each cleaning event
rows.plain: Make sure cleaning does not damage or knock out of place the guides and moving parts that handle the boxes.
rows.imp_rat: Guides or conveyor components damaged during cleaning could affect casing operation, but the batch ordering places it below higher-priority items; both relevance and severity are held at the minimum to respect the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning procedures reduce but do not eliminate incidental component damage, which recurs where components are fragile or access is tight. Damaging guides during cleaning forces unplanned realignment that eats into the window. Avoiding cleaning-induced damage depends heavily on careful, experienced handling. Damaged guides and conveyor parts incur replacement material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of warehouse caser unavailability beyond the scheduled daily maintenance window
rows.plain: Keep the warehouse packing machine from being out of service longer than the planned daily maintenance period. This helps work resume on time.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability beyond the daily maintenance window mirrors item 13 with modest relevance and bounded severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established daily checklists make these windows predictable, though minor unplanned issues still occasionally extend them. Daily maintenance windows are tightly proceduralized routines, keeping most overruns small. Daily tasks are routine and require only standard operator or technician competence. Short daily windows limit cost exposure from any overrun.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of signs of wear or damage missed during the weekly warehouse caser inspection
rows.imp_rat: Missed wear or damage signs during weekly inspection is a preventive-maintenance gap; the batch-5 ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual inspection is inconsistent across inspectors and fatigue-prone, so defects are routinely missed despite standardized forms. Weekly inspections are time-boxed and often rushed, so subtle wear signs get less scrutiny than they need. Catching early wear or damage depends heavily on the inspector's experience and attentiveness, which varies widely and is the core weakness today. The inspection itself costs little, but missed wear translates into expensive catastrophic failures later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the weekly inspection waits for the warehouse caser to become available
rows.imp_rat: Weekly inspection waiting for caser availability is a minor scheduling delay ranked below prior batch; relevance and severity stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Planned maintenance windows generally secure caser access for inspection, though production priorities occasionally push the slot. The weekly inspection sometimes waits for a production lull, causing modest schedulable delay. Coordinating access needs planning rather than technical skill. Short access waits impose little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of tension or alignment settings shifting when adjustments are secured or the caser is restarted
rows.plain: The settings that control tightness and straightness should stay where they were placed after being secured or after the box-packing machine is restarted. This helps the machine continue working correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Tension/alignment settings shifting on restart is a setup-stability error affecting tuning effort (low relevance for this batch) with correctable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Tension and alignment settings commonly drift when locked or after restart because mechanical locking and repeatable positioning features are inconsistent across casers. Re-adjusting settings that shift on restart adds repeated tuning time. Achieving settings that hold requires an experienced technician's feel for locking mechanisms and torque. Repeated readjustment wastes labor but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the operator waits for the caser to become available for the pre-shift check
rows.imp_rat: Operator wait for the caser before pre-shift check is a minor delay ranked below prior items; both proxies stay at the 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Shift-handover scheduling reliably frees the caser for pre-shift checks in most operations, with occasional overrun conflicts. Pre-shift availability waits are usually short since casers are typically idle between shifts, the direct time gain here. No special skill is involved in the operator simply accessing the machine. Brief waits carry minimal cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of warehouse caser consumable replacements completed after the prescribed service interval
rows.plain: Scheduled replacement parts and supplies in the warehouse box-packing machine should be changed on time, not after their required service date. This helps prevent avoidable equipment problems.
rows.imp_rat: Consumable replacements done after the prescribed interval is a maintenance-timing error with modest relevance here and gradual, correctable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive-maintenance scheduling systems reliably trigger replacements, though occasional missed intervals still occur under workload pressure. CMMS-scheduled reminders keep most consumable replacements on time without delay. Following a service-interval schedule needs little specialized skill. Consumables are inexpensive, and timely replacement avoids costly failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of warehouse caser consumables seated or sealed incorrectly during replacement
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly seated or sealed consumables during replacement can cause caser malfunction, yet ranking discipline forces relevance and severity to the 1.0 ceiling for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard replacement procedures and torque specs exist and mostly ensure correct seating, though rushed swaps still produce occasional misseals. Consumable replacement is a routine quick task, so time is not the pain point. Correct seating and sealing depends on the technician's hands-on care and knowledge of tolerances, which is the main source of error. Consumables are inexpensive, but improper seating causes leaks, jams and product loss that add up.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of inconsistent sensor responses under unchanged test conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the sensors give the same answer when the conditions being tested have not changed. This helps people know that the readings are dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent sensor responses under unchanged conditions is a test-repeatability error during evaluation, not central to production casing (low relevance), with consequences limited to retesting effort (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor drift, contamination and wiring faults produce inconsistent readings under identical conditions, and status-quo calibration intervals only partly contain this. Chasing inconsistent sensor responses lengthens troubleshooting and test cycles. Distinguishing a flaky sensor from a real condition change demands experienced diagnostic judgement that is scarce. Sensor replacement and recalibration incur moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required trigger points left unverified because representative packages are unavailable
rows.plain: Reduce the number of sensor activation points that cannot be checked because the needed test samples are unavailable. This helps confirm that the sensors will respond at the right levels.
rows.imp_rat: Unverified trigger points due to missing representative packages is a validation-completeness gap; the ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Representative packages are frequently unavailable during maintenance, so trigger points routinely go unverified and verification completeness suffers. Unverified trigger points require rescheduling verification when representative packages become available, delaying closeout. Judging acceptable substitute packages or deferring verification needs test-engineering knowledge that is scarce. Sourcing representative packages for testing incurs modest material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of warehouse caser unavailability beyond the planned maintenance window
rows.plain: Return the box-packing machine to service as soon as possible when sensor adjustments take longer than planned. This limits the time the machine cannot be used.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability beyond the planned maintenance window has modest relevance but is bounded in consequence; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: PM scheduling via CMMS is standard and mostly effective, yet unexpected findings during maintenance routinely push work past the window. Planned maintenance windows are scheduled with buffers, so overruns are common but bounded by mature scheduling practice. Adhering to a planned window depends on experienced technicians estimating task duration accurately. Overruns cost lost production time, which drives sites to invest in planning tools.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of controller configuration parameters omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitted controller parameters from backup risks incomplete restores, though ranking discipline caps both proxies at 1 here.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are often partial or done manually without verification, so configuration parameters are frequently omitted and only discovered after a failure. Backing up parameters is fast when done, so time is not the limiting factor. Knowing every parameter that must be captured requires controller expertise many maintenance staff lack. Backups are essentially free to make, so omissions are not a cost-driven failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to locate and retrieve the backup after controller replacement or failure
rows.imp_rat: Time to locate and retrieve the backup after controller failure is a recovery-support task; ranked lower this batch, both relevance and severity are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are stored inconsistently without version control or a known repository, so retrieval is slow and the right file is often uncertain. Finding the right backup after a controller failure can take hours of searching through drives, emails, or folders. Locating and validating the correct backup version needs someone who knows where and how they were stored. Search time is labor cost plus extended downtime that dwarfs the storage cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of warehouse caser condition readings that misrepresent the caser's actual condition
rows.imp_rat: Condition readings misrepresenting the caser could hide problems, yet the batch ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Manual gauges and uncalibrated tools plus subjective assessment mean readings often misrepresent actual condition without anyone noticing. Taking readings is quick, so time is not the issue behind misrepresentation. Interpreting whether a reading reflects true condition requires diagnostic judgement that many techs lack. Instruments are inexpensive, but bad readings drive misdirected repairs that waste money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of warehouse caser condition-data outages during continuous monitoring
rows.plain: Keep information about the box-packing machine available during ongoing monitoring, with outages lasting as little time as possible. This helps workers notice changes in the machine’s condition.
rows.imp_rat: Condition-data outages during monitoring reduce visibility but do not directly halt casing; both relevance and severity are low here and constrained to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous monitoring systems suffer intermittent sensor, network and power dropouts, and few casers have redundancy so outages recur. Data outages are usually short but gaps in the monitoring stream still delay trend detection. Diagnosing sensor or network dropouts requires instrumentation skills not always on the maintenance crew. Redundant sensors and buffering hardware add cost that many sites only partially deploy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between warehouse caser degradation crossing an action limit and notification of Facilities Management
rows.plain: Notify the facilities team quickly when the warehouse case-packing machine’s condition becomes bad enough to require action. Early notice can help prevent further problems.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in notifying Facilities Management after degradation crosses an action limit is a maintenance-reporting lag, tangential to the core casing throughput job and with modest consequences, so it stays at the 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Where alarms are wired into the CMMS notification chain works consistently, but manual escalation paths still drop or delay a meaningful fraction of events. Modern condition-monitoring and CMMS alerting can push notifications to Facilities Management within minutes once a limit is crossed, though many sites still rely on periodic manual review. Configuring meaningful action limits and alert routing requires reliability-engineering skill that is unevenly available. Automated alerting infrastructure is a moderate up-front cost but avoids costly late escalations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of detected warehouse caser degradation events that do not trigger maintenance action
rows.plain: Make sure signs that the case-packing machine is getting worse lead to a maintenance response. Acting on these signs can help prevent a larger failure.
rows.imp_rat: Detected degradation events not triggering maintenance is a condition-monitoring follow-through error, of limited relevance in this batch with gradual consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Even when degradation is detected, weak alarm-to-work-order linkage means a meaningful share of events never convert into maintenance action. Degradation events that fail to trigger action delay the eventual repair until failure. Deciding which detected events warrant maintenance requires reliability-engineering judgement often lacking at the floor level. Missed triggers lead to costlier run-to-failure repairs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of completed maintenance activities omitted from the warehouse caser's service history
rows.plain: Reduce the share of completed upkeep work missing from the warehouse box-packing machine's service history. This helps people know what work has already been done.
rows.imp_rat: Omissions from the service history are an administrative record-keeping error, marginal to executing casing (low relevance) with easily-absorbed consequences (low severity), and capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, end-of-shift logging routinely omits some completed activities, leaving service histories chronically incomplete despite CMMS availability. Logging each completed activity takes time technicians often skip under pressure. Recording work is a discipline issue rather than a skill gap. Data entry costs little, so the barrier is behavioral not financial.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of warehouse caser maintenance entries containing inaccurate task, part, or service observation details
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate maintenance-entry details degrade record quality, a peripheral concern to running the caser; both proxies capped at 1 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS and paper logs capture data but free-text and manual entry make task, part, and observation details frequently inaccurate or incomplete. Filling in log details is quick, but is often deferred until memory fades, degrading accuracy. Accurate entries require knowing correct part numbers and descriptive discipline that varies by technician. Inaccurate records cost little immediately but drive wrong parts orders and repeat diagnosis later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completing warehouse caser maintenance and recording the maintenance details
rows.imp_rat: Lag between completing maintenance and recording details is administrative and non-central to casing throughput; low-consequence, so both proxies sit at the mandated 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mobile CMMS and tablets shorten the gap where deployed, but paper-then-transcribe habits still leave frequent delays. The lag between finishing work and logging it is the core error and is often hours or shifts long when done from memory later. Recording promptly needs no special skill, just discipline and access to the logging system. Delayed recording has little direct cost but erodes data value used for planning.

rows.source_job: Protect the caser from environmental damage
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of dust and particulate matter that accumulates on sensitive caser components
rows.plain: Keep dust and other tiny particles from building up on the machine’s delicate parts. This helps protect the machine from damage and keeps it working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Dust accumulation on components is a gradual maintenance-hygiene error marginal to the casing function (low relevance) with slow, manageable effects (low severity); capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled cleaning PMs address this, but dust ingress in warehouse environments still recurs between intervals. Cleaning dust off sensitive components is a short, recurring housekeeping task. Routine cleaning needs minimal skill, though knowing which components are sensitive helps. Neglected dust accumulation drives premature wear and sensor faults, wasting components and energy over time.

rows.source_job: Protect the caser from environmental damage
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration caser components remain exposed to temperatures outside their allowable limits
rows.plain: Limit how long parts of the case-packing machine are exposed to temperatures that could damage them. This helps protect the machine from environmental harm.
rows.imp_rat: Components exposed to out-of-limit temperatures could damage equipment, but for this scope it ranks below the prior batch; relevance and severity are constrained to 1 by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous thermal monitoring with trip logic is not standard on casers, so exposure detection is inconsistent and reactive. Temperature excursions on caser components are often caught only at the next inspection, leaving exposure durations longer than desired. Interpreting whether a component temperature is genuinely out of limits requires technician knowledge of allowable ranges that is not always documented. Extended thermal exposure risks component degradation, but active temperature control add-ons are a cost sites often defer.

rows.source_job: Protect the caser from environmental damage
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency that environmentally sensitive caser areas remain exposed after operator or technician access
rows.plain: Areas of the box-packing machine that can be harmed by the surroundings should be covered or protected promptly after someone opens or enters them. This helps prevent damage from conditions such as dust or moisture.
rows.imp_rat: Environmentally sensitive areas left exposed after access is a housekeeping error, peripheral to the casing job in this batch (low relevance) with localized consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on human memory and checklists to reseal sensitive areas after access means exposures still slip through routinely. Discovering an area left exposed after access forces re-cleaning or re-sealing that costs time. Knowing which environmentally sensitive areas must be resealed depends on trained awareness that varies by technician. Contamination from exposure can spoil product, a high-cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-device actuations that fail to place the warehouse caser in its required safe state
rows.plain: Make sure each safety device puts the box-packing machine into the required safe condition when it is triggered. This helps prevent injury or equipment damage during a dangerous situation.
rows.imp_rat: Safety-device actuations failing to reach a safe state carry high inherent stakes, but the batch-5 ceiling forces both relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Safety-rated devices and periodic proof-testing make correct safe-state actuation largely dependable, though undetected failures between tests remain a residual risk. A safety actuation that fails to reach safe state triggers immediate emergency response, but recovery is slow. Verifying safe-state behavior requires functional-safety competence that specialized teams provide but floor staff often lack. Safety-device testing and redundant hardware carry real cost that regulated sites nonetheless fund.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required safety-device checks omitted before or after service
rows.imp_rat: Omitted safety-device checks carry safety stakes, yet the ceiling mandates both proxies remain at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout/tagout and safety-check protocols are well institutionalized and audited, making omissions relatively uncommon though not eliminated. Safety-device checks are short and rarely skipped for time reasons. The checks are procedural and require little specialized skill once trained. Performing the checks costs almost nothing, so omission is not cost-driven.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the required safety-device response before or after service
rows.plain: Clearly state how the safety device must respond before or after service. This helps confirm that the equipment will protect people as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in required safety-device response is a specification-clarity error somewhat removed from routine casing (low relevance) and clarifiable before harm (low severity); capped by batch rank.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and functional-test criteria reduce ambiguity for common devices, but non-standard or modified safety devices still leave the required response unclear. Clarifying the expected safety-device response takes little time once documentation exists. Ambiguity here forces technicians to rely on individual judgement about correct safety-device behavior, exposing a skill dependency. Ambiguity itself costs little directly until it leads to a wrong response.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine asset identifier differs from the identifier in the ownership documents
rows.plain: Make sure the machine’s identifying number matches the number in the ownership papers. This prevents confusion about which machine was transferred and who owns it.
rows.imp_rat: Asset identifier mismatch with ownership documents is a recordkeeping defect of minimal job relevance, capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Nameplate IDs and document IDs are entered separately over a machine's life, so mismatches occur but are usually catchable. Correcting a mismatched asset identifier against ownership documents adds administrative delay at closing. Verifying asset IDs against paperwork is clerical and needs little specialized skill. Identifier mismatches rarely cost much beyond administrative rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency between the transfer terms in the buyer and seller records
rows.plain: Make sure the buyer's and seller's records state the same conditions for handing ownership of the machine from one party to the other. This helps prevent disputes about the transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent transfer terms between buyer and seller records is an administrative discrepancy of low relevance; ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Independent record-keeping by each party means term inconsistencies recur without a shared source of truth. Reconciling mismatched transfer terms between buyer and seller records can prolong closing. Aligning contract terms is standard commercial/legal administration work. Discrepancies can trigger disputes or re-drafting costs but are usually modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain all authorizations needed to transfer machine ownership
rows.plain: Spend less time collecting every approval needed to change who owns a machine. This helps the ownership change happen without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain ownership-transfer authorizations is an administrative transfer step, not central to cartoning operation (low relevance), with merely delayed timelines as the consequence (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard authorization matrices make the process fairly predictable, though bottlenecks recur. Gathering internal and external authorizations to transfer ownership is a recognized, often slow approval chain. Navigating approval workflows is routine administrative work. Authorization mainly consumes staff time rather than direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the manufacturer disputes the machine's warranty or recall eligibility after return work begins
rows.plain: Make sure the machine still clearly qualifies for warranty or recall service after the return process starts. This helps prevent the manufacturer from disputing the claim later.
rows.imp_rat: Warranty or recall disputes after return work begins are end-of-life contractual matters remote from cartoning operation (low relevance), with financial-only consequences (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Eligibility hinges on records and OEM goodwill that are inconsistently maintained, making disputes a real risk. A disputed warranty or recall claim mid-return stalls the work while eligibility is argued. Establishing warranty/recall eligibility needs documentation and OEM-liaison expertise. A lost dispute shifts return-work costs onto the disposing party.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required decommissioning approvals and sign-offs missing at final review
rows.plain: Get all required approvals and signatures before the box-packing machine is finally taken out of use. This confirms that the shutdown was reviewed and accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Missing decommissioning sign-offs at final review is an end-of-life compliance detail (low relevance) with correctable consequences (low severity); ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Decommissioning checklists exist in many plants but are inconsistently completed, so gaps surface at final review. Chasing missing sign-offs delays final review when approvals lag. Knowing which approvals are required takes procedural familiarity that compliance staff usually hold. Missing approvals stall disposal and can incur compliance penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment-status and production-line records conflicting with the machine's decommissioned state
rows.plain: Make sure all equipment and production records show that the packaging machine has been retired. Conflicting records could make people think it is still in use.
rows.imp_rat: Status records conflicting with a decommissioned state is a data-consistency issue peripheral to cartoning (low relevance) with low consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple disconnected systems mean records routinely lag the machine's actual decommissioned state. Updating every equipment and line record takes time and is often deferred after physical removal. Requires system access and knowledge of which records reference the machine, spread across roles. Stale records cause misordered parts and planning errors with modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required stored programs, recipes, production counts, and configuration parameters omitted from the export
rows.plain: Make sure the exported machine information includes all saved programs, recipes, production totals, and setup details. This matters so the machine can be replaced or restored without losing important instructions or settings.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted stored programs or recipes from export is an administrative export completeness defect, peripheral to cartoning; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without a validated inventory checklist of all data types, exports routinely miss hidden or non-obvious parameter sets, making completeness inconsistent. Chasing down omitted recipes and parameters after the fact wastes time and sometimes cannot be recovered once the machine is gone. Knowing every location where programs, recipes, counts and parameters are stored on a given controller demands deep machine-specific expertise that few disposal teams have. Omitted configuration data forces expensive re-engineering or re-commissioning on the receiving machine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of exported machine control data that is corrupted or unreadable
rows.plain: Make sure exported machine-control information is complete and readable, so it can be used without missing or damaged data.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted exported machine control data is a data-handling defect only tangential to running cartons, and the mandatory ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary export formats and aging storage media mean corrupted or unreadable dumps are a recurring surprise with no routine verification step. Discovering corruption usually only happens on re-import attempts, forcing time-consuming re-exports if the source is still available. Validating export integrity requires controls-engineering familiarity with the machine's proprietary file formats that is uncommon among disposal crews. Corrupted exports are relatively cheap to detect but costly if the data is irretrievable after the machine is scrapped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exported data sets whose source machine or configuration state cannot be determined
rows.plain: Make sure exported control data shows which machine produced it and what settings or condition the machine had at the time. Without this information, the data may be difficult to understand or trust.
rows.imp_rat: Undeterminable source machine for exported data is a traceability ambiguity peripheral to cartoning, and the ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc file naming and missing metadata mean source and state attribution frequently cannot be reconstructed reliably. Determining which machine and configuration an unlabeled data set came from wastes investigative time later. Attributing orphaned data sets to a source machine requires someone who knows the fleet's history and naming conventions. Ambiguous provenance can force re-exports or render data useless, wasting the effort spent capturing it.

rows.source_job: Erase proprietary data from machine controller
rows.stmt: Reduce the time lost because required administrative access to controller data is unavailable
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for permission to access and erase information from a machine’s control computer. This helps the machine be cleared of the previous owner’s private information sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay from unavailable administrative access to controller data is peripheral to the core cartoning job and consequences are minor; capped low by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard handover practice ensuring administrative access persists to disposal, so access availability is inconsistent and often discovered missing only at the moment of need. When a machine is decommissioned the administrative passwords and OEM login credentials are frequently lost or held by staff who have left, so retrieval or reset commonly stalls the export for days or weeks. Recovering or bypassing lost controller credentials often demands OEM technicians or specialist knowledge that the disposing site rarely retains at end-of-life. Emergency OEM callouts or credential-reset service fees add unbudgeted expense whenever access has lapsed.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of drainage delays caused by adhesive residue obstructing fluid drainage paths
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for leftover liquid to drain when glue residue blocks the drain paths. This helps the machine become ready for use sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Drainage delays from adhesive residue is a minor maintenance nuisance with low job relevance; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Adhesive buildup is a predictable, well-understood nuisance that experienced crews handle consistently. Hot-melt adhesive residue clogs drainage paths and slows fluid draining during teardown, adding delay. Clearing adhesive obstructions is fairly routine mechanical work needing modest maintenance skill. Extra solvent, labor and disposal of adhesive-laden fluids add minor cost.

rows.stk: Sustainability Officer / Environmental Health and Safety Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoning machine components whose material composition or regulatory status cannot be verified
rows.plain: Recycle or dispose of machine parts only when their materials and legal handling requirements can be confirmed. This helps prevent unsafe or improper disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable component material composition or regulatory status is a documentation-completeness gap tangential to the machinery's functional job (low relevance), with compliance-risk consequences that remain manageable here (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Older machines lack material declarations, so composition and regulatory status frequently cannot be confirmed. Verifying material composition or regulatory status of unlabeled components slows recycling/disposal routing. Identifying materials and their regulatory classification needs materials and compliance knowledge often absent from scrap crews. Unverifiable components may be routed to costlier hazardous-disposal streams unnecessarily.

rows.stk: Sustainability Officer / Environmental Health and Safety Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of staging for recyclable cartoning machine components awaiting pickup and facility acceptance
rows.plain: Spend less time holding recyclable machine parts while waiting for pickup and acceptance at the receiving facility. This helps the parts move to recycling sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Staging recyclable components for pickup is a disposal-logistics delay peripheral to cartoning (low relevance) with minor consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recycler pickup schedules are fairly established but acceptance delays for mixed materials still occur. The error is directly the staging duration while recyclables await pickup and facility acceptance, which varies with hauler scheduling. Little skill is needed beyond coordinating logistics with recyclers. Extended staging ties up floor space and may incur storage cost.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of serviceable components damaged while being removed from the cartoning machine
rows.plain: Damage fewer reusable parts while taking them out of the box-packing machine, so more of them can be used again.
rows.imp_rat: Serviceable components damaged during removal is a decommissioning-handling issue remote from the cartoning function (low relevance), with recoverable parts-value loss (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Under scrap-oriented removal practices, incidental damage to reusable parts is common and inconsistently avoided. Damaging parts during removal forces refurbishment or sourcing replacements, adding time to any resale prep. Extracting serviceable components intact requires careful, knowledgeable disassembly rather than fast teardown. Each part damaged in removal is lost resale/reuse value.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of nonserviceable components accepted for spare inventory or resale
rows.plain: Do not put broken or unusable parts into storage for future use or sale. This prevents people from receiving parts that will not work.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting nonserviceable components for spare inventory or resale is peripheral to the cartoning machinery job and its consequences are absorbable; per the batch ceiling it stays at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Absent formal serviceability testing, acceptance decisions vary widely by who happens to handle the teardown, so bad parts routinely slip through. Inspecting each component for serviceability adds substantial time when done properly, and rushed disposals skip it. Correctly distinguishing serviceable from nonserviceable cartoning parts demands experienced technicians who are rarely dedicated to end-of-life triage. Accepting dead stock into inventory or resale wastes storage and warranty cost, but that loss is diffuse and often unmeasured.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of recovered components lacking identity, configuration, or condition records
rows.plain: Keep records for more of the parts recovered for possible reuse, including what each part is, how it is set up, and what condition it is in. This helps people decide safely whether a part can be used again.
rows.imp_rat: Recovered components lacking identity records is an end-of-life documentation gap (low relevance) with low-stakes consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery is often done without systematic record capture, so many components arrive undocumented. Tagging each recovered component with identity and condition records adds handling time usually cut. Requires someone who can identify configuration and condition, expertise seldom applied at teardown. Unlabeled recovered parts lose resale value and cause rework, a real loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that no qualified buyer submits an acceptable offer within the disposition window
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that no suitable buyer will make an acceptable offer before the time allowed for selling the used machine runs out. This helps the machine find a buyer on time.
rows.imp_rat: No qualified buyer within a disposition window concerns end-of-life resale, not the cartoning job (low relevance), with limited operational consequences (low severity); ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Thin markets for specialized cartoning machinery make timely qualified offers genuinely uncertain. The disposition window is fixed, and finding a qualified buyer within it is often a slow, uncertain search. Reaching the right niche buyers requires broker networks and market access many disposers lack. No acceptable offer means scrapping at a loss or paying to hold, a direct financial hit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount the asking price differs from the machine's supportable secondary-market value
rows.plain: Set a asking price close to what the used cartoning machine is realistically worth today. This helps avoid pricing it too high or too low.
rows.imp_rat: Asking price diverging from supportable secondary-market value is a peripheral valuation error with absorbable consequence, held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Thin comparable data for niche packaging equipment makes any single asking price highly uncertain and inconsistently derived. Establishing a defensible market value takes time gathering comparables that most disposers do not invest. Valuing specialized cartoning machinery needs appraisal judgement and market knowledge rarely present in-house. Mispricing directly leaves money on the table or stalls the sale, a real and felt financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material discrepancies between the listed and actual machine condition, configuration, and included components
rows.plain: Make sure the machine’s advertised condition, setup, and included parts match what is actually present. Accurate details help buyers know what they are really getting.
rows.imp_rat: Material discrepancies between listed and actual machine condition rank below the prior batch; relevance and severity are held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Listings are compiled from stale documentation and memory, so material mismatches between paper and machine are common. Reconciling listed against actual configuration and included components is tedious and often deferred until a buyer disputes it. Accurate description requires someone who knows the machine's as-built state, which drifts from records after years of retrofits. Discrepancies trigger price adjustments, returns, and reputational cost, though these are absorbed rather than prevented.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine's cleaning and contamination status cannot be substantiated to a buyer
rows.plain: Make sure you can show the buyer that the used cartoning machine was cleaned properly and is free from contamination. This helps the buyer trust its condition before purchasing it.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to substantiate cleaning status to a buyer is a resale documentation gap peripheral to the job; ceiling limits both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning records are seldom retained specifically for disposal, so substantiation to a buyer is inconsistently available. Reconstructing cleaning evidence for a buyer after the fact takes time and may delay a sale. Producing defensible cleaning/contamination substantiation requires quality-system documentation expertise. Inability to substantiate cleanliness lowers resale value or forces re-cleaning costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of closing delays caused by incomplete or conflicting ownership-transfer documentation
rows.plain: Complete the sale of the used box-packing machine without delays caused by missing or conflicting papers about who owns it. This helps the buyer and seller finish the handover.
rows.imp_rat: Closing delays from ownership-transfer documentation are peripheral administrative friction relative to the core job of running cartoning machinery (low relevance), and the consequences are recoverable schedule slips (low severity). Ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established asset-transfer processes catch most gaps, though conflicting records still surface with some regularity. Incomplete or conflicting ownership documentation is a common cause of closing delays in asset transfers. Resolving documentation gaps draws on legal and procurement expertise that most organizations can access. Delays tie up capital and may incur legal fees but are bounded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records attributed to the wrong cartoning machine or service period
rows.plain: Keep stored records from being linked to the wrong cartoning machine or period of service. This helps people find the correct history for each machine.
rows.imp_rat: Archived records misattributed to the wrong machine is a recordkeeping error tangential to operation (low relevance) with limited consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset-tagging conventions catch most cases, though similar machines and reused IDs still cause misattribution. Correctly filing records to the right machine and period is quick when asset IDs are used consistently. Low skill needed; it is a matter of disciplined identifier use. Misattributed records cause occasional retrieval waste but little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to retrieve a requested retired-machine record during an audit or investigation
rows.plain: Find a requested record for a retired machine more quickly during an inspection or investigation. This helps people answer questions about the machine’s past.
rows.imp_rat: Slow retrieval of a retired-machine record during audit is an archival-access delay peripheral to cartoning (low relevance) with modest consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Where document management systems are in place retrieval is dependable, though legacy paper archives remain hit-or-miss. The error is retrieval time during an audit, which is short when records are indexed digitally but long for paper or scattered archives. Minimal skill needed to search a well-indexed archive. Slow retrieval consumes audit labor but rarely large direct spend.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved switchover schedule conflicts among production, quality, and maintenance teams before machine retirement
rows.plain: Resolve schedule conflicts between the production, quality, and maintenance teams before the old machine is retired. This helps the change happen without disrupting work or checks.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved switchover schedule conflicts among teams are peripheral to the core job, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Production, quality, and maintenance often reconcile schedules informally, leaving conflicts unresolved until they bite. Working through switchover conflicts across teams consumes meeting time before retirement. Cross-functional scheduling judgement is needed and typically held by a few coordinators. Unresolved conflicts cause line downtime and idle labor, a tangible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected operators, planners, and suppliers omitted from retirement notifications
rows.plain: Leave out fewer operators, people who plan the work, or suppliers when announcing that the machine will no longer be used. This helps everyone affected prepare for its retirement.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from retirement notifications is peripheral to the core cartoning job (low relevance) and its consequences are absorbable coordination gaps (low severity); ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Distribution lists are frequently incomplete, so some affected parties are routinely missed. Building the full recipient list takes time that rushed retirements shortchange. Identifying every affected operator, planner, and supplier requires organizational knowledge that varies by person. Omitted parties incur scramble and rework costs downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that retirement notifications arrive too late for stakeholders to adjust plans and contracts
rows.plain: Tell affected people about the machine’s retirement early enough for them to change their plans and agreements.
rows.imp_rat: Late retirement notifications are peripheral to the acquisition job with absorbable impact, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established retirement-planning calendars catch most cases, but informal ones still let timing slip. The core error is lead-time: notifications often arrive too late for stakeholders to adjust contracts, though planning cycles do give some buffer. Knowing which stakeholders need how much lead time takes coordination judgement that experienced planners generally have. Late notice forces rushed contract changes and expediting costs that are avoidable with earlier warning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting cartoning machine retirement dates communicated to stakeholders
rows.plain: Make sure everyone receives the same date for when the case-packing machine will be retired. Clear information helps people plan for its replacement or removal.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting retirement dates communicated to stakeholders is a minor ambiguity error, capped at 1.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: A single authoritative retirement date is often absent, so multiple versions circulate through email and meetings. Resolving conflicting dates takes back-and-forth that delays finalization. Reconciling dates is coordination work needing organizational awareness more than technical skill. Conflicting dates cause misplanned resources but rarely large direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of retirement notifications that omit impacts on production plans or supplier contracts
rows.plain: Make sure retirement notices explain any effects on production schedules or supplier agreements. This matters so affected people can prepare and make needed changes.
rows.imp_rat: Notifications omitting production or contract impacts are downstream administrative errors (low relevance) with manageable fallout (low severity); ranking forces 1.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Notifications commonly state the retirement fact without the impact analysis, leaving recipients to infer consequences. Detailing production and supplier-contract impacts in each notice adds preparation time often skipped. Articulating downstream impacts needs someone who understands both the line and the contracts, a rare combination. Omitted impacts cause supply and planning disruptions with real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that retirement schedule changes fail to reach previously notified stakeholders
rows.plain: Make sure any changes to the machine’s retirement date or plan reach everyone who was told about it earlier.
rows.imp_rat: Retirement schedule changes not reaching notified stakeholders is a low-stakes communication error, capped at 1.0.
