rows.sat_rat: Without a maintained notified-party registry, updates inconsistently reach everyone originally informed. Propagating schedule changes to prior recipients is quick if a distribution list exists, slow if reconstructed each time. Little specialized skill is needed, just disciplined follow-through that is often lacking. Missed updates cause misaligned plans and wasted preparation, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of secondary-market listings or sales used as comparables despite material differences in configuration, age, or regulated-use history
rows.plain: Use only comparable used-equipment sales when estimating what the machine is worth. Differences in setup, age, or history in regulated work can make a comparison misleading.
rows.imp_rat: Using poorly matched comparables in valuation is peripheral with modest consequence, capped at 1.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Comparable selection is ad hoc and unregulated, so unlike listings are routinely treated as equivalent. Screening comparables for material differences takes analysis time that quick resale postings bypass. Recognizing when age, configuration, or regulated-use history invalidates a comparable requires market expertise that is scarce. Poor comparables distort pricing, causing lost value or unsold assets, a cost typically eaten later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hidden wear or damage is missed during the condition assessment
rows.plain: Find hidden wear or damage before deciding what the machine is worth, so its value is not judged too high.
rows.imp_rat: Missing hidden wear during condition assessment is a lower-ranked error here; both relevance to the CFJ and severity are held at the floor by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual-only assessments without teardown or instrumentation frequently miss latent faults, making outcomes highly inconsistent. Thorough condition assessment that catches hidden wear takes far longer than the quick visual walk-down typically performed at disposal. Detecting concealed wear in drives, cams, and servos requires deep machine-specific expertise that is scarce and seldom applied to retiring equipment. Missed damage is discovered later by the buyer, imposing rework or return costs, but upfront diagnostics are viewed as avoidable spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which remaining useful life is overstated when technical obsolescence is overlooked
rows.plain: Avoid claiming that the machine will remain useful longer than it really will because its technology may become outdated. This helps produce a more realistic estimate of its remaining value.
rows.imp_rat: Overstating remaining useful life via overlooked obsolescence ranks below the prior batch; relevance and severity stay at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Without a structured obsolescence review, remaining-life claims are routinely optimistic and unverified. Assessing technical obsolescence against remaining life is an extra analysis step usually skipped under disposal time pressure. Judging obsolescence of controls, parts availability, and regulatory fit demands specialist foresight that few disposal handlers possess. Overstated remaining life inflates carrying value and misleads buyers, but the cost lands downstream and is rarely charged back.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unresolved contamination status restricts physical inspection of the decommissioned machine
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that unknown pollution or chemical contamination prevents people from safely examining the retired machine. This matters when deciding what the machine is still worth.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contamination status restricting inspection has some severity but sits below the prior batch, so both proxies are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: In food/pharma cartoning contexts contamination status is often poorly documented, so inspection restrictions arise unpredictably. Unresolved contamination holds up physical inspection until clearance is obtained, stretching the disposal timeline. Assessing and clearing contamination status requires EHS specialists and documented cleaning validation not always on hand. Delays and re-cleaning to clear inspection add labor and holding costs.

rows.source_job: Remove machine anchors and foundation fixtures
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of broken anchors and mounting-bolt remnants left embedded in the floor after cartoning-machine removal
rows.plain: Avoid leaving broken metal fasteners stuck in the floor after removing the box-packing machine. This makes the floor safer and easier to repair or reuse.
rows.imp_rat: Broken anchors and bolt remnants left in the floor after removal is a decommissioning cleanup detail tangential to the cartoning job (low relevance), with minor rework cost (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Anchors frequently shear off during removal, a common and expected outcome even with competent crews. Leaving broken anchors means later grinding or coring work that extends restoration. Cleanly extracting embedded anchors and bolts takes skilled use of the right tools rather than brute force. Remnant removal and floor patching add labor and material cost.

rows.source_job: Remove machine anchors and foundation fixtures
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of floor damage extending beyond the original fixture and grout footprints during cartoning-machine site restoration
rows.plain: Limit damage to the floor so it does not spread beyond the areas originally occupied by the machine supports and their filler material. This reduces repair work when the machine is removed.
rows.imp_rat: Floor damage beyond the original footprint during site restoration is a removal/facilities concern peripheral to the cartoning function (low relevance), with limited repair cost as consequence (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Damage extent depends heavily on crew care, so outcomes are variable but generally containable. Repairing floor damage beyond the footprint adds restoration time to the project. Removing fixtures without spreading damage into surrounding floor requires careful demolition skill that varies by crew. Extra floor damage means larger patch/grind/resurface bills.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, plant manager, finance manager
rows.source_job: Terminate service contracts for the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active maintenance, software subscription, and support agreements tied to the retired cartoning machine that are overlooked
rows.plain: No current maintenance, software-use, or support agreement for the retired box-packing machine should be missed. This helps prevent paying for services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked maintenance/subscription agreements on a retired machine is a decommissioning administrative oversight (low relevance) yielding minor ongoing charges (low severity). Held at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Agreements tied to a specific machine are rarely centrally tracked, so some are consistently missed at disposal. Overlooked agreements surface later, forcing reactive cleanup rather than timely termination. Assembling a complete register of agreements tied to one asset requires cross-departmental diligence. Overlooked active agreements keep billing money for a retired asset.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, plant manager, finance manager
rows.source_job: Terminate service contracts for the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that termination notices miss contractual deadlines and trigger renewal or additional charges
rows.plain: Send cancellation notices before the contract deadlines so the service does not renew or create extra charges.
rows.imp_rat: Missed termination-notice deadlines triggering renewal charges is a contract-management error remote from cartoning operation (low relevance), with the consequence being a modest financial charge (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual calendar tracking of contract deadlines commonly fails, so missed notices are a recurring, costly surprise. Missing a termination deadline is instantaneous, but the resulting renewal charges force time-consuming disputes. Tracking notice windows is administrative but demands vigilance across many contracts. A missed notice directly triggers renewal fees or extra charges, a real cost hit.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, plant manager, finance manager
rows.source_job: Terminate service contracts for the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing required maintenance, software access, or support before cartoning machine decommissioning activities are complete
rows.plain: Keep needed maintenance, software access, and help available until the machine is fully taken out of service. This prevents the work from being delayed or left unsupported.
rows.imp_rat: Losing support before decommissioning is a lifecycle logistics error only loosely tied to the cartoning job; ceiling limits both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Decommissioning timelines rarely align with contract expiry tracking, so needed access is often lost unpredictably before work finishes. Support or software licenses can lapse mid-decommissioning, causing waits while access is renewed or renegotiated. Tracking which support and software entitlements are still needed through decommissioning requires contract and technical judgement spread across departments. Losing support before completion can force premature license repurchase or costly workarounds.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, plant manager, finance manager
rows.source_job: Terminate service contracts for the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved disputes over final fees, credits, or other contractual obligations tied to the retired cartoning machine
rows.plain: Settle disagreements quickly about the final charges, credits, or other duties connected with the packaging machine that is no longer being used. This helps both sides finish ending the service contracts.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved fee or credit disputes on a retired machine are contractual back-office matters (low relevance) with absorbable consequences (low severity); ranking limits to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contract terms resolve most obligations, but ambiguous end-of-life clauses still spawn lingering disputes. The error is the duration disputes stay unresolved, which drags when contract terms are ambiguous. Resolving fee and credit disputes needs contractual and negotiation skill usually available through procurement. Prolonged disputes tie up payments and incur administrative and legal cost.

rows.stk: Procurement manager, plant manager, finance manager
rows.source_job: Terminate service contracts for the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of billing after the intended cancellation date because contract termination remains unconfirmed
rows.plain: Stop charges for the machine from continuing long after the service was meant to end because the cancellation has not been confirmed. This prevents paying for service that should already be finished.
rows.imp_rat: Billing beyond the cancellation date due to unconfirmed termination is an administrative billing waste, peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance), producing minor recoverable cost (low severity). Ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Without confirmation tracking, lapsed cancellations recur and are often caught only on later invoices. Billing continues past the intended cancellation date while termination remains unconfirmed, prolonging charges. Confirming termination is routine administrative follow-up. Unconfirmed cancellations mean paying for services no longer used, a direct financial leak.
